They are also a common concealment.” He set the tablet down and picked up another. “Awil-Sin’s caravan lodged at the southern inn for two nights before departure. The innkeeper’s declaration lists his guests: the merchant, four drivers, two guards, and one female member of his household, described as a niece travelling to family in the upper district.”

I looked at him. “You think—”

“I think the niece arrived at the inn on the same evening that Kirum-Adad’s household received the linen merchant’s family for the preliminary gift exchange. I think the niece carried a cedar box and wore a mourning-woman’s veil that would have obscured her face to any casual observer. And I think,” he said, rising from the floor with the peculiar economy of movement that was characteristic of him in those moments when the calculation had resolved, “that a young woman clever enough to arrange all of this in four days is a young woman we should speak with honestly, rather than return to her father without first understanding what we are returning her to.”

He paused in the doorway of the gatehouse. Outside, the market was loud with the particular mid-morning chaos of competing declarations and the argument of donkeys.

“She left a trail, Shulgi-erin. Enough of a trail to be found, but not so obvious a trail that a careless man would find it. She left it deliberately. She wanted to be found — but only by the right person, and only after enough distance had been established to make negotiation necessary rather than optional.”

He walked out into the market noise and I followed him, because I always followed him, because there was nothing else a man with eyes in his head could reasonably do.

The third caravan was the difficulty.

Awil-Sin’s caravan was northbound on the Haran-Harsamna road, three days ahead of us, which was beyond any practical possibility of direct pursuit. But the cargo declared for Awil-Sin’s second overnight stop — a waystation two days north of Haran where several routes converged — showed a transfer. A sealed cedar chest, re-declared as personal effects belonging to a merchant’s widow, had been loaded onto a westbound pack-train bound for the sanctuary precinct at the edge of the upper district, where the temple of the moon-god maintained a house of refuge for women under the god’s protection.

A house of refuge. I understood, then, why Imgur-Enlil had looked at the window when Kirum-Adad mentioned his wife’s death.

The personal seal — belonging to her mother — was what had made it possible. A woman carrying her deceased mother’s seal could present herself at a temple refuge as the widow of a merchant household, placing herself under divine protection. The temples did not return women under divine protection without the woman’s consent and a formal proceeding. The law, in this matter, was deliberately slow. Iltani had known this. She had planned around it with a precision that I found, against my own expectations, deeply admirable.

“The seal was the key,” Imgur-Enlil said, as we walked. “Her mother must have told her. Or she found the old records herself — she is, by all accounts, literate. The cedar box, the veil, the false niece-story, the waystation transfer — any one of those steps might have been improvised. But the seal was known in advance. She had been preparing this for months.”

“Her father does not know.”

“Her father,” Imgur-Enlil said, with a quietness that carried weight, “believes the contract was a good arrangement. He is not wrong by the measures he uses. The linen merchant’s son is prosperous, the alliance is advantageous, and Kirum-Adad is a man who understands commerce better than he understands his daughter. These are not unusual conditions.” He was quiet for a moment. The road north of Haran smelled of dust and thyme and the dried-dung smoke of the waystation ahead. “What did you think of him? When he described what was missing from the room?”

I considered. “He listed the cedar box before the daughter’s clothing.”

“Yes,” Imgur-Enlil said. “He did.”

The sanctuary precinct occupied a walled compound at the edge of the upper district, built around a courtyard where several ancient trees provided shade that the rest of the city had long since cut down. The gate-keeper was an elderly woman with the particular immovable authority of someone who had stood between frightened people and the world for several decades. She informed us that the sanctuary did not confirm or deny the presence of those under the god’s protection, and that any inquiry regarding a protected person must be submitted as a formal petition to the temple administration, which would be reviewed at the next full assembly of priests, which was in eleven days.

Imgur-Enlil thanked her with complete sincerity. Then he asked whether he might sit in the courtyard shade for a moment, as the road had been long, and whether the sanctuary might have water to offer a traveller.

The gate-keeper, after a considering pause, allowed it.

We sat in the courtyard shade. Imgur-Enlil drank the water that was brought to us. He did not look toward the inner buildings. He sat with his eyes half-closed and the particular quality of stillness about him that I had come to understand was not rest but its opposite — a listening so complete that it required the body to be entirely quiet.

After a time, a young woman came out of the inner buildings and crossed the courtyard toward the well. She was perhaps twenty years old, and she walked with the careful composure of someone who had been frightened and had chosen, deliberately, to stop showing it. She wore plain linen, but her hands, when she lifted the water vessel, were the hands of a household that had owned cedar boxes.

Imgur-Enlil said, quietly and without looking up: “The linen merchant’s son — does he know the contract was sealed without your consent, or was he told that you had agreed?”

The young woman went very still.

“I ask,” he continued, in the same mild tone, “because it determines what kind of man we are dealing with, and I prefer to have accurate information.”

After a long pause, she set down the water vessel and sat on the edge of the well, and told us. The son had been told she had agreed. He was, she said, not unkind — she did not wish him harm — but the arrangement had been made between fathers, and no one had asked her, and she had understood from the moment the gift exchange was proposed that no one intended to ask her. She had spent four months observing the sanctuary’s procedures through the enquiries of other women she knew. She had waited until she had her plan complete before she moved.

Imgur-Enlil listened to all of this without interruption. When she finished, he was quiet for a moment. Then he said: “You left a trail that could be followed by a careful reader of cargo records, but not by a careless one. You wished to be found, but on your terms.”

“I wished to be found,” she said carefully, “by someone who would understand the difference between a daughter who is lost and a daughter who has chosen her location.”

Imgur-Enlil looked at her for a moment with an expression I had rarely seen on his face — something that was not quite admiration and not quite recognition, but occupied the space between them.

“Your father,” he said, “asked me to find you. He did not ask me to return you. That is a distinction he did not consider making, but it is one I will make on his behalf, because it is the correct distinction.” He rose. “I will tell Kirum-Adad that you are safe and under the god’s protection, and that the formal petition must be submitted to the temple administration. This will take eleven days. During those eleven days, it would be reasonable for the linen merchant’s family to withdraw from the contract if they chose to do so, as the circumstances have raised questions about the original agreement. It would also be reasonable for Kirum-Adad, in that interval, to have a conversation with his daughter that he did not have before. Whether either of these things occurs is not my determination to make.”

He bowed to her — the small formal bow of the merchant house, one professional acknowledging another — and walked back toward the gate.

I rose to follow, then stopped. The young woman was watching him go with an expression I could not entirely read.

“Is he always like this?” she asked.

“I have found no instance,” I told her honestly, “when he was any other way.”

I do not know everything that passed between Kirum-Adad and his daughter in the eleven days that followed. I know that Imgur-Enlil delivered his report to the merchant house in terms so measured and so carefully constructed that the house-master received them as a successful resolution — which, by every formal measure, they were. The daughter had been located. She was safe. She was under divine protection, which was a matter for the temple administration. The matter was concluded.

I know that the linen merchant’s son withdrew from the contract before the formal petition was reviewed, on grounds recorded as “changed circumstances in the merchant’s household.” I know that Kirum-Adad paid a fee to the temple for his daughter’s period of sanctuary, which is the appropriate form, and that the fee was generous.

I know that Iltani, when she returned to her father’s household, brought with her a letter of commendation from the sanctuary’s chief priest, describing her as a woman of considerable administrative capability who had been of considerable assistance during her stay. I know that Kirum-Adad, who understood commerce better than he understood his daughter, recognised a commercial asset when one was placed in front of him, and adjusted his understanding accordingly.

These things I know because they appeared, in one form or another, in records that I kept.

What I did not record — what I set down here now, in this separate account, because it belongs to the truth of what happened rather than to the truth of what was declared — is what Imgur-Enlil said to me on the road back from the sanctuary precinct, in the late afternoon when the light was going copper and the dust of the road smelled of the day’s heat releasing.

He had been silent for some time, in the manner that meant he was not finished thinking. Then he said:

“She is more careful than her father. She will survive him by a considerable margin, in every sense that matters.” A pause. “She left enough trail to be found. She timed her departure to ensure that eleven days of formal procedure would intervene between discovery and return. She brought her mother’s seal, which gave her legal standing she could not otherwise have claimed. She arranged a transfer between two caravans at a waystation to break any direct line of inquiry.” He was quiet again. “She solved this problem months before it became acute. By the time she moved, every contingency had been addressed.”

“She is remarkable,” I agreed.

“She is,” he said. “And she did all of this — all of this — in order to gain eleven days in which she could be spoken to as a person rather than transferred as a commodity.” He looked ahead at the road, at the city of Haran assembling itself out of the dust on the horizon. “Eleven days. That is what the entire architecture was purchased to provide. Eleven days and a conversation.”

The bitterness in his voice was not for her. I had known him long enough to understand that.

I did not write that part down, the first time. I write it now because it is the true record, and because the ledgers — which show only what was declared — have never been sufficient to explain what was real.

So records Shulgi-erin, in the year of the moon-god’s dry festival, that those who read the cargo tablets may know what the cargo tablets do not say.

Children:

- Iddiyatum — see next entry.

IDDIYATUM

~1930 BC — Haran, Mesopotamia

He has been granted — the name a statement of gratitude for a gift that the family had possessed for generations, though they had no word for it that distinguished it from intelligence in general or from the scribal craft in particular. Iddiyatum was a merchant’s scribe in Haran, bilingual in Sumerian and Akkadian at the precise moment when the old Sumerian tongue was fading from daily use into the ceremonial register — the language of the temple and the law, no longer of the market or the household — and Akkadian was asserting itself as the lingua franca of the entire Near East.

He understood what the shift meant. A language dying is not merely a cultural event but a political and commercial one: the contracts, the legal codes, the diplomatic correspondence of three centuries were encoded in a tongue that fewer and fewer people could read with confidence, and the men who could read it fluently — who could move between the old legal formulae and the new administrative language without loss of meaning — were suddenly invaluable. Iddiyatum cultivated that fluency deliberately, treating the death of Sumerian as an opportunity rather than a loss, and built a practice as a legal translator and document interpreter that drew clients from across the upper Euphrates trading world. He read texts the way his great-great-grandfather had read grain tallies: for the discrepancy, the alteration, the carefully introduced ambiguity that changed a document’s meaning without changing its surface.

In the marketplace archives of Haran, a record keeper named Enzu was tasked with preserving copies of legal filings and commercial agreements. Enzu’s role was to record faithfully what was presented, though he did not always grasp the subtleties that lay beneath the text. His tablets, preserved among the merchant house records, capture not only the proceedings but the sequence of discovery as Iddiyatum examined the contested contract.

The Matter Of The Estate Of Ibbi-Adad

Transcribed by Enzu, Keeper of Records, from his own observations and from conversations with the principals, in the thirty-first year of the city-lord of Haran

I have been a keeper of records for eleven years, and in that time I have witnessed the filing of perhaps four thousand tablets. I have seen disputes over barley and disputes over silver, disputes over the ownership of an irrigation ditch and disputes over the paternity of a donkey. I have seen men weep and men shout and men fall silent in the way that men fall silent when they understand that the clay has spoken against them and there is nothing more to be said.

I had not, before the matter of Ibbi-Adad’s estate, seen anything like Iddiyatum.

He came to the archive building on a morning in the month of Dumuzi, when the heat of the upper Euphrates valley had already settled over Haran like a wet linen cloth pressed against the face. He came quickly — he always moved quickly when a problem had hold of him, I would later learn — and he arrived with a document folded in linen under his arm and a look about his eyes that I can only describe as a kind of controlled fever.

He was a thin man, with large, ink-darkened hands that seemed too long for his wrists, and eyes that moved across a tablet the way a falcon’s eyes move across a field: not randomly, not pleasantly, but with a specific and predatory patience. He wore a scribe’s robe, but it was not entirely clean, and one edge of it had been burned at some point and mended without particular care. He smelled of lamp oil and of something sharper — the bitumen and lime solution that scribes sometimes use when they wish to test whether a surface has been re-smoothed. He had been testing surfaces recently. He had been testing surfaces this very morning.

“You are the record-keeper,” he said. It was not a question.

I confirmed that I was Enzu, keeper of records for the district archive of Haran.

“Then you will have the registration copy of the testament of Ibbi-Adad the merchant, filed in the month of Nisannu, approximately sixty days before his death.”

I did. I went and found it.

The matter of Ibbi-Adad had come to the archive in the form of a dispute between two parties: Marduk-nasir, who was Ibbi-Adad’s nephew and his agent in the lapis lazuli trade out of Kanesh, and a woman named Amat-Ishtar, who had been Ibbi-Adad’s household manager for seventeen years and who appeared in the Akkadian version of the testament as the primary heir to the Haran property — the warehouse on the eastern quay, the two houses in the merchant quarter, and the silver reserves held against the third Kanesh caravan.

The Sumerian original of the testament, which Ibbi-Adad had dictated when he still had his full faculties, before the river sickness had taken his voice and then his strength over the following months, named Marduk-nasir as the heir to the Haran property. The Akkadian version, filed here with us, named Amat-Ishtar.

This discrepancy had produced the dispute, and the dispute had produced, after some weeks of unsuccessful local adjudication, the involvement of Iddiyatum, hired by Marduk-nasir to examine the translation.

Marduk-nasir himself had come with Iddiyatum that morning, though he stood some distance from him in the way that clients of formidable men sometimes stand — respectful, slightly afraid, not entirely certain what they have set in motion. He was a heavyset man in middle age, with a merchant’s careful eyes and a merchant’s careful hands, and he watched Iddiyatum move through the archive with an expression that mixed hope with something approaching apprehension.

“You have read the Akkadian filing,” I said to Iddiyatum, not as a question.

“I read it last night.” He had laid the linen-wrapped document on my table and was examining the registration copy that I had produced, holding it at an angle to the light from the high window — the window faced north, which was how archive buildings were designed, to avoid direct sun on the tablets. “And the Sumerian original this morning, at the temple library, where a copy is held. Sit down, Enzu. I will need you to follow this carefully.”

I sat.

“The testament of Ibbi-Adad was dictated in Sumerian and inscribed by a temple scribe named Ur-Namma — a good scribe, trained in the old style, from a school that still teaches the traditional sign forms. He was the only person present of sufficient literacy to record a testament of this complexity at the time Ibbi-Adad dictated it, since the merchant’s own household scribe had died of the river sickness two weeks previously and had not yet been replaced.”

“That is my understanding as well,” I said.

“The Sumerian text names Marduk-nasir as the heir to the Haran property, with a gift of silver to Amat-Ishtar in recognition of her service. This is consistent with everything known about Ibbi-Adad’s commercial intentions — Marduk-nasir runs the Kanesh operation, which is the commercial future of the estate, and the Haran property is the base from which the Kanesh operation is managed. To give Haran to anyone but Marduk-nasir would be to cut the root from the tree.” He set down the registration copy and turned to look at Marduk-nasir briefly. “Your uncle was not a sentimental man in commercial matters.”

“He was not,” Marduk-nasir agreed, with the slight hoarseness of a man who has been saying this for weeks without being believed.

“The Akkadian filing reverses this arrangement. Amat-Ishtar receives the Haran property; Marduk-nasir receives a share of the Kanesh profits, contingent on the caravan’s success — which means contingent on Amat-Ishtar’s continued goodwill, since she now controls the Haran warehouse that outfits the caravans.” Iddiyatum set the two documents side by side on the table and stepped back, looking at them with his arms folded. “The question before us is whether this reversal represents the genuine final wishes of Ibbi-Adad, expressed in a second Sumerian draft that was then translated, or whether it represents a fraudulent alteration of the single existing Sumerian original.”

I looked at the two tablets. To my eye, they were simply two tablets. The signs were well-formed on both. The clay was dry and hard on both.

“I cannot see any —” I began.

“No,” he said, not unkindly. “You would not, unless you knew precisely where to look. That is not a criticism. You are trained to verify that the forms are present, not to verify that the forms are authentic. Those are different skills.” He drew the lamp from its bracket on the wall — the lamp smelled of sesame oil, which was the better quality, not the fish-oil that some archives used — and held it over the Akkadian filing. “Come and look here. Not at the text. At the clay itself, in the region of the third and fourth lines.”

I came and looked. He tilted the lamp so that the light ran almost parallel to the surface.

And then I saw it.

In the region of the third line, where the name of the heir was inscribed, there was a difference in texture that was almost invisible in ordinary light but clear in the raking lamp-glow: a slight flatness, a smoothness that was not quite the same as the surrounding surface. Not the smoothness of a well-made tablet, but the smoothness of a surface that had been gently worked while the clay was still at a particular stage of dryness — not wet enough to accept deep re-inscription without visible damage, but not so dry as to crumble. There was a craft to it. There was real knowledge of clay in it.

“The original sign in that position was the cuneiform for Marduk-nasir’s name-sign cluster,” Iddiyatum said. “It was smoothed — very carefully, with a wet reed applied with extreme patience — and Amat-Ishtar’s name-sign cluster was inscribed in its place. The surrounding signs were left untouched, which is why the document reads coherently. Only this one substitution was necessary to transform the entire inheritance.”

“But —” I was still looking at the tablet. “How can you be certain the name was changed and not simply that this region of the clay was imperfect in manufacture?”

He smiled, and it was the smile of a man who had been waiting for precisely this question.

“Because,” he said, “of the line-spacing. Look at the line above and the line below. The scribe who made this filing — a man named Warad-Sin, who works from the scribal house near the west gate — has a consistent habit: he compresses his signs slightly when he approaches the right edge of a tablet and has too many signs remaining for his line. It is the mark of a scribe who learned on narrower tablets than he now uses. You can see this compression in lines two, five, and six of this filing. But in line three — the line where the name appears — there is no compression. The spacing is generous. More generous than Warad-Sin’s hand ever produces naturally.” He set the lamp back in its bracket. “Because the name that replaced the original was shorter. Amat-Ishtar’s name-sign cluster is shorter than Marduk-nasir’s. The forger did not account for the spacing. He could not, without re-inscribing the entire line, which would have been visible. So he left it, and the generosity of the spacing is itself the evidence.”

Marduk-nasir had come forward during this explanation. He was staring at the tablet with an expression I had not seen on a man’s face before: the expression of someone who has been telling the truth for weeks and has only now, in the presence of someone who can demonstrate it, begun to truly believe himself.

“Then it was Warad-Sin,” he said. “Amat-Ishtar hired Warad-Sin to alter —”

“I said the alteration was made by someone with knowledge of clay and scribal technique,” Iddiyatum said. “I did not say it was Warad-Sin. Sit down. There is more.”

He had, I should record, been in the archive for only a little over an hour at this point. He had arrived at the second meal-time of the morning; it was not yet midday. He had not eaten anything, so far as I observed. He did not appear to notice this.

He had also sent me, twenty minutes after his arrival, to fetch a particular class of document from the storage room: specifically, the filings of commercial agreements that Amat-Ishtar had witnessed or countersigned in the previous three years. There were eleven of them. He had gone through them in the way I now understood he went through all documents — at an angle to the light, quickly and then slowly, quickly for the content and slowly for the surface. He had said nothing while he read them, which I had taken as a neutral sign. It was not.

“Enzu,” he said, “what can you tell me about the scribal training of Amat-Ishtar?”

I stared at him. “She is a household manager. I would not expect her to have —”

“She countersigned three of these documents. Not in the way a woman of ordinary education makes a mark — not a seal impression alone, not a thumbprint. She inscribed her own name in cuneiform. Correctly, in both the Sumerian and Akkadian orthographic conventions, in two of the three documents.” He held up the relevant tablets. “She received scribal training. Not full scribal training — she did not go through the edubba school — but training sufficient to read a testament, to understand what had been written in it, and to understand what would need to change.” He set them down. “She did not hire Warad-Sin. She did not need to. She did it herself.”

The silence in the archive room was very complete. Outside, in the street, I could hear the water-seller calling his price and the distant complaint of a donkey being loaded at the quayside.

“She had access to the filing?” I asked, because someone had to ask it.

“She was named as a witness to the original Sumerian dictation — one of three present. Ur-Namma inscribed it; Ibbi-Adad dictated it; Amat-Ishtar and two merchants of the district were present as witnesses. When the filing was brought here for registration, it was carried by Amat-Ishtar herself, as Ibbi-Adad was by that time too ill to leave the house. She was alone with it from the moment she left the sickroom to the moment it was placed in these hands.” He gestured at the archives around us. “I do not know at what point on that journey she made the alteration. I suspect she had prepared the wet reed and the materials in advance. I suspect she had practiced on scrap clay. The alteration is too clean to have been improvised.”

Marduk-nasir stood up. His voice was controlled but only barely. “Then we bring this to the district judge. The evidence is —”

“Sit down,” Iddiyatum said again.

It was the third time he had said it, and this time there was something in his voice that was different. Not colder — it was not a cold voice, exactly, though it was precise — but quieter. Something had shifted in him. The fever-brightness had gone from his eyes and been replaced by something that I found, and still find, difficult to name.

He sat down himself, which he had not done before. He set both hands flat on the table, looked at them for a moment, and then looked at Marduk-nasir.

“Before we discuss what to do with this evidence,” he said, “you will tell me about the original testament.”

What came out of Marduk-nasir in the next quarter-hour was not given willingly. It came out the way water comes from pressed cloth: slowly, then in a rush, then slowing again as another layer of resistance was encountered.

Iddiyatum pressed him with questions, but the questions were not the questions I would have asked — not questions about the theft, not questions about Amat-Ishtar. They were questions about Ibbi-Adad himself. About the seventeen years of Amat-Ishtar’s service. About what kind of service it had been. About the nature of her position in the household. About whether there had been a period — perhaps eight years ago, perhaps ten — when Ibbi-Adad’s business had failed, when a caravan had been lost to raiders on the Kanesh road, when silver had been desperately needed.

Marduk-nasir answered carefully, and then less carefully, and then not carefully at all.

Ibbi-Adad had faced ruin eleven years ago. A caravan carrying three years of accumulated lapis lazuli profit had been taken on the Kanesh road, and he had owed silver to the trade associations that he could not pay. He had faced the complete dissolution of his merchant house.

Amat-Ishtar had saved it. Not with her own silver — she had no silver of her own — but with the silver of her family, her father’s estate in the wool trade, which she had obtained by a method that Marduk-nasir described with increasing difficulty as the interview went on. Her father had not given the silver willingly. Her father had been shown certain documents that Ibbi-Adad held — documents relating to a boundary dispute in which her father had used a surveyor’s false measurement — and her father had understood that the silver was the price of those documents remaining private. The transaction had been entirely legal, in the sense that no law had been broken in its execution, and entirely extortionate, in the sense that it had destroyed her father’s ability to do business for three years and had contributed, Marduk-nasir believed, to his death from a sickness of the liver the following year.

Amat-Ishtar had known what was done. She had been present when Ibbi-Adad made the arrangement with her father. She had come to work in Ibbi-Adad’s household six months after her father’s death, with no family remaining and no resources, because there was nowhere else to go.

She had worked in his household for seventeen years.

The room was very quiet when Marduk-nasir finished.

“And the Sumerian testament,” Iddiyatum said. “Which leaves her a gift of silver. Is the silver sufficient to establish her independently?”

Marduk-nasir looked at the table. “It is... a modest gift. Appropriate to service.”

“Appropriate to seventeen years of service performed by a woman of scribal education who managed a complex merchant household and whose father’s life and estate were taken to save the business she spent those seventeen years managing.”

Marduk-nasir said nothing.

“I see.” Iddiyatum stood up. He stood for a moment with his back to both of us, looking at the high north window of the archive room, at the rectangle of white sky above the Haran rooftops. The cedar-resin smoke drifted past him in a thin thread.

Then he turned around.

“I have examined the documents thoroughly,” he said. “My examination reveals that the Akkadian filing is consistent with the Sumerian original, within the expected variations of translation between a ceremonial and a commercial register.” He began wrapping his own documents in their linen. “The spacing variation in line three is attributable to Warad-Sin’s habit of adjusting his sign-size when a name cluster is of unusual length. The surface texture variation is a manufacturing irregularity in the clay batch, which I have observed in two other tablets from the same archive period. There is no evidence of alteration.”

Marduk-nasir was on his feet. “That is not — you cannot —”

“I am telling you what my examination found.” Iddiyatum looked at him with the expression of a man who is not angry, which in the circumstances was more difficult to bear than anger would have been. “I am also telling you that if you pursue this matter further, through another examiner or through direct complaint to the judge, I will be compelled to share with that examiner or that judge the full scope of my research — including the commercial history of the Ibbi-Adad merchant house, including the circumstances of the silver loan eleven years ago, including the documents that remain, I believe, in the household archive, now in the possession of Amat-Ishtar. Documents which may bear examination in their own right.” He finished wrapping the linen and tucked it under his arm. “I do not think that outcome would be in the interest of anyone connected to the Ibbi-Adad name. But I leave the choice entirely to you.”

He thanked me for the use of the archive. He asked me to record the finding as inconclusive, with no fraud detected. He left.

The following is added in Enzu’s hand, in a different ink, apparently composed some time after the primary account.

I have thought about that morning many times in the years since. I have thought, in particular, about the moment when Iddiyatum stood at the window. I do not know what he was thinking, but I think I know what he was weighing.

On one side: the law. The document was altered. The heir named is not the heir chosen. The property belongs, by the testament’s true meaning, to Marduk-nasir.

On the other side: a woman who spent seventeen years serving the man who destroyed her father. Who educated herself, quietly, in a craft she had no school to teach her — taught herself to read the contracts she filed, to understand the testament she witnessed, to see with her own eyes what had been written about her. Who, when the moment came, found the only instrument she had — a wet reed, a dry tablet, patience, and perfect knowledge of what needed to change — and used it.

The law would have given the property to Marduk-nasir and sent Amat-Ishtar into the street with a modest silver gift and a closed case. Iddiyatum gave her the warehouse on the eastern quay, the two houses in the merchant quarter, and what remained of her life.

He was paid, I should note, by Marduk-nasir. Marduk-nasir did not receive a refund.

I have never decided whether what Iddiyatum did was just. I have decided that it was something, and that the something is worth recording, and that the official record — which reads: no alteration detected, case closed — is not the whole of what happened in the archive room on that morning in the month of Dumuzi, in the heat, in the cedar-resin smoke, in the thirty-first year of the city-lord of Haran.

This is the whole of what happened. I have set it down in clay.

Let it endure.

Children:

- Ili-iddinam — see next entry.

ILI-IDDINAM

~1900 BC — Babylon, Mesopotamia

His name was the mixed thing his generation was — Amorite and Akkadian wound together, the name of the new dynasty that was rising in Babylon grafted onto the family’s Mesopotamian trunk. Ili-iddinam moved south from Haran to Babylon in early adulthood, following the Amorite dynasty’s consolidation of power in the great city on the Euphrates, reading in its rise the same signal his ancestor had read in the rise of Ur: this is where the world is organising itself, and this is therefore where a man of ability should be.

Babylon under the early Amorite kings was a city in the process of becoming what it would be under Hammurabi — the legal and commercial capital of the known world, the place where the great synthesis of Sumerian tradition and Amorite energy was producing something genuinely new. Ili-iddinam arrived as a scribe and rose quickly to become a man of property and local consequence, having applied his family’s gifts to the trade dealings of a city in its rising influence with the careful calculation of someone who understood that timing — the reading of when a place or a situation is about to become what it will be — was the essential prerequisite of every other form of intelligence.

Among the tablets that record these years is one kept by Nanna-iddin, a junior scribe in the service of the same house that employed Ili-iddinam in his early years. Nanna-iddin wrote in the careful hand of one who must account for every mark before it dries in the clay, and his record survives because he was thorough in copying contracts and because, in one particular season, he was asked to observe more than he understood. His account, preserved in a bundle of administrative tablets, does not praise Ili-iddinam but records what was done, and what was seen, and what was asked.

The Account Of Nanna-Iddin

Set down in the third year of Ili-iddinam’s tenure in that house, during the reign of Sumu-la-El of Babylon

I had been at my tablet since the first light, copying a grain-loan contract for the third time because my first impression of the reed had been too shallow and the second too deep, when the gate-warden called up from the street below that a man was dead in the counting house of Imgur-Adad the merchant, and that his apprentice had run through the Zababa quarter screaming like a temple-slave who has seen an omen. The smell the morning wind carried was not the smell of the canal, which we knew, nor of the slaughter-yard two streets east, which we also knew. It was the smell of spilled cedar oil mixed with something beneath it — something that had no name I wished to give it then.

Ili-iddinam was on the roof.

He spent the first hour of every morning on the roof of our house, not in prayer, as the household assumed, but in what I had learned by then was a form of active stillness — his eyes moving across the rooflines of Babylon the way a man reads a tablet he has nearly memorised, checking for what has changed, what is missing, what has been impressed too hard and is beginning to crack. He was already descending the stairs when I reached the bottom of them, his outer garment thrown over one shoulder and his reed case under his arm, as if he had been waiting for the gate-warden’s voice rather than startled by it.

“Imgur-Adad,” he said, not to me, but to himself, in the particular tone he used when he was adding a quantity to a calculation he had already begun.

“You know him?” I asked.

He looked at me briefly — that look, which I had not yet learned to interpret, which seemed to measure not my question but the gap between what I had noticed and what I had not. “I know his contracts,” he said. “Come.”

The counting house of Imgur-Adad occupied the ground floor of a mudbrick building on the Street of the Seal-Cutters, wedged between a workshop that produced cylinder seals for the great temple administrators and a narrow house of no apparent commercial purpose whose owner, I would later learn, Ili-iddinam had noted with interest some months before. The morning light had not yet reached the street properly; the buildings on the eastern side threw long shadows that made the lane feel like the passage between two cliff faces, and the canal water that seeped beneath the western foundations had left a dark tide-mark along the base of every wall.

Imgur-Adad lay on his side behind his counting table, one arm extended as if reaching for the nearest of the three clay tablets that lay on the table’s surface, his seal-ring still on his finger, the lamp beside him burned down to cold oil and ash. The lamp was the first thing Ili-iddinam examined. He crouched beside it and placed two fingers above the wick without touching it, then turned to look at the small high window that admitted the only outside air.

“The window,” he said, “faces north. The lamp burned through the night. A man who burns a lamp all night in a sealed counting house is a man who was working when he did not expect to be interrupted.”

Ku-Nannum, the officer of the watch who had arrived before us and who resented, as I would learn all such officers resented, the presence of Ili-iddinam at any scene he was called to examine, looked up from his own inspection of the body with an expression I can only describe as professionally unimpressed. “He is dead,” Ku-Nannum said. “Whether he worked at night or day is no question for the judge. The question is who entered.”

“The question is who entered, yes,” Ili-iddinam agreed pleasantly, and said nothing further for some time.

He moved around the table with the slow precision of a scribe approaching a tablet he has been told contains an error — certain that the error is there, uncertain only of where the reed slipped. He examined the tablets on the table’s surface without touching them, reading them, I understood, as documents and as objects simultaneously. He examined the door and its bolt. He examined the merchant’s hands, which he turned over gently and held up toward the north window’s light. He examined the floor near the cedar-oil lamp, where a thin stain had spread and dried, and crouched there for a long time.

Ku-Nannum, whose patience was that of a man who has stood in administrative lines his entire professional life and developed his patience as a fortification rather than a virtue, cleared his throat with significant authority. “The apprentice says his master had enemies,” he said. “A rival grain-merchant, Ea-gamil, who brought complaint against Imgur-Adad before the assembly only last month. I am told the assembly found against Ea-gamil and that he departed in a state of rage that the assembly scribe will confirm.”

“Ea-gamil,” Ili-iddinam said, without looking up from the floor.

“He is the obvious—”

“Yes,” Ili-iddinam said. “He is obvious.” He straightened, and the quality of his attention shifted in a way that always made me feel I was watching a man emerge from deep water — the slow return of focus to the world that was visibly present rather than the world he had been examining inside his own reasoning. He looked at the three tablets on the counting table. “You have read these?”

Ku-Nannum had not read them, because Ku-Nannum was not a scribe. He said so with the dignity of a man who considered this a professional division of labour rather than a limitation.

“I ask,” Ili-iddinam said, “because they are the whole of the matter.”

What followed — and here I must confess that Ili-iddinam required me to be present, which at the time I understood as clerical utility and later came to understand as something else entirely, as the need that certain extraordinary minds have for a fixed point, a known quantity, a face that represents the ordinary intelligence of the world they must translate their findings into — what followed was an explanation that proceeded with the slow, deliberate patience of a teacher who knows the student has not yet earned the full answer but who intends to arrive there by stages.

He laid the three tablets before us on the table and indicated each in turn.

“Imgur-Adad was party to three contracts that remained unsealed as of yesterday morning,” he said. “This one — a silver loan to a ship-owner, Awil-Marduk, secured against his next shipment from the south — was due for its first interest payment in seven days. It required only the parties’ seals and could be closed at any time. This one —” he moved to the second tablet “— a grain-storage agreement with the temple of Ishtar, through their representative administrator, required the co-seal of that administrator, who was available only on appointed days, the next of which was three days hence. And this one —” the third tablet “— a property surety lodged by a man named Warad-Sin in exchange for Imgur-Adad’s guarantee of his ability to meet his obligations before the city assembly.”

He paused, in the manner of a man who is certain the next step is visible to his audience and is waiting, not unkindly, for them to take it.

I did not take it.

Ku-Nannum did not take it.

Ili-iddinam showed no surprise at this. “Warad-Sin’s surety,” he continued, “was contingent on a clause I will show you here —” and he touched the relevant line with the tip of his reed, not pressing, not marking “— which specified that the guarantee would become void in the event of Imgur-Adad’s death before the seal-breaking of the first commercial day following the deposit of the surety tablet. The surety was deposited four days ago. The first commercial seal-breaking of the day occurs at sunrise. Imgur-Adad died —” he placed the two fingers above the lamp’s dead wick again, this time looking at us so we would understand the gesture “— some hours before dawn.”

The room had changed. Not in fact, but in the way a room changes when the thing that is wrong in it has been named.

“Warad-Sin,” I said, and heard my own voice as if I was listening to a scribe reading aloud a contract he had not written.

“Warad-Sin,” Ili-iddinam confirmed, “had deposited a surety he could not pay. He had done so knowing that if Imgur-Adad guaranteed him before the assembly and he subsequently defaulted, Imgur-Adad would pursue him to the value of his house, his inventory, and the labour of his sons. He could not meet the payment that would become due in eighteen days. He could not withdraw the surety without disgrace and the loss of every credit relationship in Babylon. But if Imgur-Adad died before the morning’s seal-breaking, the clause rendered the surety void. It was, from Warad-Sin’s position, a very precisely timed problem.”

“Ea-gamil had more obvious reason,” Ku-Nannum said, but his voice had taken on the quality of a man who argues a position he is no longer certain of, out of professional obligation rather than conviction.

“Ea-gamil,” Ili-iddinam said, “has a reason that everyone in the Zababa quarter knows, which means it is a reason that someone who intended to commit this act would wish to be known. Ea-gamil was also, as I happen to know because I reviewed a tablet record of debts for another client of this house two months ago, recently extended a significant loan by the temple administrators — a loan which provides him every incentive to remain respectable, quiet, and outside the attention of the judge. Angry men who make threats before assembly secretaries are rarely the men who act. They have spent their anger in the spending of words.”

He picked up his reed case. He looked at the extended hand of Imgur-Adad, still reaching toward the nearest tablet, with an expression that was, for one moment, not entirely mathematical.

“Warad-Sin,” he said, “will have oil on his sandal. Cedar oil, from this lamp —” the dark stain near the table’s foot “— which was knocked in the struggle and which the killer did not notice because the room was dark and because a man who is killing for the first time does not notice what he has stepped in. You will find the sandal. You will find it has not been cleaned because he had no reason to anticipate scrutiny and because his mind, since last night, has been occupied with survival rather than evidence.”

Ku-Nannum was already moving toward the door.

They found the sandal.

They found Warad-Sin with it still on his foot, sitting in his own counting house three streets away, working through his own tablets with the furious concentration of a man trying to perform normal life until normal life becomes real again.

I know, because I was required to attend as a witness to the reading of the relevant contract clauses before the junior judge, and I saw his face when Ili-iddinam entered the room behind Ku-Nannum. There was no surprise in it. There was something worse than surprise — recognition, the particular recognition of a man who has understood, in the interval since the act, that somewhere in the city there is a mind that would have known to look at the tablets first, and who had spent three streets and three hours wondering who that mind belonged to.

Ili-iddinam said nothing to him. He spoke only to the junior judge, and only about contracts, and only in the dry, precise language of clause and counter-clause that was his natural tongue. Ku-Nannum received the public acknowledgment, as was appropriate. The junior judge’s record names the city guard’s diligence. The temple of Ishtar’s administrator, reviewing the case months later, complimented the efficiency of the system.

I kept my own account, as I was in the habit of keeping, in the margins of the administrative tablets I was copying at the time, because the clay dries quickly and because some things, if they are not pressed into the record in the moment, leave no mark at all.

The cedar-oil smell stayed in the Street of the Seal-Cutters for three days. I know because I walked past it, those mornings, and Ili-iddinam walked past it also, on his way to and from the roof, and never once looked toward it. He had, I had begun to understand, no further use for a problem once it was solved. The finished tablet went to the archive. The question that had animated him was gone, and what remained in its place was — for a day, perhaps two — a particular quietness in his face, a stillness that was not peace but the absence of sufficient occupation, a lamp in a room with nothing yet to illuminate.

Three days later a new client arrived from Nippur with a question about a missing grain shipment, and the stillness was gone as if it had never been.

I recorded that too.

Here the account ends.
The tablets containing Nanna-iddin’s marginal notations were recovered from a private archive in the ruins near the old Zababa quarter. The main administrative tablets of the house survive in the standard temple and palace collections of the period. The cedar-oil stain on the Street of the Seal-Cutters is long gone, but the contracts remain.

Children:

- Awil-Marduk — see next entry.

AWIL-MARDUK

~1870 BC — Babylon, Mesopotamia

Man of Marduk — the name a declaration of allegiance to the god whose cult was rising with the city, whose elevation to king of the gods under Hammurabi would not occur for another generation but whose trajectory was already visible to anyone watching the religious politics of Babylon with the attention they deserved. Awil-Marduk watched them with precisely that attention. He served as a junior official in the expanding Babylonian administration — in the office responsible for the registration of property transactions, a role that placed him at the intersection of law, commerce, and political intelligence, because in Babylon in 1870 BC the disposition of property was a reliable index of the disposition of power.

His father, Ili-iddinam, had built a life on recognising when conditions were being held in artificial balance. Awil-Marduk inherited the same inclination, but his temperament was slower, more cumulative. Where his father moved from anomaly to conclusion with measured confidence, Awil-Marduk preferred to hold the anomaly in place, to extend it across time until it either dissolved or revealed itself as part of a larger structure. He did not rush to understand; he preferred to endure not knowing until understanding could no longer be avoided.

He worked under the supervision of Bel-ēṭir, a senior scribe whose authority rested on long familiarity with the register and an instinctive resistance to questioning patterns that disrupted established administrative expectations. Bel-ēṭir recorded transactions faithfully, but he was cautious in interpretation and impatient with speculation. He permitted Awil-Marduk access to the full archive in part because he considered it impractical to refuse, and in part because he assumed the younger man would tire of it before finding anything of consequence.

The Account Of Bel-Ēṭir

Copied from my private clay into this tablet at the insistence of no one — for I do not yet know whether I am recording a triumph of administration or a conspiracy of two men against a third, and until I am certain, I will record it only for myself.

The air in Babylon in the dry month is marked by the river retreating from its banks — exposed mud, baked brick, the particular sharpness of bitumen where the canal-linings crack in the heat — and it was in this season that Nur-Adad came.

He did not come in the morning, when the clerks were present and the reed-styli moved in their rows. He came at the hour when the lamp-oil burns lowest and the junior officials have gone home to their households. He came, in other words, at the hour when only Awil-Marduk remained.

This was already a thing worth noting. Awil-Marduk was still there because he was always still there, working through the archive by the particular quality of attention that I had initially taken for diligence and had gradually come to recognise as something more unsettling — the patience of a man who does not tire of not knowing, who treats each unanswered question as a kind of nourishment. I myself had gone to the inner room to seal the day's completed tablets, and so I heard what passed between them before Nur-Adad was aware that anyone else remained in the building.

Nur-Adad was a temple official — an accountant for one of the smaller annexes of the Esagila complex, the great temple of Marduk himself, whose priests were not yet the most powerful men in the city but whose ambitions ran considerably ahead of their formal authority. He was a man of middle years, comfortably housed, professionally unremarkable. I knew his file. He appeared in the register twelve times across six years, all transactions in order, all stamps present. He was, in the language of our office, a clean record.

He entered the archive trembling.

I heard him before I saw him — the unsteady footfall, the breathing of a man who had been rehearsing a confession long enough that he had exhausted the rehearsal and now faced the performance with nothing left. He settled onto the stool across from Awil-Marduk's worktable and set something down on the clay surface. I moved slightly to the doorway and saw: a clay tablet, small, unstamped, the writing dense and compressed in a hand that suggested deliberate anonymity — the specific effort of someone who normally writes differently.

"I have received three of these," Nur-Adad said. "Over sixty days."

Awil-Marduk did not look at the tablet immediately. This was characteristic of him — the deliberate delay, the refusal to grant the object its expected centrality. He looked instead at Nur-Adad's hands, which were still arranged on the table as though the tablet might move without warning.

"What do they say?" he asked.

"That certain transactions — in my administration of the annex — have not been reported with complete accuracy to the temple's senior accounts. That this discrepancy is known. That unless I arrange for a particular document to be sealed in a particular way — a land transfer, a field near the second canal — the matter will be brought to the Šangû."

The Šangû. The chief administrator of the Esagila. A man whose recorded patience with irregularity in subordinates was not extensive.

"And have you?" Awil-Marduk asked.

"Have I what?"

"Failed to report with complete accuracy."

Nur-Adad's hands moved. "There are... in the management of a temple annex... small adjustments, over time. Nothing that diminishes the god's portion. Redistribution of surplus. Reclassification of—"

"Yes," said Awil-Marduk quietly, which ended that particular speech. He picked up the tablet and read it without visible reaction, the way a man reads a property description — attending to particulars without committing to interpretation. Then he set it down and folded his hands.

"Sit," he said, though Nur-Adad was already sitting.

"I am sitting."

"Then remain sitting. You will want to sit for what I tell you next." He paused, and the lamp between them made his shadow large on the archive wall, laid over the stacked tablets like a second catalogue. "Leave the letter with me. Return to your household. Speak to no one of this, not your wife, not your deputy, not the annex administrator. Come back in fifteen days."

"Fifteen days — and the next letter may—"

"Will arrive in eleven days," said Awil-Marduk, "on the basis of the current interval. I know. Leave it with me."

He had not, as it happened, known precisely. When I pressed him on this later — I am not too proud to admit I pressed him, curiosity being, in a senior scribe, a professional obligation — he acknowledged that the interval had been estimated. But the estimate, he explained, was not arbitrary.

"Sixty days, three letters. The first at the beginning, the second at thirty days, the third at forty-five. The interval is contracting."

"Meaning urgency," I said.

"Meaning impatience," he corrected. "These are different things. Urgency is external — it belongs to the situation. Impatience is internal. It belongs to the sender."

I considered this. "You already believed you knew who sent them."

"I had a preliminary view."

"Before examining the tablet."

"Before examining the tablet in front of Nur-Adad," he said, which was not quite the same thing, and which I chose not to pursue at that moment.

In the fifteen days that followed, Awil-Marduk did not go to the Esagila. He did not question anyone employed by the temple, or approach the annex, or make any inquiry that could be classified as investigation. He sat in the archive. He worked through the register.

He had been working through the register for, by my rough accounting, nearly two years. Not all of it — the register of our office covered property transactions across the city, and even Awil-Marduk's patience had geographic limits. He was working, specifically, through the transactions connected to the temple complexes: the direct transactions, the secondary transactions of families with temple employment, and — this was the element I had not fully appreciated until later — the transactions of senior officials within those temples, recorded not under the temple's administrative designation but under their private names, as private citizens making private purchases.

It is a feature of our register that it does not distinguish between a man acting professionally and the same man acting personally. Both transactions are recorded. The register has no interest in the man's capacity at the moment of signing; it records only that he signed. This is, for the purposes of administration, a simplification. For the purposes of the kind of reading Awil-Marduk was engaged in, it was something considerably more useful.

I watched him work. He would take out a series of tablets — not sequentially, but according to a logic I could not initially follow — and lay them in arrangements on the worktable, and sit with them, and make no mark and speak no word. Then he would return them to their shelves in a different order from how they had arrived, and I understood that the different order was itself a notation, a physical annotation of a relationship he had identified and was now preserving in the archive's own structure.

On the eighth day, he asked me a question.

"Bel-ēṭir. The field near the second canal — the one the letters require Nur-Adad to seal. What is its history in the register?"

I knew the register better than Awil-Marduk. This had been my source of patience with him — I assumed that whatever he was doing, I could, at the moment it became relevant, locate the relevant record faster than he could. I was generally correct. I went to the section, ran my eye down the tablet column, and told him what I found.

"Three owners in twelve years. The first, a private citizen — deceased. The second, a merchant collective — dissolved. The third is the current holder of record: an official of the Esagila administrative staff."

"Name."

I read it.

Awil-Marduk was quiet for a moment. Then: "And who appointed Nur-Adad to his position at the annex?"

I was less quick with this, but I found it. "The same man."

"And who would receive the field, in the transfer Nur-Adad is being required to arrange?"

I looked at the letter. The designated recipient was a designated intermediary household — a form used in transactions where the true beneficiary wishes not to appear in the register by name.

"The trust cannot be traced through our records," I said.

"No," said Awil-Marduk. "But it can be traced through the pattern of other designated intermediary households that have received property from officials this same man appointed, over a period of four years, all of which trusts have subsequently dissolved and transferred their holdings to a single private residence outside the city walls." He paused. "I have been working through that pattern for some months. The field near the second canal would have been the fourteenth transfer."

I set the letter down on the table.

"He is blackmailing his own subordinate," I said.

"Testing him," Awil-Marduk corrected. "The letters are not from a blackmailer seeking money. The letter does not request money. It requests the completion of a property transaction that the official could, at any point, have simply ordered Nur-Adad to complete by administrative direction. He chose instead to construct this pressure. He wished to know whether Nur-Adad, under duress, would act with discretion or would seek help."

"He wished to know if Nur-Adad could be trusted."

"He wished to know if Nur-Adad could be controlled. These are related but distinct qualities." Awil-Marduk looked at the lamp between us. Its oil was burning with a slight impurity — there was something in the smell, a vegetable sharpness beneath the sesame — and the light it gave was fractionally yellow. "Nur-Adad came to me. This is now part of what the official knows, whether or not he yet knows it. Nur-Adad has demonstrated that when threatened, he seeks outside counsel rather than compliance. He is not, by the official's standards, controllable."

"Then Nur-Adad is in danger," I said.

"Nur-Adad was always in danger. He was in danger the moment this official appointed him, because the appointment was itself a form of possession. Men who are useful to this kind of network are not permitted to simply leave it. They are tested, then used, then disposed of when they cease to be manageable. Nur-Adad came to me at precisely the right time — not to escape the danger, but because his coming here has changed the nature of it."

I looked at him. "How?"

"Because now there is a record," said Awil-Marduk.

On the fifteenth day, Nur-Adad returned.

He was less visibly frightened. The fear had settled into him in the interval and become familiar, which is the way of things with sustained dread — it stops presenting as acute crisis and begins presenting as ordinary life, and ordinary life is something men have a remarkable capacity to endure.

Awil-Marduk received him without ceremony. He returned the anonymous letter, which Nur-Adad received with the caution one extends to a thing that has already caused harm.

"Complete the transaction," Awil-Marduk said.

Nur-Adad stared at him. "You are telling me to comply."

"I am telling you to complete the transaction, yes. Seal the document. Transfer the field."

"Then — the blackmail succeeds? He has what he wanted?"

"He has the field," said Awil-Marduk. "And he has the confirmation that you can be directed. Which is what he wanted from you." He folded his hands on the table. "And I have the transaction, which will now appear in the register under your name and his designated trust, as the fourteenth in a sequence that I have already mapped and filed in supplementary notation. The transaction itself is now part of the record. It cannot be made not to have happened."

Nur-Adad was quiet for a long moment. The sound from the street outside — the gate-hinge of a nearby compound, which had needed oiling for months and which creaked at each hour of significant movement — turned once, and was silent.

"What does that mean?" Nur-Adad asked. "For me."

"It means you are no longer the most dangerous position in this structure," said Awil-Marduk. "You were selected because you were exposed. After you complete the transaction, you are simply another man who has done what he was told. The exposure is no longer unique to you — there are thirteen others in the register with identical profiles. You are now one node in a network that is documented. That documentation is the protection."

"You are protecting me with the evidence of my own wrongdoing."

"I am ensuring," said Awil-Marduk, "that if anything happens to you, the record exists to explain why." He paused. "The official is not going to want that record examined. As long as you are alive and compliant, there is no reason to examine it. You understand the logic."

I understood it. Nur-Adad, after a moment, indicated that he understood it as well. He left — more steadily than he had arrived, though with the particular gait of a man carrying something he cannot set down.

The transaction was completed four days later. It appears in our register in standard form: a field near the second canal, transferred from Nur-Adad, acting in his private capacity, to a designated intermediary household, witnessed and stamped according to procedure.

I sealed it myself.

Awil-Marduk did not report the matter to any superior authority. He did not bring it to the attention of the palace administration, or to the Šangû of the Esagila, or to the senior judges of the city. He made his supplementary notations in the archive — a series of cross-referenced tablets that a careful reader of the register would eventually locate if they were looking for a specific pattern and knew where to begin — and he said nothing.

I asked him why.

"The Šangû," he said, "is not the appropriate authority. The network extends past him. I do not yet know how far."

"Then the palace administration—"

"Is the appropriate authority when I know how far." He returned a tablet to its shelf. "I do not yet know how far."

"And in the meantime, the official continues."

"In the meantime, the official continues," he agreed, "and the register continues, and when I know how far, there will be a complete map rather than a partial one. A partial map is more dangerous than no map, because it allows the mapmaker to believe the territory is smaller than it is."

I looked at him across the stacked tablets and the low lamp and the accumulated smell of old clay and the archive's particular silence, which was the silence of many things that had been said, set down, and sealed.

"You have been working toward this map for two years," I said.

"Longer," said Awil-Marduk, with the equanimity of a man to whom this is not a source of impatience. "But two years in this archive." He looked at the register around us — the tablets in their rows, the records of who had given what to whom and when and in the presence of which witnesses. "The register contains everything. It simply does not yet contain enough."

He sat down at his worktable and picked up his stylus, and he returned to reading.

I sealed the lamp. I went home. I wrote this account on private clay and stored it in my household archive, behind the tablets I do not wish my wife to see and in front of the tablets I do not wish my son to see, in the place reserved for things that are true but not yet safe to be official.

I do not know when that will change.

I am beginning to think Awil-Marduk does.

Here ends the account of Bel-ēṭir, senior scribe, Office of Property Transactions, Babylon.

Children:

- Nabi-ilum — see next entry.

NABI-ILUM

~1840 BC — Memphis, Egypt

The gods have called — and what they called him toward was south, and west, and the long road through Canaan to the Delta of the Nile. Nabi-ilum left Babylon with a trading expedition organised through one of the Babylonian merchant houses, carrying tin and textiles along the route that ran through the Levantine cities to the Egyptian border, and arrived in Memphis with the intention of returning within two seasons. He did not return. Egypt held him.

Memphis, the great administrative city of Egypt, sat at the heart of the Thirteenth Dynasty, a bureaucracy of established refinement, sustained by a long scribal tradition and marked by careful attention to the recording of administrative reality. Nabi-ilum found in the Egyptian scribal culture something that resonated with the deepest habits of his family — the absolute priority of the accurate account, the reverence for the document that said precisely what was true and nothing else — and he settled among it, married an Egyptian woman of the administrative class, and raised his son in the double tradition of Babylonian analytical rigour and Egyptian scribal discipline. The hybrid would prove, in the generations that followed, exceptionally productive.

The record that follows is preserved in a series of administrative tablets attributed to Paser, a junior translator attached to the palace office. His account is not a formal report but a working record, written as events unfolded and later copied for preservation. Paser does not claim authority over the interpretation of events; he records what he observed, what he was asked, and what he heard without understanding its full consequence at the time.

The Working Record Of Paser

Copied for preservation at the instruction of the Chief Scribe. The events herein occurred in the year of the second inundation of my service. I set down what I observed without embellishment, as my training demands, though I confess the events moved faster than my reed could follow.

The body of Djedhor the merchant was found, or rather announced, on the morning of the third day of the month of Thoth, when his household steward arrived at the office of the Harbour Master weeping with professional conviction and declared that his master had been taken by the river the previous evening while inspecting his mooring.

I say announced because no body was produced. The steward explained, with the fluency of a man who had rehearsed, that the current had taken it south. The Harbour Master, a careful man named Amenhotep-sa-Hapu, noted this in his register and dispatched two river-men to search the eastern bank. He also, as was his habit in matters touching the warehouses of the northern quarter, sent word to the office of the Foreign Assessor.

It was in this capacity that I first encountered Nabi-ilum.

I had been warned. My senior, old Userhat, had described him once as the Babylonian who reads a bill of lading the way a priest reads the future — and is more often correct. I had taken this for the exaggeration of an old man who admired foreigners too easily. I revised this opinion within the first hour.

He arrived at the Harbour Master’s office before I did, though he had been sent for after me. He was already seated when I entered, not at the table but beside the narrow window that overlooked the quay, turned slightly so that he could observe the warehouse district through the opening while appearing to attend to nothing at all. He was a man of perhaps forty years, lean in the manner of someone who forgets to eat when his attention is elsewhere, with the deep-set eyes common to the eastern families and hands that were perpetually ink-stained at the fingertips. His robe was Egyptian linen but cut in a slightly foreign fashion, as though he had adopted the material without fully surrendering the form.

He did not look at me when I entered. He was watching the quayside.

“The steward,” he said, without turning, “came from the direction of the southern warehouses, not the northern. He entered through the servants’ gate rather than the main approach. He has river-mud on his left sandal only, which is consistent with a man who stood briefly at the water’s edge this morning — not with a man who spent the night searching for his drowned master.”

Amenhotep-sa-Hapu, the Harbour Master, blinked. “You observed all of this as he arrived?”

“I observed it as you were reading the notation he brought. You were attending to the document. I had nothing else to attend to.”

He said this without pride, simply, as a craftsman might note that he had used the time while the kiln heated to sharpen his tools.

I introduced myself. He turned then and looked at me with the appraising directness that I would come to know well — a look that took in the ink on my fingers, the particular cut of my kilt which marked me as palace-trained, the slight elevation of the left shoulder that comes from years of bending over a low writing surface, and assembled them into a sufficient account of me in approximately the time it takes to draw one breath.

“Paser,” he said. “You translate between the administrative registers — the palace accounts and the foreign merchants’ bills. You have read Djedhor’s shipping documents.”

“I have translated summaries of them, yes.”

“Then you are already more useful than anyone else in this room.” He stood. “Come.”

The warehouse was sealed with the Harbour Master’s cord, the clay stamp unbroken. Nabi-ilum examined the seal without touching it for a long moment, then looked at the ground before the door.

“The seal is intact,” Amenhotep-sa-Hapu said. “Nothing has been removed since we placed it.”

“Nothing has been removed since long before you placed it,” Nabi-ilum said. He crouched and pressed two fingers into the earth just before the threshold. “Stand here. What do you see?”

The Harbour Master stood beside him and looked at the ground. “Earth. The ordinary surface.”

“And what do you not see?”

A pause.

“The wheel-ruts,” I said. I do not know what prompted me to speak — something in the way he had framed the question made me look for the absence rather than the presence. “Cedar comes on heavy carts. There should be ruts from the last delivery.”

Nabi-ilum looked up at me with an expression I could not immediately classify. It was not surprise. It was something more like the satisfaction of a man who has been given, unexpectedly, a good tool.

“The translator observes,” he said. “Yes. The last delivery of cedar to these warehouses, according to the register of the northern quarter, was forty-three days ago. Heavy timber on heavy carts in the soft ground of the post-inundation season. The ruts should be here. They are not. The surface has been worked over.” He stood and turned his attention to the door. “Amenhotep, open it.”

The warehouse contained grain.

Not cedar. Grain — old grain, by the smell, the kind kept as a buffer against the lean years, legitimate and unremarkable. It filled the space completely. There was no evidence that cedar had ever been here, or that it had been hastily removed. The grain had settled into the corners with the patience of things that have been sitting undisturbed for months.

Amenhotep-sa-Hapu stared at it. “The theft was reported three days ago. His steward said twenty hundred-deben weights of cedar, gone between one evening and the next morning. You cannot move twenty hundred-deben weights of cedar and replace it with—”

“No,” Nabi-ilum agreed. “You cannot. Which is why it was never moved.” He turned and walked back into the light. I followed. After a moment, so did the Harbour Master.

Outside, Nabi-ilum sat on the low wall that edged the quayside and pressed the tips of his fingers together in a way I would come to recognise — the posture of a man who was not resting but thinking with his whole body rather than merely his hands.

“Sit,” he said to me. “You have the registers.”

I had brought them as a matter of habit, rolled in my carrying-cloth. I set them between us.

“The Babylonian bill of lading first.”

I unrolled it. He did not read Babylonian cuneiform — or rather, he read it as a native, which meant he read it differently from the way I translated it, attending not to the meaning of the symbols but to the physical impression of the tablet, the pressure of the stylus, the spacing.

“This tablet was written by two different scribes,” he said. “The original notations, here” — he pointed without touching — “and the quantity notation, which was added later by a different hand. The clay has a different surface quality at this point. It was re-wetted and impressed after the original record was set.”

I looked. He was correct. The grain of the clay was different. I had translated this document twice and had not seen it.

“And the Egyptian record?”

I unrolled the second document. He studied it differently — more slowly, his eyes moving along the lines in the manner of a man reading rather than a man reading and simultaneously thinking about something else.

“The Egyptian document records the cedar delivery correctly,” he said. “Forty-three days ago, twenty hundred-deben weights, consigned to Djedhor’s northern warehouse, received and sealed. This record has not been altered.” He set it down. “So the cedar arrived. It was recorded arriving. And now the warehouse contains only grain.”

“Then it was removed after all,” Amenhotep-sa-Hapu said. He had been listening from a few steps away, as men do when they wish to appear to be attending to other matters.

“If it was removed, there would be ruts.” Nabi-ilum looked at the ground again, then at the river. “Paser. The Babylonian merchants who consigned this cedar — do you know their names?”

“The house of Warad-Sin. They operate the northern Levantine route.”

“And do they have a factor in Memphis?”

“They use Djedhor’s agency. He represents them for import and storage.”

A silence settled. Below us, a depet moved slowly upriver, its sail barely filled. Nabi-ilum watched it.

“The cedar,” he said, “was shipped twice. It arrived here forty-three days ago, was documented, sealed, and within a very short window — before the ground had time to bear the weight of full carts — was loaded again onto a vessel and sent somewhere else. Southward, I would judge, toward Thebes or beyond. The Babylonian bill of lading was then altered to show a smaller quantity, creating a discrepancy between the Egyptian record and the Babylonian record that any translator would struggle to reconcile without examining both documents together. Djedhor then reported the theft of the cedar that was never here, to be compensated by his Babylonian principals for a quantity that he had in fact already sold to someone else. He would collect the compensation from Warad-Sin’s house, and the proceeds of the second sale from wherever the cedar went. He then, to avoid the subsequent accounting, staged his own death.”

Amenhotep-sa-Hapu said, very carefully, “You are saying there is no body.”

“I am saying there is a steward with mud on one sandal who came from the south this morning, and a sealed warehouse that has not seen cedar in forty-three days, and a man who owes a great Babylonian house a very considerable debt that he cannot repay.” He stood. “The question is not where the body is. The question is which of the southern villages is currently sheltering a moderately prosperous Egyptian merchant who arrived recently with no obvious occupation and a tendency to keep indoors.”

I am compelled to record that we found him within four days, which felt like a miracle and which Nabi-ilum treated as arithmetic.

He was in a farming village two days south of Memphis, lodging with a cousin who had, apparently, been paid generously to ask nothing. He had with him documentation in a name I will not record here — a second identity, well-prepared, which suggested this had been contemplated for some time. He was thinner than his household records described him, and had shaved his head in the manner of the farmers rather than the merchants, which made him look, according to Amenhotep-sa-Hapu, like a completely different man.

According to Nabi-ilum, it made him look like a merchant who had shaved his head.

Djedhor was returned to Memphis under escort. He did not speak during the journey, which the guards interpreted as guilt and Nabi-ilum interpreted, I think, as something closer to exhaustion — the particular exhaustion of a man who has been maintaining a false self and has finally been permitted to stop.
