“Almost certainly. A man who had, perhaps, finally added his own years of short payment and arrived at the true sum.”

The light in the shed was the flat yellow of morning oil-lamps — we had lit two, small things that made our shadows enormous on the tablet-shelves. Outside, the furnace-smoke had begun its drift from the north. I heard, very clearly, the sound of the shaft wheel turning — men beginning the morning’s work below the earth, the same men whose labour had been measured small and sold for less than it was worth.

I thought about what Hanno-Baal had said: a man who had finally added his own years of short payment and arrived at the true sum. I thought about arriving at a sum like that. What it would feel like in the body. What it might make a man do, before dawn, on a road he had walked his whole working life.

He laid it before the port-captain in the courtyard that evening, with Ahmose-mer-Ra seated across from him and the Tamassos weight-records arranged between them on a linen cloth.

He spoke for some time, unhurried. He explained the mechanism of the fraud with the same tone he used when explaining anything — the tone of a patient teacher who has already understood his student’s confusion and is simply providing the path out of it. He showed the port-captain the columns. He explained the arithmetic. He explained what the gap between the two sets of records meant in practical terms — how much copper, over two years, how many men, how many seasons of short weight.

Ahmose-mer-Ra said nothing for a long time after.

Then: “The motive for killing him was, then, the dispute.”

“The dispute was the spark. The kindling was two years old.”

“If this record becomes public—” The port-captain stopped.

“The Alashiyan administration appointed Rib-Hadda. They certified his weights. They accepted his consignment records.” Hanno-Baal’s voice was still entirely neutral. “If the fraud is made public, the questions about administrative oversight become somewhat pointed. More pressingly, the consignment records — which now appear to show the true quantities shipped — contradict the official shaft output records, which means every receiving house in the eastern sea has tablets demonstrating they received more copper than was officially produced. The accounting difficulty would be considerable.”

Ahmose-mer-Ra looked at him for a long time. “What do you propose?”

“Aziru is innocent. I propose you know this, and that you act accordingly, and that the matter of who struck Rib-Hadda remains an open question in the official record — a robbery, perhaps, or an unknown enemy. Rib-Hadda was not, on examination, a man without enemies.” He paused. “I further propose that the weight-standards at Tamassos be quietly revised, and that the new overseer be a man whose arithmetic is both accurate and whose honesty is monitored with rather more attention than his predecessor’s.”

“And the man who—”

“There is no man, in the official record. There is an open question. The official record, as I understand it, is your domain entirely.”

Another silence, longer.

“Aziru,” said the port-captain finally, “will be released on the ground that the witness account was insufficient.”

“A sound administrative decision,” said Hanno-Baal.

Aziru was released the following morning. He came to our house that afternoon, still carrying the specific anger of a man who has been briefly imprisoned for something he did not do, and he sat in the courtyard and drank our wine and said several things about Cypriote justice that I will not copy here. Hanno-Baal listened with patient courtesy and said little.

At some point in the afternoon, when the worst of the heat had passed and the courtyard’s single olive tree was making its small shade, a man appeared at the gate. He was perhaps forty years, with the particular build of someone who has spent his working life underground — shoulders that slope forward, hands that are wide in the palms, a skin the colour of the Tamassos hillsides. He wore a miner’s kilt and carried nothing. He stood at the gate for a moment, looking at Hanno-Baal.

Hanno-Baal looked back at him. His face was, as always, unreadable by any means I possessed.

“There is a ship leaving for Byblos at the tide before dawn tomorrow,” Hanno-Baal said, without greeting, without preamble. “The shipmaster is called Abdi-Milki. He owes the house of Melqart-Azar a favour and will ask no questions about a passenger presented with this token.”

He held out a small cylinder seal — one of our house’s secondary marks, used for minor commercial letters. The man at the gate looked at it. He did not move.

“The official record,” Hanno-Baal said, “contains no man. It would be untidy if one appeared.”

The man at the gate crossed the courtyard, took the seal, and left without speaking. His sandals on the packed earth made almost no sound.

Aziru watched all of this and then looked at Hanno-Baal with an expression that was mostly bafflement and a small, reluctant admiration. “Do you know what you just did?”

“I balanced an account,” said Hanno-Baal. He picked up his wine cup, examined it, and set it down again without drinking. “The weight was never right. Now it is somewhat less wrong. That is, in my experience, the most that can usually be achieved.”

He said nothing else. He sat very still for a long time, and I could see the energy going back into him — the coil tightening again, the torpor settling over him like the smoke from the furnaces to the north. The case had been solved. The account balanced. There would be another one, or there would not, and the interval between was a kind of small death that he endured by habit if not by choice.

I wrote it all down. I do not know, even now, whether I was recording commerce or justice or something that has no name in any of the languages I know. I recorded it because it seemed to me that the weight of what he did — the precise calibration of it, the decision about what truth was worth releasing into the world and what truth was worth holding back — deserved to exist somewhere outside his own silent head.

The smoke from the Tamassos furnaces drifted south all that evening. Three furnaces, working. Full capacity, this time — or near enough to make no difference to a man who was not counting.

I noted this too, and cannot say exactly why.

Lower margin, later hand, ink partially flaked: the miner’s name is not recorded. The seal was found in a house in Byblos some years later, in a context the Byblos records do not explain. No connection was drawn.

Children:

- Baal-yaton — see next entry.

BAAL-YATON

~1510 BC — Knossos, Crete

Baal has given — the great god of Tyre, patron of the city and of its sailors, whose name this man bore as a declaration of origins and whose gift was the western sea. From Cyprus the trade routes ran west and north into the Aegean, toward the great island whose fleet had dominated the eastern Mediterranean for centuries — Minoan Crete, the Keftiu of the Egyptian records, whose palace at Knossos was the most sophisticated administrative complex in the pre-classical world. Baal-yaton joined a trading voyage west and did not return.

He stayed on Crete because Crete held him in the way that the extraordinary always holds the man of intelligence who encounters it for the first time. The Minoan palace culture was unlike anything in the Semitic world from which he came — its art, its religious practices, its administrative system with its undeciphered Linear A script, its women in positions of priestly and possibly political authority, its absence of the defensive walls that every other palace culture considered essential. He settled at Knossos as a merchant and factor, learned enough of the Minoan tongue to function in the palace economy, and spent thirty years in the most culturally distant world any member of the family had yet inhabited. He found it entirely navigable, once he had learned to read it — which was, of course, the family’s particular competence, and which had never yet failed to find application in any world it encountered.

The surviving record of his work at Knossos is preserved in a set of tablets maintained by his scribe, Eteocretan Helian, a palace-trained clerk assigned to foreign merchants permitted to operate within the palace economy. Helian’s writing shows a tension between Minoan administrative convention and Canaanite commercial structure; he writes in the Minoan system, but the structure of his entries reflects the influence of Baal-yaton’s methods. What follows is reconstructed from those tablets, some of which were later copied into a secondary archive after the destruction of the palace, and whose ordering has been partially restored through comparison with other administrative sequences.

The Affair Of The Sanctuary Of The Double Axe

Helian writes this. I set it down because Baal-yaton will not. He says records are for grain and oil and the movement of copper ingots; that the other kind of record — the kind that catches a man whole, preserved in his moment the way a body is preserved in natron — serves no commercial purpose and is therefore beneath his attention. He said this while lying on the floor of our shared workroom with a lump of black obsidian balanced on his chest, staring at the ceiling and refusing to eat for the second consecutive day, waiting, as he always waited between cases, for the world to become interesting again.

I am a palace-trained clerk. I know my obligation. I write this because someone should.

The smell of Knossos in high summer is specific and unforgettable: cedar oil burned in the lamp niches against the humidity, beeswax worked into the frescoed plaster to keep the colours from bleeding, the faint copper sweetness of ritual blood from the sanctuary levels above, and beneath all of it, always, the old wet-stone smell of the palace itself, which has been building and rebuilding for six hundred years and carries every season of itself in its walls. I had been breathing it for twenty years. I no longer noticed it.

Baal-yaton noticed it on his first morning and has noticed it every morning since. He says a man who stops smelling his environment has stopped thinking about it, which is fatal. He says this while simultaneously maintaining that he has no interest in anything that cannot be expressed as a ratio.

He had been in one of his black periods for eleven days when Iphimedeia came.

I heard the gate-hinge — that particular hinge on the third west corridor, which has a dry, ascending shriek that no amount of oil entirely cures, because the bronze pin is slightly ovoid from a casting imperfection and catches at the top of every swing — and I heard, beneath it, the sound of a woman walking in the deliberate, unhurried way of someone who does not expect to be stopped. Palace women of rank move differently from palace women of function. I had learned to hear the difference.

Baal-yaton did not open his eyes.

“She has been here before,” he said to the ceiling. “She knows the corridor. She is not staff or she would have come through the service passage. She is ranked but she is not sending a subordinate, which means the matter is one she cannot commit to an intermediate without defining its edges. She is afraid of its edges.”

The obsidian rose and fell on his chest as he breathed.

“Also,” he added, “she has been running.”

I had not heard running. I opened the door.

Iphimedeia, Second Keeper of the Sanctuary of the Double Axe, stood in the corridor with her hair disarranged and her eyes carrying the particular brightness of someone who has not slept and is deciding, in this very moment, whether to trust a foreign merchant with the most dangerous secret she has ever held.

She smelled of smoke and something older. Something like fear, but ceremonial.

“Come in,” said Baal-yaton, still on the floor. “And close the door behind you, Helian. What I am about to be told is apparently not for the corridor.”

Iphimedeia was perhaps forty, which in a Keeper of the Sanctuary meant she had survived three changes of the palace hierarchy, two reconstructions of the ritual calendar, and whatever internal politics the sanctuary conducted behind its painted screens. She had the hands of someone who worked — lamp-trimming, offering-preparation, the physical maintenance of sacred space — and the eyes of someone who had learned, very early, that what she saw and what she was permitted to say about what she saw were two entirely different registers.

She sat on the stool I provided. Baal-yaton sat up from the floor, set the obsidian aside, and looked at her with the expression I have come to think of as his working face: not cold exactly, but depopulated, as though everything in him that is not an instrument of observation has temporarily gone elsewhere.

“The Axe of the Founding is gone,” she said.

Baal-yaton waited.

“It is the — it is the original axe. The first axe. The one from which all the sanctuary’s ritual authority derives. Kept in the inner shrine, wrapped in linen renewed at each new moon, seen by no one below the rank of First Keeper except at the Renewal Festival.” She stopped. Started again. “It was there at the new moon sealing. It is not there now.”

“When was it discovered missing?”

“This morning. By myself. I came to prepare the lamp oil for the dawn rite.”

“Who has been informed?”

Something moved across her face. “No one. I came here first.”

Baal-yaton turned to me. I was already writing. He has trained me to begin writing before he begins asking; he says the record of a conversation is more valuable than the conversation itself because the record cannot be revised by the experience of knowing how it ends. I write in Minoan administrative script, which is not designed for dialogue, which is Baal-yaton’s problem and not mine.

“Why here first?” he said.

“Because if I report it to the Administrator, the sanctuary will be searched. Everyone within it questioned. The suppliers, the junior keepers, the musicians, the olive-oil factor who delivered last week — everyone who has come near the outer precincts. And the Administrator’s investigators will find nothing, because —” She stopped.

“Because?” said Baal-yaton.

The lamp between them guttered in a draft from somewhere in the palace’s deep interior, and for a moment her face went half-dark, and I thought: she is about to tell us something she has never told anyone, and the act of telling it will change the shape of the world she lives in, and she knows this, and she is doing it anyway.

“Because I am not certain it was ever there,” she said.

Baal-yaton stood. When he moves from stillness to motion, there is no intermediate state; he is simply suddenly standing, the way a fire is suddenly burning. He went to the corner of the room where the cedar shelves hold what I have come to think of as his working library — tablets, and wax records, and several dozen inscribed clay balls whose sealing system I have never entirely decoded, and three copper plates covered in his own notation system, which is not Linear A and is not the Canaanite alphabet and appears to be something he invented for purposes of pure compression.

He found what he wanted. A tablet. He held it at an angle to the lamp.

“The palace renovations,” he said. “Three years ago. The sanctuary level was closed for two seasons. Structural reinforcement after the shaking — the third shaking in a generation, which means the palace administrators were, reasonably, concerned about lateral load in the upper courses. The renovation archive would be in the West Magazine sequence.”

I looked at him. “How do you know about the renovation archive?”

“Because I had a dispute about an olive oil consignment eighteen months ago and the resolution required me to trace a delivery record back through three archival sequences. I ended up in the renovation records by accident and read them because I was there and they were in front of me and it seemed wasteful not to.” He set the tablet down. “The sanctuary was sealed for two seasons. The inner shrine was inaccessible. Who held the documentation of that inaccessibility?”

Iphimedeia’s face was very still. “The Administrator’s office. And the First Keeper at the time.”

“Who is now?”

“Dead. Four years ago. She had no deputy. I was appointed from outside the sanctuary hierarchy when she died. There were — there were very few who remembered the old protocols.” She pressed her palms together in her lap. “When I took the position, the documentation I received was — the Administrative Coordinator called it a complete inventory. I accepted it as one.”

“But you did not conduct an independent verification.”

“The inner shrine is not something you — you don’t open it and look in to check. The sanctity of the space—”

“Is the reason no one has looked,” Baal-yaton said, not unkindly. “Yes. I understand.”

He picked up his travelling cloak, which in Knossos means a linen wrap of Minoan cut, which he wears with the resigned air of a man who has accepted that the world is not organized for his comfort. “We are going to the renovation archive. Then we are going to the sanctuary. Then I am going to tell you something you already know, and we will decide together what to do about it.”

Iphimedeia stood. “You need a sanctuary pass for the upper levels. I can arrange—”

“I have one,” said Baal-yaton. “I have had one for six years. I received it in exchange for locating a discrepancy in the shrine-oil delivery records that no one else had been able to find. The Administrator believed it was theft. It was arithmetic.” He paused at the door. “Helian. Bring the wax board.”

I brought it. I always bring it.

Here I should explain something about the palace, because if you have not been in it, you cannot understand what it costs a mind to navigate it. The palace of Knossos is not a building. It is an accretion — six centuries of addition, reconstruction, and renovation layered on themselves like geological strata, so that to move through it is to move simultaneously through space and through time. A corridor that begins in the new western section ends, without warning, in a storage magazine that is older than living memory. Stairwells drop into levels that are not on any current plan. The administrative system that manages it — the Linear A records, the clay balls, the sealed tablets — reflects this palimpsest exactly: it is a system built to track an organism that is always changing faster than the record of it.

Baal-yaton moved through it as he moved through everything — without apparent uncertainty, as though the palace had agreed in advance to arrange itself for his convenience. I have watched people who do not know the palace try to navigate it. They stop. They retrace. They ask for guides. Baal-yaton, who has been here thirty years, simply reads it — reads the wear patterns on the floors, the oxidation rates on the bronze lamp brackets, the administrative stamps on the magazine seals — and navigates not the palace as it currently is but the palace as its records indicate it should be, which is usually close enough.

The renovation archive was in the West Magazine lower level, behind a sequence of pithoi whose contents were no longer current but whose displacement would require a formal request. Baal-yaton simply stepped around them with the ease of a man who has done it before, which, I realized, he had.

He found the sanctuary renovation tablets in perhaps twelve minutes. He read them in perhaps four. I watched his face, which was doing its depopulated thing, which means the part of him that processes is running and the part that presents results is offline.

Then he found something. I saw it in the slight change in his breathing before I saw anything else.

“Helian,” he said. “Read this entry.”

I read it. It was a materials accounting from the second season of the renovation — a record of items removed from the sanctuary level for safekeeping during structural work, listed by type and weight and storage location.

The Double Axe of the Founding was not listed.

“Now read this one,” he said, and handed me a second tablet from two entries later.

This one was a return accounting — items restored to the sanctuary on completion of the renovation, listed in the same format.

The Double Axe of the Founding was not listed.

An object that was not removed for safekeeping, and therefore did not need to be returned. An object that existed, in the administrative record, on neither side of a two-season interval during which the space it supposedly occupied was inaccessible to everyone with the authority to notice its absence.

“When was the First Keeper’s last independent inventory of the inner shrine?” Baal-yaton asked Iphimedeia.

She had gone very pale under her oil-smooth complexion. “I don’t — the records don’t show an independent inventory. The shrine is inventoried at the Renewal Festival, but the inventory is conducted by the First Keeper, and the First Keeper’s record is the inventory. There is no secondary verification.”

“There was not,” Baal-yaton agreed. “Because a secondary verification would require a second person with sanctuary access to the inner shrine, and such people are rare, and the creation of such a person is a political event with implications for the authority of the First Keeper.” He set the tablet down with the precise, contained movement of a man who is being very careful not to show how interested he is. “Let us go to the sanctuary.”

The sanctuary occupies the upper south wing, which catches the afternoon light but not the morning light, so that in the early hours it is dim and close, lit by oil lamps in wall niches and by the small shaft of skylight above the central court. The frescoes are the oldest in the current palace — the women in their flounced skirts leaping the bull, the processional figures with their offering vessels, the great double axes painted on the columns in red and black — and they have the slightly unfocused quality of images that have been restored so many times that the original is somewhere beneath all the restoration, preserved by being buried.

The inner shrine was behind a painted wooden screen. Iphimedeia opened it with a key worn on a cord at her wrist, and the smell that came out was: ancient linen, cedar, lamp-oil gone slightly rancid, and the mineral smell of old stone that has been enclosed.

There was a low offering table of polished limestone. On it: two smaller double axes in bronze, mounted on short poles, which were current ritual objects and unquestionably present. Lamp holders. A libation vessel. Against the back wall, a stone ledge, and on the ledge, a cavity in the stone approximately the width of a man’s forearm, lined in the kind of fine plaster that is used on the most sacred surfaces.

Empty. But not recently emptied.

Baal-yaton crouched before the cavity and held his lamp close. I came to look. The plaster was smooth and very clean. Too clean — not cleaned recently, but untouched. The fine dust that accumulates even in sealed spaces had settled on the plaster at a density consistent with years of undisturbed accumulation.

“What is the last documented record of the Founding Axe being seen?” he asked.

Iphimedeia stood very still in the doorway of the inner shrine, and I thought that whatever she had half-known before this morning, she was completing the knowing now, and it was not a comfortable process. “The Renewal Festival. But — the Renewal Festival documentation is a ceremonial account, not an administrative one. The First Keeper would describe the unwrapping, the inspection, the re-wrapping. There are no measurements. No weight records. No secondary witnesses.”

“Because the ceremony itself provides all the authentication required,” Baal-yaton said. “The First Keeper says the axe is there. The ceremony proceeds. The axe’s presence is confirmed by the fact of the ceremony’s completion.”

“Yes.”

“And the First Keeper who died four years ago conducted the last four Renewal Festivals.”

“Yes.”

He stood. “The dust in this cavity has not been disturbed in longer than four years. Possibly longer than eight. Possibly longer than twelve. I cannot date dust with the precision I would like, but I can say with certainty that nothing has been removed from this space within any timeframe that is administratively relevant to your current crisis.” He brushed the lamp-shadow across the plaster one more time, as though checking his work. “The Founding Axe was not stolen this morning. The Founding Axe was not stolen at any point before this morning. The Founding Axe was not here to be stolen.”

The lamp in the outer shrine flickered in the draft from the open screen.

“It never existed,” Iphimedeia said. She said it very quietly, in the tone of someone who has known this for some time and is only now permitted to say it aloud.

“Or it existed once and was destroyed or lost in circumstances sufficiently embarrassing or sufficiently threatening to sanctuary authority that the record of its existence was maintained while the object itself was not,” Baal-yaton said. “The distinction matters less than the mechanism. Someone — not you, not your predecessor, not anyone alive — decided that the authority of the sanctuary derived from the axe, and that the axe could be replaced with the record of the axe without any practical diminishment of that authority. They were correct. For however many generations, they were correct. The ritual is the authentication. The ceremony is the object.”

He stepped back from the inner shrine and waited for Iphimedeia to lock the screen, which she did with the same practiced key-turn she had used to open it.

“The renovation,” she said. “Someone in the Administrator’s office would have known. When the sanctuary was cleared—”

“They would have found nothing to clear. And they would have recorded nothing, because to record nothing would be to create the record of an absence, and someone in the Administrator’s office — someone who understood exactly what the sanctuary’s authority meant to the palace’s stability — would have understood that the record of an absence was more dangerous than the absence itself.” He paused. “The renovation records simply do not mention the Founding Axe. This is not an oversight. It is a professional courtesy extended across several generations to a fiction that was doing important work.”

We sat in Iphimedeia’s outer office, which smells of beeswax and the particular aromatic herb the Minoans use to scent the oil for lamp-trimming. Baal-yaton had taken a cup of water, which is the closest he comes to refreshment when he is working; I had taken a tablet and was rendering the morning’s discoveries into as neutral an administrative form as I could manage, which is what I do when I am deciding how I feel about something.

“I want to understand how you knew,” Iphimedeia said. She was composed now — the composure of someone who has been holding a weight for a long time and has finally set it down, and the absence of the weight feels strange and not entirely like relief.

Baal-yaton settled back. When he is about to explain, there is a particular quality to his stillness — not the ennui-stillness, but a focused, pleasurable stillness, the stillness of a man who has worked very hard to arrive at a place and is taking a moment to acknowledge the arrival.

“You came to me,” he said, “instead of to the Administrator. This told me you suspected the disappearance was more complex than a theft. A theft is the Administrator’s problem; a complexity is mine. You are not a woman who confuses the two.” He held up one finger in the Canaanite counting gesture, which he does when he’s listing, and which always looks slightly foreign in this palace. “You told me you were uncertain whether the axe had ever been there. This was extraordinary precision. You did not say you thought it might have been moved, or misfiled, or substituted. You used the word ‘ever.’ This suggests you had already, privately, conducted some portion of the reasoning I then conducted formally.”

Iphimedeia looked at her hands. “I found a reference, in the old Keeper’s personal records, to a ceremony conducted during the renovation period. A ‘reconciliation of the inventory.’ I did not know what it meant. I did not ask.”

“Because asking would have required defining the question. Yes.” He put up a second finger. “The dust in the cavity. The plaster of an inner shrine that is opened ceremonially at each Renewal Festival should show the disturbance pattern of those openings — the slight compression and re-settling that comes from air movement and the proximity of lamps. It did not. It showed the undisturbed accumulation of a sealed space. This alone was sufficient, but not necessary — the renovation records confirmed the matter independently.” A third finger. “The administrative design of the verification system itself. Any object whose existence is authenticated only by the ceremony that displays it, and whose display is witnessed only by the person responsible for its custody, is an object that exists as a category of record rather than as a physical fact. This is not unusual in administrative systems that are very old. Categories of record develop a persistence of their own, independent of whatever physical fact originally generated them. The palace knows this. I know this. Anyone who has spent thirty years reading Linear A tablets knows this.”

He put his hand down.

“The more interesting question,” he said, “is what happens now.”

I want to record this part carefully, because it is the part that I find most — I will say “instructive,” since I cannot find a word that means “both troubling and right.”

Baal-yaton stood and walked to the small window of Iphimedeia’s office that looks out over the west court, where in the early afternoon the bull-leapers practice on wooden frames and the palace children watch them from the pillared gallery. The light in that court is extraordinary — the particular gold of Cretan afternoon, filtered through the dust of the dry season, which makes everything in it look slightly consecrated.

“The sanctuary’s authority rests on the Founding Axe,” he said. “The Founding Axe does not exist. If this becomes publicly known, what happens to the sanctuary’s authority?”

“It falls,” Iphimedeia said. “Or at minimum it must be entirely reconstituted, which takes a generation and requires the palace administration’s active support, which is not — which is not certain.” She paused. “The sanctuary manages the ritual calendar. The agricultural ceremonies, the sea-offering before the trading voyages, the consecration of new palace spaces. If the sanctuary’s authority is questioned—”

“The calendar is disrupted,” Baal-yaton said. “The farmers plant at the wrong time, or don’t plant, or plant in the wrong spirit. The traders don’t make the sea-offering and the sailors are frightened, and frightened sailors don’t sail, or sail badly. The palace spends a generation rebuilding what it had.” He turned from the window. “The fiction was doing important work. It has been doing important work for perhaps two hundred years. The question is whether the truth serves more than the fiction.”

“You’re a Canaanite merchant,” Iphimedeia said. “You don’t believe in the Founding Axe.”

“I believe in the agricultural calendar,” he said. “I believe in sailors making their sea-offerings before they sail. I believe in whatever keeps this palace stable, because a stable Knossos is a reliable trading partner, and a reliable trading partner is more valuable to me than any quantity of abstract truth.” He said this with absolute directness, which is the Canaanite way, and which the Minoans always find slightly startling. “I also believe in the woman sitting in front of me, who has spent four years administering a sanctuary whose central object she had private reason to doubt, and who has conducted herself with sufficient integrity to come to me this morning rather than simply close the inner shrine and continue as before.”

Iphimedeia was quiet for a moment. Then: “What would you recommend?”

“Nothing was stolen,” he said. “Nothing was ever there to steal. There is no crime. There is a question of administrative documentation, which I will leave entirely to you, except to note that a sanctioned Keeper, properly documented and with full administrative authority, has the power to commission a new ritual object, to consecrate it according to the proper forms, and to install it as the legitimate continuation of whatever tradition it continues.” He picked up his cloak. “The Founding Axe exists as a tradition. Traditions can be materially re-expressed. This is, in fact, precisely what traditions do, across centuries, and how they survive.”

He looked at her steadily. “The Administrator will hear from me that I was consulted on a documentation question, found no irregularity requiring official action, and consider the matter concluded. Helian’s record of this morning will be maintained in my private archive, not in the palace sequence.” He nodded toward me. “He is, as I have told him many times, an exceptionally thorough scribe. I have also told him that thoroughness requires judgment about which archive receives which record.”

He left. He does not say goodbye; he simply stops being in the room.

I walked back to the workroom with him through the corridor with the bronze-pin hinge, which cried out on the upswing as always, and I set the wax board down, and I looked at the collection of copper plates and clay records that cover the walls of Baal-yaton’s half of the room, and I thought about what I had just watched him do.

He was already on the floor. The obsidian was back on his chest.

“You have a question,” he said.

“The renovation record,” I said. “You knew about the renovation archive from the olive-oil dispute. You had been in it before.”

“Yes.”

“But you had read the sanctuary tablets then. When you were in the archive for the oil dispute. You had read them because they were there and you read everything that is in front of you.”

He did not answer.

“You already knew,” I said.

The obsidian rose and fell.

“I knew there was an absence in the record,” he said, finally. “I did not know what the absence meant, because I did not then have sufficient information about the internal accounting of the sanctuary to interpret it. It was a notation in my memory — a gap in an otherwise complete administrative sequence — waiting for a context that would give it meaning.” He paused. “This morning provided the context.”

“You could have brought this to the Administrator’s attention eighteen months ago.”

“I could have. I did not, because I had no framework for it and because the palace is full of gaps in the administrative record and not all of them represent crises. Some of them represent decisions made by clever people in previous generations that deserve the respect of being left alone.” Another pause. “And because it was not, at that time, anyone’s problem.”

“But now Iphimedeia has made it her problem.”

“Iphimedeia,” he said, “made it her problem the day she accepted the position and did not look in the inner shrine. She has been living with the problem for four years. I have simply helped her find its true dimensions, which are rather smaller than she feared.”

The afternoon light moved across the plaster walls, which in the palace’s west section are painted with a running spiral pattern in blue and ochre, very old, in the style before the current palace. In the lamp-niche above his head, the cedar oil burned.

“You will write it down,” he said. It was not a question.

“I have already written it down.”

“Good,” he said, and closed his eyes. “In thirty years, when you are dead and I am dead and Iphimedeia is dead, someone may be glad that someone wrote it down. And if not—” He settled the obsidian more precisely on his sternum. “— the palace has survived six hundred years of records that no one will ever read. One more will make little difference.”

I finished the tablet. I sealed it under my own seal, not the palace seal, and placed it in the cedar box where I keep the records that are not for the palace sequence.

This is that record.

— Helian, Second Clerk, West Corridor Administrative Section
In the year of the Third Harvest Following the Great Renovation At Knossos, in the palace of the Minos, in the island called Keftiu by the Egyptians.
May the Keeper of the Sanctuary commission a beautiful axe.
May it serve as well as the one it replaces.
May no one look too closely at the dust.

Children:

- Akar-wos — see next entry.

AKAR-WOS

~1480 BC — Knossos, Crete

The name was the family’s old name-element — Akar, the Semitic thread that had run through the lineage since the great-grandfather’s migration to the Tyrian coast — grafted onto a Minoan sound that made it pronounceable to the palace functionaries among whom he grew up. Akar-wos was born on Crete to a Canaanite father and a Minoan mother, and grew up as the thoroughgoing hybrid that the island’s cosmopolitan culture made possible — Semitic in his commercial instincts and his analytical cast of mind, Minoan in his language and his knowledge of the palace economy, and possessed of the interpreter’s particular gift of seeing each world clearly because he was never entirely of either. He worked in the palace economy at Knossos as a merchant-interpreter, facilitating the transactions between the Minoan administration and the foreign traders — Egyptian, Syro-Palestinian, Cypriote, early Mycenaean — who came to the island in the considerable numbers that Knossos’s commercial dominance attracted.

Linear A, the Minoan script, is still undeciphered by modern scholars; Akar-wos read and wrote it as a matter of professional necessity, in the same way that his ancestors had read Sumerian or demotic Egyptian — as the local language of record that the gift was applied to, rather than as an end in itself. The tablets he produced in the course of his work — recording the terms of transactions, the quantities of goods, the obligations of counterparties — were among the administrative documents of a palace culture that would be destroyed within two generations, its script falling silent with it. He did not know that. He knew only that the accounts balanced, that the transactions were recorded accurately, and that a man who maintained the archive with honesty in a world where the archive was the institution was performing a service without which everything else failed.

The account of his work is preserved through the records maintained by his scribe, Lycon of the inland estates, a functionary assigned to assist with agricultural accounting who was drawn into Akar-wos’s orbit when discrepancies in storage and distribution required broader oversight. Lycon’s training was practical rather than scholarly, and his writing reflects a concern with outcomes rather than theory. He records events as they affected the functioning of the estate and the palace, and his account, though fragmentary, preserves the sequence of Akar-wos’s reasoning with unusual clarity.

The Account of Akar-wos

Recorded by Lycon of the Inland Estates, in the Matter of the Boy Kirwa-na

The oil had been wrong for three days before Akar-wos said anything.

I had not noticed it myself — I am a practical man, trained in the movement of grain and the counting of sheep, and I confess that the lamps of the western corridor held no particular meaning for me beyond the fact that they burned. But Akar-wos had noticed from the morning of the boy's disappearance, and it was only later, when he had assembled everything into its terrible and inevitable shape, that he explained what the smell had told him. The lamps in the storage wing were burning the second-grade pressed oil — the oil from the inland estates, thick and slightly bitter, the kind that leaves a particular heaviness in the air, the kind that the palace reserve never used except in the agricultural outbuildings. Someone had substituted it for the good oil without filing the exchange in the archive. And Akar-wos, who had read the archive that morning as he read it every morning, had known at once that the substitution was unrecorded, which meant it was not a clerical error. Which meant it was deliberate. Which meant that whoever had made the substitution had access to both the palace stores and the inland supply, and had reason not to wish the exchange documented.

He said nothing of this to me until the end. That was his way. He gathered and he waited, and when the moment came, he arranged the facts with the precision of a man who has already known the answer for some time and is now simply allowing others the experience of arriving there.

But I am moving ahead of myself. I should record the matter as it unfolded, for that is my function here, and I have learned that Akar-wos is poorly served by accounts that begin at the resolution rather than the beginning.

The boy was called Kirwa-na.

He was perhaps fifteen years, the apprentice and property of a Canaanite merchant named Baal-sharru, who operated under the palace's licensed trading arrangements and kept a small set of rooms in the eastern quarter. Baal-sharru had been at Knossos for three seasons — long enough to have established the useful relationships, short enough that his accounts had not yet been comprehensively audited — and he traded primarily in dyed cloth and the smaller luxury goods that moved easily along the Egyptian and Syro-Palestinian routes. He was not a man I had paid particular attention to. He was not a man, I think, that anyone had paid particular attention to, which was, as I later understood, precisely the condition he had cultivated.

Kirwa-na had been seen at the evening distribution on the fourth day of the month. He had not been seen on the fifth. Baal-sharru reported his absence to the palace administration with what I initially read as appropriate anxiety, and it was in the course of that report — filed with the duty functionary in the proper manner, assigned a tablet entry in the proper manner, allocated to the proper channel of inquiry — that Akar-wos first became involved.

He had not been assigned to the matter. I want to be clear on this point, because it bears on what came afterward. The duty functionary was a capable man named Orea-ko, and it was Orea-ko who held the formal warrant for inquiry. Akar-wos had simply been present in the archive room when Baal-sharru's report was delivered — he was there for his own purposes, the reconciliation of a Mycenaean cargo against the palace's import tallies — and something in the report had caused him to look up from the tablets he was reading.

I had been assigned to assist Orea-ko as a secondary recorder, which was how I came to be present. And I saw Akar-wos's eyes move to Baal-sharru's face with the particular attention he reserved for things that had snagged against his understanding of how the world was arranged.

He said nothing. Orea-ko completed his recording of the report, assured Baal-sharru that the matter would be pursued through the standard channels, and dismissed him. Akar-wos watched him go.

"He described the boy as his apprentice," he said, when Baal-sharru had passed out of the corridor. "Three times."

Orea-ko looked at him. "He is his apprentice."

"He is his apprentice," Akar-wos agreed. He had already turned back to his tablets. "Three times in a report of perhaps a hundred words is a frequency that suggests the word is doing work the speaker does not wish us to examine."

Orea-ko was a good functionary — methodical, fair, and concerned with procedure. He was not, I think, constitutionally inclined toward the lateral movement of thought that Akar-wos performed as easily as breathing. He noted Akar-wos's observation with professional courtesy and returned to the standard channels. The standard channels led, as they generally did in cases of missing persons of low standing, toward the harbor: a runaway, probably. A boy who had found passage on an outbound ship, or fallen into bad company among the sailors. Orea-ko sent his inquiry to the harbor registrar and settled down to wait for a response.

Akar-wos went to the archive.

I followed him, which perhaps requires explanation.

I had been assigned to assist with the inquiry, but Orea-ko had dismissed me with the observation that there was nothing to be done until the harbor registrar responded. I was free until the afternoon. I had been working alongside Akar-wos for two months by this time, long enough to understand that when he moved with that particular quality of focused quietness — the slight tightening around the eyes, the hands that ceased their habitual tablet-smoothing and hung still at his sides — he had seen something that repaid attention. I am a practical man, as I have said. I followed.

The archive at Knossos is a room that rewards patience. The tablets are stored in clay bins along the walls, organized by category and season, and the smell of the place is the smell of the administrative work of the palace — the particular dry-dust smell of baked clay, and beneath it, always, the faint residue of the good oil that keeps the lamps burning through the long sessions of recording. Except that on this particular morning, as I have noted, the smell was wrong. The second-grade inland oil, thick and slightly rancid. I noticed it only because Akar-wos paused in the doorway and breathed deliberately, and in doing so directed my attention to what I would otherwise have passed through without registering.

He did not remark on it then. He went to the storage tallies.

The storage tallies record the movement of goods into and out of the palace's warehouses — the great magazines along the western wing, and the subsidiary storage facilities that served the estate network. They are not, in the ordinary course of administration, interesting documents. They record quantities and categories and the identifying marks of the officials who authorized each movement. I had spent considerable portions of the previous two years working with them, and I can attest that they are thorough, accurate, and very nearly entirely tedious.

Akar-wos read them with the absorption of a man engaged in a text of great pleasure.

After perhaps an hour, during which I occupied myself with a re-examination of the Mycenaean cargo tallies that had occupied him before Baal-sharru's arrival, he looked up and said, without any particular emphasis:

"How many estates does the inland network maintain?"

I told him. Fourteen principal estates, seven subsidiary holdings.

"And the estates that supply the secondary lamp oil — the pressed oil, not the cold-extraction — how many of those?"

Three, I said. The holdings of Tarwi-sa, of the southern valley estate network, and the estate managed by a man named Dawu-rimi whose family had held the management contract for the better part of a generation.

"Dawu-rimi," Akar-wos said. He looked at the tally tablet in his hand for a moment, then set it down with the care he gave everything that had become evidence. "And what is the distance from the palace to Dawu-rimi's estate?"

Half a day, I told him. Perhaps a little more in the late-season heat.

"Then we should start now," he said, "if we are to have light for the return."

I should record what Orea-ko said when we informed him of our intention, because it is illustrative of the procedural difficulty that attended Akar-wos's methods throughout his career at Knossos.

Orea-ko pointed out, correctly, that he was the assigned investigator. That the standard inquiry was proceeding through the harbor registrar. That an excursion to a private estate required either invitation or administrative authorization from a superior, and that neither was currently in hand. That Dawu-rimi's family held a long-standing relationship with the palace that made precipitous action politically inconvenient. And that if Akar-wos had an observation to contribute to the inquiry, the appropriate method was to record it on a tablet and submit it through the standard filing process.

Akar-wos listened to all of this with the attentiveness he brought to everything.

"The boy may be there now," he said.

"The boy may be at the harbor now," Orea-ko said.

"The harbor registrar's response will arrive tomorrow morning at the earliest. If the boy is at the harbor, he will still be findable tomorrow morning. If the boy is at the estate, tomorrow morning is not the same question."

There was a silence. Orea-ko was a fair man. I believe he recognized the logic. But he was also a careful functionary in an administration that rewarded careful functionaries, and private estates with long-standing palace relationships were precisely the territory where careful functionaries did not tread without authorization.

"I cannot authorize the excursion," he said finally.

"I am not asking for authorization," Akar-wos said, with a mildness that contained nothing mild in it. "I am informing you of my movements, as a courtesy."

He turned and walked out. I followed him. This was becoming a habit.

The road to the inland estates follows the river valley out of Knossos, and in the late-season heat it is a road that demands respect. The dust rises with every step and settles on the skin like a second garment, and the olive trees along the verges give a broken, inadequate shade that teases rather than relieves. We walked in the company of a supply driver who was making the return journey with an empty cart — Akar-wos had arranged this in perhaps four minutes of conversation at the transport yard, with a facility that always slightly surprised me, given his general disdain for unnecessary social commerce.

He was quiet for much of the journey. This was not the quiet of a man with nothing to say, but the quiet of a man whose mind was fully occupied and who had simply suspended the social portion of his functioning until the work was complete. I had learned to read the distinction, and to provide conversation only when he showed the particular slight restlessness that meant he wanted to think aloud.

Somewhere past the midpoint of the journey, in the deep shade of a cork oak stand where the driver had stopped to water the oxen, he spoke.

"Tell me what you know of Baal-sharru's trading arrangements," he said.

I told him what I knew, which was not extensive. Licensed trader, cloth and luxury goods, three seasons established, accounts in reasonable order.

"And his relationship with the estate network?"

None that I was aware of. His operations were coastal and maritime, not agricultural.

"Then why," Akar-wos said, with the quality of a man producing something he has been holding for some time, "does a quantity of inland lamp oil — second-grade pressed oil of the variety produced exclusively at Dawu-rimi's estate, in a quantity consistent with perhaps three large storage jars — appear in the palace magazine records as received against a transaction authorized by Baal-sharru's trading license, two days before the boy disappeared?"

I stared at him.

"The authorization is irregular," he continued, with the particular pleasure he took in arrangements of fact. "Baal-sharru holds a coastal trading license. He has no authorization to participate in the inland estate supply chain. The transaction was recorded, which is why it exists in the archive — but it was recorded in such a way as to require cross-referencing three separate tally categories to find it, which is not how an authorized transaction would be recorded. An authorized transaction is simple. This transaction was complex. Complexity of that kind, in a record-keeping system as standardized as the palace archive, is not the result of confusion. It is the result of effort."

He had spent the hour in the archive not verifying whether a transaction existed, I understood, but following backwards the chain of what that transaction implied about relationships that should not exist.

"Baal-sharru," I said slowly, "received oil from Dawu-rimi's estate."

"Received oil from Dawu-rimi, yes. In a quantity that would represent a significant portion of the secondary estate's discretionary surplus. The question is what Dawu-rimi received in return, and why neither party wished that exchange to appear straightforwardly in the archive."

The driver was re-harnessing the oxen. We rose and followed the cart back into the dust and the heat.

"The boy," I said.

"The boy," Akar-wos agreed. "Who is described, three times in a hundred words, as Baal-sharru's apprentice. Which is the legal category that carries the most extensive obligations of personal custody and the most legally awkward questions if the custodian can be shown to have surrendered that custody as surety."

I understood then. Not the full architecture of it — that came later — but the shape. Baal-sharru had a debt to Dawu-rimi, or an obligation of some kind that he did not wish recorded in the normal fashion. The oil was payment, or part of payment. And the boy, the apprentice over whom Baal-sharru held legal custodial responsibility, had been sent to the estate as surety for some portion of the arrangement that the oil had not yet settled.

"He reported the disappearance," I said. "Wouldn't that—"

"Expose him? Only if the report were properly followed. The harbor registrar's inquiry leads outward, to the ships and the sailors. It does not lead inland. Baal-sharru knew this. Reporting the disappearance was the act of a man establishing his own innocence within the system he was confident would not find the answer."

He paused, and then said something I have remembered precisely in all the years since, because it seemed to me to contain something essential about how he understood his work.

"He forgot that the archive remembers everything. Every tablet. Every authorization. Every gram of oil. He thought the complexity of the arrangement made it invisible. But complexity in a standardized system is not invisibility. It is a signature."

Dawu-rimi's estate announced itself before we saw it: the smell of pressed olives and the sharper smell of the secondary oil in production, and then the low stone walls of the outbuildings appearing through the heat haze, and the gate with its particular bronze hinge that gave a two-note creak as the driver's cart passed through — the lower note first, then the higher, a sound so specific to that gate and that hinge that I can still produce it exactly in memory, thirty years later, as clearly as if I stood before it now.

The estate manager himself came out to meet us. He was a compact, capable-looking man of perhaps fifty, with the weathered look of someone who had spent his working life outdoors, and his expression when he saw Akar-wos held a particular quality I recognized — the expression of a man who had prepared a set of answers for a question and was now calculating whether the question he was being asked was the question he had prepared for.

Akar-wos gave him no time to calculate.

"I am here on behalf of the palace administration," he said, with a formality that was technically accurate and substantively somewhat elastic. "I am inquiring after a boy named Kirwa-na, an apprentice of the trader Baal-sharru, who has been reported missing from the palace quarter. The storage tallies record a transaction between this estate and Baal-sharru's trading license two days ago. I would like to reconcile the record."

Dawu-rimi said, with the steadiness of a man who had thought about this possibility, that he had no knowledge of any boy. That the transaction was a routine supply arrangement. That the archive would confirm everything was in order.

"The archive does confirm everything," Akar-wos said pleasantly. "That is why I am here and not at the harbor."

The silence between them was the particular silence of two men who understand each other precisely.

Then, from somewhere in the interior of the main outbuilding — behind the heavy linen hanging that served as a door in the heat of the season, behind the smell of the pressed oil and the dust of the threshing floor — came the sound of someone moving. A small, careful movement, the movement of a person who has been very still for a long time and has made the error of shifting weight at the wrong moment.

Akar-wos did not look toward the sound. He kept his eyes on Dawu-rimi, and he said, very quietly:

"The boy."

It was not a question.

Kirwa-na was fifteen years old and had been at the estate for four days. He was not, in the physical sense, harmed. He had been housed in the outbuilding, fed adequately, kept away from the other estate workers. He was, in every sense that the arrangements between Baal-sharru and Dawu-rimi had structured, a piece of security — a pledge held against payment, the same function that a jar of oil or a bolt of dyed cloth might serve, but with the distinction that he was also a person, and that the palace administration's laws regarding the custody of apprentices were considerably more explicit than its laws regarding the custody of jars.

When the hanging was pulled aside and he came blinking into the afternoon light — slight, dark-eyed, with the look of someone who has spent four days being carefully frightened — Akar-wos looked at him for a moment with what I can only describe as a rigorous absence of sentiment. It was not coldness, exactly. It was the look of a man confirming that the last element of a calculation has resolved correctly.

Then he said, in the local Canaanite tongue — which I understood only partially, and which I record here as best I could reconstruct — something that I believe translated approximately as: You are found. Do not speak yet. There will be time.

The question of what happened afterward is one I have considered carefully in deciding how to record this account, because the resolution was not, in the strict procedural sense, the resolution that the palace administration would have sanctioned.

Orea-ko arrived the following morning with the harbor registrar's response — negative, as Akar-wos had anticipated — and with, I suspect, a degree of private chagrin at having been outmaneuvered. He interviewed the boy, interviewed Dawu-rimi, and prepared the formal inquiry documents.

Baal-sharru was questioned. The transaction in the archive was scrutinized. The debt arrangement between the two men — which had its origins in a cargo dispute of the previous season, the details of which I never fully understood — was examined.

And here is what I must record carefully: the formal outcome was, on the face of it, a resolution in favor of all parties. The boy was returned to the palace quarter. The transaction was re-filed under the appropriate category with appropriate documentation. Dawu-rimi paid a fine consistent with procedural irregularities in the supply chain. Baal-sharru received a formal notation in his trading license regarding the improper deployment of an apprentice as surety, a notation that would complicate but not end his operations at Knossos.

No one went before the palace court. The arrangement between the two men was acknowledged to exist, its irregular elements corrected, its core terms permitted to stand.

I asked Akar-wos, on the evening of Orea-ko's formal resolution, whether this was justice.

He considered the question with the attention it deserved.

"Baal-sharru is a bad man," he said, "in the specific and limited sense that he was willing to use the boy as a token in a transaction. He is not a singular criminal. He is a merchant who encountered a debt he did not wish to acknowledge openly and resolved it through a method that the systems of the archive do not easily accommodate. Dawu-rimi received a boy instead of oil and kept him uncomfortable but intact for four days, which tells me that Dawu-rimi was making a point, not building a habit. The boy is returned. The debt is resolved through proper channels. The archive is corrected."

"And Baal-sharru?"

"Baal-sharru will be more careful with his archives in future." He paused. "Which is perhaps more useful than a court proceeding that would involve the boy testifying to arrangements that he only partially understood, that would require Dawu-rimi's family's palace relationships to be examined in ways that would disrupt several legitimate supply agreements, and that would result in Baal-sharru's license being revoked, his operation concluded, and the boy — who has nowhere else to go — left without a master or a livelihood."

He picked up the tablet he had been working on, the eternal reconciliation of one account against another.

"The archive is the institution," he said. "I serve the archive. The archive records what happened. What happened is that a boy was held improperly and then returned. The record reflects this. That is what the archive can do."

I wrote all of this down. It is my function. I record what I observe, and I leave the question of what it means to those who read it.

What I observed was this: a boy came blinking into the afternoon light at Dawu-rimi's estate, and Akar-wos looked at him with rigorous, careful attention, and said in the local Canaanite tongue that there would be time.

There was time. The boy was found.

The oil in the western corridor lamps was replaced with the correct grade the following week. The smell went back to what it should have been. I noticed it only because Akar-wos paused in the doorway on the morning it changed and breathed, once, with what I recognized, by then, as satisfaction.

He went in and began to read the archive.

Here the account ends.
The tablets from which this record was drawn were recovered from the ruins of Knossos after its final destruction. The script (Linear A) remains undeciphered, but the sequence and context preserved in later Mycenaean administrative layers allow this reconstruction. The archive, as Akar-wos served it, outlasted the palace that housed it.

Children:

- Akaros — see next entry.

AKAROS

~1450 BC — Mycenae, Greece

The Mycenaeans had displaced the Minoans as the dominant power of the Aegean by the time Akaros arrived at Mycenae — the shaft-grave culture that had been absorbing Minoan influence for a century now asserting its own supremacy, the great palace at Mycenae with its shaft-grave citadel and its Linear B archive replacing Knossos as the centre of the Aegean world. Akaros rendered the family name-element into Mycenaean Greek — the oldest Greek we have, preserved on those clay tablets — and served in the palace economy as his father had served at Knossos: as a merchant-interpreter, the man who could speak to the Canaanite trader and the Egyptian factor and the Hittite envoy in terms each could follow, and render their competing interests into the administrative language of the palace. He was also, and this was not incidental, a reader of men in the Mycenaean court — a world of warrior aristocracy and political intrigue where the ability to assess a man’s true intentions from his conduct rather than his words was a survival skill as much as a professional one.

He had grown up on Crete with the full inheritance of the family’s written record, the private archive that now spanned a hundred and fifty years of observation and accumulated to something that no single mind had encompassed before. His father had been the first to understand the network as a single entity. His father’s father had been the first to suspect it. He was the first to have the complete picture available to him from the beginning of his professional life, and the picture changed how he read the world around him. He arrived at Mycenae not merely as a merchant-interpreter but as a man already looking for the network’s presence in a new palace culture, and he found it within three years.

The record that follows was preserved by the scribe Dorykles, attached to the wanax’s archive, whose duty was to copy and maintain sensitive administrative cases beyond their initial filing. Dorykles was not trained as an investigator, and his account reflects both his diligence and his limits, yet it is from his hand that this particular sequence survives, with occasional marginal notes where he struggled to follow Akaros’s reasoning.

The Account Of Dorykles

Set down in the thirty-first year of the palace’s present administration, in the season of the barley harvest.

I record this at the instruction of Akaros the interpreter, who told me that an account made by a man who does not fully understand what he witnessed is more trustworthy than one made by a man who does. I confess I found this a peculiar reason for choosing me. I record it nonetheless.

The archive room had no window.

This was its purpose and its virtue. The tablets could not be baked by direct sun before their contents were verified; the dust of the citadel could not settle on a fresh inscription and fill the characters before the checker had read them; the traders and factors and military men who passed through the Cyclopean Gate had no angle from which to observe what the palace chose to record and what it chose not to. The archive was a sealed world within the sealed world of the citadel, and the air was full of scents: of damp clay, of lamp-oil pressed from olives grown in the lower fields below the walls, of the particular mineral cold that stone accumulates in darkness even in the months of summer heat. The lamp that burned in the corner when we found it had been burning long enough to blacken the ceiling above it in a half-circle that spread like a stain. The smoke had nowhere to go. Neither, in the end, had Thalos.

He was the senior scribe of the Linear B archive. He had served the palace for nineteen years, and he lay now on his side against the western shelving rack with his face turned toward the door and his reed still gripped in his right hand, as a man who falls in a field grips the tool he was carrying because the hand does not know yet that the work is done. The back of his skull had been struck once. The clay tablet on which he had been working lay on the floor beside him, fallen from the writing board when he fell, not yet dry.

The wanax’s chamberlain, Phrenos, had been the one to call me. He was a thick-necked man who administered the palace with the efficiency of a man who has never in his life been required to imagine what another person might be thinking. He was competent. He was reliable. He was, as I had learned in four years of working alongside him, constitutionally incapable of considering that a situation might contain more than one explanation.

“The slave Naxios,” he said, as we stood outside the archive waiting for the guard to unlock it, “was found this morning with clay on his hands and a lamp-wick that had burned to nothing. He has no account of his movements after the second watch.”

“Then why,” said the voice behind us, “did you send for me?”

Neither Phrenos nor I had heard Akaros approach. The interpreter had the quality — I had noticed it in the three years since he had arrived at Mycenae — of existing in a room for some time before other people became aware of him, as though he had first to decide whether the room was worth announcing himself in. He was leaning against the citadel wall with his arms folded, watching Phrenos with the expression of a man who has been told a story he recognises and is waiting for the teller to reach the part where they realise they have omitted something essential. He was not a large man. He had the colouring of his Cretan origins, darker than the Mycenaean aristocracy preferred, with a scholar’s stillness that sat incongruously on a frame that suggested, when he moved, that the stillness was a choice rather than a physical fact.

“Protocol,” Phrenos said. “A death in the wanax’s archive is a matter of the palace record. You are engaged to review matters of the palace record when —”

“When the answer is not obvious.” Akaros unfolded himself from the wall. “You have a slave with clay on his hands and no alibi. You have a dead scribe. You have concluded the one killed the other. You have sent for me, which means some part of you suspects that the obvious answer has a fault in it, or you would have simply had the slave questioned and not wasted this particular morning. Show me the room.”

The three of us entered the archive.

Akaros stood in the doorway for what seemed, in the confined and smokeful dark, a very long time. He did not move toward the body. He turned, slowly, to take in the shelving, the lamp, the door-hinge, the position of the single writing board that stood on its stand in the centre of the room, and then he crouched where he was and examined the threshold.

“There is clay on the floor here,” he said. “Fresh. From a tablet that was carried, not from the clay store, which is behind the eastern rack and therefore — where are you standing, Phrenos?”

“I am standing in the doorway.”

“You are standing on the only clay trail that connects this threshold to the body. Please move.” He waited while Phrenos stepped aside with the expression of a man who is being deliberately insulted. “The trail goes from the body to the door. Not from the door to the body.”

“The slave —”

“If the slave had entered to commit a murder, the clay would travel from the door to the body and then back again, as his sandals picked up whatever had spilled from the impact and redistributed it on his way out. This clay travels in one direction only, outward, which means it came from the tablet.” He rose and moved to where Thalos lay. He did not touch the body but crouched beside it at close range, examining the hand that still held the reed, the angle of the fall, the position of the head relative to the shelving. “He was struck from behind and to the left. He was working when it happened. He fell to his right. The tablet fell from the board.”

He picked up the fallen tablet.

I watched his face when he read it.

He read it twice. Then he stood very still, and I saw something happen behind his eyes that I cannot describe precisely: a narrowing, and then a releasing, as though something that had been held under tension had been permitted to run. It was the only moment in that room that I would describe as anything other than cold.

“Phrenos,” he said, “what does this tablet contain?”

Phrenos crossed to look. “It is an inventory record. The Iph-section, third register. Holdings of the textile division.”

“Read me the third line.”

Phrenos read: wo-ka-de a-re-pa-te-ja so-we-no we-re-ke. “To the work-parties: oil-allocated persons, the sowing-workers.”

“Now read the fourth.”

“me-ri-to a-re-pa-to-jo a-mo-ra-ma.” “Honey of the un-oiled — no. That is not a proper formulation. A-re-pa-to-jo is the genitive of a-re-pa-te, which is —” Phrenos paused. I could see him assembling the grammar. “A scribe of the oil-allocation? But the genitive construction here is malformed. The possessive does not agree. And a-mo-ra-ma is not —” He stopped again. “That is not a word.”

“No,” Akaros said. “It is not. Thalos was a scribe of nineteen years service. He had been copying Linear B records since before you arrived at this palace, Phrenos, and his hand is visible throughout the archive. I have read enough of his work in the course of my duties to know that he did not make errors of grammatical agreement. He certainly did not invent words.” He set the tablet down on the writing board with care. “This line was not a mistake. It was a record. He knew he was dying — the blow, perhaps, was not immediately fatal, or perhaps he heard the footstep and understood what was coming — and he used the only instrument he had available.”

“He wrote something into the tablet,” I said.

“He wrote a name into the tablet.” Akaros looked at me with an attention that was, I confess, briefly uncomfortable, as though I had done something unexpected. “Poorly. Under duress, with a blow already taken or a blow imminent, using a cipher that any trained scribe would eventually unravel but that would survive a cursory reading by someone who was not. The word a-mo-ra-ma does not exist in the Linear B lexicon. The syllable cluster, however — a-mo — is recognizable as the first element of a name, if you read the fourth line not as a sentence but as an interruption. He was mid-word when he fell, or chose to stop mid-word because stopping mid-word would make it look more like a dying error and less like a deliberate inscription.”

Phrenos had gone very still.

“A-mo,” Akaros continued, examining the tablet again, though I believe by then he had it complete in his mind and was consulting it only as a courtesy to those of us who needed to see him look at something before we could follow his reasoning, “as the first syllabic element of a Mycenaean name. The grammatical error in the preceding line is not, in fact, an error. A-re-pa-to-jo is not the genitive of a-re-pa-te in the form Phrenos initially read it. It is the genitive of Arepatos. Thalos was not constructing a sentence. He was constructing a name in the genitive — the name of a possessor, a master, the man on whose instruction or in whose presence this work was being done. The a-mo-ra-ma is the beginning of a second name, unfinished. Two names. The first complete. The second cut short.”

I looked at Phrenos.

Phrenos did not look at me.

“Arepatos,” Akaros said, “is the name of the second-ranking administrator of the textile division, who would have had legitimate reason to visit the archive in the evening to check the accuracy of an inventory submission before it was sealed. He would have been admitted without question. He would have been alone with Thalos for as long as he required.” He set the tablet down. “The slave Naxios was in the textile division storehouse until the third watch; I spoke with the storehouse keeper before I came here this morning because the storehouse keeper is an honest man who records everything and I thought his records might be useful. Naxios cannot have been in this room at the time the lamp-oil suggests this tablet was last inscribed. The clay on his hands came from an entirely different source, which I suspect Phrenos already knew when he arrived at his conclusion, but a slave without an alibi is a satisfying answer, and satisfying answers are the chamberlain’s professional preference.”

Phrenos said nothing. His face had the specific quality of a man who has calculated that there are no remaining useful words.

“The second name,” I said. “The a-mo-ra-ma. Did he mean —”

“I have a theory about the second name,” Akaros said. “I will not record it here. A theory is not a name, and a name set down without evidence is an accusation, and an accusation against certain names within this palace is an action with consequences that require consideration before I commit them to a tablet that Dorykles here will copy three times and file in different sections of the archive.” He gathered his cloak and moved toward the door, and then stopped with his hand on the door-frame and looked back at the tablet where Thalos had left his final record. “He was a careful man. Nineteen years without an error in the register. I wonder if he knew, when he heard the footstep, that someone would eventually be careful enough to notice.”

He did not say this to me or to Phrenos. He said it to the room.

Arepatos was brought before the wanax’s tribunal four days later. The evidence of the clay trail, the grammatical inscription, and the storehouse records that disproved the case against the slave Naxios were presented by the chamberlain Phrenos, in the manner of these things, as the product of the palace’s investigative process.

Akaros was not present. He was, as I learned when I went to find him, sitting on the eastern wall of the citadel in the late afternoon, watching the plain below, in the particular state of motionless inattention that he sometimes fell into between cases — not sleeping, not working, simply absent in some interior way, his mind browsing through its own contents like a factor checking a warehouse. He had, I was told by the palace cook, eaten nothing since the previous morning, which the cook found offensive. A cup of the Cretan wine he favored sat beside him, untouched.

“They have convicted Arepatos,” I told him.

“Yes,” he said.

“And Naxios is released.”

“Yes.”

I sat beside him, which he permitted without acknowledgment. Below us the plain ran gold and dry to the hills in the long light. The evening was beginning to carry the smell of cook-fires from the lower town.

“The second name,” I said. “The a-mo-ra-ma.”

He looked at me then, and I had the brief sensation — not unpleasant, but disorienting, the way that looking over a great height is disorienting — of being read with some precision.

“Dorykles,” he said, “you are a careful man. That is why I asked you to write this account. I want you to understand that the careful man’s virtue is also, in certain circumstances, his greatest danger.” He picked up the wine cup, found it empty, and set it down again. “There is a second name. It is the name of a man with whom Arepatos was transacting business that the textile inventory was intended to conceal. Arepatos did not kill Thalos because Thalos had made an error. He killed Thalos because Thalos was too good a scribe to make the error that Arepatos needed the record to contain. Thalos had noticed the alteration and was preparing to report it.”

“Then the man Thalos was trying to name —”

“Is known to me.” He said it with the particular flatness that, I had learned, indicated that a subject was adjacent to something very large. “He is not a man who can be brought before the wanax’s tribunal on the evidence of an incomplete syllable-cluster in a dying inscription. He is also not a man who can be allowed to know that I know what his name’s first two syllables would have been. These are, you understand, competing constraints.”

“What will you do?”

He was quiet for a moment. The cook-fire smell was stronger now, and somewhere below the citadel gate a cart-wheel had caught on the road-stone and was crying against it in a long, rhythmic complaint.

“I will do,” he said, “what can be done with the evidence I can use in the forum I have available. Which means Arepatos dies for Thalos’s death, and the business arrangement that produced that death becomes slightly more expensive and difficult for its architect, which is not justice but is the closest available approximation of it.” He rose from the wall. “Record that, Dorykles, at the end of your account. Not the name — I will not give you the name — but the principle. That the full truth is not always the truth that can be spoken. That a good scribe knows the difference between what the tablet contains and what it means. And that Thalos knew it too.”

He went inside.

I sat for a while longer, watching the plain go dark.
