Then I came back to the archive — to this archive, the one that has no window, though the lamp had been replaced and the floor cleaned and Thalos’s writing board moved to the storage rack where the closed accounts are kept — and I began to write.

This record is set down complete and filed in the third section of the sensitive administrative cases, per standing instruction. Dorykles, scribe of the second rank, in the season of the barley harvest, thirty-first year.

The account of the second name is not filed. It is kept. There is a difference, as I have been taught.

Here the account ends.
The record was preserved as part of the sensitive administrative cases of the Mycenaean palace archive at Mycenae.

Children:

- Akaros the Younger — see next entry.

AKAROS THE YOUNGER

~1420 BC — Athens, Greece

He followed the trade routes north and west from Mycenae to Athens — a secondary palace-centre in the Mycenaean period, less rich and less powerful than Mycenae but active, its position on the Attic peninsula giving it access to the silver of Laurion and the trade of the Saronic Gulf. Athens in 1420 BC had not yet become the city it would be; it was a palace on a rock, a wanax with his warriors, a community managing the agricultural surplus of Attica and the trade dealings of the Aegean. Akaros the Younger arrived as his father’s representative in the Athenian trading community and stayed as his own man, as the family’s members had stayed in places across the breadth of the eastern Mediterranean world — reading the new context quickly, establishing a position within its particular economy of information and trust, and finding, within a few years, the local expression of the pattern that had been following the family, or that the family had been following, since Egypt.

He is called the Younger to distinguish him from his father, and in every way that the records allow us to assess, he was his father’s equal and his father’s student — the same cast of mind, the same facility with multiple languages, the same quality of attention that made him, in every room he entered, the person who understood most completely what was actually happening. He had absorbed the family archive and its century and a half of accumulated observation, and he brought to Athens the additional advantage of knowing what he was looking for before he began looking.

The record that follows survives in the hand of the scribe Menekles, attached to the Athenian palace administration. Menekles was diligent and exact in copying commercial and administrative matters, yet he was not trained to interpret the subtleties of technical inspection, and at several points in his account he notes his uncertainty as to why Akaros attended to certain details that seemed peripheral at the time. His record preserves both the sequence of events and the limits of his own understanding.

The Matter of the Drowned Guard of the Laurion Inventory

Set down by Menekles, Scribe of the Second Register, in the service of the Lawagetas of Athens, in the thirty-first year of the wanax

The air around the harbour district in the hot months is a thing a man does not quickly forget. Salt-rot and old fish-brine and the particular sweetness of cedar resin sweating out of the timber stacked along the quayside — this was the air of Athens in the season when the matter of Dromios and the Laurion silver came before the palace administration, and it is the air I associate, even now, many years later, with the first time I watched Akaros the Younger look at a dead man and begin, very quietly, to smile.

I should explain that smile before I explain anything else, because it alarmed me greatly when I first encountered it and because understanding it is, I think, the key to understanding the man. It was not the smile of a man who was pleased by death. It was the smile of a man who had been handed a puzzle of a particular and rare quality, and who recognised it immediately as worthy of his attention. Akaros the Younger smiled at the drowned body of the warehouse guard Pellos in the same way a craftsman smiles when he is given a piece of flawed bronze that has defeated lesser workers — with a kind of private, anticipatory pleasure.

I am Menekles. I copy grain tallies and wool assessments and the inventories of the palace storerooms. I am not a man given to narrative, and I ask the reader's patience for the inadequacies of this account, which I set down at Akaros's own instruction, and which he read through only once before returning it to me without comment. He did not correct it. He may have found the inaccuracies too numerous to address, or he may have considered them of no consequence. I cannot determine which, and that uncertainty is itself, I think, characteristic of the man.

The matter came to the palace administration on the second day after the new moon, when Dromios of the Laurion Consortium arrived at the Lawagetas's hall in a state of controlled distress that I noted at the time as unusual. A man who has suffered a genuine loss arrives in pieces. Dromios arrived assembled.

He was a broad man, heavy through the shoulders, with the careful eyes of a merchant who has spent thirty years watching other men's hands. He traded in silver — not the raw ore, which was the wanax's concern, but the refined and weighed ingots as they passed from the smelters into commercial circulation. He had, by his own account, been accumulating a private reserve of silver against a large purchase he intended to make in the spring — Cretan copper, the finest grade, which required substantial advance payment. This reserve, amounting to some forty ingots of standard palace weight, had been stored in one of the warehouse spaces near the lower harbour, guarded by a man named Pellos, who had been in his service for eleven years.

Three days ago, he said, Pellos had not appeared at his usual post. Two days ago, a fisherman had found the body of a man floating in the shallower water north of the main quay. The body had been identified by Pellos's brother as Pellos. And when Dromios had gone to inspect the warehouse, the silver was gone — not the chests, not the storage jars, not the seals, but the silver itself, as though it had been removed one ingot at a time over a considerable period and replaced with nothing.

The Lawagetas had called for Thessippos, who commanded the palace guard and who handled such matters in the ordinary course. Thessippos was a capable soldier who understood crime as a variant of military strategy: establish the enemy, assess his resources, pursue. He had concluded, rapidly and with professional confidence, that the killer and thief was a harbour man named Eryx, who was known to move stolen goods, who had been seen near the warehouse district in the days before Pellos's disappearance, and who had, with unfortunate convenience, left Athens on a trading vessel two days prior. He wanted authorisation to send men to intercept this vessel.

It was, as I recall it, a clean and satisfying account. Thessippos was pleased with it. Dromios received it with the careful gravity of a man absorbing a painful truth.

Akaros the Younger, who had been standing near the hall's eastern column since the interview began, said nothing until both men were finished. Then he said: "Where is the body now?"

They had laid Pellos in one of the lower storage rooms near the quay-master's office — a cool space, used in better circumstances for olive oil — and the smell was, in the heat of that season, considerable. Thessippos remained near the doorway. Dromios did not come at all, having pleaded the distress of his loss. I followed Akaros inside because I had been told to follow Akaros, and I will not claim that I was comfortable doing so.

Akaros crouched beside the body for a long time without speaking. The oil-lamp I carried gave unsteady light, and the shadows moved on the dead man's face in a way I did not enjoy observing. But I watched Akaros, because watching Akaros was more instructive than watching the body, and what I saw was the quality of attention I mentioned before — a complete, almost aggressive concentration, the kind that makes a man temporarily deaf to everything outside its object.

"His hands," Akaros said eventually.

I looked at the hands. They were the hands of a man who had drowned, or so I assumed, which is to say that I saw nothing informative in them. I said as much.

"His palms are unmarked," Akaros said. "A man who drowns in harbour water — where are the marks?"

I did not understand.

"He would fight," Akaros said, with the patience of someone explaining something elementary to a bright child, which was the mode he generally adopted with me and which I have chosen to take as a compliment. "Not necessarily against a person. Against the water. Against a piling, a rope, a hull-plank — whatever his hands found. The palms of a drowned man, particularly one who does not go willingly, carry the evidence of that final grasping. This man's palms are smooth. The skin is softened by water — he has been in the harbour, that part is true — but there is no trauma."

He turned the hands over. He pressed the skin of the forearms, watching it. He lifted the head with a gentleness that startled me and examined the back of the skull with the tips of his fingers.

"No blow," he said. "He was placed in the water after death, but he did not die by drowning, and he did not die by violence."

"Then how?" I asked.

Akaros set the head down carefully. He remained crouching, looking at the face with an expression I can only describe as professional sorrow — not grief, but the acknowledgment that something sad had occurred. "Poppy," he said. "The smell is faint now, and the harbour smell is considerable, but it is there. He was given a great deal of it. He went to sleep and did not wake, and someone then carried him to the water and placed him there to be found, and waited three days before placing the alert."

"Someone murdered him."

"Someone arranged for him to be found as a murdered man, which is a somewhat different thing. The poppy was a mercy." He stood. He looked at me for a moment with those depthless grey eyes that I never entirely became comfortable with. "Tell me what you know of Dromios."

I told him what I knew, which was the common knowledge of the commercial district — that Dromios had been in Athens for twenty years, that he was prosperous and careful and had a reputation for honouring his weights, that he had a house on the upper slope and two sons in the wool trade. I told him also what was slightly less common knowledge: that Dromios's Cretan copper purchase had been spoken of in the warehouses for months, and that the price he had agreed was aggressive, and that there were men who thought he had overcommitted.

Akaros listened to all of this with his eyes half-closed, which was the expression he wore when he was most intensely engaged, though I did not know that yet. When I finished, he said: "And what do you know of Eryx?"

I knew less. Eryx was a marginal figure — a carrier of ambiguous goods, the kind of man who existed in the harbour district of every Aegean port and was known to everyone and trusted by no one.

"Who told Dromios that Eryx had been seen near the warehouse?" Akaros asked.

I did not know. I had assumed it was common report.

"Find out," Akaros said. And then, in a different register, quieter and more private: "The silver was never in the warehouse."

He explained it to me that evening, sitting on the step of the house I had arranged for him near the lower market, in the last of the daylight, with a cup of the thin local wine he drank in quantities that suggested less pleasure than occupation — something to do with his hands when his mind was moving faster than polite conversation permitted.

"A man who is genuinely robbed," he said, "does not control the time of discovery. The loss is discovered when it is discovered — by the thief's departure, by the guard's absence, by the chance inspection. Dromios waited three days. Why three days?"

"To allow Eryx to depart," I offered, which was Thessippos's theory restated.

"To allow decomposition," Akaros said. "Three days in harbour water, in this heat. The body becomes... more generally a body. Harder to examine with precision. The poppy is harder to smell. The detail of the unmarked palms becomes, to a less attentive observer, simply the hands of a drowned man." He paused. "Dromios is a careful planner. He planned to the day."

"But why?"

"Because the Cretan purchase will ruin him if he completes it. The price he agreed was formed on intelligence that has since proven false — the Cretan market has moved. He is committed by his own word, and in this community his word is his value, and to withdraw would be to cease to exist commercially. So he does not withdraw. He reports a loss. He reports a loss equal, by remarkable coincidence, almost precisely to the advance payment he owes. He collects from the palace the administrative recognition of the loss — which is not repayment, we have no mechanism for repayment, but which releases him from the obligation to complete the purchase on the grounds of calamity beyond his control. The silver was never in the warehouse. It has been in his house, or in the house of a kinsman on the mainland, since before Pellos was given the poppy."

"And Pellos?"

Akaros was quiet for a moment. The lamp in the doorway behind us guttered in a breath of harbour wind, and the smell of salt and cedar came through the evening heat.

"Pellos knew what the warehouse did and did not contain," he said. "He had served Dromios for eleven years and was trusted accordingly. He could not be permitted to speak when the investigation began. But Dromios — and this I do believe — could not bring himself to commission a violent death for a man who had served him loyally. The poppy was a choice. An expensive choice, requiring a physician or someone with a physician's knowledge, and a decision to spend that money on a cleaner end. It does not mitigate what was done. But it is there."

I sat with this for a moment. "And Eryx?"

"Eryx was seen near the warehouse because Dromios arranged for him to be seen there, and arranged for the report of it to reach Thessippos's ears through the ordinary channels of harbour gossip. Eryx is probably entirely ignorant of his role in this. He is a useful shape — the kind of man a story needs, the kind that everyone already half-believes guilty of something."

The following morning Akaros went to the warehouse, and I went with him, and Thessippos came because he had decided that accompanying Akaros was more professionally prudent than not accompanying him, even though he had also decided that Akaros was wrong and that Eryx's vessel should be intercepted at once.

The warehouse was a low mudbrick building, one of four or five along that section of quay, smelling of old grain and the harbour. The door-seal had been broken in the discovery and not reapplied. Inside, the light came through a single high opening and lay in a bar across the floor.

Akaros walked the perimeter of the room first, which was not what I had expected. He ran his fingers along the mudbrick, examined the join between the wall-base and the floor, crouched at each corner. Thessippos watched this with the carefully neutral expression of a man who has decided to reserve judgment and finds it effortful.

"What are you looking for?" Thessippos asked, after some time.

"Wear," Akaros said. He pointed to the floor. "Forty ingots of standard palace weight. Perhaps eighteen, twenty minas per ingot. This is not a quantity a single man moves in an evening. Moving it produces — " he gestured at the floor, where I could see, now that he indicated it, a faint pattern in the dust: not disordered disturbance, but a systematic regularity, like the traces of repeated careful movement. "This is not recent. This pattern is months old. The dust has resettled."

He went to the wall where the storage jars stood and examined each in turn. The jars were sealed, their contents declared by the marks on their shoulders.

"What do these say?" he asked me.

I read them. Olive oil, wool-fleece, and one jar marked simply with a weight notation that I assumed referred to metal.

"Open that one," Akaros said to Thessippos, indicating the weight-marked jar.

Thessippos looked at him. "That is Dromios's sealed property."

"Yes," Akaros agreed pleasantly.

There was a pause. Then Thessippos broke the seal.

The jar contained sand. Clean, dry sand, of the kind found on the northern beaches. Packed with care, to weight.

Thessippos stared at it. I stared at it. Akaros did not stare at it, because he had already looked at everything he needed to look at and had moved to the doorway, where he stood examining the hinge-pin of the door with the focused attention of a man reading a palace tablet.

"The hinge," he said. I came to look. The hinge-pin was new bronze, bright against the older patination of the door-plate, and the wood around it was freshly mortised — replaced recently, and replaced with care. "This door has been rehung. The old hinge was, I think, one that could be lifted from the outside without breaking the seal. A common enough shortcut in a building that the owner accesses frequently. It was replaced — carefully, professionally — some time in the last season. To ensure that the official record would show an unbroken seal."

He turned back to the room. The bar of light had shifted with the morning's progress and now lay across the wall rather than the floor, illuminating the texture of the mudbrick with a low, raking angle.

"He is a thorough man," Akaros said, and I heard in his voice something that was not quite admiration and not quite respect, but occupied the space between them. "The sand, the door, the timing of the body's discovery, the introduction of Eryx as a figure — each piece placed with intelligence. He thought of everything." He paused. "Except the hands."

Thessippos wanted to arrest Dromios that afternoon. I think this impulse is understandable — he had been redirected from one conclusion to another, his professional efficiency had been demonstrated as insufficient, and arrest is a satisfying gesture, a clear line drawn. He was a man of clear lines.

Akaros sat with him in the Lawagetas's outer hall and spoke to him for a considerable time, and I copied what was said as faithfully as I could, though the exact form of Akaros's argument I cannot fully reconstruct. What I recall is this: that Akaros laid out the question of what a formal process would require. Witnesses to the silver's existence. Records of its deposit. Dromios's record-keeping was meticulous and would show nothing that contradicted him; the palace's own tallies would show only what Dromios had reported. The drowned man could be demonstrated, by someone with the relevant knowledge, to have been given poppy — but demonstration to the standard that the Lawagetas's administration required was a different thing from the knowledge of a Mycenaean trading-house agent. Eryx could be produced, examined, and demonstrated to be innocent, but Dromios could claim that Eryx had been previously known to him as suspicious, that his suspicion was honest and his report well-intentioned. A formal process would take months. It would expose the palace administration's commercial oversight to scrutiny that would be, Akaros noted gently, uncomfortable for several parties.

And — this was the part that I found most remarkable, and which I am least certain I have reconstructed accurately — Akaros said something about the Cretan purchase. He said that the Cretan market had indeed moved, and that the intelligence Dromios had acted on had been, in his assessment, honest intelligence that had simply proven wrong. He said that Dromios had built his position in Athens over twenty years with reasonable integrity, and that the Laurion Consortium, which employed many men and moved considerable commerce through the palace's quays, would be disrupted significantly by a public process against its most senior Athenian member. He said that the Lawagetas had, in his view, more to gain from a private resolution.

Thessippos asked what form a private resolution would take.

Akaros said that Dromios would withdraw his claim. He would cite, if asked, a re-examination of his records that revealed an error in accounting. He would make a donation to the sanctuary of the relevant deity — Akaros suggested the amount, which was substantial — in acknowledgement of his relief that no crime had in fact occurred. He would arrange for Pellos's family to receive, from an unnamed source, a provision that would sustain them for the remainder of the mother's life. And he would, Akaros said, know — clearly and without any ambiguity — that he was known.

Thessippos was quiet for a long time after this. I could see him calculating — not, I think, the ethics, but the administrative reality that Akaros had laid before him. Eventually he said: "And Eryx's vessel?"

"Let it come back on its own schedule," Akaros said. "Eryx has done nothing. The harbour gossip that named him will have moved on to something else by the time he returns. He will not even know he was a figure in this."

I accompanied Akaros to Dromios's house the following morning. I do not know everything that was said, because Akaros spoke with Dromios privately for a period, and when he emerged his face was as composed as it always was, giving away nothing that was not intended for giving.

What I know is that Dromios, within the week, filed the corrected accounting. The donation reached the sanctuary. I do not know what became of the provision for Pellos's family, but I believe it was made; Akaros was, in my experience, precise about the commitments he extracted.

What I also know, though it is not a matter of record, is that the silver — all forty ingots — was found some months later, in a legitimate transaction, in the Corinthian market, having passed through channels that I could trace, if I chose to trace them, back to a warehouse in the Saronic trading district. Akaros had told me to look for it. He had told me where it would likely surface. He was, again, correct.

I have been asked, in the years since, to describe Akaros the Younger, and I find that I always return to the same evening — the step of his house, the thin wine, the last of the harbour light. He was not a comfortable companion. He had the quality, which I have since encountered in no other person, of making you feel, in his presence, that you were thinking slightly less clearly than was possible for you, that the gap between what you understood and what he understood was not a permanent feature of existence but a temporary inadequacy that more attention might address. This was not unkind. He never made me feel stupid. He made me feel that stupidity was a problem I could solve, which is a different thing and, in some ways, a more unsettling one.

He ate very little during a case and slept at irregular hours. He spoke several languages with a fluency that he deployed so naturally that I sometimes forgot he had not grown up in Athens — Mycenaean Greek, of course, but also the Cretan dialect that he had learned from his father's archive, and enough of the Levantine trading tongue to conclude a negotiation without intermediaries. When there was no case before him, he was worse company — restless and slightly hollow, as though the part of him that animated the rest had gone elsewhere and left the body to manage as best it could.

He collected nothing. He owned, as far as I could determine, two changes of clothing, a set of weights and measures, and a small archive of his family's correspondence that he carried in a sealed chest wherever he went. He spoke of this archive with something that was not quite reverence but was the closest he came to it — the records of a century and more of the same quality of attention, deployed in Egypt and Ugarit and the Mycenaean heartland, the accumulated sight of a family that had trained itself, generation by generation, to look at what was actually there.

He stayed in Athens seven more years after the matter of Dromios and the silver, and in that time I was present for four further cases that I have also attempted to record, with the same inadequacies of understanding that characterise this account. He then followed a trade report north, toward the Thessalian coast, and I did not see him again, though I received, once, a clay tablet in a hand I did not recognise that contained only a weight calculation and a single phrase that I believe referred to a case I had not been present for.

I include this account in the palace records at his instruction and with the Lawagetas's knowledge, though not, I should note, under any pressure from the Lawagetas's administration. The official record of the matter of the Laurion inventory notes only that a review of accounting procedures identified an error, that no crime had occurred, and that the case was closed. Thessippos received commendation for his prompt and efficient handling of the situation.

This is as it should be. The account above is for whoever comes after, who will know how to read it.

Written in the thirty-second year of the wanax, in the month of the new grain.

Menekles, Scribe of the Second Register.

Children:

- Holkos of Corinth — see next entry.

HOLKOS OF CORINTH

~1390 BC — Corinth, Greece

Holkos — the Greek word for that which is drawn, the ship hauled over the Corinthian isthmus from one sea to the other, the thing that crosses between worlds by the application of sustained effort rather than by any natural ease of passage. The name was not given lightly in a trading family. It described a function and declared a character: the man who moves things between places that cannot otherwise be connected, who applies his knowledge of both shores to the problem of the crossing, who understands the isthmus not as an obstacle but as an opportunity.

His father had chosen the isthmus city with the strategic foresight that had guided the family’s migrations across a millennium: the junction between the two seas was where the next chapter of the world was being written. Holkos of Corinth managed the overland transfer of cargoes at the crossing — the narrow neck of land where ships and their goods were hauled from the Saronic to the Corinthian gulf to avoid the dangerous voyage around the Peloponnese. Working under the authority of the Mycenaean palace administration whose trading network extended to the isthmus, he assessed the cargoes, organised the labour, and maintained the records of every consignment that passed through his hands with the precision of a man who understood that the document was the transaction and the transaction was the relationship. He was the last member of the lineage to use a Greek name that retained the Semitic name-element in any recoverable form; his son would be Greek, and his son’s son more Greek still, and the old Semitic thread would recede entirely into the family’s memory, present only in the archive that was their truest inheritance.

The account that follows is preserved by Menekles’ successor, the scribe Pythodoros, whose notes were appended to the Corinthian administrative record after the matter was resolved. Pythodoros was more reflective than precise, and where the earlier entries lean on observation, his hand occasionally reaches toward interpretation, though he repeatedly corrects himself when his inferences outpace the evidence.

The Matter Of The Death Of Arkelaos The Rhodian

Set down by Pythodoros, assistant to the scribe Menekles, in the administrative record of the Corinthian crossing, in the reign of the wanax, in the season of the early harvest. I have appended my own observations where the record of Menekles leaves gaps, though I confess that my hand reaches more readily toward meaning than my master’s did, and I have tried, where I noticed this fault, to pull it back.

There is a smell to the isthmus that no man who has worked it ever forgets, and that no man who has merely passed through it can adequately describe. It is the smell of two seas at once — the salt of the Saronic sharper, carrying the mineral edge of the eastern trade, the Corinthian Gulf to the west softer and darker, faintly of pine resin where the timber ships discharge. Between these two olfactory worlds lies the strip of land across which everything must be carried, and in the carrying, the smells mix and do not resolve. The oil that keeps the wooden sledge-runners greased against the packed earth of the crossing-track — no formal road, nothing so grand as that, only the worn groove of a thousand crossings before this one, rendered navigable by fish-head grease and old marrow-bones, a vile compound that works admirably — sits beneath everything else like a persistent bass note. In the heat of summer it rises, and the crossing-track becomes a lane of particular stench that the workers no longer notice and that visitors remark upon without exception.

It was into this smell that I, Pythodoros, descended on the morning the body was found, following at a careful distance behind Holkos of Corinth, who walked as he always walked on a matter of business: quickly, with his chin slightly forward, his narrow eyes already reading the ground before his feet had reached it.

I should say something of Holkos before I record what he did, because this account will be consulted by those who did not know him, and the things he did will seem, without the man himself before you, either miraculous or invented. He was not tall. He was lean in the way that men who forget to eat during a working problem become lean — not from discipline but from simple inattention to appetite. His hair was very black and very straight and he kept it bound for practical reasons he would explain at length if asked, and which I will not reproduce here. His eyes were the particular grey-brown that catches light at unusual angles, and when he was thinking — which was most of the time — they fixed on nothing visible to anyone else. When he was not thinking, he was insufferable in a different way: he would sit for half a day in the small room he kept behind the weighing-house, staring at the ceiling, occasionally asking me to read aloud the tallies from the previous month’s transfers, which he would then correct from memory without consulting the clay tablets themselves. This was how he rested his mind. It was not restful to observe.

He had no vice that I ever discovered, unless you counted the riddles. He collected them. He paid for them. Sailors off the eastern ships would bring him wordplay in Ugaritic or in the strange clipped trade-dialect of the Cyprus ports, and he would spend an evening with them and emerge satisfied or dissatisfied and in either case wanting no company for the rest of the night. I believe the riddles served the same function for him that wine serves for other men — they occupied a portion of the mind that, left empty, became destructive. He had once described it to me: the brain is a storage vessel, Pythodoros, and if you put nothing in it, it begins to consume the vessel itself. He had said this with great seriousness. I had thought he was making a philosophical remark. He was not. He was explaining his own architecture.

He was thirty-eight years old, or thereabouts. He had been managing the crossing for eleven years. There was no man in Corinth who knew more about the movement of cargo across the isthmus, and there was no man in Corinth to whom the palace administration was more reluctantly grateful — reluctantly, because he was almost always right when they were wrong, and institutional memory for such things is reliable.

The body of Arkelaos of Lindos had been found by a water-carrier at first light, beneath the easternmost storage lean-to where the offloaded cargo waited to be assessed and tallied. He was lying on his side with his knees drawn up, which the water-carrier had taken at first for a sleeping man, except that sleeping men do not have the back of the skull laid open in the way that Arkelaos’s skull had been laid open. The instrument had been heavy and had been swung from above and behind. There was a great deal of blood on the packed earth floor. The blood had dried. This meant the night, or the early part of it.

Menekles had sent for Holkos. Menekles was the official scribe of the crossing and thus, by the logic of the palace administration, the officer responsible for irregularities occurring on palace-administered ground. He was a careful and methodical man whose procedures were impeccable and whose imagination was, through no fault of his own, limited. I do not say this unkindly. Menekles had trained me and I owe him my formation. But I observed, over the years I served him, that he approached a problem by selecting the solution that most resembled a problem he had solved before, and then fitting the available evidence to that solution. This works admirably for most problems. It does not work for the problems that Holkos was called to address.

Menekles had already formed his view by the time we arrived. He stated it to Holkos as we stood at the edge of the storage lean-to, the body before us, the morning light coming sideways through the open east face of the shelter.

“The cargo-workers,” Menekles said. “The Rhodian was a difficult man. He argued the weight of his consignment. He accused the weighing-crew of shortchanging him. There were words yesterday evening.”

“There are always words,” Holkos said, without looking at him. He was looking at the body. “The crossing-workers argued with three other merchants yesterday, by my count. Do you propose to examine them all?”

“The argument with the Rhodian was the most heated.”

“Heated arguments do not produce corpses. What produces corpses is motive, opportunity, and the momentary suppression of the instinct against killing. The workers had the first and possibly the last. What did they not have?”

Menekles waited.

“Opportunity,” Holkos said. “Or rather — they had opportunity in abundance, which amounts to the same problem as having none at all. When every man has access to the scene, the fact of access tells us nothing.” He crouched. He crouched with a sudden fluency that was strange in a man who had been standing still — he went from stillness to crouched examination in a single motion, as if a decision had been made at some level beneath conscious thought. “The blood has been dry for some hours. It dried unevenly — faster at the edges, which means it spread as it dried. The spreading stopped here.” He indicated a boundary with one finger, not touching the earth. “The body was not moved after the blood dried. He died here, or within this distance of here.”

He studied the skull. He studied the entry of the wound without touching it. He looked at the packed earth around the body and then looked at the ground outside the lean-to. Then he stood.

“Walk me to the log-house,” he said. “I want the transfer record for yesterday.”

The log-house was a small structure of mudbrick adjacent to the weighing-house, where the clay tablets of the daily record were kept before they were sealed and dispatched to the palace. The tablets were indexed in the way Holkos had introduced eight years before — a system of his own invention, which Menekles used faithfully without ever being entirely comfortable with its logic. It organized transactions not by the identity of the merchant, as had been the practice, but by time-position in the day’s sequence, each entry keyed to the sequence of transfers rather than to the names of the parties. This, Holkos had explained when he introduced it, meant that gaps in the sequence were visible. Under the old system, a missing transaction simply did not appear, and the absence could not be detected. Under the new system, a missing transaction left a wound in the record that announced itself.

I had thought this theoretical when he explained it. I had not imagined it would be consequential.

He asked for the tablets for the previous day’s transfers. There were seven. He laid them on the flat reading-stone in the order of their sequence numbers and studied them for what seemed like a long time but was probably not. Then he turned the fourth tablet to face me and Menekles, and he put his finger on a section of the clay.

“The Rhodian’s cargo,” he said.

The entry was there. Arkelaos of Lindos. Copper ingots, twenty-two talents assessed weight, passed from the Saronic side, received into storage for eastern re-dispatch. The tally was complete and formally correct.

“What do I look at?” Menekles said.

“The sequence number of this entry,” Holkos said. “And then the sequence number of the entry before it, and the entry after it.”

Menekles looked. I looked. The tablets were numbered at the upper left edge in the standard form. The Rhodian’s entry was sequence eleven. The entry before it was sequence nine. The entry after it was sequence twelve.

There was no ten.

“A scribal error,” Menekles said immediately. “A missed number in the sequence.”

“In eleven years of the new system,” Holkos said, “a number has been missed in the sequence on three occasions. On each occasion the error appears on the line itself — the number is written and then corrected, or the line is drawn through. There is no such correction on the line nine, which runs directly into the line eleven. This was not a slip of the hand. The number ten was omitted from the sequence entirely.” He turned to look at us both with equal patience. “Which means either that someone destroyed the tenth tablet — which would show as a gap in the physical stack — or that someone was supervising the sequence closely enough to omit a number on purpose and write the subsequent entry as twelve to imply that ten had already passed.”

“To hide a transaction,” I said, before I had thought about whether to speak.

“To hide the absence of one,” Holkos said. “The tenth position in the sequence was the transfer immediately before the Rhodian’s cargo was received. Whatever occurred during that transfer — or whatever occurred instead of a transfer — has been removed from the record.”

“Who writes the sequence numbers?” Menekles asked.

The question fell into the log-house like a stone into still water, and we all felt the ripples.

The sequence numbers were written by the supervising scribe on duty. Yesterday, the late sequence — transfers eleven through fifteen, covering the late afternoon and early evening — had been recorded under the hand of Dexios.

Dexios was Menekles’s second assistant. He had been with the crossing administration for four years. He was a careful copyist and a plausible man of around twenty-five, with a round face and an agreeable manner. He had, as Menekles now recalled with some difficulty, reported this morning that the crossing-workers had argued violently with the Rhodian.

He had been, in other words, the man who planted the seed that had become Menekles’s theory before Holkos had arrived.

What followed was of a different character. I will not dress it as more dramatic than it was, because the plainness of it was itself a kind of drama.

Holkos sent me to bring Dexios to the log-house without explanation or ceremony, merely asking him to come because a question had arisen about the afternoon’s records. Dexios came agreeably, as he did everything. He was the kind of man who moves through institutional life on the energy of agreeableness — not servile, but perpetually oriented toward the smooth resolution of minor conflicts, anticipating small preferences, being useful before being asked. It is a social gift of real value. It is also a very good mask.

He came in. He looked at the tablets on the reading-stone. Something happened in his face that lasted perhaps as long as it takes a bird to pass a window — and then it was gone, resolved back into agreeableness.

Holkos had been standing at the side of the room. He now moved to the reading-stone and stood behind the tablets, which meant he was standing facing Dexios, with the tablets as a kind of altar between them. He said nothing for long enough that even Menekles shifted his weight.

“Tell me about the Rhodian,” he said finally.

Dexios told him, competently, the same account Menekles had given us — the argument over weights, the heated words, the general ill-feeling.

“How long did the argument last?”

“Perhaps a quarter of the afternoon.”

“And during the argument, what was occurring with the transfers?”

“They were held. No transfer could proceed while the Rhodian was contesting his assessment at the weighing-stone.”

“So the sequence paused.”

“Yes.”

“For a quarter of the afternoon. And in a sequence of fifteen transfers recorded yesterday, how many occurred during that pause?”

Dexios’s mouth opened slightly. He recovered. “None. The pause—”

“Would appear as a gap,” Holkos said pleasantly, “between two otherwise consecutive sequence numbers. As the gap between nine and eleven appears on this tablet. But you told Menekles the argument lasted a quarter of the afternoon. Surely you would have noted the pause in the record — a marginal notation, a comment on the delay, something to explain the gap to anyone reviewing the sequence. It would be standard practice. Where is your notation?”

There was no notation.

“You did not note it,” Holkos said, “because a notation would require you to specify the duration of the pause and account for your own whereabouts during it. And you cannot account for your whereabouts. Because your whereabouts were the east storage lean-to, where you met Arkelaos of Lindos while his cargo sat unsupervised, and where you struck him from behind with the wooden weighing-mallet whose head I observed this morning was missing from its handle in the weighing-house, though the handle remains hung on its peg. A detail I noticed. A detail you did not think to remove.”

Dexios said nothing.

“What I cannot read directly,” Holkos continued, and his voice had the quality of a man who is completing an arithmetic in a ledger rather than accusing another man of murder, “is the why. I can speculate. The Rhodian was carrying copper ingots. Twenty-two talents assessed weight. But the Rhodian was known at the eastern ports as a man who carried other things inside copper ingots when the arrangement benefited him — valuables of a kind that do not appear on palace transfer records, and for which the copper is ballast and concealment both. Someone at the receiving end of those hidden goods arranged for their recovery at the crossing, before the cargo reached its eastern destination. You were their man here. The Rhodian objected — perhaps he wanted more, perhaps he refused the arrangement, perhaps he had already removed the concealed material himself and was trying to cross with both the legitimate cargo and whatever was hidden in it. And the argument over weights was real, but it was not about weights.”

He stopped.

The log-house was very quiet. Somewhere outside, a donkey was being loaded and was expressing its opinion of this.

“There is a ship at Lechaion,” Holkos said, “which docked late yesterday and which has not yet completed its arrival formalities. It was waiting. I think if Menekles sends guards to Lechaion now, he will find that ship’s captain is expecting something to be brought to him this morning. Something that is no longer available, because the man who was supposed to bring it is standing in this room.”

He turned to Menekles. “Send for the guards. The case is yours to close.”

Here I, Pythodoros, must depart from strict account and allow myself the interpretive latitude that I promised to restrict. Holkos will not thank me for it. He does not read these administrative appendices.

The ship’s captain at Lechaion was taken with a quantity of worked gold concealed in the bilge-timber, consistent with the kind of hollow ingot-work the Rhodian had been known to employ. There was, behind this, the shadow of an arrangement extending along the trade-routes — a network of facilitators at various crossing-points who arranged for palace-assessed cargoes to shed their hidden contents before reaching their recorded destinations. Dexios had been recruited to it four years ago, which explained his appointment to the crossing, his agreeableness, and the precision with which he had managed to be useful without ever being scrutinized.

Holkos, when I observed to him that the network itself remained operative despite our resolution of the case, said only: we have cut one thread, Pythodoros. The loom remains. He said it with neither satisfaction nor distress, in the tone of a man noting that the tide is going out, which it always does and always will.

Dexios was remanded to the palace authority and removed from the crossing. The official record credits Menekles with the identification of the responsible party and commends the systematic value of the sequential log — Holkos’s system — in exposing the irregularity. Menekles accepted this with the dignified discomfort of an honest man receiving credit he knows is not entirely his. He mentioned Holkos in a private communication to the wanax’s administrative officer. Whether the mention was recorded or discarded, I cannot say.

I record it here. That is the only jurisdiction I have.

One matter I will append, though it bears on nothing official.

The morning after Dexios was taken away, I came to the log-house and found Holkos sitting with a piece of worn clay in his hand — an old tablet, not one of ours, covered in a script I did not recognize. He was working at it slowly, in the way that other men sharpen tools: methodically, without great pleasure, as necessary maintenance.

I asked him what it was.

He said it was a riddle. From the Cyprus trade. He had had it three months and had not solved it.

I asked if he expected to.

He turned it in his hands. The light came through the east window, which was a good window for morning light, positioned as it was above the reading-stone.

“The riddle,” he said, “is not the point. The point is that it is not yet solved.”

He put it down, took up the day’s first tablet, and began to work.

This is the account of the matter of Arkelaos of Lindos, set down truly and completely, according to the observation of Pythodoros, in the season of the early harvest.

Children:

- Holkos of Athens — see next entry.

HOLKOS OF ATHENS

~1360 BC — Athens, Greece

Athens in the Mycenaean age was not yet the city of Pericles and philosophy but a palace-centre of the second rank — a citadel on a limestone rock, a wanax and his scribes and his warriors, a Linear B archive recording the obligations of dependent farmers and the contents of bronze-smiths’ stores. Holkos of Athens served in that palace economy as his father had served in Corinth and his grandfather before that in Mycenae itself — as a merchant-interpreter, moving between the Greek palace world and the trading networks that connected it to Cyprus, to the Levant, to Egypt, and to the Hittite empire to the east.

He was a man whose value lay in languages and in the particular intelligence that languages, when truly mastered, convey: not merely the words but the assumptions behind them, the conventions that a speaker of one tongue considers too obvious to articulate to a speaker of another. Holkos of Athens had that intelligence in full, and he used it to move goods and information between worlds that could not otherwise communicate with the precision that commerce and diplomacy required. He raised his son in the same tradition — the same formation, the same alertness, and the same conviction that the most consequential work is usually done at the boundary between two things that do not quite understand each other.

The account that follows is preserved by the scribe Menandros, whose duties in the Athenian archive included the copying of trade disputes and agricultural records. His notes, appended after the resolution of the matter, show both care and limitation: he records what he was shown, but often notes his inability to follow the reasoning until Holkos later explained it to him directly.

The Account Of Menandros

I will say first what every man in the citadel already knows, which is that I am not a clever man. I can copy a tablet without error from the first sign to the last. I can sum a column of barley figures and arrive at the same answer twice. I can distinguish the hand of Arkitas from the hand of Deukalion at twenty paces in poor lamplight. These are the competencies of my office, and I have always considered them sufficient.

It was Holkos of Athens who taught me — if that is the correct word for a process that felt more like having one’s assumptions removed one at a time, with the clinical patience of a man extracting splinters — that to see what is written on a tablet and to read what its writing discloses are operations so different from one another that calling them by the same name is a kind of fraud.

I did not understand this when he first said it to me, in the lower corridor of the archive, while the body of Arkitas lay cooling at our feet.

I understand it now.

The morning had the particular quality that late summer mornings in Athens sometimes acquire: a stillness so absolute that the air itself seems to be waiting. The smell of the citadel at that hour is always the same — limestone dust, the rendered fat of last night’s lamps, and beneath both of those the older smell of the rock itself, which is a smell like the inside of a sealed jar, dry and mineral and slightly airless, as though the hill is slowly breathing out something it has held since before men came to put walls upon it.

The gate-hinge of the archive — I will not forget its sound, a distinctive bronze shriek, a rising pitch that caught at the top of the swing — made that sound at the moment I arrived to begin the morning copy-work, and I pushed through to find the room occupied more than I had anticipated.

Arkitas lay between the central table and the east shelf, on his back, with his arms arranged in a way that suggested he had not arranged them himself. His oil lamp had burned to nothing on the table beside the tablet-press. The tablets from the previous evening’s session were laid in their drying rack along the north wall, still soft enough that the morning chill had not fully set them.

This was the strangeness that I noticed first and that I could not explain: the tablets were there. Arkitas was dead. But the assessment session from the previous evening — a disputed cargo matter involving two merchants and considerable quantities of copper ingots — had apparently been completed, because the tablets in the rack numbered exactly what they should have numbered, and they bore entries in what appeared to be a proper hand.

The other man who had been present for that session — a merchant called Eruthros, who had sailed with Cretan copper from a Cypriot intermediary and disputed the palace’s assessment of his declared weight — was nowhere in the archive.

I sent for the Lawagetas’s man, who arrived with three palace guards and the expression of a person who has learned that a problem exists but not yet what shape it takes. His name was Theron, a careful, broad-shouldered official of middle years who had managed the palace’s inspection of cargo movements for longer than I had held my post. He looked at Arkitas. He looked at the tablets in the rack. He looked toward the door.

“The merchant Eruthros,” he said. “Where is the merchant Eruthros.”

This was the correct question, and within the hour Eruthros had been found on the lower slopes of the citadel, near the merchant quarter, breaking his fast with apparent calm at the house of his factor. He was brought back to the archive.

He said: Arkitas had been alive when he left. The assessment had been completed. He had simply gone to sleep. He said this with the particular composure of a man who has rehearsed the sentence.

Theron believed this to be a lie. So did I. But Eruthros was a man of standing in the trading network — Cypriot contacts, Levantine contacts, a factor in Corinth and another, it was said, in Rhodes — and the matter required more than Theron’s intuition and my unease. It required, the Lawagetas’s secretary eventually decided, a fresh pair of eyes.

Holkos of Athens had completed a trading mission to the eastern ports that same month and was present in the citadel for his accounting. He was known to the palace. He was asked.

He came in the late morning.

I had seen Holkos at work before — twice, in minor matters of disputed provenance and one question of a falsified consignment record — but never in a matter of this weight, and I watched him now with the attention I would give to a man performing a craft I did not practice.

He was perhaps fifty, though the age was difficult to assign. A narrow face, deeply browned by travel, with eyes of an unremarkable grey-green that moved across a surface the way water moves across stone: covering everything, exerting a continuous, even pressure, leaving nothing quite dry. He wore a plain wool travelling cloak that he did not remove. He had brought nothing with him except a small polished obsidian disc, which he occasionally turned in his fingers in moments of apparent abstraction. Later I learned this was his habitual occupation for the hands when the brain required them to be still.

He entered the archive and did not look at the body.

He looked instead at the lamp on the table. He looked at the tablet-press. He walked to the drying rack and examined each tablet in sequence, holding them at an angle to the light from the high window. He spent particular time on the last two entries. He set them back carefully, in order.

He looked at the floor near the east shelf. He crouched and examined something I could not see — I was standing too far back, and Theron’s guards filled most of the corridor-mouth. He straightened without comment.

Then he looked at the body.

He did so for approximately the time it takes to recite a short prayer — unhurried, complete — and then he stood and turned to the wall where the current-year assessment tablets were stored in their cubbies, and he began reading them. Not the fresh tablets. The old ones. The records of previous assessments in which Arkitas had served as the recording scribe.

Theron shifted his weight. I could feel his impatience from across the room. He was a man who reasoned in straight lines, and he had already drawn his straight line from Eruthros to the body, and he wanted the formality of confirmation so that he could send his guards to make an arrest.

“Can you tell us,” he said, “whether the merchant’s account is false or true?”

Holkos did not turn around. “I can tell you rather more than that,” he said. “But I prefer to tell it all at once. It would lose something, divided.”

He assembled us finally — Theron, myself, two of the guards, and Eruthros, who had been permitted to remain on the understanding that his cooperation was expected — near the north wall, where the drying rack held its tablets in morning light.

He picked up the second-to-last tablet.

“You are a scribe, Menandros,” he said to me, not unkindly. “What do you see?”

“An entry,” I said. “A cargo assessment. The standard format — commodity, declared weight, assessed weight, the differential, the palace’s share.”

“Whose hand?”

I looked. “Arkitas,” I said. “The hand of Arkitas. I know it well.”

“And this one?” He handed me the last tablet — the final entry of the evening.

I looked. I said, with confidence: “Also Arkitas.”

Holkos smiled, very slightly, in a way I did not care for, because it was not the smile of a man who has been agreed with. It was the smile of a teacher whose student has just provided the demonstration the lesson requires.

“Menandros,” he said, “you know the hand of Arkitas because you have worked alongside him for years and you see its forms as a unified whole, a single impression. I am looking at it for the first time this morning, which means I am looking at it as a collection of individual decisions — where a sign-maker chooses to begin a sign, how he completes the downstroke, whether he lifts the stylus between the horizontal wedges or draws them in a single motion.” He set the two tablets side by side on the table edge. “The hand of Arkitas, across your archive records, is a hand formed in the Mycenae tradition with one peculiarity: he begins the sign for ka — the syllable that opens the commodity notation for bronze, ka-ko — with an upward motion. Not unusual; it is one of the variant forms. Consistent, across twenty tablets I examined. He has done it this way since he first learned the sign.”

He tapped the final tablet.

“The last entry of the evening was made by a person who begins ka with a downward motion. It is the more common form. It is not the form Arkitas used. The hand is an imitation — a good one, made by someone who had studied Arkitas’s records carefully enough to reproduce the proportions and the spacing, but who could not entirely suppress their own ingrained habits when they reached a sign they had always written differently.”

The room was quiet.

I looked at the two tablets again. And I — who had read them as identical — now saw it. The last entry’s ko sign descended first. All of Arkitas’s others ascended. It was a small thing. It was, once seen, completely impossible to unsee.

“The final entry,” Holkos continued, “was made after Arkitas was dead. It was made to complete the record — to give the appearance that the assessment session had concluded in proper form, that both parties had settled their figures, that Eruthros’s cargo had been assessed at a weight favorable to Eruthros, and that Arkitas had certified it. A record that certified it meant Eruthros would owe the palace considerably less bronze than the true weight demanded. A record made by Arkitas’s own hand — or what appeared to be his hand — could not be disputed.” He set the tablet down. “Eruthros killed him. He studied Arkitas’s sign-forms well in advance — probably from public records, from previous assessments involving his cargo. He came prepared. He brought his own stylus, which is why the depth of the impression on that final tablet is slightly heavier than Arkitas’s own entries: a different instrument, a different grip. He made the entry. He arranged the body. He left.”

Eruthros had not moved. He was looking at the tablet with an expression from which something had just departed — the composure, I think, like water running from a cracked vessel.

“The body,” Theron said. “The cause.”

“A blow behind the left ear. The floor has a scuff-mark near the east shelf consistent with a man falling backward against it. Arkitas was struck while working, possibly in a moment of dispute over the figures, possibly by prior arrangement that was never intended to resolve peacefully. Either way, he fell, and he did not rise, and Eruthros had his opportunity.” Holkos picked up the obsidian disc from the table where he had set it. “The lamp had burned low by then — you can see by the residue that it had been burning for many hours. Eruthros worked by its final light to complete his entry. That is why the impression pressure varied: he was working in near-darkness, pressing harder than necessary to be certain the signs were legible.”

Theron was already looking at his guards.

What happened next is the part I find most difficult to record without distorting it, because I am not certain I fully understood it as it occurred, and I have only Holkos’s later explanation to supplement my own account.

Before the guards moved, Holkos said: “A moment, Theron.”

Theron paused.

“Eruthros,” Holkos said, in a different register — still quiet, but with something in it that was not quite the same as the preceding clinical tone. Something that suggested the clinical portion was finished and something less comfortable had begun. “The cargo that was disputed. The copper. Where is it from, precisely?”

Eruthros looked at him.

“Not the answer you gave the archive,” Holkos said. “The actual answer.”

A long silence. The second lower note of the gate-hinge drifted in from the corridor, where someone had opened the door to look in at us.

“Alashiya,” Eruthros said at last. This was Cyprus, or the kingdom on Cyprus — significant because it was under some degree of Hittite influence, and movements of large copper quantities from that source carried political implications beyond the commercial.

“And the man who arranged the contract,” Holkos said. “In Corinth.”

Eruthros’s jaw shifted.

“I know his name,” Holkos said pleasantly. “I have worked those routes for thirty years. I know who coordinates the larger movements of Alashiyan copper through the Aegean without palace knowledge. I know how that coordination works, and I know approximately how many palace officials in how many citadels have received gifts to facilitate it.” He paused. “I am not going to speak that name in this room.”

Theron turned to look at him sharply.

“Because,” Holkos continued, as though Theron had asked a question rather than merely looked at him, “if I speak it in this room, the matter becomes a matter for the Lawagetas, and then for the Wanax, and then — quite rapidly — for people considerably more powerful than any of us. Arkitas died because he assessed honestly and would not amend his figures. He was a competent man doing his work with integrity. He deserves justice. Eruthros will give him justice. But the name behind Eruthros, and the network behind that name, is a matter for another day, in another room, and I will take it up directly with the appropriate party when I am ready to do so and not before.” He looked at Theron. “Eruthros killed Arkitas. He falsified a palace record. The case against him is complete and requires nothing further. Take him.”

Theron took him.

I do not believe Theron understood what had just been withheld from him, and why. I did not understand it myself until Holkos explained it to me that evening, walking back through the lower citadel in the long amber light.

“The network,” he said, turning the obsidian disc, “is not dismantled by naming one man in one archive to one Lawagetas’s official. It disperses. It learns. It finds new men in new archives. Eruthros is caught, and Eruthros will pay for Arkitas. But the man in Corinth is of more value to me unaware of my knowledge than he would be if I had named him today and watched him vanish to Rhodes before nightfall.” He looked sideways at me. “Do you understand?”

“I think so,” I said.

“You don’t yet,” he said, without unkindness. “But you will write it down accurately, which is the more important quality.”

I should record, for the purpose of completeness and because I believe it will be useful to whoever reads these notes after me, what Holkos said when I asked him how he had known to examine the sign-forms specifically — rather than the content of the tablets, or the weight of the stylus impressions, or any number of other details he might have examined first.

He was quiet for a moment, in that particular way he had of being quiet that was not the quiet of a man who does not know what to say but of a man who is deciding precisely which part of a larger answer to offer.

“Forgery,” he said, “requires the forger to become someone they are not. And the places where a person cannot fully become someone else — cannot abandon their own formations, their own ingrained motions — those are exactly the places where the forgery speaks loudest. Not where it is most similar to the original, but where it slips.” He tapped two fingers against his temple. “A man’s hand is not merely a skill. It is twenty years of habit compressed into motion. You cannot borrow another man’s twenty years, however carefully you study his results. You can borrow his proportions. You cannot borrow his direction.”

He turned the disc once more.

“I look,” he said, “for the moment a man stops pretending and starts, without realizing it, being himself.”

Eruthros of the Cypriot trade-route was brought before the Lawagetas and bound over for palace judgment. The record certified his guilt in the matter of the death of Arkitas, scribe of the first rank, and in the falsification of a cargo assessment tablet. The archive record has been corrected. The falsified tablet has been preserved in its incorrect state alongside this account, as Holkos requested, on the grounds that it is of greater documentary value intact than destroyed.

The name Holkos declined to speak in the archive, I also decline to write here. It is known to me. It will be useful, in time, to one who knows how to use it.

Menandros, Scribe of the Second Rank
Athens, Forty-Third Year

Children:

- Holkos the Elder — see next entry.

HOLKOS THE ELDER

~1330 BC — Athens, Greece

He was called the Elder to distinguish him from the son who would bear his name and surpass him — a distinction that pleased him, he is said to have remarked, since a father whose son exceeds him has succeeded in the most important project of his life. Holkos the Elder managed the family’s trading connections through the reign of the Mycenaean palace system’s last confident generation, before the pressures that would eventually destroy it had become visible in their full severity.

He was an assessor of men as much as of goods — the two being, in the palace economy of Mycenaean Greece, largely the same problem. The wanax’s administration required constant evaluation of the reliability of dependent producers, the honesty of factors, the genuine versus the stated capacity of craftsmen and farmers to deliver what they had promised. Holkos the Elder conducted these assessments with a speed that surprised those being assessed, because long practice had given him a precision of reading that most men did not know was possible and that he could not, if asked, have easily explained.

The record that follows is preserved by the scribe Phaedimos, whose role in the chamberlain’s office was to maintain accounts of negotiations and private meetings. His notes are unusually detailed in description, though he often remarks that he did not understand the full implications of what he recorded until after the events had concluded and Holkos explained them to him in private.

The Account Of Phaedimos

Set down from my own notes and from what Holkos the Elder later chose to explain to me — though he was careful, as always, to explain only as much as served his purposes.

The morning it began, the oil lamps in the scribal hall were burning the cheap stuff — the pressed lees of the second pressing, not the clean oil kept for the wanax’s own chambers — and the smell of it sat in the back of the throat like a complaint. I had been at my tablet since before the light came properly through the slit windows, copying the season’s grain assessments from the preliminary records onto the master ledger, when Drosos came in.

Drosos was the kind of man the palace produced in abundance: capable, presentable, possessed of sufficient intelligence to execute instructions without requiring them to be repeated, and deeply untroubled by questions that exceeded his instructions. He served as collection officer for the smith-bronze quota in the district of the lower valley, which meant he was responsible for ensuring that the dependent farmers who had been allocated bronze ingots for agricultural tools returned the expected portion of finished implements to the palace stores. It was a position that required the counting of things and the filing of accounts. Drosos was very good at counting things.

He dropped a tablet on the table beside me with the particular firmness that means a man believes he is doing something important.

“There is an irregularity,” he said. “In the account of Kerkyon of the River Farm.”

I looked at the tablet. It was a secondary accounting — a field officer’s personal record, not a master document — and it showed Kerkyon’s registered bronze obligation, the ingots issued, and the shortfall in returned implements: eighteen bronze points for ox-goads, undelivered.

“Substantial,” I said, because it was.

“I have filed the accusation with the chamberlain’s office. Kerkyon will be summoned.” Drosos permitted himself the smallest possible expression of satisfaction. “I thought you should know, since you will be preparing the formal notation.”

After he left, I sat for a moment with the smell of bad oil and the sound of the building settling in the morning heat — the particular groan of the storage corridor’s wooden beam that I had learned to anticipate every summer, regular as a gate-hinge suffering from the same seasonal complaint. I noted the tablet’s contents in my daily record, as my function required. I did not yet think there was anything unusual about the matter.

I would not have brought it to Holkos the Elder at all, except that I happened to encounter him that same afternoon in the corridor outside the chamberlain’s office, where he had come to review a separate matter concerning the reliability of a factor in the eastern olive district. He had the look he sometimes wore when his mind was occupied but his body was bored with standing still — a kind of coiled stillness, like rope before the tension is applied. He asked me what I was carrying.

I told him about Kerkyon. It was the sort of thing one mentioned in passing.

He asked to see my notes.

He read them in the way he read everything — not scanning but consuming, the way a smith looks at metal rather than the way an ordinary man looks at metal. Then he handed them back.

“Where is the registered obligation written?” he said.

“In Drosos’s field tablet, which—”

“Not Drosos’s field tablet. I mean the master register. The palace record of what Kerkyon was assessed to owe this season.”

I thought about it. “That would be in the preliminary assessment rolls. Which I was copying this morning.”

Something in his expression changed in a way I could not then have named, though I understand it now as the difference between a man who is merely interested and a man who has seen something that the rest of the world has not yet seen. “The preliminary assessment rolls,” he said, “that you were copying onto the master ledger this morning. Which is to say, the preliminary rolls have not yet been superseded.”

“Not until I finish the master transfer. They remain the governing document until then.”

“Then,” Holkos the Elder said, with the mildness of a man stating something he considers obvious, “I think we should look at them.”

He required two things of me that I found, at the time, peculiar.

The first was that we visit Kerkyon’s farm before we looked at the rolls. I pointed out that the farm was below the lower valley road and that the afternoon was already advanced. Holkos observed that the afternoon was indeed advanced, which was precisely why we should go now, before Drosos’s official summons arrived in the morning and Kerkyon spent the night believing himself a criminal. I did not follow the reasoning but I had learned, by then, that failure to follow Holkos’s reasoning was not evidence that the reasoning was wrong.

The farm sat in a crook of the river where the bank had built up good alluvial soil over generations. The kind of farm that had clearly been managed carefully for a long time — the field boundaries straight, the drainage channels maintained, the trees at the edge of the cultivation line properly pruned. Kerkyon himself was a man of perhaps forty-five years, built with the particular solidity of men who have done physical work since childhood and never quite stopped. He came out of the barn when he heard us on the path and stood with his hands at his sides in the posture of a man who does not yet know whether he should be afraid.

Holkos the Elder looked at him for perhaps the space of four heartbeats.

Then he walked past him into the barn.

I followed, because I did not know what else to do. The barn smelled of straw and animals and the particular sharp mineral smell of worked bronze, because in one corner there was Kerkyon’s smithing area — a small operation, the kind a marginal farmer maintains to produce the implements his allocation requires — with the tools, the fuel store, the water basin, and the finished work waiting for collection. I counted the waiting implements. I counted them again. I was not a specialist in bronze-work quotas but I had been copying assessment numbers all morning and the figures were fresh.

Holkos the Elder was doing the same thing, but he was also doing something else: he was examining the actual condition of the implements, the finish of the work, the length of time the metal had been sitting in that corner. He picked up one of the ox-goad points and looked at it in the light from the doorway. He set it down. He looked at the fuel store — the quantity remaining, the marks of what had been used. He looked at Kerkyon’s hands.

“You finished early this season,” Holkos said to the farmer. Not accusatory. Simply observational, the way one notes that a cloud has moved.

Kerkyon blinked. “The bronze worked well. Good pour. I had it done before the feast of the second month.”

“And when was the collection officer last here?”

“Drosos? He came in the first month, at the start. To check the ingots were received.” A pause. “He wrote something. He always writes something.”

On the walk back up to the palace, as the light went orange over the hills and the air began to carry the first suggestion of evening cool, I asked Holkos what he had seen.

He was quiet for long enough that I thought he would not answer. Then:

“Tell me what you observed in that barn.”

“The implements were present. In sufficient quantity.”

“In sufficient quantity,” Holkos agreed. “More than sufficient quantity, in fact. Kerkyon completed his quota and then continued working because the conditions were favorable. He is that kind of man — the farm is that kind of farm. When work goes well, he does not stop. He has three additional points beyond his required number sitting in that corner.”

I considered this. “Then the accusation is simply wrong. Drosos miscounted.”

“Drosos,” Holkos said, “did not miscount.” He said it with the particular precision he used when he wanted a sentence to carry weight. “A man who miscounts leaves the error in the numbers. A man who is wrong in a different way leaves the error somewhere else.”

I did not understand him then. I have since learned that when Holkos the Elder said something I did not understand, the correct response was to hold it and wait.

We returned to the scribal hall in the early evening. The lamps were lit — the good oil now, for the evening work, cedar-clean and steady — and I retrieved the preliminary assessment rolls from the shelf where I had left them. Holkos sat across from me and I read him the relevant figures: Kerkyon’s name, his farm designation, the ingots issued, the registered obligation.

Holkos asked me to read the obligation figure again.

I read it.

“Now,” he said, “look at your own morning notes. What figure did Drosos’s field tablet show for the registered obligation?”

I looked. I looked again.

The figure in Drosos’s field tablet was larger. Not enormously — not a number that would announce itself as a fraud to a casual eye. But larger. Eighteen points, where the preliminary assessment roll showed twelve.

I said: “That is not the same number.”

“No,” Holkos agreed. “It is not.”

“Drosos’s tablet shows—”

“Drosos’s tablet shows what Drosos chose to write on Drosos’s tablet.” Holkos leaned back. In the lamp-light his face was calm with the specific calm of a man who has solved an equation and is waiting for the other person to check the working. “The master register has not yet been completed — you told me so yourself. The preliminary rolls are the governing document until the transfer is finished. Which means the registered obligation exists in two places at the moment: the palace preliminary roll, and Drosos’s own field record. And the numbers are different.”

“Drosos inflated the obligation,” I said. Slowly, because I wanted to be certain I was not constructing something that was not there. “He wrote a higher number in his own record than the assessment actually requires. And then he waited until he could file an accusation of shortfall — a shortfall that only exists against his number, not the real one.”

“Kerkyon,” Holkos said, “would have been summoned. He would have protested his innocence. Drosos would have produced his field tablet, which is the document of record as far as the collection officer’s office is concerned. Kerkyon would not have known which version of the number was the true assessment — farmers are not generally invited to inspect the palace rolls. In the confusion, the palace would have taken the implements Kerkyon had made beyond his quota — those three additional points — and Drosos would have...”

He did not finish the sentence. He did not need to.

“Sold them,” I said. “Or traded them. Bronze is bronze.”

“Bronze is bronze,” Holkos agreed. “It has been, I would estimate, a reliable arrangement. Kerkyon is almost certainly not the first.”

The lamp flame moved in a draft from the corridor, throwing brief shadows across the tablet in my hands. Outside, somewhere in the storage buildings, that wooden beam made its evening complaint — the same sound it made every night, faithful as a bad conscience.

The question of what to do with the information occupied me considerably less than it seemed to occupy Holkos the Elder.

I assumed, in my straightforward scribal way, that we would take the discrepancy to the chamberlain. There was documentary evidence. There was a clear comparison between the preliminary roll and the field tablet. The conclusion was not difficult to reach.

Holkos said nothing for a time. He had the ennui on him now — the other version of himself, the one that set in after the problem was solved, when the world became temporarily uninteresting. He sat with one hand flat on the table and looked at the lamp as if it had said something disappointing.

“The chamberlain,” he said at last, “will want to know how I arrived at the discrepancy.”

“You observed it from my notes, and from our visit to the farm, and from—”

“From an informal visit to a farm that no official process authorized, conducted at an hour when the relevant official had already concluded his own inquiry.” He looked at me. “The chamberlain will also want to know who else has been affected. Which means an audit of Drosos’s records across the district. Which means, if Drosos has been careful — and I think he has been careful — a period during which the irregularities may be obscured before the auditors arrive.”

I had not thought of that.

“And Drosos,” Holkos continued, in the same level tone, “is not working alone.
