A collection officer who has been running this arrangement for — I estimate, from the condition of Kerkyon’s barn and the pattern of the seasonal notes — at least three seasons, has accommodation from someone in the assessment office who is preparing the preliminary rolls in the first place. The discrepancy has to be introduced somewhere. Drosos could not have done it alone.”

The lamp. The smell of good cedar oil. The beam in the storage corridor. The whole familiar weight of the palace settling around us in the evening.

“So we cannot simply report it to the chamberlain,” I said.

“We can report it to the chamberlain,” Holkos said. “But the way in which we report it determines whether the grain is saved or scattered.”

What he did next I have reconstructed partly from my own observation and partly from what he explained afterward, on the condition that I would not record it in any form that would reach official eyes.

He spent the following morning doing nothing that appeared to be anything. He sat in the outer scribal hall and read the current season’s assessment rolls — all of them, not merely the bronze quotas but the grain, the linen, the livestock — with the patience of a man who is not in a hurry because he already knows approximately what he is going to find. He ate the bread that was brought for the scribal staff, which he normally did not bother with. He was very still.

In the early afternoon he asked me, in passing, which scribe in the assessment office was responsible for the bronze quota rolls.

I told him. The name was Alektruon.

He asked me, as if idly, how long Alektruon had held that position.

Four seasons, I said.

He nodded as if this confirmed something he had already decided. He went back to his reading.

By evening he had what he needed, though he did not tell me then what it was. He said only that he would speak with the chamberlain in the morning, and that I should be present.

I was present.

I will not set down the full substance of what passed in that room, partly because some of it pertained to individuals whose situation has since been resolved in ways the palace has chosen not to publicize, and partly because Holkos was careful — as he was always careful — to give the chamberlain the conclusions without all of the working.

What I can record is this:

He presented the tablet comparison — the preliminary roll figure against Drosos’s field tablet figure — without drama, simply as a documentary fact. The chamberlain was a careful man and he saw immediately what it meant.

Holkos then presented three additional discrepancies he had identified in the morning’s reading, from previous seasons, in the records of two other farmers in Drosos’s district. Smaller variations, handled more delicately, but following the same pattern.

He then observed that the preliminary rolls containing the relevant assessments had all passed through the same scribal position in the assessment office, and that cross-referencing those assessment decisions against the external ledger — the ledger that recorded what was actually delivered to the factor at the point of collection, before Drosos’s own records were consulted — revealed a consistent shape of erasure.

The chamberlain asked how this had not been detected in the standard review process.

Holkos said, with the courtesy he used when pointing out that someone had asked a question whose answer was uncomfortable: the standard review process compared the collection officer’s records against the assessment rolls. If both documents were prepared by parties working in concert, the comparison would confirm rather than reveal the arrangement. The external ledger — the factor’s record at the point of final collection — was the only document neither of them had controlled, and it was not part of the standard comparison.

The chamberlain was quiet for a moment.

Then he asked what Holkos recommended.

This was the part I have thought about most in the years since.

Holkos said that Kerkyon should be told immediately that the accusation against him had been found to be without foundation and was withdrawn. He said this with a firmness that left no room for procedural equivocation.

He said that the audit of Drosos’s records should be conducted by officers from the grain quota administration, who had no connection to the bronze quota system, and should be framed as a routine seasonal cross-check rather than an investigation — because an announced investigation would accelerate the destruction of records.

He said that Alektruon’s position should be quietly transferred to a different scribal function before the audit concluded, on administrative grounds, without accusation.

And then he said: the individuals involved should face the chamberlain’s judgment in private, without the matter reaching the wanax’s formal court.

The chamberlain raised an eyebrow at this. “You advocate for leniency?”

“I advocate for containment,” Holkos said. “A public proceeding names names. Named names invite questions about how long the arrangement operated and who knew. Those questions, pursued thoroughly, will reach walls you would prefer not to breach.” He paused. “The arrangement was not large enough to justify the walls it might damage. Adjust it quietly. The farmers will know justice was done; they do not need the proceedings published.”

I thought, at the time, that I disagreed with this. I was the moral element in that room — I felt the weight of Kerkyon’s night, the sleeplessness of a man who does not know if he will be condemned for something he did not do. I wanted a public accounting.

I have since come to understand that Holkos had made a calculation I was not equipped to make. The walls were real. The chamberlain knew it. The quiet resolution protected something that mattered more, and distributed a justice that was, if not visible, at least actual.

Kerkyon received a formal notification, delivered by palace messenger, that an administrative review had determined his quota obligations had been fulfilled and that no further proceeding would occur. It was written in the dry language of the scribal office and said nothing about why. But it said what it needed to say.

I asked Holkos, afterward, when we were alone and the cedar oil was burning and the storage-corridor beam had made its evening noise, why he had wanted to visit the farm first. What he had learned from Kerkyon’s hands, from the fuel store, from the condition of the implements that he could not have learned from the documents.

He was in the languid mode — the problem was finished, the world was flat and uninteresting, and he was looking at the wall with the expression of a man for whom the next few days promised nothing stimulating.

“I needed to know,” he said, “whether Kerkyon had anything to hide.”

“Because if he had—”

“Because if he had, the arithmetic would have had two explanations instead of one. A man who is genuinely withholding does not keep his surplus visible in an unlocked barn.” He picked up a small piece of clay from the table, turned it in his fingers, and set it down. “The farm told me the accusation was false before the tablet confirmed it. The tablet only told me who had made it false.”

I noted that the preliminary rolls had been the instrument — that if I had completed the master transfer before the accusation was filed, the preliminary document would have been superseded and the comparison would have been impossible to make.

Holkos said: “Yes.”

He said it in the way that meant he had considered this already.

I asked whether he thought the timing had been deliberate.

He looked at me then, and there was something in his face that was not quite amusement and not quite sadness — a quality I have seen in very few men, which is the expression of someone who understands exactly how much the world is arranged against simple honesty, and who has made a kind of settled peace with that understanding without being destroyed by it.

“Phaedimos,” he said, “everything in that arrangement was deliberate. The only thing that was not deliberate was you.”

He meant that I had been in the hall when Drosos filed the preliminary report. He meant that I had been copying the rolls that morning, so that the figures were fresh. He meant that I had mentioned it to him in a corridor, because I happened to meet him there, and he had happened to ask what I was carrying.

He meant, I think, that it had been a near thing.

I have set this account down as faithfully as I am able, recording what I observed and what was later explained to me. I have omitted those elements that the chamberlain asked should not be written, and I have preserved those elements that seem to me to illuminate something worth illuminating — which is that the detection of an untruth requires, before it requires anything else, a correct understanding of what the truth should look like.

Holkos the Elder would have said this was obvious.

Most things, when he was finished with them, seemed obvious.

That was the nature of the man.

—Phaedimos, scribe of the chamberlain’s office
Second season of the current administration

Children:

- Holkos the Younger — see next entry.

HOLKOS THE YOUNGER

~1300 BC — Hittite Anatolia

The trade routes between Mycenaean Greece and the Hittite empire ran east across the Aegean, through the cities of the western Anatolian coast, and deep into the Hittite heartland — a commercial artery that the two great powers of the age maintained with the pragmatic recognition that the goods each wanted were in the possession of the other. Holkos the Younger followed those routes east in early adulthood, drawn by the same appetite for the frontier of the known world that had taken Milqart-yaton to Minoan Crete two centuries before.

He settled in western Anatolia, in the zone where Mycenaean and Hittite cultural influence overlapped and the sharp edges of both empires were softened by the necessities of trade. He learned Luwian, the language of the western Anatolian populations, and enough Hittite to function in the imperial administrative context, and built a position as a factor between the two worlds that was both commercially productive and, for a man of his particular gifts, endlessly interesting. Every transaction was a negotiation between different assumptions about value, obligation, and trust; every negotiation was an opportunity to read the men on the other side of the table more precisely than they were reading him.

The account that follows was preserved by Nikomachos, a clerk attached to the Hittite-licensed trading house. He was not a philosopher nor trained in the subtleties of inquiry, but he possessed a careful hand and a habit of recording what he saw in the margins of shipment tallies and correspondence tablets. It is through his record that the sequence of events has come down, though his own understanding of what he witnessed shaped what he chose to note and what he left unsaid.

The Record Of Nikomachos

Set down in the season of the Pleiades rising, in the year that the great king’s grain-ships came late to the coast.

There is a smell particular to the trading post at Arziya that I have never encountered anywhere else along the western roads. It is cedar oil and rotting barley and the particular sweat of men who have walked a long way in leather sandals and then been made to wait. The smell was in my nostrils on the morning Arnuwanda-rabi summoned me, and it has lodged itself in my memory alongside everything that followed, so that I cannot think of one without the other.

The trading post squatted at the junction of two dust-roads like a man sitting down in the middle of an argument — stubborn, graceless, and impossible to go around. Its walls were the colour of old bone. The gate-hinge, a great bronze pin in a bronze socket, had been singing the same flat, mournful note since before my employment, and it sang that morning as the factor Pulli-ziti’s wife was shown through it into the courtyard.

Her name was Tiwatali. She was a small woman made of hard decisions, with the kind of stillness about her that speaks not of peace but of someone accustomed to waiting for disaster. She told Arnuwanda-rabi that her husband had left twelve days past with the consignment of copper ingots for the garrison post at Hattena, and had not returned. Hattena was three days’ journey. He should have been back in eight.

Arnuwanda-rabi heard her out with the expression he wore when he was calculating whether a cargo was worth its risk — a slight compression of the lips, a lowering of the brows. He thanked her for coming and promised enquiries. When she was gone he called for more wine and told me to find Holkos.

I had known Holkos the Younger for four years by that morning, long enough to have ceased being surprised by him and not long enough to have ceased being unsettled. He was perhaps thirty years of age, or perhaps older — his face was one of those faces that the Fates seem to have assembled from the oldest features in their workshop, all blade and precision, so that he had always looked forty and would likely look forty when he was sixty. He was Achaean by birth and tongue, though he spoke Luwian without an accent and Hittite well enough to argue prices with imperial scribes, which is a high bar. He had come east on the trade roads as a young man, as men of appetite sometimes do, and had stayed because the west could not offer him sufficient complication.

His room at the back of the trading house was a place of organised disorder. He kept cedar tablets stacked in sequences only he understood. He kept samples of pigment from six different workshops arranged by colour on a cedar shelf. He kept a collection of knucklebones — not for gambling but for some private system of calculation I never penetrated — and he kept, on the windowsill, a small clay vessel from which came, at intervals, a thin curl of smoke, for he burned a particular resinous gum when his mind was restless, claiming that the smell cleared the passages through which thought must travel. When his mind was not restless — when a problem had presented itself that he judged worthy of his attention — he needed no gum. He needed nothing at all except the problem.

He was seated on his sleeping mat in the posture of absolute stillness that I had learned to associate with active thinking rather than idleness when I knocked. The gum was burning. Not a good sign, I thought, for the urgency of the matter.

I told him about Pulli-ziti.

He did not move for a moment. Then he said, without opening his eyes, “What was in the consignment?”

I told him: copper ingots from the coastal smelters, bound for the garrison at Hattena, fourteen ingots of standard weight, documented in the manifest I had myself prepared.

“And Arnuwanda-rabi sent Pulli-ziti alone?”

“With two pack-bearers. Neither has returned.”

“The pack-bearers’ names.”

I gave them. He opened his eyes.

“Show me the manifest.”

He was a different man with the manifest in his hands. The languor fell off him like a cloak dropped to the floor. He read it three times, which I knew because I watched him, and on the third reading his finger traced the column of weights and stopped.

“Nikomachos,” he said. “You prepared this document.”

“I did.”

“These ingots were weighed by you personally?”

“Weighed and counted.”

“Fourteen ingots at standard Hittite mine-weight. Good. And yet —” he turned the tablet toward me and pointed — “the declared value for customs passage at the garrison gate is entered here as the value of twelve ingots.”

I looked. He was correct. I had not noticed it. I said so.

“Because you prepared the weight column,” he said, not unkindly, “and someone else prepared the value column. Who enters the customs values?”

The answer, and I felt it land on me like cold water, was Arnuwanda-rabi himself.

Holkos set the tablet down on his collection of knucklebones with the precise care of a man placing a valuable object somewhere he will not lose it. “Tell me,” he said, “what is the standard penalty, under Hittite garrison administration, for a merchant factor found to be transporting undeclared goods — that is, goods present in a shipment but absent from the customs declaration?”

I knew the answer because I was a clerk and such things were my business. Detention, pending investigation. The factor himself held as surety for the discrepancy, since the factor was the responsible party at the garrison gate.

“Pending investigation,” Holkos repeated. “Which might last how long?”

“Until the merchant principal appears to answer for the discrepancy. Or pays the bond. Or —” I stopped.

“Or the merchant principal fails to appear,” Holkos said, “in which case the factor continues to wait. Yes. Get your sandals. We are going to Hattena.”

Before we left, he did one other thing. He went to Arnuwanda-rabi.

I was present for this interview, seated in my usual place at the corner of the merchant’s receiving room with my clay and my stylus, as though taking notes, which I was. Arnuwanda-rabi received us with the wariness of a man who has something to protect but is not yet certain it is threatened.

Holkos inquired, with perfect courtesy, whether there were any outstanding matters between the house of Arnuwanda-rabi and the garrison administration at Hattena that might bear on Pulli-ziti’s journey.

Arnuwanda-rabi said there were not.

Holkos studied him for a moment with those grey eyes that always reminded me of water in which something was moving underneath the surface. He thanked the merchant for his time.

Outside, he said to me: “He sweated on the left side of his neck only. A man who is warm sweats evenly. The left-side pattern appears when the body is braced for a blow from a specific direction and cannot decide whether to flee or receive it.”

“You learn a great deal from sweat,” I said.

“Sweat and manifest tallies,” he agreed. “The body cannot make false entries.”

The road to Hattena ran through a landscape that the gods had assembled in a harsh mood. The hills were the colour of old bronze and offered no shade, and the dust of the road coated the inside of the mouth and the back of the throat so that every breath tasted of the road itself. We traveled with a small merchant party going east, which gave us the cover of numbers and the misery of their company, for one of them sang.

Holkos was silent for most of the journey. This was not unusual. He had the capacity to be present without being reachable — to sit at a campfire and watch the flames while his mind was entirely elsewhere. On the second evening he asked me to describe Pulli-ziti from my own observation.

I described him: a careful, quiet man of middle years, more fluent in Luwian than in the administrative Hittite the garrison would use, reliable in his accounts, personally honest in the way that men are honest when they have decided that honesty is more economical than its alternative.

“Personally honest,” Holkos said. “And yet he did not notice the discrepancy in his own manifest.”

“He trusted the house.”

“Yes. That is a kind of honesty with a particular vulnerability.” He was quiet for a time. “What does Arnuwanda-rabi owe at Hattena?”

I did not know. He nodded as though my not knowing confirmed something.

The garrison at Hattena was a place of administrative severity. Its walls were new and its commander was a man named Piyamaradu-abi, a Hittite of the imperial school who wore his authority like armour and kept his records with a precision I recognized as a fellow sufferer of the scribal profession. He received us in a room that smelled of fresh clay and lamp-oil and the particular cedar resin used in imperial document-chests.

Holkos presented himself as a factor of the same trading house on a routine verification of delivery records. He asked, with the bored efficiency of a man who has done this a hundred times, to confirm the receipt of fourteen copper ingots, standard weight, delivered some twelve days past.

Piyamaradu-abi’s scribe consulted the tablets. Twelve ingots received and documented. A discrepancy of two had been noted in the declaration. The factor present at delivery had been held pending clarification from the merchant principal, whose bond against the discrepancy remained unpaid.

“The factor’s name?” Holkos asked.

The scribe read it. Not Pulli-ziti. A Luwian name I did not recognize.

Holkos did not blink. “And where is this factor currently?”

The holding room, the scribe said, where he had been for twelve days.

They had changed his name in the records. This was the thing Holkos explained to me quietly, outside Piyamaradu-abi’s room, while we waited for the necessary paperwork to be located. “A deliberate error in transcription at the gate,” he said. “The garrison clerk who processed him wrote a Luwian name by ear — Pulli-ziti sounds enough like the name in the record, to an ear not attentive to Luwian phonetics. It may have been accidental. Probably it was. The important thing is that it made him unfindable. A man searching the garrison records for ‘Pulli-ziti’ would find nothing.”

“How did you know to look past the name?”

“I didn’t know. I asked to see the physical record of all detainees held during the relevant period, not the index of names. The date of arrival matched.” He paused. “Arnuwanda-rabi has a debt at Hattena — I confirmed it last night from the caravan master, who knows the gossip of every post on the road. A commercial debt to the garrison supply officer, probably from a shortfall in a previous delivery. Rather than pay it, he used this shipment: declared only twelve ingots for customs purposes, so that the garrison took the two undeclared ingots against his debt as a silent settlement, while the factor standing at the gate became responsible for the discrepancy he knew nothing of. Pulli-ziti was the lock on the transaction. As long as he remained here, Arnuwanda-rabi was insulated — the factor takes the consequence, the merchant is uninvolved, and by the time anyone enquires, the debt is settled and the record is a tangle no one wishes to unpick.”

“It is monstrous,” I said.

“It is arithmetically elegant,” Holkos said, and I think he meant it as a condemnation, though his voice gave nothing away. “Pulli-ziti was chosen because he is honest. A dishonest factor might have inspected the manifest closely enough to understand what he was carrying.”

The holding room was a dry, low chamber cut into the garrison’s inner wall, lit by a single oil lamp whose wick needed trimming and smelled of old fish. Pulli-ziti was seated on a mat in the corner. He looked, as men look who have been confined without explanation for twelve days, simultaneously older and somehow reduced, as though the waiting had taken something from him that would require time to grow back.

He recognized me. I will not describe what passed across his face when he understood that someone had come for him, except to say that I recorded it in my memory and will not forget it.

Holkos managed the paperwork with Piyamaradu-abi with a precision that was itself a kind of performance. He produced documentation from the trading house — documents he had had me prepare before we left, on the authority of his own position as factor, which was technically sufficient — establishing that the discrepancy arose from a clerical error in the value column, that the full declared weight was fourteen ingots, and that the bond was paid. The bond amount he paid from his own funds, noting in a private aside to me that he would recover it through means he did not specify.

Piyamaradu-abi was satisfied. The administration had its closure. The official record would show a clerical error resolved in good order, a detainee released on proper authority. It would not show Arnuwanda-rabi’s name in any compromising connection.

As we left, the commander walked with us to the gate. He remarked that the matter seemed straightforward, in hindsight.

“These things often do,” Holkos said, “when one finds the thread.”

The gate-hinge at Hattena made no sound at all, which I noted, because I was already noting things by then.

On the road home, Pulli-ziti walked between us and said little for the first day. On the second he began to speak, quietly at first and then with more force, about what he wished to do in the matter of Arnuwanda-rabi. He used words that I will not record because they do not reflect well on anyone, and the sentiment behind them was understandable enough not to require the words.

Holkos listened to all of it without comment. On the second evening he said: “What do you want from this?”

Pulli-ziti thought. “My wages. My reputation. My name back in the records.”

“Those are recoverable,” Holkos said. “And Arnuwanda-rabi?”

“I want him to answer for it.”

Holkos was quiet for a while. The fire we were sitting beside made the particular sound that fires make in dry country — a light, papery crackling, as though it is eating something that has already given up. “I am going to tell you,” he said at last, “something that you may not wish to hear. Arnuwanda-rabi is known to the garrison supply officer at Hattena, who is known to the provincial administrator, who is known to three other administrators along this road. The commercial network in which he operates is older than either of us and will outlast both of us. If you bring a formal accusation, the accusation will be processed by men who depend on the network functioning. It will be processed slowly. You will be a Luwian factor with no patron and a record that shows a customs discrepancy, however resolved. He will be a licensed merchant with imperial documentation and twenty years of relationships.”

Pulli-ziti stared at the fire.

“What I can do,” Holkos continued, “is ensure that the right people know what he did. Not as an accusation. As information held by men who will use it when it becomes useful to them. Arnuwanda-rabi will know they know. He will spend his remaining years of trade in the knowledge that the ground is not solid. That is a different kind of justice from the one you want. It is the only kind available.”

I watched Pulli-ziti make his decision. I recorded it later in the margin of a shipping tally, because I had no other paper, and that is where I found it when I wrote this account.

He said: “Tell them.”

Tiwatali was at the gate of Arziya when we came back. Not waiting, precisely — she was speaking to a donkey driver about load weights, which was the kind of thing she apparently did with her time — but she saw us before we saw her, and she crossed the courtyard in the flat, rapid way of someone who has been making herself busy so as not to stand still.

I watched her take her husband’s face in her hands and look at him as you look at something you have been considering how to live without.

Holkos did not watch. He had already turned toward the trading house.

He did not, in the days that followed, explain to me in specific terms what he had communicated to whom, or how. Arnuwanda-rabi continued to operate his trading house. He did not dismiss Pulli-ziti, who — this surprised me — chose to remain in employment there, because the work was known and the road was familiar and he had a family to keep. The atmosphere between merchant and factor was, by my observation, the atmosphere of two men who understood each other completely and trusted each other not at all, which is a stable enough arrangement for commerce.

What changed was harder to describe. Arnuwanda-rabi began to settle his accounts promptly. He became scrupulous about manifest tallies. He developed, as men sometimes develop in middle age, a careful interest in his own moral reputation.

I do not know what Holkos said to him when they met privately, which they did once, and briefly, in the receiving room that smelled of cedar oil, and which I was not invited to attend. I know only that Holkos came out wearing the expression I had learned to associate with a calculation that has resolved correctly — not pleasure, exactly, but the quiet satisfaction of a mechanism that has performed its function.

He went to his room and let the gum go out and sat by the window for the rest of the afternoon with a clay tablet of riddles from the eastern cities, which was the activity he retreated to when the world had given him nothing sufficiently interesting to think about.

I recorded what I had seen, in the margins of my tablets, in the small careful script I use when I am writing for later rather than for now.

It is not a story about a great battle or a king’s justice or the will of the gods. The gods, as far as I could observe, took no interest in it. It is a story about a tally, and a name wrongly written, and a man who could read the difference between what was declared and what was true.

That seems to me worth recording.

—Nikomachos, clerk
Arziya trading post, western roads
In the year the grain-ships came late

Children:

- Akar-Teshub — see next entry.

AKAR-TESHUB

~1270 BC — Hittite Anatolia

The name was a declaration of belonging — “Akar” preserved from the family’s Semitic deep past, “Teshub” the Hittite storm-god whose cult dominated the religious life of the Anatolian plateau. The combination said: I am of this world and of another, and I move between them. Akar-Teshub served as a border official in the western Hittite administration, in the towns that controlled the passes between the coastal lowlands and the Anatolian interior — positions of considerable strategic importance, because through those passes moved not only goods but information, and the Hittite empire’s security depended on knowing what was moving in both directions.

He was, in the language of a later age, a counter-intelligence officer — responsible for identifying the merchants who were gathering information for Mycenaean or Egyptian purposes under the cover of legitimate trade, for assessing the loyalty of the local populations in the contested frontier zone, and for detecting the false declarations and the concealed cargoes that the border crossing generated in abundance. He was precise and he was thorough, and the towns he administered were among the quietest on the western frontier. Those who served under him attributed this to his diligence. Those who had worked against him, had any of them survived to reflect on it, might have attributed it to something harder to name.

The record of his work was preserved by Sura, a junior scribe attached to the imperial gatehouse. Sura was trained in the keeping of seals and manifests but possessed a restless attention to detail, often returning to entries to adjust wording or clarify counts. He wrote not only what was declared, but what he believed he had witnessed, and in doing so left traces of his own uncertainty. His account, copied and recopied in administrative archives, provides the sequence that follows.

The Account Of Sura

I have kept records for eleven years. I know the weight of a clay tablet before I press a single mark into it — the way the grey earth cools differently in summer and winter, the particular resistance it offers to the stylus at different hours of the day. I record what is declared, and I record what I observe, and I have learned that these are not always the same list.

What follows is true. I have considered, over many years, whether it should be written at all. I have decided that it should, though I have adjusted certain details of certain names, and I will explain at the end which details and why. The official record — the one that stands in the imperial archive at Hattusa, the one that credits the western frontier administration with the successful identification and neutralisation of an Aegean intelligence channel — does not mention Akar-Teshub. It mentions the administration. It mentions the procedure. It mentions the system.

Akar-Teshub was the system. But he would never have said so himself, and in any case he is gone now, and what he thought does not appear to have troubled him greatly.

I write this because someone should.

The smell of the pass at Arinna in the third month of summer is specific and unpleasant in a way that becomes, over years, almost comforting: the dung of oxen, the rancid fat used to grease axle-pins, the particular mineral sharpness of the limestone dust that the west wind carries up from the coastal lowlands and deposits on everything — your lips, your tablets, the inside of the great cedar-log gate that groans on its bronze hinges each morning like an animal in pain. I knew the sound of that hinge better than I knew my own heartbeat. When it began to groan differently — when the pitch changed because the socket had worn unevenly, or because someone had moved the gate in the night — I noticed.

This is relevant. Remember it.

Akar-Teshub arrived at Arinna in the seventh year of the Great King’s reign, in the middle of the busiest trading season, with no ceremony and two attendants. The previous administrator had been a broad, confident man named Halwi who laughed often and kept excellent records of what he had been told. Akar-Teshub was narrow and contained. He did not laugh. He kept records of what he had observed. I understood immediately that my duties were about to become more demanding.

His eyes were the first thing I noted. Not their colour — grey, like the clay — but their quality of attention. Most officials look at you and are already looking past you, toward the next thing that requires looking. Akar-Teshub looked at you and the impression was of something being measured. Not judged — measured. As though you were a cargo, and he was calculating the discrepancy between your declared weight and your actual weight.

He spent his first three days reading the gate records. All of them. Eleven years of my work, and the seven years of Piyassilis before me. He did not ask questions. He sat in the gatehouse with a lamp — cedar oil, always cedar oil in Arinna, the pine varieties were reserved for the temple — and he read, and occasionally he set a tablet aside in a separate stack, and he read on.

On the fourth day he came to find me.

“The merchant Kirunda of Arzawa,” he said, without preamble. “How many times has he passed this gate?”

I told him what I knew: Kirunda was a regular trader, tin and bronze-work moving east from the coastal markets, wine and oil moving west on the return. He had been passing this gate for at least six years. His seals were in good order. His declarations had never varied significantly from his actual cargoes. He was, in the language of the checkpoint, clean.

“How many times,” Akar-Teshub said again, “has he passed this gate?”

He waited. I went to the records. I counted. I told him: thirty-one eastward crossings and twenty-nine westward returns. He nodded once, as though I had confirmed something he already knew.

“And what month does he not come?”

I stared at him. He waited with the patience of a man who has given the question to the air and is prepared for it to hang there until the answer arrives on its own.

I went back to the records. It took some time. When I finished, I felt a coldness that had nothing to do with the limestone dust or the altitude.

He had never, in six years, made a crossing in the second month of spring.

I brought him the tablet with the seasonal tallies. He did not look at it immediately. He was standing at the gate opening, looking out at the road, his arms crossed, his eyes distant and somewhat absent — the way they went when whatever was happening inside him was too large for his face to contain.

“Sit,” he said, gesturing at the bench beside the gate-post. It was the bench where I spent most of my working hours. I sat.

He took the tablet then, glanced at it, set it down.

“You are a careful man, Sura,” he said. It was not a compliment. It was an observation. “You return to entries. I saw the adjustments in the second-year records. The wording changes. A ‘load of tin’ becomes ‘fourteen ingots of tin, each of standard gatehouse weight.’ Why?”

Because the first description had seemed imprecise, I told him. Because imprecision in records is a door left open.

He looked at me for a moment with the measuring expression.

“Tell me what you know of the Mycenaean probe at the Seha River valley. The one in the spring of year four.”

I knew of it in the way that all frontier officials knew of such things: a report had come through from the military command, the passes had been placed on elevated watch, the watch had been reduced after several weeks. The probe had come to nothing. The Aegeans had withdrawn.

“And the probe at Wilusa the following spring?”

The same. And again the spring after that.

“Spring,” he said. “Specifically the second month. Each time.”

I was beginning to see the shape of what he was looking at. But the shape seemed too large and too terrible, and I think I may have preferred not to see it clearly, because I said something inadequate about coincidence.

He made a short sound that was not quite a laugh.

“Kirunda of Arzawa does not cross in the second month of spring,” he said. “In each of the three years in which a Mycenaean probe occurred — not a raid, Sura, not a raid, a probe, which requires preparation, which requires knowledge of troop dispositions and supply routes and the reliability of local chieftains — Kirunda was not here. He was elsewhere. He was on the coast. He was, I would suggest, in conversation with people who sailed in from the west.”

I said: but his cargoes are legitimate. His seals are good. We have inspected his loads.

“Yes,” said Akar-Teshub. “They are exactly legitimate. Exactly. In six years his declared weights have never varied from his actual weights by more than a tenth of a unit. His seals are perfect. His manner at the gate is neither too nervous nor too easy. He has given us nothing to hold him on. He is very good.”

He paused. The gate-hinge groaned in the wind, its worn socket producing the lower note that I had been hearing for three weeks and had not yet reported because I had intended to report it on the morning of the day after next when the maintenance roster came due.

“Which is itself a thing worth noting,” he continued, “since genuine merchants are almost never perfect. They undercount, they overcount, they forget a bundle, they argue about a seal. Kirunda never argues. He moves through this gate as though he has rehearsed it. Which he has. Over six years, he has rehearsed it into a performance of innocence so complete that it became invisible.”

He turned to look at me directly.

“The gate-hinge,” he said. “It changed pitch approximately three weeks ago. You noticed.”

I said I had been intending to report it.

“I know. I am not criticising the hinge. I am noting that you heard the change. You have been at this gate for eleven years and you hear when something moves differently.” He picked up the seasonal tally tablet again. “So have I. Kirunda moves differently. He has always moved differently. We simply did not have enough seasons recorded to hear it.”

He set the tablet down with a finality that I recognised, though I could not have explained how, as the end of the observation phase and the beginning of something else.

“When,” he asked, “does Kirunda next come east?”

I consulted the pattern. Within the month, I told him. His eastward crossings fell roughly every seven to eight weeks.

“Good,” said Akar-Teshub. He did not say what was good about it. He walked back into the gatehouse and resumed reading.

I should record this, because it seems to me important and because no official document would think to note it.

Akar-Teshub, when there was no problem to occupy him, was a different person. Or perhaps the same person from whom the organising principle had been temporarily removed — the way a loom, when the warp threads are cut, collapses into a pile of wood and cord that no longer resembles anything useful.

He slept badly. He walked the walls at night. I know this because I am a light sleeper myself, and I heard his sandals on the stone. During the three weeks between his analysis of the Kirunda pattern and Kirunda’s next expected arrival, he twice reviewed the entire archive, not because he had missed anything — I think he could not miss anything; it was not in his nature — but because a mind such as his, left without sufficient material to work on, begins to consume itself.

His stimulant was riddles. Not the priestly kind, not the poetic kind — the administrative kind. He would take a discrepancy — a single mina’s weight unaccounted for in a copper cargo from seven years ago — and pursue it through the records until he found the explanation, which was usually mundane (a miscount, a wet ingot, a scribe’s hurried wedge-mark). The explanation never satisfied him for long. He needed a new problem immediately.

There was a period of perhaps four days during the second week of waiting when the problems ran out. The archive had been fully examined. No new travellers of interest had come through. The routine declarations were routine. I watched him sit in the gatehouse with the cedar-oil lamp and do nothing. He did not eat. He did not speak. He stared at the wall above the door in a way that made the two gatehouse guards visibly uncomfortable.

On the fifth day a caravan came through with a disputed seal — not a forgery, simply a seal that had been recut after the original ring cracked, and the proportions were slightly changed — and the arguments about its validity lasted most of the morning. Akar-Teshub emerged from the gatehouse, resolved the matter in approximately six minutes by identifying the original seal-cutter’s workshop from the particular tool-line left in the clay, and went back inside looking considerably more alive.

His one other concession to appetite was pomegranate. He ate them in extraordinary quantity when they were available and ignored all other food with equal indifference. I record this because it is true and because the specificity of it seems to me to matter — a man who can be precisely located by his habits, and who knows this, and does not care.

Ramuwa-Telab was the military commander of the Arinna district, a solid and capable officer who had held the post for four years and administered it with professional diligence. He was not, I think, a foolish man. He was a correctly functioning part of the imperial machine, which means he operated by the procedures the imperial machine had provided, and those procedures had not been designed to account for Akar-Teshub.

When Akar-Teshub came to him with the Kirunda analysis — the seasonal pattern, the correlation with the three probes, the argument from the man’s excessive perfection — Ramuwa-Telab listened with the expression of a man who has been presented with a set of objects and asked to believe they are a weapon.

“This is a series of dates,” he said, when Akar-Teshub finished. “A series of dates and an inference.”

“Yes,” said Akar-Teshub.

“I cannot detain a merchant in good standing on an inference. I cannot search his records — his personal records, not his cargo manifest — without authorisation from Hattusa, which takes thirty days in good weather and currently there is not good weather.”

“I know,” said Akar-Teshub.

“I cannot send a man to follow him on the coastal road without specific grounds, because if it is discovered and he is in fact innocent, we have created a diplomatic problem with Arzawa, whose loyalty we are not currently in a position to strain.”

“I know all of this,” said Akar-Teshub, with the patience of a man who has already anticipated every wall and is simply waiting for their enumeration to conclude. “I am not asking you to do any of those things.”

Ramuwa-Telab looked at him.

“What are you asking?”

“I am asking you to tell me everything you know about the content of the three Mycenaean probes. Not the official reports. What the scouts actually brought back. What was in the dispatches that did not make it into the final summaries because it was assessed as too speculative.”

Ramuwa-Telab was silent for a moment.

“And then?”

“And then I would like two of your men — in civilian dress, not military — at the gate when Kirunda comes through. Not to detain him. To observe. And when he passes, I would like one of them to take the western road at a comfortable distance and note where Kirunda stops, and who comes to his camp in the evenings, and whether any of those visitors are the same people who appeared in the speculative sections of the probe dispatches.”

“That,” said Ramuwa-Telab slowly, “is within procedure.”

“Yes,” said Akar-Teshub. “I thought it might be.”

I record this exchange because I was present, and because it illuminates the nature of Akar-Teshub’s particular genius. He knew, always, which walls he could not climb and which doors he could walk through without appearing to push. He did not fight the procedure. He found the gap the procedure had not thought to close.

Ramuwa-Telab provided the men. He also, at Akar-Teshub’s quiet suggestion, forwarded a carefully worded report to the western military command, adjusting the scheduled patrol timing on one specific mountain route — not cancelling it, simply moving it by four days. This adjustment was real. It appeared in the official schedule.

If Kirunda was what Akar-Teshub believed him to be, that information would reach the west, and the west would rely on it, and that reliance would become the proof.

Kirunda arrived on the forty-third day after Akar-Teshub’s analysis. I recognised him before he cleared the final bend in the road — the particular angle of his lead ox, a grey animal with a damaged left horn that Kirunda had been travelling with for at least four years. He came in the mid-morning, which was his habit. He had fourteen animals, a reasonable load for the season, and two servants who had been with him on previous crossings.

I processed him myself, as I always had. His seals were in order. His declaration — tin, copper ingots, three sealed jars of what he described as oil for ritual use — matched his cargo within acceptable tolerance. His manner was exactly what it had always been: polite without warmth, efficient without haste. He asked after no one. He offered nothing unrequested. He paid his gate tax in correctly sized silver pieces and moved his animals through into the eastern holding area for the standard overnight rest period.

He was perfect, as he had always been.

Akar-Teshub watched from the upper wall throughout. He did not come down. He did not approach Kirunda. He watched with the air of a man observing a piece of mathematics work itself out, and when Kirunda’s caravan was through the gate, he descended and walked into the records room.

I followed him.

“Did you see anything?” he asked me.

I had been watching carefully, because by then I understood that he expected me to watch carefully and report honestly, which is not the same as reporting flatteringly. I told him: the cargo was consistent, the seals were good, the servants showed no unusual nervousness. One thing only had caught my attention — the three jars of ritual oil were loaded differently from how I would have expected. They were in the centre of the load rather than padded on the exterior, which was the normal handling for fragile sealed vessels. They were nested, as though the priority was keeping them stable rather than protecting them from impact.

Akar-Teshub looked at me for a moment.

“As though their contents should not be shaken,” he said. Not a question.

He sent word to Ramuwa-Telab’s men that the primary interest was not Kirunda’s evening contacts. It was the sealed jars. And he went into a period of absolute stillness — not the ennui of waiting, but the focused stillness of a man who has placed a trap and is now simply waiting for physics to do its work.

The ritual oil was oil. I must record this exactly, because it matters for what followed.

The outer layer of each jar was oil, correctly sealed, of the quality and type consistent with what had been declared. The men who opened them — Ramuwa-Telab’s people, on the fourth day of Kirunda’s eastward journey, at a waystation where the merchant had stopped — reported oil.

Akar-Teshub, who had ridden there himself through the night on what I assume was the energy that difficult problems produced in him — replacing sleep entirely with forward motion when the solution was close enough to taste — asked them to describe the weight of the jar after the oil was poured out.

They described it.

“Too heavy,” he said.

The base of each jar was clay. Unfired clay — the same colour and texture as the fired exterior, but not fired, and therefore still workable. Embedded in the base of each jar, wrapped in oiled linen against the moisture, were small flat pieces of worked bone.

On the bone pieces, in a script that was not Hittite cuneiform, not Luwian hieroglyph, not Akkadian — a script that Akar-Teshub identified as a western Aegean syllabary, related to systems he had studied from captured documents during his earlier posting on the coast — were marks.

He read them in the waystation courtyard in the early morning while Kirunda slept.

He was quiet for a long time afterwards.

Then he said, to Ramuwa-Telab’s officer: “Reseal the jars. Exactly as they were.”

The officer stared.

“Exactly as they were,” Akar-Teshub said again, in the tone he used when repetition was not for emphasis but for precision. “And return them to the load. And let the merchant proceed.”

I learned what was on the bone pieces later, in fragments, and I will record what I understand of it without claiming certainty.

They were not military intelligence. They were not troop dispositions or supply routes or assessments of local loyalty, though they may have begun as that kind of traffic years earlier. What Kirunda was carrying east, on that crossing, was a communication addressed to a recipient in the inner territories — a communication that, if Akar-Teshub’s reading was correct, described the movement of the very patrol whose schedule had been adjusted four days earlier.

The adjusted schedule. The one that had been changed quietly, as a trap.

Kirunda had the false schedule. He was carrying it east. Which meant he had received it from someone at the western command — someone who had access to the military dispatch system and was feeding it outward and then, through Kirunda, back to Mycenaean contact handlers who would use it to time their next probe.

If Akar-Teshub arrested Kirunda now, the recipient in the interior would be warned. Kirunda would be interrogated, would reveal what he could or would not, and whoever was receiving the messages at the other end would vanish into the administrative landscape of the empire, and they would begin again with a different channel and a different merchant, and the next probe would come from a direction no one was watching.

So he let him go.

I had found Akar-Teshub on the upper wall when he returned, in the middle of the night, and I had asked him — because by that point I felt I had earned the right to ask — what would happen now.

“Now,” he said, “Kirunda delivers his adjusted intelligence. The recipient in the interior passes it west. The Aegeans plan their probe around a patrol route that no longer exists. The probe fails. The channel becomes unreliable in the eyes of the people who have been paying for it, and they close it down.” He paused. “Or they do not close it down, and the next cargo Kirunda carries east contains corrected information that exposes my adjustment — which tells me the recipient in the interior has military access — and I know which part of the interior to look at.”

“And Kirunda?” I asked.

He was quiet for a moment.

“Kirunda will die,” he said, “or he will not. He has been carrying this traffic for six years. He has made choices. The choices have consequences that extend beyond this gate.” He said it without pleasure. I was not sure, in the darkness on the wall above the cedar-log gate with its worn hinge, whether what I heard in his voice was coldness or something that would require more honesty to name. “I cannot arrest a man for a pattern of dates. I cannot hold a man for the weight of an oil jar. And if I could, I would lose the interior channel and gain nothing but one ruined merchant.”

He walked down off the wall without waiting for me to respond.

The Mycenaean probe in the spring of year eight failed. The official military report attributed this to improved patrol distribution in the western sectors. The report is not wrong, exactly. The patrol distribution was improved. The improvement was suggested by Akar-Teshub in a memorandum to the western command — filed under the category of routine administrative optimisation — six weeks before the probe occurred.

The interior contact was identified eighteen months later. I know this because Akar-Teshub told me, and I believe him, though he told me only that the channel was closed and not how it was closed. I did not ask.

Kirunda of Arzawa was killed by bandits on the coastal road two years after the crossing I have described. The official record of his death is ordinary. I have no reason to believe it was not ordinary. I have chosen not to examine it too closely, because some questions, once asked, require honest answers, and I am not certain the honest answer would be one I could record and remain the kind of scribe I have chosen to be.

The official credit for the failure of the year-eight probe went to the western military administration. Ramuwa-Telab received a commendation. He earned it, I believe — he gave Akar-Teshub the men and the latitude and the access to the speculative dispatches, and a commander who had refused all three would have made what followed impossible. The system, as I said, received the credit. This is correct. A system that cannot function without a singular mind is a fragile system, and the empire does not survive on fragility.

Akar-Teshub was transferred the following year to a different posting, further north, on a route where a different pattern had begun to emerge.

He came to say goodbye — not to me, exactly; he came to make a final notation in the gate records, and I happened to be present, as I generally was. He looked older. Not in the way of a man who has been ill, but in the way of a man who has spent a year processing something large and not yet finished with it. He stood at the gate for a moment before he left, looking at the hinge.

“That socket,” he said, “should have been replaced three months ago.”

“Yes,” I said.

He looked at me. The measuring quality in his eyes was, for a moment, something else — something warmer, or perhaps simply less contained, the way the same limestone looks different in different light.

“Your records,” he said, “are the best I have encountered on any posting.”

He walked out through the gate and did not look back.

I recopied this account three times over the years that followed, adjusting the wording toward precision and away from sentiment, which is what he would have done. I have changed the name of the waystation. I have changed the number of animals in Kirunda’s caravan. I have not changed anything that matters.

The cedar-oil lamp is guttering. The gate-hinge has been replaced, and the new one is silent, which I find I do not entirely prefer.

I am Sura, junior scribe of the imperial gatehouse, and this is what I witnessed. What I have written here, I have written truly.

Colophon: Let the next scribe who copies this tablet note that the numbers in the seasonal tally are exact. I counted them myself, twice.

Children:

- Holkos of Wilusa — see next entry.

HOLKOS OF WILUSA

~1240 BC — Wilusa, Anatolia

Wilusa is the Hittite name for the region the Greeks called Ilios — the territory of Troy, on the north-western tip of Anatolia, where the Aegean meets the Propontis and the control of the straits means the control of the trade between two worlds. Holkos lived there at precisely the period when the tension between Mycenaean ambition and Hittite suzerainty over Wilusa was building toward the conflict that Greek tradition would remember as the Trojan War, and he watched it build with the attention of a man whose survival depended on reading it correctly.

The war, when it came, was not a single event but a decade of siege, raid, negotiation, and attrition that involved not only the Mycenaean forces and the defenders of the citadel but every trading family, every minor settlement, every person in the region with something to lose. Holkos of Wilusa was not a warrior. He was a man who read the region’s condition with the same methodical attention he brought to a merchant’s accounts, and who understood that the most valuable intelligence is always the intelligence that arrives before it is needed. He survived the fall by understanding what the evidence around him required him to understand, and he departed in time. He took with him his family, his records, and the accumulated intelligence of a lifetime spent watching a catastrophe assemble itself from its components.

The surviving account is attributed to a man named Eutykhos, a minor steward in service to the Wilusan chamberlain. Eutykhos kept tally of grain distributions and storehouse allocations, but he also maintained private notes on matters that drew his attention beyond routine accounting. His perspective is partial and sometimes shaped by his own position within the administrative structure, yet his record preserves the sequence of observations and actions that follow.

The Private Notes Of Eutykhos

Set down in the third month of the siege, when the Greek fires could be seen from the eastern tower on clear nights, and the sound of the lower city had already changed — less hammering from the smiths' quarter, fewer children in the streets below the wall. A city under siege learns to make less noise. It is one of the first things to go.

The grain-stores of Wilusa occupied the lower level of the great citadel complex, cut into the living rock beneath the administrative quarter so that the temperature remained constant through both the killing heat of summer and the mountain cold of winter. This was not an accident of architecture. The man who had designed them had understood that a city under pressure is a city that will eventually be fed or not be fed, and that the distance between those two outcomes is measured in what you keep and what you lose. The stores were the city’s true heart. Everyone who understood Wilusa understood this. Including, as it proved, the man who would later stand in the smaller of the two grain rooms with blood on the hem of his robe and a story that did not survive contact with Holkos for more than the length of a single morning.

But I am writing this in sequence, as it happened, because that is how Holkos asked me to set it down. The sequence matters, he told me. A man who reads the end first has already decided what the middle means. A man who reads the middle first sees what is actually there.

I will try to honour that instruction, though I confess the beginning is not a pleasant thing to revisit.

The body of Pramnos the younger steward was found by the door-boy Siku at the first hour of light, when the boy came to open the store-room for the morning distribution.

I should describe Pramnos, because Holkos always said you cannot understand a death without first understanding the life. Pramnos was thirty years old, or near enough. He had served the Chamberlain Arion-Bel for eleven years, having come up from the lower administrative lists through a combination of genuine competence with the tally-notch system and a remarkable memory for faces — he could recall every merchant, every porter, every soldier who had passed through the distribution point in a given week, and he could recall them a month later when the accounts were being reconciled. He was not a man who made enemies easily. He was, if I am to be honest, the sort of man who disappears into the function he performs — present, reliable, essentially invisible. The invisibility, as Holkos would demonstrate, was the thing that got him killed.

He was found lying on his back between the third and fourth storage vessels — the great clay pithoi that stood chest-high and held the winter barley. His arms were at his sides with a neatness that struck me immediately as wrong, as though he had been arranged rather than fallen. The oil lamp in the niche above him was burned entirely out, the wick cold and flat. There was no wound visible from the doorway. The room smelled of barley-dust and lamp-black and something else, something I could not name but registered as out of place — a faint sharpness beneath the grain-smell, almost like the copper-tang of the smithy quarter, but softer. More intimate.

Siku ran for the guard. The guard fetched the duty officer, a man named Telamon-Sur. Telamon-Sur looked at the body, looked at the locked door — which Siku had unlocked with the morning key — and said, with the certainty of a man who has found his answer before he has finished asking the question: The Greeks have found a way inside the walls. There will be an assassin in the lower city by now. Seal the gates.

The gates were sealed. The lower city was searched. No assassin was found. Telamon-Sur wrote his report anyway — a matter of enemy infiltration, circumstances consistent with Greek sabotage of our administrative personnel — and had it sealed for dispatch to the King’s chancellery. Then someone, and I do not know who, thought to send for Holkos.

I had encountered Holkos perhaps a dozen times before the morning he came down to the grain-store. My impressions of him had been formed at a distance and through the medium of other men’s reports, which are always unreliable instruments.

He was a lean man, perhaps fifty, with a face that gave away nothing. He dressed simply — a plain wool chiton of undyed grey, good leather sandals, no jewelry except a single ring of Hittite bronze on the right hand that he wore not for display but for the edge of the seal-stone, which he used as a measuring tool. He had long hands and a habit of stillness that was different from the stillness of calm. It was the stillness of something attending. His eyes were dark and moved in a way that suggested they were always, simultaneously, looking at two different things.

When he was engaged in a case — and I had heard this from Premnath the archive-keeper, who had worked with him on the matter of the missing temple inventory two years prior — he was a different creature altogether. Premnath described a man who did not sleep, who forgot to eat, who spoke in rapid half-sentences that only resolved into meaning several exchanges after they were uttered. Between cases, Premnath said, he was barely present. He would sit in the administrative quarter for whole afternoons doing nothing visible, or he would be found in the market, studying the arrangement of goods on a trader’s table with an intensity that made the trader nervous. He had a particular weakness for solving the coded tallies that Aegean merchants sometimes used to hide their actual cargo manifests from port inspectors — not because he had any commercial interest, but because the puzzle was, in his words, the only thing quiet enough not to bore me to madness.

He arrived at the grain-store an hour after Telamon-Sur had sealed and departed. He arrived alone, which was unusual. He had sent no message ahead. He simply appeared in the doorway, looked at the body for a long moment, and then looked at me.

“You are Eutykhos.”

“I am.”

“You keep the distribution tallies for this store.”

“Among others, yes.”

“Bring them.”

He did not look at me again after that. He crossed to the body and crouched beside it with the composed attention of a man examining an interesting piece of pottery, and he began to work.

I brought the tallies. They were clay tablets, the small ones used for daily distribution records, stacked in the archive box by date. I set them on the ledge near the door and waited, because it seemed clear that interruption would not be welcome and that my function, for the moment, was to be available.

Holkos examined Pramnos for perhaps a quarter of an hour without speaking. He examined the hands — each finger separately. He examined the neck, tilting the head with careful hands, studying the skin. He examined the nose and the lips. He examined the eyes, pressing the lids gently back and studying what was underneath them with the lamp he had brought — his own lamp, I noticed, not the wall-fixture, and the oil in it smelled different from the stores we used, lighter, cleaner. He pressed two fingers to the centre of the chest and held them there, as though counting something that was no longer there to count. He studied the hem of the robe — particularly the inner face of the hem near the right ankle, where there was a faint staining I had not noticed.

Then he stood, crossed to the pithoi, and studied them in order. He was looking at the stoppers — the clay caps that sealed the mouth of each vessel — and at the surface of the clay around each stopper. He crouched and looked at the floor between the vessels. He looked at the ceiling, which was bare rock. He looked at the door-socket, the groove worn into the rock where the door-bar rested. He took a piece of smooth bone from a fold of his chiton and measured the gap between the bottom of the door and the threshold stone, then wrote something on the bone with a small stylus he wore tucked behind his ear.

He crossed to the tallies.

For the next hour he read them — all of them, not just the recent dates — with a speed and concentration that I found genuinely unsettling. His lips moved slightly as he read, a habit I had seen in archive-keepers but never with that quality of absolute focus, as though the world outside the tablets had ceased to exist. He arranged them and rearranged them. He put three aside. He found two more in the stack and put them with the three. He held the lamp close and studied the physical impression of the notches in a way that seemed to be about something other than their content.

Then he came and stood beside me.

“Pramnos was not killed by a Greek,” he said.

“No,” I said. I had known this since I saw the arranged arms, but I had not said it to anyone.

“He was killed by a man who had access to this room before the door was locked for the night. The door was not forced and was not unlocked with the key — the bar-socket shows no fresh scoring on the lower edge, which would be present if the bar had been lifted from outside with a tool. He was killed with something introduced through the ventilation channel above the fourth pithos — do you see the dark smear on the channel lip? That is from a vessel, ceramic, that was lowered and then withdrawn. The smell you are trying to name is hemlock oil. Not pure hemlock — diluted and mixed with cedar, which is why you registered it as familiar but could not place it. It was dripped into the room through the channel after the door was barred. Pramnos breathed it. He lay down — you can see from the arrangement that he lay down voluntarily, which means the sedation came first, gently, and he thought he was simply tired. Then the dose became sufficient. He died quietly. His arms are arranged because whoever killed him came in at first light, before Siku, opened the door, arranged the body, and left. They had a key. Not the morning key. The supervisory key. There are three. Do you know where each of them is?”

I said I knew where two of them were.

He said, “Then we already know who held the third.”

Holkos had a way, when explaining his reasoning, of seeming genuinely puzzled by the need to explain it at all — as though the connections between facts were so visible to him that setting them out in sequence required a conscious act of restraint, the way a man of complete sight might have to think carefully before describing colour to a man who sees only shadow.

He had summoned me to the outer room of the archive by midday, where we could speak without the presence of the body. He had also summoned, with a quiet directness that suggested the request was not optional, the archive-keeper Premnath, and the two distribution assistants who had worked the previous day’s allocation under Pramnos.

He stood with his back to the ventilation screen, the light behind him, and he worked through it the way a weaver works a loom — each thread placed exactly, the pattern emerging from the placing.

“The tallies,” he said, “tell three stories. The first story is the one they are meant to tell: grain received, grain distributed, the balance carried forward. This story is correct in its general form. But the second story is visible in the gap between the weights recorded and the distribution figures from the last nine days. The gap is small — four or five units per distribution cycle, not enough to notice if you are reading the daily record in isolation. But if you read the twelve-day cycle as a unit, the total divergence from the inventory figure is sufficient to provision a small vessel for an eighteen-day voyage, or to supply a party of twenty men for six days at reduced ration.”

He paused. One of the distribution assistants, a man named Koros, had gone the colour of river clay.

“The third story,” Holkos continued, is visible in the physical character of the tallies themselves. The notch-pressure for the past nine days is different on three of the tablets — slightly shallower, the angle of the stylus slightly different. Pramnos used a particular grip — you can see it in his older work, a slight leftward lean on the vertical strokes. These three tablets were not cut by Pramnos. They were cut by someone copying Pramnos’s record style with considerable skill, but not his specific grip. Someone who is left-handed, in fact, cutting notches to appear right-handed, which requires a conscious adjustment of pressure that leaves a characteristic inconsistency at the top of each vertical stroke.” He looked at Koros. “You are left-handed.”

Koros said nothing. His mouth was open.

“Pramnos noticed,” Holkos said. “He was a man with a remarkable memory for detail — this is well attested. He noticed the discrepancy in the tally notches, or he noticed the physical disparity in the grain-store, or he noticed both. He confronted someone. Or he was about to. The timing of his death — at the precise point in the distribution cycle where the accumulated diversion would have become visible in the nine-day reconciliation — tells us this was not planned far in advance. This was a response to an immediate threat. Someone was warned that Pramnos was about to speak.”

He let that sit for a moment.

“Koros diverted the grain,” he said. “He did not act alone — the supervisory key is not his to hold, and the ventilation channel above the fourth pithos is not accessible from inside this building. It is accessible from the passage behind the north face of the administrative quarter. That passage key is held by the chamberlain’s office.”

He looked at Premnath, who had been standing very quietly at the edge of the group and whose quietness, I now understood, was of a different quality from the quietness of a man who is merely listening.

I should say something here about Arion-Bel, because the archive will record him as a loyal servant of the citadel and a capable administrator, and he was both of these things, and this is also not the complete account.

Arion-Bel had been chamberlain for sixteen years. He had managed the stores through three droughts, two coastal raids, and the slow, grinding attrition of the siege’s first months, during which the trading routes through the straits had closed one by one as the Greek fleet settled into its positions and the flow of Aegean goods that Wilusa had grown dependent on over two generations of prosperity simply stopped. He had watched the city’s reserves shrink with the attention of a man who understood precisely what the numbers meant.

He had also, at some point in the months before Pramnos died, entered into an arrangement with a party outside the walls.

Holkos explained this to me later, privately, when the formal proceeding was concluded. The grain that Koros had diverted did not go to the Greeks. It went to a particular merchant family in the lower town whose routes ran east — overland, into the Hittite interior, along the road toward Hattusa. Arion-Bel was not a traitor to the city in the way that Telamon-Sur had imagined, with Greek gold and Greek plans. He was a man who had looked at the numbers and concluded that the city was going to fall — not in a year, perhaps, but in three, or five, and that the fall would come from inside, from the slow arithmetic of consumption exceeding supply — and who had begun, in the methodical way of a man trained to think in inventories, to arrange his family’s future in a place that would still exist after the citadel was rubble.

The grain was being paid to the merchants as passage price. Not for him — not yet. For his family. His wife. His three daughters. The youngest was four years old.

Premnath had known. He had not arranged the killing — that was Koros, acting on the instruction that something needed to be done about Pramnos, an instruction Arion-Bel had given in terms carefully imprecise enough that the actual doing of it remained Koros’s decision and Koros’s act. But Premnath had opened the passage. He had provided the access. He had stood in the north corridor and listened to a man breathe himself to death through a ventilation channel, and then he had descended and arranged the body before Siku arrived.

He was a thorough man, Premnath. He had simply not accounted for Holkos.

Koros was tried before the city’s judicial council on the thirteenth day of the third siege-month. The charge was the murder of Pramnos and the falsification of the royal stores record. The evidence — the tallies, Holkos’s analysis of the physical record, the testimony of the door-boy Siku regarding the arrangement of the body — was sufficient. Koros was convicted. The penalty for the falsification of royal stores was, in Hittite administrative practice, significant.

Premnath was tried alongside him as a knowing accessory to both the falsification and the death. He was convicted.

Arion-Bel was not tried.

I should be honest about this, because the record should be honest.

Holkos presented his full analysis to Telamon-Sur in a private meeting to which I was not invited but of which I received an account from Holkos afterward, who saw no reason to hide it from me. He presented the evidence regarding Arion-Bel. He explained the arrangement with the eastern merchant family. He explained the motive — not Greek collaboration, but survival arithmetic, the calculations of a man who had managed a city’s stores long enough to understand the mathematics of an extended siege with perfect, merciless clarity.

Telamon-Sur listened. He was not a stupid man. He understood what he was being told, and he understood what would follow if he acted on it.

Wilusa in the third month of the siege was a city that required believing it could survive. The chamberlain was the man who managed the belief as much as the stores. His competence was not in question — indeed, his competence was precisely the problem. A chamberlain who has run the numbers and concluded the city will fall is a fact that cannot be released into a besieged city without consequences that dwarf the loss of any particular grain allocation.

Telamon-Sur wrote in his official report that the diversion of stores had been perpetrated by Koros acting in concert with Premnath, motivated by private gain. He wrote that the supervisory key had been stolen from the chamberlain’s office and subsequently recovered. He wrote nothing about Arion-Bel.

The report went to the King’s chancellery.

Arion-Bel continued to manage the stores for another eleven months. I watched him do it. He was, as I have said, a capable man. His family — his wife, his three daughters — left the city in the eighth month, joining a convoy of non-combatant dependents evacuated at the King’s direction. They traveled east. I do not know where they arrived.

When the city fell, in its own time, Arion-Bel was not among those who departed.

Holkos said to me, when I asked him about this — about whether the resolution had been just — that justice was a word that covered several different calculations, and that the function of a just outcome was not always to punish every person who deserved punishment. Sometimes it was to remove the immediate threat, stabilize the structure, and leave certain matters for the judgment of time.

“The law,” he said, “is a counting-board. It produces accurate answers, but only for the numbers you put into it. Arion-Bel put his family on a road east. The city gets its chamberlain. The grain-store gets its integrity restored. Pramnos gets two men convicted of his death.” He was quiet for a moment. “The child is four years old.”

I wrote it down. I write it down here. I do not say he was wrong. I say only that I am not certain he was right, and that the uncertainty is one of the things I find I cannot set aside, even now.

There is one more thing I should record, because I think it is the heart of what made Holkos different from the duty officers and the judicial councillors and the archive-keepers who surrounded him in that besieged city.

On the morning after the trial, I found him in the lower grain-store again. He was standing at the fourth pithos, looking up at the ventilation channel. The body was long gone. The room had been cleaned and the morning distribution had already begun from the outer stores.

I asked him what he was looking at.

He said he was looking at a question he had not answered.

I asked which question.

“Who told Koros that Pramnos was going to speak?” he said. “Koros is a left-handed man who cut false tallies with his right hand. He is careful. He is patient. He diverting grain over nine days is meticulous, considered work. But the killing of Pramnos was rushed. It was a response to a sudden threat — I said this, and I believe it. Which means someone communicated that threat to Koros quickly, and through a channel that I do not know.”

I asked if it mattered now, with Koros convicted and Premnath convicted and the trial closed.

“It matters,” he said, “because Koros did not have the idea of the ventilation channel. He managed distribution. He knew the stores. He did not know that corridor. Someone told him it existed and explained what it could be used for. Someone with a longer reach than a distribution assistant. And Premnath opened the passage door, yes — but I am not certain Premnath was the first person to suggest it to him.”

He looked at me. The dark eyes moved in that particular way.
