Holcus was quiet for a long moment. I continued copying the commercial accounts, because I have learned that Holcus’s silences are not invitations to speak and are best treated as one treats the silence before thunder — as information in themselves.

“The letters,” he said finally. “May I retain them?”

“I would prefer—”

“I understand your preference. I ask whether I may retain them.”

Another pause. “Yes,” Strabo said.

“Good. Come back in three days. Not to this building — to the bookseller’s stall at the north end of the Tetrastoon. At the second hour after midday. You will find me there, or a message from me. Do not send anyone in your place; I will know, and I will tell you nothing.”

Menandros’s note, inserted here: I am condensing what followed, as Holcus’s account to me of his investigation occupied four hours and included a digression on the history of administrative Latin in the Greek east that I found instructive but which would not serve the reader. The essential elements are as follows.

I had worked, in my previous life — before Holcus acquired my contract from the shipping merchant who had acquired it from the estate of my previous employer, a sequence of transactions which Holcus described at the time as “the most efficient method of obtaining a literate assistant in a city where literacy is theoretically universal and practically rarer than good drainage” — I had worked extensively with documents, and I knew something of hands.

The first observation was the one I had made and not spoken: the Latin was not the writer’s most natural script. The letterforms were correct but formed with a slight excess of care, the care of a person writing in a language they had learned thoroughly as an adult rather than acquired as a child. The spacing between words was marginally too regular. These are the habits of translation, of a mind that is composing in one language and transcribing in another.

The second observation was Holcus’s, and he arrived at it by a method I could not follow in its middle stages but whose conclusion, when he stated it, seemed — as his conclusions always seem — not merely correct but obvious. The papyrus on which the letters were written was of Alexandrian manufacture but had been stored, prior to use, in a space where the dominant smell was cedar. Not cedar oil from a lamp, but cedar wood — the smell of a chest or cabinet. Cedar storage is common in certain administrative offices where documents must be protected from insects. It is not common in private houses.

The third observation concerned the seal. The letters were not sealed in the conventional manner but were folded and pressed with a thumb, and the pressure of the thumb had left, in the wax — a cheap, undyed wax — a partial impression that was not a ring-seal but the edge of a thumbnail, and the thumbnail had a specific irregularity, a slight lateral split, that Holcus examined for a long time with the focused expression he wore when other men might be said to be reading.

“We are looking,” he said, on the second day, “for a man who writes formal Latin as an acquired skill, who has access to the financial records of the period in question — which means either a current or former administrative position — and who stores documents in a cedar chest. We are looking, specifically, for a man in the office of the Prefect’s financial assessors. There are fourteen men in that office. Of those fourteen, I need to know which store their personal documents in cedar.”

“How do you propose to discover that?” I asked.

“I propose that you discover it,” he said, “by the straightforward method of visiting the households of fourteen administrative officials on some plausible pretext and observing whether you can smell cedar on any document they have handled recently. You have a nose, Menandros. Use it.”

I will not record the fourteen visits, except to note that the pretext Holcus devised — that I was verifying delivery of a commercial assessment on behalf of a Syrian trader whose name Holcus gave me, and who, I later determined, was entirely fictional — worked in twelve of fourteen cases, and in two cases I was turned away by porters, which was itself information. Of the twelve households I entered, three had the smell of cedar about their document storage. Of those three, one kept his documents in an open basket that would not trap the scent. One used cedar oil in his lamps, which was common and explained nothing. The third had a chest.

The man with the chest was not Lucius Caelio. He was a junior assessor named Philippus, a Greek who had Latinised his professional name and who was, in the relevant period eleven years ago, a senior clerk in the provisioning office — present, therefore, for exactly the transactions Strabo had described. He had since risen two grades, which was respectable but not remarkable, and which suggested a man whose advancement had been earned rather than bought.

I reported this to Holcus. He received it with the expression I had learned to identify as satisfaction that he is performing no external movement to indicate.

“Philippus,” he said.

“The nail of his right thumb,” I said, “has a lateral split. I noticed it when he took the document I brought.”

Holcus looked at me for a moment with what I can only describe as approval, and said nothing, which from him is the equivalent of a considerable compliment.

Menandros’s note: I have debated whether to include Holcus’s full analysis as he delivered it at the Tetrastoon on the third day, since Strabo’s responses were largely monosyllabic and the substance of the reasoning was entirely Holcus’s. I include it because this is the part that I could not follow at the time but which, having thought about it since, seems to me to be the most important thing the case contained.

The bookseller’s stall occupied a good position at the end of the colonnade, catching the light from the south and protected from the harbour wind by the line of the stoa. Holcus was there before us. He was reading a scroll that he returned to its slot without purchasing, and when Strabo arrived he was already seated on the low wall beside the stall as though he had been there for some time and found it entirely comfortable.

“It will stop,” Holcus said, without preamble. “The letters will stop. I would estimate that you will receive no further communication.”

Strabo stared at him. “You have — recovered the documentation? You have spoken to—”

“I have recovered nothing. I have spoken to no one about this matter except my clerk.” Holcus examined the nearest scroll without unrolling it. “Let me tell you what you already know but have not yet acknowledged to yourself. The alignment you made eleven years ago was not singular. It was the alignment that virtually every man of your rank made, in one direction or the other, because the situation made neutrality impossible and survival mandatory. You made your calculation. Others made theirs. The man who has been sending you these letters made his — and the calculation he made was identical to yours.”

A long silence. The sound of the harbour, distant. A vendor of heated wine somewhere in the colonnade.

“He supported the same faction,” Strabo said slowly.

“He supported the same faction. He was present for the same undertakings, not merely as a witness to yours, but as a participant in his own right. The documentation he claims to possess is documentation that implicates you both. This is why there has been no demand. There is no demand he can make — no money he can extract, no political favour he can require — that would not create in you an incentive to expose him in return. The threat is the entirety of his leverage, and the threat is perfectly self-cancelling. He discovered this, I suspect, some time after he dispatched the first letter, which was sent in anger or in fear rather than in calculation. The intervals between letters — eight days, then eleven, then twenty-one — are not the rhythm of a man building pressure. They are the rhythm of a man losing his nerve. The final interval was twenty-one days. I believe you will find that no fourth letter comes.”

Strabo sat with this for a moment. “Then he was — he wrote in panic.”

“He wrote because he believed himself uniquely vulnerable and you uniquely safe. Then he discovered, or remembered, or was reminded by someone wiser than himself, that the ground beneath his feet and the ground beneath yours is the same ground. A man does not drain a swamp by pointing at another man who is also standing in it.”

“Who is he?” Strabo said.

Holcus looked at him with what I recognised as a careful neutrality. “That is not a question I am going to answer.”

“I have a right—”

“You have no right that would survive examination,” Holcus said, not unkindly. “If I tell you his name, you will be in possession of a weapon. You do not currently need a weapon; you need the letters to stop, and they will stop. If you have a weapon, you will be tempted to use it, and if you use it, he will use his, and two men who were both, eleven years ago, doing what every man of their position was doing will destroy each other in a spectacle that will be very satisfying to whoever currently finds it useful to remind the court that the Severan alignment was a long time ago and everyone would like to forget it, but cannot quite do so while certain men remain in certain offices. You understand me.”

Another silence. The grain-merchant’s cart went past in the street beyond the colonnade, the driver shouting at his mule in a mixture of Latin and a dialect I didn’t recognise.

“You’re telling me,” Strabo said, “that the safest thing I can do is nothing.”

“I am telling you that the safest thing you can do is precisely what you have always done,” Holcus said. “Which is to survive by being more useful than you are dangerous, and to leave the management of dangerous men to those whose profession it is.” He stood, with the abrupt energy of a man who has set down a weight he has been carrying and found himself unexpectedly lighter. “My fee is modest. Menandros will discuss it with you.”

He had already turned toward the scroll-seller’s stall before I could protest that this was not, in fact, part of my agreed duties.

Menandros’s concluding note.

No fourth letter came.

Strabo paid promptly and without complaint, which Holcus said was the surest sign that the advice had been correct: men who believe they have received good value do not haggle after the fact.

He filed no report with the office of the City Governor, whose representative — a humourless Pannonian named Bruttius who handled cases involving the upper administrative ranks — had come to Holcus twice during the previous year for consultations whose outcomes he had duly credited to his own department’s investigative acumen. Holcus had permitted this without comment each time. I had found it galling. Holcus said it was the correct tax on the privilege of operating without interference, and that any man who required personal credit for his work was a man who did not yet understand what work was for.

He wrote nothing in his tablets about the Strabo affair. He erased what little he had written — the edge of a nail, the smell of cedar, an equation about leverage — on the morning after the meeting at the Tetrastoon, and returned to examining the ceiling with the focused intensity of a man reading a language written in plaster.

He was in this condition for six days before the next client knocked.

I have chosen to record the affair here because I think it is the truest example I have observed of what he actually does, which is not, as I formerly believed, to solve crimes. It is to locate the precise weight of a situation — the exact point at which the pressure on each side of a balance is equal — and to leave it balanced, rather than tipping it, because a balanced scale tips itself, eventually, and a tipped scale breaks.

He would say that this is elementary. He would say that the world is mostly made of such equations, and that most men are too frightened of the variables to work them through to their conclusion.

He is, I continue to find, correct.

Here the tablet sequence ends.

Children:

- Constantinus Holcus — see next entry.

CONSTANTINUS HOLCUS

~210 AD — Byzantium

Constantinus Holcus was born in Byzantium in the worst decades of the third-century crisis — the fifty years of plague, invasion, and imperial instability that saw Rome come closer to dissolution than at any point since Hannibal stood at the gates. He served in the legal administration of the eastern empire through those decades with the steadiness of a man who understood that institutions are most necessary precisely when they are most threatened, and that the administrator who keeps the records accurate and the procedures intact through a period of chaos is performing a service that history rarely adequately acknowledges.

He lived long enough to see Diocletian’s great administrative reforms begin to stabilise the empire and to watch, in his final years, the early stirrings of the Christian question that would define the next century. He did not live to see Constantine’s conversion or the founding of the new capital — those events belonged to his son’s generation — but he understood, from the patterns he had spent a lifetime reading, that both were coming.

The surviving account of his work during the legal confusion following the accession of Maximinus Thrax is preserved in a court copyist’s folio attributed to one Theodoros, a junior clerk whose careful transcription preserves detail faithfully but whose marginal frustrations make clear that he often struggled to follow the full extent of Holcus’s reasoning. It is from this document that the following narrative is drawn.

The Affair of the Duplicated Grants

Drawn from the Folio of Theodoros, Junior Copyist of the Eastern Imperial Registry, in the Consulship of Diocletian and Maximian

The smell of the Registry in summer is not a smell that leaves a man. It is the smell of old papyrus souring in damp air, of lamp-oil pressed from the inferior olives they send east when the better grades go to Rome, and beneath those, always, the smell of river-water seeping through foundations that were laid in a more optimistic century. I had grown accustomed to it over four years of service. I had grown accustomed to the sounds, too — the scratch of reed styluses, the thump of seal-dies, the particular creak of the upper archive door that announced the arrival of anyone who climbed to the older records.

I had not grown accustomed to Constantinus Holcus.

He arrived at the Registry each morning before any of the other senior administrators, before the lamps were trimmed, and he was often still there when I departed in the evening. I do not say he worked during those hours. More often he sat, motionless, in the way that a lizard on a warm stone is motionless — not resting, but attending, every sense directed inward to some computation invisible to the observer. On those mornings when he sat so, I would enter, see him installed in his chair with his long fingers pressed together beneath his chin and his grey eyes fixed on the middle distance above my head, and I would withdraw to my copying-bench in silence. To interrupt that stillness was, I had learned, to receive a look of such flat displeasure that one felt the interruption had been a kind of violence.

But there were other mornings — and these were more unsettling, in their way — when I arrived to find him already in motion, when the lizard had become something faster. On those mornings he moved about the archive with a quick, angular energy that seemed almost too large for his body, pulling folios from shelves and replacing them with the decisive snap of a man who knows exactly which volume he wants and cannot understand why the others are in his way. On those mornings he spoke rapidly and continuously, sometimes to me, sometimes, I suspected, to no one in particular, and the thoughts came out already in the middle, as though the beginning and the end were obvious and only the connecting tissue required externalisation.

I record this not because it is flattering to my superior — I am a copyist, not a flatterer — but because the account of the Duplicated Grants case would make no sense without understanding the man through whose mind it passed.

The affair began with a smell that was not the usual smell.

I noticed it first in early August, when the heat in Byzantium makes the city feel like a cooking-vessel with the lid on — the sea-light white and painful, the streets compressed between their walls, the water in the public troughs growing green by midday. The smell was burning wax, and it was wrong because it was coming from the direction of the sealed archive, and the sealed archive had not, to my knowledge, been opened in three weeks.

Holcus noticed it at the same moment. I know this because he was in one of his languid phases — had been for several days, sitting with a copy of Ulpian’s Institutes in his lap that I was reasonably certain he had not actually opened — and the smell reached us both and he was across the room before I had finished identifying it, his whole posture transformed, the book abandoned on the floor with no more ceremony than a man drops a tool he no longer requires.

“The archive,” he said. Not a question.

The door was unlocked. This was the first wrongness. The second was the wax — a seal-die had been used on the floor, not the table, and recently; the impression was still soft at its edges when Holcus crouched over it with the small lamp he had taken from my hand without asking. The third wrongness was the absence of Gaius Metrophanes, the archival clerk whose duty it was to be present whenever the sealed room was opened, and who was not there.

Holcus looked at the soft wax for a long time. Then he looked at the shelf from which a folio had been recently removed — the dust on that section of wood was disturbed in the shape of the missing volume. Then he looked at the door-frame, which bore a faint scrape at approximately waist height that was fresh enough to show raw wood beneath the old paint.

“Someone was in a hurry,” I offered, feeling that I ought to contribute something.

“Someone was in a hurry,” he agreed, which surprised me, as he rarely agreed with anything I said without qualification. “But not with the seal. With the leaving.”

He said nothing more for some time, and I did not ask, having learned that questions posed before he was ready to answer them produced only silence with an impatient quality to it.

Gaius Metrophanes was found two days later in the lower cistern on the western side of the city, in water that had been sitting long enough in the August heat that the retrieval was an unpleasant business for everyone involved except Holcus, who observed the proceedings with the calm attention he brought to all problems that interested him.

Centurion Lucius Rufianus, the garrison officer assigned to the matter by the military prefect, was a broad-shouldered man with a competent face and the manner of someone who has learned to be efficient with unpleasantness. He looked at the body and then at Holcus and then at me — I had become, through some administrative accident that I did not remember agreeing to, the person who accompanied Holcus to crime scenes, which was not in my original contract of service — and he said, “Drowned. Or fell.”

“Or was placed,” Holcus said.

Rufianus’s expression did not change much, but something shifted behind it. “The cistern is on a public street. Someone would have seen.”

“The cistern access is in an alley. The alley is, at most times, unobserved. He was placed there at night, which means the person who placed him knew the alley, knew the cistern, and was confident that a man of Metrophanes’ size could be managed without assistance.” Holcus was walking around the body with his hands clasped behind his back, and his shadow moved oddly in the lamp-light. “He did not drown. The water here is barely chest-deep. He was placed after death, and the death was not drowning. Look at the hands.”

I looked. I will not record in detail what I saw, because some details belong in medical reports and not in administrative folios. I will record only that Holcus was right, and that Rufianus, after a moment, acknowledged it.

“The hands tell you he did not drown,” I said, later, when we were walking back toward the Registry through streets that were still warm from the day’s heat even at the second hour of night. “How did you know to look at the hands first?”

“Because,” Holcus said, in the tone he used when something was self-evident, “the hands are where a man leaves the story of his last minutes. If he drowned, the hands would tell one story. If he was struck, another. If he was held under — though this is not holding-under — another still. The hands are the index to the body’s history. A good reader always checks the index first.”

He said nothing more. I wrote it down when I got home, as I had begun to do with everything he said, because I had the uneasy sense that I was recording something that ought to be recorded, even if I was not always certain what it meant.

There is a moment in the investigation of fraud, Holcus told me the following morning — he was in his energetic phase again, having apparently not slept — when the document stops being a document and becomes a machine. This was, he said, the moment that interested him.

I did not understand this and said so.

“A document is inert,” he said, pulling folios from the archive shelving with his quick, decisive motions. “It records. A machine has moving parts, and the moving parts must move in a particular sequence, or the machine does not work. The land-grant process under Diocletian’s new administrative framework is a machine. Grant is proposed. Survey is conducted. Survey is witnessed. Seal of local authority is applied. Grant is registered in the provincial folio. Provincial folio is forwarded to the central registry. Central registry seal is applied. The machine has eight steps. Every step produces a document. The documents, laid in sequence, form a chain that a reasonable official can follow.”

“Yes,” I said. I had processed several hundred such chains and found them tedious but not mysterious.

“Now,” Holcus said, setting a folio on the table between us and opening it to a registration page, “look at this.”

I looked. It was the registration of a land parcel in the Thracian territory east of Adrianople, recorded in the provincial folio in the name of one Marcus Severinus Afer, dated to the previous autumn.

“The provincial seal is applied in sequence before the central registry seal,” Holcus said. “Always. The machine requires it. The provincial office processes, and then forwards to us. We receive, and then seal. Our seal is, in every case, newer than theirs.”

“Yes.”

“In this registration, our seal impression is older.”

I looked again. The seal-die left characteristic marks in the wax — small imperfections in the bronze that an attentive copyist learns to recognise, because they are the difference between an authentic document and an imitation. The impression of the central registry’s seal on this registration bore an imperfection — a slight burr at the lower edge of the eagle’s wing — that I recognised as characteristic of the die that had been replaced the previous winter. The provincial seal bore the clear lines of fresh bronze.

The central registry seal on this document was older than the provincial seal that should have preceded it.

“This is impossible,” I said.

“It is not impossible,” Holcus said, with the particular satisfaction of a man who has been waiting for that response. “It is an instruction. It tells us that the central registration was prepared first and then sent backward through the machine, to be equipped with a provincial seal after the fact. This is not an error. Errors go one way. This went the other way. Someone here — in this registry — created the central registration before the provincial process had occurred, and then arranged for the provincial documentation to be added later.”

The room felt colder than it was.

“The same parcel of land,” Holcus continued, placing a second folio beside the first, “appears in the provincial record of another district, registered six months later in the name of Quintus Domitius Placidus. Two men believe they own the same field east of Adrianople. Both have what appear to be complete documentation chains. Both have paid the registration fees. Only one of them has genuinely purchased land. The other has purchased a fabrication.”

“How many times?” I asked.

His expression, which was not a warm expression, became, if anything, more concentrated. “That,” he said, “is what we are going to determine today.”

The answer was eleven. Eleven parcels of land in the Thracian and Bithynian territories, each sold twice under cover of the administrative reorganisation — the chaos of the new forms, the new procedures, the new registration requirements that Diocletian’s reforms had imposed across the eastern provinces — each with a documentation chain that would satisfy any ordinary inspection, and each with the tell-tale impossibility that Holcus had identified: the central seal appearing before it could have been applied, and a provincial record created afterward to fill in the gap.

The process required, Holcus explained — to me, and then to Rufianus, and then to the provincial prefect, who was a large man named Cassius Avitus who had the look of someone who had not expected his afternoon to contain this — a clerk with access to the central seal. A clerk who could produce registration documents without the genuine provincial record existing yet, using the seal that created legitimacy. A clerk who had understood that the reorganisation created a window: buyers in Bithynia could not easily verify provincial records in Thrace, and buyers in Thrace could not easily verify what had been registered centrally, and in the middle of that distance and confusion, eleven pieces of land had been sold eleven extra times, and the fees had gone somewhere that was not the imperial treasury.

“There are,” Holcus said, “four clerks with access to the central seal. Two of them are old men whose hands have trembled too long to press a die with the precision these impressions require. One of them has been posted to Alexandria for the past eight months and has demonstrably not been in Byzantium. The fourth is Appius Calvinus Rufus, who has been in this registry for six years, who has a property in the Lycian territory that he could not have purchased on his clerk’s salary, and who was on duty in the sealed archive on twelve separate occasions during the period in which these registrations were created — I have checked his duty roster against the dates — and who was also the last person to speak with Gaius Metrophanes before Metrophanes went to examine the old registration records and discovered something that made him alarmed enough to return to the sealed archive after hours.”

The room was very quiet. The prefect looked at Rufianus. Rufianus looked at Holcus. I looked at my notes, because looking at my notes was something to do that was not looking at anyone else.

“You cannot know,” Avitus said, “that Metrophanes found something alarming.”

“He had been assigned to clear old registration records from the pre-reform period,” Holcus said. “A routine task that put him in direct contact with the original grants for several of the parcels that had subsequently been sold twice. He would not have understood immediately what he had found — the discrepancy is subtle and requires a trained eye. But Metrophanes was a careful man who kept careful records of his own work. I found his personal notation-tablet in his desk this morning.” He produced a small wax tablet, the kind every clerk carries. “He had written the parcel numbers of three of the eleven grants, with a question mark beside each. He was going to ask someone about them. He went to the sealed archive, I believe, to compare what he had found in the old records against the new registrations. Someone was already there.”

“You found his notation-tablet,” I said, “this morning. When I was not here.”

“You were not here yet,” Holcus said, without any particular emphasis.

“You went through a dead man’s desk before the official examination.”

“I found the notation-tablet. Yes.”

The prefect cleared his throat. “Where is Calvinus Rufus now?”

“At his desk,” Holcus said. “I checked before coming here. He has been at his desk all morning, which is, in itself, the behaviour of a man who is working very hard at appearing normal. A man with no reason for anxiety would have taken the morning off. It is August. Everyone takes mornings off in August when they can.”

They found the property records in Calvinus’s house that afternoon — the separate accounts, the letters from buyers in three provinces, the careful double-ledger that was, Holcus said, examining it with something that might have been professional admiration, quite good work. The man had a talent for administrative documentation that had been misdirected.

Calvinus himself, when Rufianus’s men arrived, sat very still for a long moment and then said, with a strange precision, “He was going to find the third one. The property outside Nicomedia. That one, they would have traced back very quickly.”

He said nothing else for a long time.

“Metrophanes had a daughter,” I said, when Holcus and I were walking back to the Registry in the late afternoon, the sea-light beginning to go orange and the streets filling with the particular smell of evening in August — bread from the bakeries, fish from the harbourside, the old smoke of a city that has been burning lamps for centuries. “A daughter and a wife. He was, by all accounts, a careful and honest man.”

“He was,” Holcus said.

“He noticed something wrong in the records and went to look more closely, and that is what killed him.”

“Yes.”

“Is that,” I asked, and I was aware, even as I asked it, that it was not a question suited to an administrative folio — “is that something that troubles you?”

He walked three steps before answering.

“The careful and honest man who notices the discrepancy in the records,” he said finally, “is performing the exact function that the careful and honest records-system is designed to produce. He did what he was supposed to do. The tragedy is that the system which produced him did not also protect him. That is a systems failure.” He paused. “Whether it troubles me is not a question with useful implications. Calvinus Rufus will be tried. The property in Lycia will be seized. The buyers will be partially compensated from the forfeited estate. The records will be corrected.” Another pause, shorter. “Metrophanes’ family will receive the death-in-service provision. I have already submitted the paperwork.”

I had not known he had done this. It was not his responsibility to do it.

“You submitted it this morning,” I said. “Before you came to the prefect.”

“The prefect would have taken several days to process it through the standard channels,” he said. “The family has immediate expenses.”

He did not say anything else, and I did not ask anything else, and we walked back to the Registry in the ordinary sounds of the city — a gate-hinge somewhere on a side street, creaking in a particular minor key that I have never been able to forget — and he went straight to his chair and sat in the lizard-stillness again, and I went to my copying-bench and began to write this account, which I have tried to make as accurate as possible, and which I suspect no one will read for a very long time.

One postscript, which I include because it seems relevant to any future reader’s understanding of the case’s final disposition.

The property in Lycia — the one purchased with the fraudulent proceeds — was, after the forfeiture proceedings, found to contain a secondary deed, registered before the fraud began, in the names of Calvinus’s two sons, who were minors. Holcus reviewed this secondary deed for approximately four minutes, set it down, and then told Avitus that it appeared to have been registered before the fraudulent activity commenced and was therefore not subject to forfeiture under the relevant statute.

Avitus looked at him for a long moment. “You’re certain?”

“I am certain,” Holcus said, “of what the statute says.”

The property was not forfeited. The sons — who were twelve and nine years old, and who had, as far as any investigation revealed, no knowledge of their father’s activities — were permitted to retain it under the guardianship of an uncle.

I asked Holcus afterward whether the secondary deed was genuine.

He looked at me with his grey eyes and said nothing.

I wrote this down too, in my private notes, not in the official folio. Some records, I have concluded, are better kept separately.

Here ends the account of Theodoros, copyist, in the forty-third year of his life, which was the second year of the joint consulship. The official records of the Registry credit the resolution of the Duplicated Grants fraud to the office of the Provincial Prefect, whose investigators, working in coordination with the garrison, identified and apprehended the guilty party. This is accurate in the narrow sense. I leave the fuller record to whatever future reader the gods — or, I suppose, whatever power supersedes them — may eventually provide.

Children:

- Byzantos Holkos — see next entry.

BYZANTOS HOLKOS

~260 AD — Byzantium

The name shifted back toward Greek in his generation — “Holkos” rather than “Holcus,” the Latin veneer thinning as the eastern empire recovered its Greek identity with increasing confidence. Byzantos Holkos was born in Byzantium and came into his career precisely as Constantine was transforming the city into Constantinople — the new Rome of the east, built with the speed and ambition that only an emperor’s will could command. He served in the imperial household administration during the great building programme of the 330s, managing the documentation of land acquisition, construction contracts, and the complex resettlement of populations that the creation of a new capital from an existing city required. He was a man of the transition — present when the city changed its name, its religion, and its destiny within the space of a single decade.

He worked as a notarios — a legal secretary and drafter of official documents — in the imperial chancery, a position that placed him at the centre of the documentary life of the eastern empire. His particular value was a facility for detecting the falsified document — the forged seal, the altered date, the interpolated clause — that made him the court’s first resort in cases of suspected fraud in legal instruments. It was a gift with very old roots, and he exercised it with the quiet, unhurried confidence of a man who had inherited it rather than acquired it.

The account that follows is drawn from the preserved notes of one Nikodemos, a site supervisor in the imperial building works, whose role as both observer and questioner placed him in proximity to Byzantos Holkos at the moment the matter unfolded. His record is fragmentary in places, but consistent in the parts that concern the inquiry itself, and it is from his notes, with minor corroboration from administrative copies, that the narrative is reconstructed.

The Matter of the Death of Phokas the Overseer

Reconstructed from His Notes and Certain Administrative Copies Preserved in the Chancery Archive

The body was found at the third hour, when the morning mist still lay heavy on the Golden Horn and the smell of fresh lime-mortar hung over the whole hill like a second fog. Byzantium had always smelled of salt and fish and the particular sweetness of rotting wood from the old wharves; Constantinople, as we were learning to call it, smelled of ambition — of cut stone and wet plaster and the burning pitch of torches worked through the night by men who could not afford to stop. It was into this changed air that Phokas fell, or was placed, or was carried — the question being precisely the one that required answering.

I found him myself, which is why the account falls to me to write. He was lying beside the south foundation wall of the new basilica, his head resting against a stone block as though he had sat down to watch the work and simply never risen. A man who did not know the building trade might have thought him sleeping. I knew better the moment I saw the angle of his neck.

Phokas had been overseer of the eastern contracts — the section of the great programme that managed the acquisition of private land along the Mese, the old central street that the Emperor’s architects were widening and re-colonnading. It was thankless work. The land had owners. The owners had documents. The documents, when examined carefully, had a troubling habit of saying whatever was most convenient for whoever held them.

He had a leather satchel still looped over one arm. Inside it was a contract of sale — a substantial parcel of land near the Forum of Constantine, dated some three years past, carrying the seal of the urban prefecture. The seal was perfect. The parchment was old. The ink had the proper brownish fade of documents left in a dry archive. Everything about it announced authenticity, and it was this that made me uneasy, because Phokas had told me two days before that he had found a document he could not quite trust.

I sent for Byzantos Holkos that same morning, before the site foremen had assembled the work gangs. This required some negotiation, because Holkos served in the imperial chancery and his time was not freely distributed, but I had dealt with him twice before in matters of questionable land titles, and I knew that he would come quickly if the word seal appeared in my message. It did, and he came.

He arrived at the sixth hour, walking through the site with the particular gait he always had on such occasions — not the abstracted drift of a man taking in scenery, but a kind of focused stillness, as though he were listening to something the rest of us could not hear. He was perhaps sixty-five years of age at this time, lean in the way of men who forget to eat when their minds are occupied, with the deep-set dark eyes common to his family and a habit of wearing his notarial robes loosely, as though he had dressed in some haste and then immediately forgotten he had dressed at all.

He looked at Phokas for a long time without speaking. Then he crouched beside the body, examined the neck, the hands, the sandals, and finally the satchel, all without touching anything he had not first indicated he intended to touch.

“The satchel was on his arm when he was found?” he said.

“As I see it now.”

“Then whoever killed him either did not know what was in it or did not care. Which is interesting.” He rose. “May I take the document?”

I gave it to him. He held it close to his face, which was not, as I had initially supposed, a sign of poor eyesight, but simply the way he worked — bringing his whole attention to bear on a small area, excluding everything peripheral. He tilted it toward the morning light. He pressed the seal gently with one thumb. He turned the parchment over and examined its reverse. He held it briefly to his nose.

Then he said: “Nikodemos. When did the urban prefecture change its seal die?”

I did not know. This was not my particular area of knowledge.

“Four years ago,” he said. “A new emperor requires new instruments of administrative identity, as a rule. This seal is an impression of the current die. The document is dated three years past.” He paused. “You see the difficulty.”

I did. A document made with a tool that did not yet exist when it was supposedly made.

“But—” I said.

“Yes. The parchment is genuinely old. The ink is correctly aged. The hand is consistent throughout, with no signs of interpolation. Someone made this document very carefully, Nikodemos. Not carelessly, not in haste, not for crude profit. They made it well. And they made it with access to a copy of the current prefectural die that I would very much like to locate.” He folded the document with what I was coming to recognise as a kind of professional reverence — the care one takes with fine work even when one is about to prove it fraudulent. “Was Phokas investigating this particular contract?”

“He told me he had doubts about it. He had not yet brought it formally to the chancery.”

“He told you. He told no one else?”

“I believe not.”

Holkos was quiet for a moment. The construction noise surrounded us — the rhythmic crack of stone-dressing, the shouts of gang-masters, the low grinding of the cranes — and he stood in it with his eyes half-closed, as though the sound helped him think rather than hindering him.

“He told you,” he repeated, “and the next morning he is dead. And the document is still on his person.” He opened his eyes. “We will need to find who submitted this contract for registration, and we will need to do it quietly, before the owner of the land in question learns that his document has come to my attention.”

There was, in the matter of Holkos’s working methods, a quality I found difficult to name for some time, and which I eventually settled on calling directed patience. He did not rush. He did not, in the early stages of an inquiry, share his suspicions. He asked questions with the neutrality of a man who has no particular investment in any answer, which meant that people answered him fully and without caution, because they did not feel they were being guided toward anything.

He spent the remainder of that day in the administrative offices of the land acquisition section, reading contracts. He was not searching, as far as I could tell. He was reading. He read seven contracts in the course of the afternoon, making occasional small marks in the wax of his personal tablet. When he was finished, he sat for a while with the tablet face-down on the table, looking at nothing.

I waited. I had learned, in our previous dealings, that this was what was required of me.

“There are four,” he said at last. “Four contracts in this section bearing what appears to be a copy of the current seal. All four concern land parcels previously occupied by families who were displaced in the initial resettlement — the Greeks who had been here when the city was still Byzantium, who had property rights under the old regime that were neither confirmed nor formally extinguished when the building programme began. The official land claims against them were registered by a single landowner.” He consulted his tablet. “A man named Kalliphon. A substantial property owner who had been acquiring land aggressively since the Emperor announced the expansion, on the argument that the displaced families had no valid title.” He paused. “The forged contracts, each of which predates the alleged acquisition by several years, record prior sale to the displaced families themselves — at prices suggesting genuine transactions. They could not have been evicted under them. They were protected by them.”

I worked through this slowly. “Someone was forging documents to protect the families?”

“Yes. Using a copy of the prefectural die to manufacture papers that looked more legitimate than the legitimate claims against them.” Holkos looked at his hands. “It is, considered purely as a technical matter, extremely accomplished work. The parchment was sourced correctly. The ink was properly compounded. The die was copied from the actual current instrument — which means either the forger had access to the chancery itself, or to someone who did.” He was quiet for a moment. “And Phokas found one of these contracts in the course of his acquisition work. And now Phokas is dead.”

“Then Kalliphon—”

“Kalliphon discovered someone was undermining his claims. He could not identify who. He identified what — a forged contract protecting a family whose land he intended to take. He could not bring the forgery formally to the chancery without drawing attention to all four contracts and to his own acquisitions under the legitimate claims that the forgeries were designed to defeat. He is not a stupid man.” Holkos rose. “He dealt with the problem the way men deal with problems when they cannot use the law. He found the forger and he killed him. The document remained on the body because removing it would have drawn attention to the fact that someone knew of it.”

“The forger,” I said. “You know who it was.”

He did not answer immediately. He walked to the window of the administrative office, which looked out over a section of the new construction — scaffolding, blocks, the organised chaos of an empire building itself. The late afternoon light made the limestone almost golden, and the smell of the kilns drifted up from the lower ground.

“I have a strong suspicion,” he said. “I would like to confirm it before I say the name aloud.”

The confirmation required two more days, during which Holkos moved through the chancery with an air of such complete normality that I began to wonder whether his suspicion had simply dissolved. He processed his usual documents. He attended the morning briefing of the notarial staff. He ate a meal of bread and oil and dried fish that one of the junior scribes brought him and which he consumed with the mechanical inattention of a man who regards eating as an administrative task.

On the evening of the second day, he came to find me at the site and said: “Walk with me.”

We walked along the old Byzantium street that would soon become the extended Mese, stepping around piles of dressed stone and coils of rope. The torches were being lit along the scaffolding for the night work, and the smell of burning pitch mixed with the salt air from the Horn.

“The die,” Holkos said. “A copy of a seal die is not made in an afternoon. It requires skill, time, and access to the original — ideally, more than one impression of it, to account for any variation in the stamping. There are perhaps four men in Constantinople who could produce a copy of sufficient quality to deceive a standard examination, and only one of them had any connection to the families along the Mese.”

“Who?”

“A man named Eudoxios. He is a copyist and document restorer — he works under contract to the chancery, restoring damaged legal instruments. He had legitimate access to prefectural seal impressions as part of his restoration work. He lived for twenty years in one of the quarters now being absorbed by the building programme. His sister’s family was among the displaced.” Holkos paused. “I found, in the chancery restoration records, four instruments assigned to Eudoxios for work in the year before the contracts in question were dated. The instruments happened to carry prefectural seals. The restoration notes are thorough — Eudoxios was a meticulous man. Too meticulous, perhaps. His records of the restoration work are more detailed than any comparable entry in the same ledgers.”

“Detailed enough to explain the time he spent with the originals.”

“Yes. He had an alibi for the making of the copies, built into the official record before the copies were ever needed. A careful man.” Holkos stopped walking. We were standing near the south foundation wall, near the place where Phokas had been found. The torchlight threw long shadows across the fresh stonework. “He is dead, Nikodemos. Eudoxios. I confirmed it this morning — he died four nights ago, the night before Phokas, of an apparent fall from the steps of his house. He lived alone. No witnesses.”

I stood with this for a moment.

“Kalliphon found him first.”

“Yes. He found the forger, established how the forgeries had been made, killed him, and then went looking for the documents themselves — which is when he encountered Phokas, who happened to be carrying one. He killed Phokas and left the document, perhaps calculating that a single suspicious contract, unconnected to the restoration records and with its maker dead, would lead nowhere. He may have been correct, had Phokas not told me about it.”

“Told me,” I said.

“Yes. You. And you sent for me.” He was quiet for a moment. “Kalliphon is a man of property and connection. The urban prefect has received at least two letters of commendation on his behalf from senior members of the court in the past year. He will not be touched by a direct accusation without evidence that would withstand examination by men who wish not to find it convincing.”

I heard something in his voice that I had not heard before. Not quite hesitation. Something more like deliberation.

“Then what do we do?” I said.

What he did, in the end, was not what I had expected.

He went to the families.

There were four households — eleven people in total, in the cramped lodgings near the old harbour where the displaced had been settled while their status was argued. He visited each of them over the course of a morning, arriving without announcement and speaking quietly. I went with him. He explained, with the same neutral precision he brought to everything, that the documents protecting them were forgeries, that the man who had made them was dead, and that the documents would not survive formal scrutiny if Kalliphon chose to challenge them in the courts.

Then he told them what to do.

The method was not dramatic. It required only that the families apply for the confirmation of their title through a specific administrative channel — one that Holkos knew to be managed by a notarial superior of impeccable integrity and no particular friendship with Kalliphon. The applications, supported by witness testimony to long-standing occupation and two legitimate instruments that Holkos identified from the actual archive, were sufficient to grant provisional title pending full imperial review. This review, he estimated, would take between three and seven years, during which the families could not be displaced.

By the time Kalliphon’s lawyers moved to challenge the confirmations, the matter had risen, through a sequence of bureaucratic escalations that Holkos had quietly anticipated, to a level of the administration at which Kalliphon’s influence was considerably less effective.

I asked him, on the evening after the last family visit, about Kalliphon himself. About what would happen to him. About Phokas, and Eudoxios, and the justice they had not received.

Holkos was in one of his quiet periods — the long, grey intervals between cases when he sat in his office in the chancery with old documents he had already read and a clay cup of dark wine he rarely drank, and looked at the wall. He had a particular quality of stillness in these times, like a lamp turned low. I had been told by colleagues who had known him longer that it sometimes lasted for days, and that it was better not to disturb it. But I asked.

He was quiet for a while. Then he said: “Kalliphon will face a land dispute that will consume his legal resources for the better part of a decade. He has, in the course of his acquisitions, made enemies of men more strategically positioned than I am, and those men will use the disruption to the title chain — which I have ensured is thoroughly documented — to advance their own claims. He will not be comfortable.”

“But he will not be tried for Phokas.”

“No.”

“Or for Eudoxios.”

“No.”

I said nothing. He looked at the wall for a moment longer, then at the cup of wine, which he turned in his hands without drinking.

“Eudoxios was a forger,” he said. “What he did was illegal, and the legal system would have punished him for it while acquitting the man who ordered his death, because Kalliphon’s lawyers are better than his. Phokas was a careful professional who noticed something he should perhaps have brought to me more quickly, and who died because he had not quite decided what he had found. The law, as it currently operates in this city, is not equipped to address either of these men’s deaths in a way that would result in justice rather than paperwork.”

He set down the cup.

“The families are in their houses,” he said. “Eudoxios’s work stands — not as law, but as fact on the ground, which is more durable than law in a city being rebuilt from the foundation. Kalliphon will spend the next ten years fighting a bureaucratic war he did not choose on ground I have selected. This is what I can do. It is not what the law can do, and it is not what a court would do, but it is what will actually occur.” He looked at me for a moment with those deep-set eyes that always seemed to be measuring something several layers beneath the surface of whatever conversation was nominally in progress. “You are disturbed by this.”

“I think I am,” I said. “Yes.”

“That is correct,” he said. “You should be. Write it down exactly as you are disturbed by it. The discomfort is the honest part of the account.”

He was right, as he generally was. I have written it down.

The official records of the deaths of Phokas the overseer and Eudoxios the copyist list them as accidental. The land titles of four families along the old Mese are on record in the chancery archive as confirmed pending imperial review, supported by legitimate instrument and witness testimony. The name Byzantos Holkos does not appear in any of these documents.

He would have considered this correct.

I record it here because someone should, and because the discomfort, as he said, is the honest part.

—Nikodemos, site supervisor, in the service of the building programme of Constantinople, in the consulship of Optatus and Paulinus

Children:

- Holkos the Byzantine — see next entry.

HOLKOS THE BYZANTINE

~290 AD — Byzantium

Holkos the Byzantine reached the height of his career in the city that was still being built around him — Constantinople in the 350s and 360s, its great churches and colonnaded streets not yet finished, the smell of new stone everywhere, the sense of a world being deliberately constructed rather than gradually accumulated. He was among the first generation for whom the city was simply home rather than a transformation, and he brought to it the analytical habits of a family that had been reading the world’s changes since before Greece existed.

He taught at one of the schools attached to the new ecclesiastical foundations of the city, where the curriculum combined the classical philosophical tradition with the Christian intellectual culture that was now the empire’s official framework — a synthesis that was still being worked out in his lifetime and that produced, in those willing to engage with both traditions honestly, a habit of rigorous inquiry that owed as much to Plato and Aristotle as to the Gospels. He was known principally as a teacher — the kind remembered not for what he said but for the questions he asked, the ones that left students unable to rest until they had found the answer. He did not tell his students what to think. He showed them how, which was the harder gift, and the more durable one.

The record of his most significant intervention in public affairs survives in the account of his student Anastasios, preserved in a set of teaching notes later copied into the archive of a church school in Constantinople. The document bears signs of repeated copying, yet the essential exchange is preserved with sufficient clarity to reconstruct both the method and the reasoning that followed.

The account of Holkos of Constantinople

Set down by his student Anastasios, from notes preserved among the teaching records of the Scholē tou Hagiou Paulou, copied in the reign of Theodosius. What follows is reconstructed from those notes and from my own memory of the events, which I have endeavoured to set down with fidelity to the truth, though I confess that some exchanges I have rendered from impression rather than verbatim recollection, the hour being late and my stylus slow.

Constantinople smelled, in those years, of two things simultaneously: the lime-dust of construction and the cedar-oil of the lamp-shops that lined the Mese. You could not walk from the Milion to the Forum of Constantine without inhaling both — the empire’s future and its administrative present — and Holkos claimed that a man who could not distinguish those two smells by origin and proportion had no business forming opinions about the city’s character.

He made this observation to me one morning in the early spring of the year in which this account begins, standing at the window of the upper room we shared in a building near the scholē, watching the mule-carts bringing dressed stone toward the unfinished precinct of the great church. He had been in what I had learned, over three years as his student, to recognise as his difficult season — the interval between significant problems, when his mind, finding nothing sufficient to engage it, turned upon itself with a kind of slow corrosive patience. He had spent the previous week producing a complete grammatical analysis of the Septuagint’s rendering of Job, not because he had any theological interest in the question, but because he required the mental labour. He had eaten perhaps once each day. He had spoken to me only when I brought him food, and then only to note, without apparent interest, whether I had brought the right thing.

The morning I describe, he had woken early and was already at the window when I rose, wrapped in his dark teaching robe, his long fingers working a small smooth stone around and around in his palm — a habit I had come to associate with the preliminary stages of genuine engagement. His eyes were remarkable. People remarked upon them constantly and he found this tiresome. They were the eyes of a man who was always, in some portion of himself, reading something that was not present in the room.

“Anastasios,” he said, without turning from the window, “you have been standing there for some time. You want to tell me something but have decided, incorrectly, that the timing is wrong.”

I had, in fact, been trying to determine whether he was in a state to receive news. He was not always. “There is a man waiting below,” I said. “He has been waiting since before dawn. He is a notary of the imperial chancery — I could tell by the ink on his fingers and the particular cut of his—”

“I know the cut,” Holkos said. “What does he want?”

“He would not say to me. He said the matter was one for the teacher himself.”

Holkos turned from the window. The corrosive patience in his face had changed to something else — a sharpening, like a stylus being brought to a better point. He set down the smooth stone on the windowsill with a careful precision that I recognised as suppressed excitement.

“Send him up,” he said. “And bring the lamp with the cedar oil — the small one, not the fish-oil lamp. If this man is a notary of the chancery, we should give him the professional courtesy of a familiar smell.”

The notary’s name was Philippos. He was perhaps fifty years old, built with the compact solidity of a man who had spent decades carrying the weight of institutional secrets, and he sat across from Holkos with the careful posture of someone managing, through long practice, an emotion he considered beneath his dignity to display. Fear, I thought. Or something adjacent to it — the particular anxiety of a man who has understood that something is wrong in the machinery he tends, and cannot determine precisely where.

He had brought a document case. He set it on the table between them without opening it.

“Teacher,” he said — the forms were important to him; I had noted that he had used the correct form of address even when speaking to me on the stairs — “I am told you are a man who reads accurately.”

“I am a man who teaches reading accurately,” Holkos said. “The distinction matters. What do you read in that case?”

Philippos looked at the case. “Documents from the imperial chancery. Seven of them. Land deeds, principally — two registrations of inheritance, one transfer of estate between living parties, and four confirmations of earlier grants. All authenticated in the standard manner. All bearing the correct seals and subscriptions.”

“And all incorrect,” Holkos said.

Philippos’s hands were very still. “You cannot know that. I haven’t shown them to you.”

“No. But you have shown me yourself.” Holkos leaned back in his chair with the ease of a man who has already completed the calculation and is merely waiting for the other party to catch up. “You are a senior notary of the chancery — a man, I think, responsible for registration rather than drafting, which means you spend your days verifying that the documents produced by others conform to the standards of your office. You are very good at this work. The care with which you have handled that case, the way you set it down — not placed, but set — tells me that the contents are both valuable and troubling to you personally. If they were merely incorrect in a routine administrative sense, you would have referred the matter through normal channels; a senior notary does not consult a scholē teacher about a simple scribal error. Therefore the incorrectness is of a kind that the normal channels cannot address. Either because the forger has anticipated those channels, or because — and I suspect this is the more disturbing of the two possibilities — the forger is working within them.”

The silence that followed was of the particular quality produced when a man hears something he has feared articulated clearly for the first time. Philippos looked at Holkos the way students sometimes looked at him mid-lecture: with the combined expression of relief and unease that accompanies the recognition that someone else has already been where you are afraid to go.

“Open the case,” Holkos said, not unkindly.

I will not reproduce here a complete account of the documents themselves, both because the technical details of their production are difficult to convey to a reader unfamiliar with the specific conventions of chancery notation, and because Holkos himself cautioned me, when I asked to copy his examination notes, that some specificity was better omitted from any document likely to be widely read. He said: a detailed account of how a lock was picked is less a history than an instruction. I have honoured his caution.

What I will say is this: the documents were, as Philippos had said, correctly formed in every particular that a normal examination would check. The seals were proper. The subscriptions were in the correct hands — or appeared to be. The formulaic language was exactly as it should be, down to the abbreviated forms that varied by period and office. A thorough inspector, checking against the standard criteria, would have found them satisfactory.

Holkos did not check against the standard criteria. He examined them the way he had once, in a lecture I will never forget, described the proper method of reading any text: not asking what it says, but asking why it says it in precisely this way rather than another. Every choice is a choice. Every choice has a reason. Find the reason and you find the man.

He was at the table for perhaps two hours. He did not speak. He had the lamp I brought — the cedar-oil lamp — positioned to his left, and he held each document at varying angles to the light, sometimes turning them and examining the verso, sometimes laying two documents side by side and moving his finger between them with the slow methodical movement of a man comparing musical notations. He asked me, at one point, to bring him a bowl of water. He held a corner of one document above the steam and watched what happened to the surface of the ink. He noted this in his own hand on a wax tablet and then said nothing further for another hour.

Philippos sat and watched. I sat and watched Philippos watch. The lamp burned and the smell of cedar filled the small room and somewhere below in the street a gate-hinge, badly maintained and never repaired despite repeated notice to the building’s owner, gave its habitual irregular complaint as the morning traffic increased. Holkos appeared not to hear any of this. His eyes moved between the documents with the dispassionate attention of a man examining a theorem.

Finally he straightened, set the last document back in the case with the same careful precision with which Philippos had removed them, and folded his hands.

“Seven documents,” he said. “Produced, in my estimation, by one hand working over a period of at least two years, possibly three. Not a copyist’s hand — not someone skilled in the mechanical production of text, though the mechanical production is flawless. A thinker’s hand. Someone who understood the system he was producing documents for before he began producing them. He knew what would be checked. He accommodated every check. But in accommodating every check, he revealed himself, because the act of accommodation left traces that a normal document, produced without awareness of being examined, does not leave.”

“What traces?” Philippos asked.

“The care is the trace. A genuine document produced by a working notary has the qualities of a mind moving through familiar territory — small efficiencies, habitual abbreviations that vary slightly depending on the hour of the day, minor irregularities of ink pressure that correspond to the natural variation of a hand working quickly. These documents are too consistent. Not in the obvious sense — he was clever enough to introduce superficial variations. But the consistency of intent underlies everything. Every decision is optimised. A real document has the texture of ordinary human labour. These have the texture of argument.”

He paused. Outside, the gate-hinge sounded again. The cedared air was very still.

“Whoever made these,” Holkos said, “is not a forger in the common sense of the word. He is not a craftsman who has learned to copy. He is a man who studied the system, understood its logic completely, and then constructed documents that satisfy the system’s logic more purely than genuine documents do. He does not mimic. He perfects.”

Philippos was quiet for a moment. “Can the forgeries be proved?”

“To my satisfaction, yes. To the satisfaction of a legal proceeding, working from the established methods of documentary examination — I am less certain. The established methods check for the things he has already addressed. To demonstrate his method, you would need to explain the absence of imperfection, which is a more delicate argument than the presence of error.”

What followed occupied, in its initial phase, some weeks. I will compress it.

Philippos returned three times to the upper room with additional documents — not further forgeries, but genuine examples drawn from the same period and office, to allow Holkos to extend his comparative study. Each time he came, Holkos examined what was brought and then sent him away with questions to which Philippos returned, on his next visit, with answers. The questions concerned the administrative processes: at what stage each type of document moved through the office, which hands were required to touch it, what the intervals were between drafting and authentication, how disputes about registered deeds were normally resolved.

I understood, from watching this, that Holkos was not merely examining the documents but mapping the system. He was constructing, in his mind, a model of the chancery’s operation — how work moved through it, where the pressures and delays fell, what opportunities existed for a document to be substituted or altered between one stage and another. He said to me, during one of these intervals, while he was annotating his wax tablets by lamplight: “The forger did not attack the documents directly. He attacked the system’s assumptions. To understand his method, I must first understand what he understood. I must think as he thought.”

I asked him whether that was not a dangerous way to approach the problem — to think as the criminal thought.

He looked at me with the expression he used when a student had asked a question whose premise required examination before the question itself could be addressed. “Every genuine act of understanding requires temporarily inhabiting the mind you are trying to understand,” he said. “The danger is not in the temporary residence. The danger is in failing to come back out. I have not yet found that difficult. Though I will admit,” and here he returned to his tablets, “that this particular mind is more comfortable to inhabit than most. It is orderly in there. Pleasingly orderly.”

The question of the forger’s identity did not become clear to Holkos through any single dramatic recognition. It came through accumulation — the gradual convergence of small observations toward a point that had, in retrospect, been inevitable from the beginning. I will record the moment of convergence as I witnessed it, which was in the upper room, on a grey afternoon, when Holkos set down his stylus and sat very still for perhaps ten minutes without speaking.

Then: “Anastasios. The seventh document — the one confirming the old estate transfer from the Bithynian register. What do you notice about the confirmation clause?”

I examined it. I had, by this point, spent enough time in proximity to Holkos’s method to know that noticing required more than reading. I looked at the clause carefully. “The formula is correct,” I said. “Standard confirmation, the right subscript abbreviation—”

“The abbreviation,” Holkos said. “Yes. Which abbreviation?”

I looked again. “The contracted form. Conf. With the standard suspension mark.”

“Contracted form.” He was very still. “Now look at the fifth document. The registration of the Thracian inheritance.”

I looked. “The same contraction.”

“And the second document. The estate transfer between living parties.”

“The same.”

“Good. Now. These three documents were, according to Philippos’s account of the chancery’s process, produced in different offices by different hands, some years apart. The convention for that particular abbreviation had, over that span of years, varied. The office that produced the Thracian inheritance document should have used the older, uncontracted form. The office that produced the Bithynian confirmation should have used the newer contracted form but with a different suspension mark — the diagonal rather than the horizontal. Our forger knew both conventions. He chose, consistently, the contracted horizontal. It is his preferred form. The form his own hand returns to when the task of imitation is nearly complete and some small portion of attention has been released.”

I said, slowly: “You know whose preferred form that is.”

“I have been corresponding,” Holkos said, “with three chancery teachers and two retired senior notaries, in the course of establishing what conventions applied to which offices and periods. One of them — a man named Dorotheos, who teaches documentary form at one of the other scholai — has been notably more helpful than the others. He has answered every question I have asked with a precision and completeness that initially I attributed to particular diligence. I now attribute it to something else. He has been, in effect, monitoring my progress. Checking, through my questions, what I have understood and what I have not yet reached. He has been reading my investigation the way I have been reading his documents.”

The cedar lamp had burned low. I trimmed it without thinking, an automatic domestic action. The sound of the street below had diminished with the afternoon.

“He is the forger,” I said.

“He has the right kind of mind,” Holkos said. “He understands the system completely — he teaches it. He has access to the necessary materials and knowledge. He has a preference for the contracted horizontal form. And he has, for the past several weeks, been attempting, through the precision of his answers, to establish exactly the boundaries of my knowledge — what I can prove and what I cannot. He has been doing so with considerable skill.” He paused. “I have enjoyed the correspondence.”

I argued against it. I argued for some time, and with, I believed, considerable force. Holkos listened to all of my arguments with the attentive courtesy he always displayed when he had already decided something and was allowing the other party to exhaust themselves before explaining why.

“You want to meet him,” I said, having reached the end of my arguments. “Knowing that he is dangerous — knowing that men who operate through criminal arrangement, of whatever form, do not receive gracefully the attention of someone who can identify them—”

“I don’t want to meet a forger,” Holkos said. “I want to meet a mind. The forger is incidental.” He considered this. “Or rather — the forger is the mind, in this case. That is unusual. Usually the crime is external to the criminal. A man commits a fraud for money, for advantage, for revenge. The crime is a means. In this case, I believe the crime is the thing itself. He is not forging documents in order to achieve something. He has constructed an elaborate, invisible argument with the system of documentation itself. He wants to know whether the system can be beaten. He has beaten it. He wants to know if anyone noticed.”

“And you noticed.”

“And I noticed.” Holkos stood from the table and moved to the window, resuming the posture in which I had found him on the morning Philippos first came. “He has been waiting for me, Anastasios. The correspondence makes this clear. He did not know who I was when I began making enquiries, but he identified me — identified the quality of the enquiries — almost immediately. He has been, since then, conducting his own examination. Of me. We have been examining each other through the medium of polite letters about archival convention.” He paused. “It would be discourteous not to meet.”

The meeting was arranged, through a further exchange of correspondence whose surface concerned a question about a pre-Constantinian documentary form, in the library of Dorotheos’s scholē — a room that was, I thought when we arrived, precisely the kind of environment its occupant would choose. It had the quality of extreme order imposed on large quantities of material: codices and document rolls arranged with a regularity that was not merely practical but declarative, as if the room itself were an argument about the proper relationship between knowledge and its organisation. The smell was of old papyrus and of the particular preservative oil used in well-maintained archives — a smell I had encountered in only a few places in Constantinople, all of them institutions with very long memories.

Dorotheos was, by appearance, unremarkable. That is the first and most significant thing I can record about him. He was perhaps sixty, with the kind of face that resists description — not ugly, not handsome, simply a face that fulfilled its function without declaring anything beyond it. His hands were a scholar’s hands, ink-marked and careful. He rose from his desk when we entered, and his expression was that of a man welcoming colleagues with whom he has had a productive correspondence: pleasant, interested, entirely composed.

“Teacher Holkos,” he said. “Your student. Please, sit.”

There was a document on the desk between them. A deed — a land registration, I could see, though I could not read the text from where I stood. Dorotheos had placed it there deliberately, as a host places on the table the dish he intends to serve.

“I thought it might be useful,” Dorotheos said, settling back into his chair with the ease of a man on his own ground, “to have a concrete object for our examination. Rather than conducting it at one remove, through correspondence.”

“Very practical,” Holkos said. He looked at the document without moving to examine it. “Is it one of yours?”

“It is genuine,” Dorotheos said. “I thought that was the correct choice. To begin with something genuine.”

There was a quality to their exchange that I will attempt to describe though I am uncertain I have the vocabulary for it. It was not hostile. It was not even precisely tense. It was the quality of an encounter between two very similar instruments that have been precisely calibrated against different standards, and are now, for the first time, placed in direct proximity to allow comparison. Each man was measuring the other. Each man knew he was being measured. Neither appeared troubled by this.

“You chose the contracted horizontal form,” Holkos said, still not touching the document. “Consistently. It is your natural form, I think — the one your hand returns to when attention is distributed elsewhere.”

“A reasonable inference.” Dorotheos’s tone was exactly the tone of a scholar discussing a textual variant — professionally interested, not personally invested. “You are correct that it is my natural form. I am aware that it became so. I attempted, in the earlier documents, to vary it. I concluded, eventually, that variation introduced its own pattern. Consistent use of a natural form is, paradoxically, less identifying than inconsistent suppression of it.”

“You concluded correctly.”

“And yet you identified it.”

“I identified it because I was not using the standard criteria. I was looking for what was not wrong, which is a different task from looking for what is wrong. Your documents are correct in every way that the standard examination checks. They are too correct, which is invisible to the standard check but apparent to a different kind of attention.”

Dorotheos considered this with the undisguised pleasure of a man hearing a good argument. “You are saying that perfection is itself a defect.”

“I am saying that perfection, in a context that normally produces imperfection, is an anachronism. It stands out precisely because it does not belong.”

The afternoon light came through the library’s high window at a long angle, crossing the desk and illuminating the deed between them. Dorotheos looked at it for a moment, then at Holkos.

“What can you prove?” he asked. The question was entirely direct — the directness, I thought, of a man who respects precision too much to approach it obliquely.

“To the satisfaction of a rigorous philosophical argument: everything I have described. To the satisfaction of a legal proceeding, in the current state of documentary jurisprudence: very little. The argument from the absence of imperfection is not a form of proof that the existing examination procedures recognise. A court would require a positive demonstration of falsification — a specific error, a specific anachronism, a forged seal or a subscribed hand proved to be wrong. I have found no such error. You made none.”

“No,” Dorotheos agreed. “I made none.”

“The seven documents represent, in aggregate, a significant transfer of land rights to parties who were, I infer, associated with interests you were serving. The scale of the fraud is considerable.”

“The scale of the problem was considerable,” Dorotheos said. “The solution required to be commensurate.”
