There was a pause. The light moved on the desk. Somewhere in the scholē below, I could hear the sound of students reciting — the rhythmic communal voice of a class working through a text together.

“Who are they?” Holkos asked. “The interests you served.”

“You know I won’t answer that.”

“I know. I asked because a complete examination requires the question to be posed, even when the answer is not available.”

Dorotheos almost smiled. “You are very thorough.”

“You are thorough. I am thorough in response.” Holkos was quiet for a moment. “You knew someone would eventually notice. That was part of it.”

It was not a question. Dorotheos treated it as the accurate observation it was. “I have been producing documents for eleven years,” he said. “They have passed every examination conducted upon them. They will continue to do so, in all probability, because the examination procedures do not ask the question you asked. You asked it because you think differently from the procedures. There are very few people who think as you think.”

“And yet one found you.”

“And yet one found you,” Dorotheos agreed, gently transposing the observation in a way that made its full meaning available.

Holkos looked at him. Then he looked at the deed on the desk, which remained between them, undisturbed, the afternoon light lying across its surface like a judgment.

“You should know,” Holkos said, “that I have conveyed my analysis, in general terms, to Philippos. He is aware that the standard examination procedures are insufficient for the kind of work you have done. He is, I believe, in the process of advocating for the development of new examination criteria — criteria of the kind that would, eventually, produce the result you see before you now.”

“I see before me,” Dorotheos said carefully, “a teacher and his student, having a conversation about documentary history.”

“Yes.”

“And the analysis you have conveyed to Philippos — it is, as you say, general in nature.”

“It addresses the systemic vulnerability. It does not, at this stage, identify a specific individual.”

Dorotheos was very still. He had the stillness of a man who has understood the precise scope of what has just been placed before him, and is completing the calculation that determines what it means.

“You have a student with you,” he said.

“I do.”

“He will record this.”

“He records everything. It is his function, and his gift.”

Dorotheos looked at me for the first time in the conversation — directly, with the same assessing quality he had brought to Holkos. I held the look because I thought it was required of me, though I will admit that the look was uncomfortable. It was the look of a man reading a text and deciding how seriously to take what he reads.

“What will he record that you permit him to record?” Dorotheos asked.

“That is, ultimately, his decision,” Holkos said. “Though he generally takes my advice in matters of editorial judgment.”

The light had moved further. The voices of the students below had changed timbre — the recitation had ended, replaced by the quieter sound of individual work.

“You have not told me what you intend,” Dorotheos said.

“No.”

“You intend for me to know that you exist. That a person exists who asks the question you asked, and will in future ask it more systematically. That the system is being developed that will, eventually, see what you have seen.”

“I intend for you to know what is true,” Holkos said. “What you do with true information is your own concern.”

He rose from the chair. He did not extend his hand — the gesture would have been, I think, wrong in some way that neither of them would have been comfortable articulating. He looked at Dorotheos for a moment with the evaluating attention he brought to any problem of genuine interest, and Dorotheos returned the look with the same quality.

“Your correspondence has been,” Holkos said, “the most instructive I have received in some years.”

“And yours,” said Dorotheos, “has been the only correspondence in eleven years that I was uncertain how to answer.”

We walked back along the Mese as the afternoon light failed, the smell of construction lime fading with the heat and the cedar-oil lamps beginning to show in the shop fronts on either side. I waited until we were clear of the scholē’s precinct before I spoke.

“You let him go,” I said.

“I reached the limits of what I can prove,” Holkos said. “Those are not the same thing.”

“You know who he is. You know what he did. Philippos has the authority to bring a formal accusation—”

“Philippos has the authority to bring a formal accusation that would fail,” Holkos said, with a patience that was not unkind. “Because the proof I have does not satisfy the existing criteria for proof. And a failed accusation against a teacher of the chancery, without evidence of the type the proceedings require, would accomplish several things: it would warn the interests he has served, who would then have time to make their arrangements; it would discredit Philippos for bringing an unsubstantiated case; it would discredit me; and it would teach Dorotheos exactly which aspects of his method I was unable to break, which would allow him to make those aspects stronger.” He paused. “A prosecution I cannot win is not justice. It is a very expensive way of making the problem worse.”

“And instead?”

“Instead, he knows that I exist. He knows the kind of attention that will now be applied to the system he has been exploiting. Philippos knows that the system requires reform and will advocate for it. New examination criteria will be developed, of the kind that would identify this type of work. In five years, perhaps ten, the vulnerability he exploited will be substantially closed.” He walked for a moment in silence. “And he knows all of this. I made certain he understood it.”

“You warned him.”

“I informed him of the state of the world.” He glanced at me. “It is not the same thing, Anastasios.”

I considered this for some time as we walked. The gate-hinge of our building, when we reached it, made its habitual complaint as Holkos pushed through, and he paused for a moment — which he had not done before — and looked at the hinge with the attention he brought to things that had, suddenly, become data.

“Someone should repair that,” he said. “I have been hearing it for three years and have always meant to notice it properly.” He looked at it a moment longer. “In the morning, remind me to speak to the owner.”

He went up the stairs. I followed, with my notes and my uncertainties and the memory of two men in a library, each completely mapped by the other, neither having yielded anything the other could use. I sat at my own small table by the window and wrote, by the light of the cedar lamp, what I have set down here — which is, as Holkos would say, not everything, but everything that is true.

The rest is editorial judgment. And that, he tells me, is the writer’s proper burden.

Here ends the account of Anastasios, student of the scholē. The copyist notes that several passages in the original were damaged by water and have been restored by inference from context, with care taken to preserve the sense where the letter was lost. Whether this is what the author would have wished, the copyist cannot say. He proceeds on the assumption that a teacher who instructed his students to read carefully would have wanted the same care applied to his own words.

Children:

- Holkos-Theodoros — see next entry.

HOLKOS-THEODOROS

~325 AD — Gothic territory

Theodoros — gift of god in Greek — was the name added to mark the family's Christian accommodation, grafted onto the old name-element as a declaration of faith and a practical adaptation to a world in which such declarations were increasingly necessary. Holkos-Theodoros served in the frontier administration of the eastern empire, posted to the territories where Roman authority met Gothic power along the lower Danube, in the generation before the great migrations would sweep that frontier away entirely.

He was an intelligence officer in all but name — assessing Gothic military capacity, political organisation, and strategic intention from the evidence of traders, deserters, and his own careful observation of the movements and dispositions of the tribes beyond the river. His reports to Constantinople described, with a precision that his superiors found impressive and his successors found prophetic, the nature of the Gothic threat: not a raiding culture but a migrating one, driven by pressures from the east that would not be relieved by treaty or tribute, and that would eventually produce a collision with Roman power of a kind that Roman power might not survive. He was right in every particular, and retired from the frontier service before the catastrophe he had predicted arrived.

The account that follows is preserved in the records of Flavius Marcellinus, a notary attached to the Danubian command, who served as both clerk and observer in the proceedings that unfolded at Noviodunum. Marcellinus did not possess the same acuity of inference as Holkos-Theodoros, but he possessed diligence and an honest eye for detail, and his record reflects both the limits and the clarity of his position. He questioned what he did not understand, and in doing so preserved the questions that revealed the path of reasoning as much as the answers themselves.

The Matter of Alaric the Trader

Resolved at Noviodunum in the Year of Our Lord Three Hundred and Seventy-Four, in the Consulship of Gratian and Equitius

What follows is set down by my own hand, Flavius Marcellinus, notary attached to the headquarters of the Danubian command, in the garrison town of Noviodunum. I have written it not for the archive — which will record what it has been told to record — but for a single reader, to whom it will be sent under seal. I have changed no material fact. I have softened no conclusion. If what I describe reflects poorly on men of rank, that is the fault of those men, and not of my pen. I write it because I was there, and because Holkos-Theodoros has asked me to write it, and because I have learned, in the short time I have known him, that when he asks for something he has already calculated why it is necessary.

The Danube in autumn smells of iron and rotting reed. This is not a poetic observation. It is a practical one, which I offer because it explains why the body of Alaric the trader was not discovered until the third morning after his death, by which time the river had done its slow work on him, and the garrison soldiers who pulled him from the shallows near the eastern landing were required to stand well upwind to make their report.

The morning was the colour of old lead. A river mist had come in during the night and settled into every corner of Noviodunum — into the grain stores, into the colonnade of the principia, into the gap beneath my own door — so that the town felt submerged, as though the Danube had decided to take it back. The torches in the gateway burned with flat, orange halos that illuminated nothing. The sound of the water was everywhere and the sound of the frontier, which is the sound of horses and iron and men speaking languages that are not Latin and not quite Greek, was swallowed up entirely.

I had been in Noviodunum for eleven months. I had learned to read the river mist as a harbinger of difficult days, and so when the duty officer hammered at my door before the morning meal to tell me that a body had been found at the eastern landing, I was not surprised. I was only cold, and I pulled my cloak around me and followed him down through the fog to where the body lay on the planking in the grey light, and I began to write in my tablet what I saw.

Alaric — so he was identified by the freight master, who knew his face from a dozen crossings — was a Gothic trader of perhaps forty years, broad through the chest and shoulders, with the fair beard that marked him as a man of the northern tribes and the calloused hands that marked him as a man who worked. He had no visible wounds that the soldiers could find in that light and that cold. No blade had entered him. No cord had marked his neck. He lay in the posture of a man who had sat down to rest and found the rest permanent: slumped against the grain of the planking, one arm extended, as though pointing at something across the river.

Tribune Gallus, who commanded the garrison, arrived as I was completing my notes. He was a compact man with a soldier's efficiency of movement and a bureaucrat's love of obvious conclusions.

"Drunk," he said, looking at the body. "Fell in. The Gothic traders drink through the night when they make crossing. It is a custom of theirs."

I wrote this down.

"Or," said Gallus, as a second thought arrived to support the first, "there was a quarrel. The Gothic traders also quarrel. One of the other traders. Pushed him in. We will question them."

I wrote this down also.

It was at this point that I became aware of a third presence at the landing — a man who had arrived so quietly, and who stood so still in the mist, that I had taken him for one of the mooring posts. He was lean, perhaps fifty years of age, with close-cropped grey hair and eyes that even in that flat morning light appeared to be doing something more than merely looking. He wore the plain wool cloak of a frontier administrator, without ornament or insignia. He was studying the body with an expression I had not seen before on a man's face at the sight of a corpse: not horror, not sympathy, not the brisk professional distance of the soldier, but something that resembled nothing so much as the expression of a mathematician encountering an elegant problem.

"Holkos-Theodoros," said Gallus, without warmth. "I was not informed you were in Noviodunum."

"I am rarely announced," said the man with the grey eyes. He did not look at Gallus. He was still looking at the body. "Who identified him?"

"The freight master."

"By face, or by documentation?"

Gallus paused. "By face."

"Then we know who the river left us. We do not yet know who the freight master's documentation will confirm." He crouched beside the body with the ease of a man half his age and examined the hand — the extended arm, pointing across the water. "Your notary," he said, without turning. "What does he observe?"

I realised he was speaking to me. "A trader," I said. "Gothic. Dead, I would estimate, two to three days. No visible wound."

"Good. And the hand?"

I looked at the hand. It was extended, as I had noted, fingers partially curled. "It points toward the far bank," I said. "The Gothic shore."

"It does," said Holkos-Theodoros. "Note also that the palm faces upward. Note also the discolouration beneath the nails of the first two fingers — not river mud, which would be uniform, but something dried before his entry into the water. The river has softened it but not removed it. And note — " he stood, and turned, and looked not at the body but at the planking around it, " — that the body was placed here."

"Placed," repeated Gallus.

"The eastern landing is down-current from the garrison. A man who fell from the garrison wharf would have been carried past this point, not deposited at it. A man who fell from the Gothic shore would not reach this bank at all without assistance from the current that does not presently run in that direction. The body was not carried here by the river. It was left here by a person, or persons, who wished it to be found by the garrison, in a posture that would invite the conclusion that he fell or was pushed from the far bank." He looked at Gallus with an expression that was entirely without accusation and therefore somehow more pointed than accusation. "Your two theories are, I think, exactly what was intended."

Tribune Gallus looked at the body, then at Holkos-Theodoros, and I saw the particular expression of a competent professional man who has been told, in the mildest possible terms, that someone is considerably cleverer than he is.

"I will want to see his documentation," said Holkos-Theodoros. "His passage records, his freight manifests, his letters of introduction if he carried any. All of it, back to his first crossing."

"The freight master will have records going back perhaps a year," I said.

"Then we will begin with a year." He looked at me directly for the first time. I had the impression of being measured and filed away. "You are Marcellinus. You write quickly and you write what you see, not what you are told. I observed your tablet. Good. I will need a clerk."

It was not a question.

The freight master's records were kept in a low building beside the customs post, smelling of damp papyrus and the particular mustiness of official documentation that has been consulted rarely and sheltered inadequately. His name was Sosias, a thin Alexandrian Greek of bureaucratic temperament who had survived three garrison commanders by the simple method of keeping his records in perfect order and expressing no opinions about anything that occurred above his administrative station.

He provided what he had. I copied.

What emerged from Alaric's crossing records was this: he had been coming to Noviodunum for six years. He traded in dressed leather and occasionally in horses, both of which were items of genuine commercial value. His crossings averaged four times per year in the warm months. His freight manifests balanced — goods declared on entry, goods declared on departure, duties paid without dispute, no irregularities that Sosias had found reason to flag.

And yet.

Holkos-Theodoros sat with the records spread before him on the customs table and was quiet for a long interval. This, I came to understand, was not a pause for thought but a method of it — a deliberate stillness in which he processed what he was reading the way a millstone processes grain, silently and with great interior force. He read not quickly but completely. He read, I realised, not each record in isolation but all of them in relation to each other, looking for what a single document could not show.

"His first crossing," he said at last, "was in the consulship of Crispus. His last was eleven days ago. How many crossings total?"

I counted from my copies. "Twenty-three."

"In twenty-three crossings, how many times did he cross eastward — returning to the Gothic shore — within three days of his westward arrival?"

I counted again. This took longer. "Seven times."

"Seven times in six years he cut short a trading visit that should, by any commercial logic, have lasted at minimum a week — long enough to sell his leather, arrange his next purchase, rest his animals. The other sixteen crossings ran between eight days and three weeks. Standard commercial rhythm. But seven times, something called him back early." He turned a record toward me. "Look at the dates of those seven short returns. Do they suggest anything?"

I looked. I am a notary. I am trained to observe dates. "They are not evenly distributed," I said slowly. "They cluster. Three of the seven fall within a month of the major garrison rotations."

"Yes," said Holkos-Theodoros, and there was in his voice the particular quiet satisfaction of a man who has found that a student has answered correctly. "And of the remaining four?"

I studied them. Two fell in autumn. Two in spring. "The seasons of campaign," I said. "When Gothic movements would be most likely, and most worth reporting."

"When information about Gothic movements would be most valuable," he said. "To someone paying for it."

He stood and walked to the small window that looked out onto the grey river, and for a moment his stillness had a different quality to it — not the millstone-stillness of processing, but something older and more tired.

"Alaric was not a trader," he said. "Or rather, he was a trader in the way that a mask is a face. He traded in leather because leather provides a reason to cross, a reason to return, a reason to carry sealed document rolls that are declared as freight manifests and are not freight manifests. He was an asset. He was run from this command. He brought information about Gothic political organisation, troop dispositions, and the pressures building from the east — pressures which I have been reporting on for fifteen years and which I know to be real, and which certain officers of this command find it convenient to characterise as exaggerated, because the alternative is to admit that what is coming cannot be stopped by the means presently at their disposal."

He turned from the window.

"His reports were going to Constantinople. Someone in this command discovered this. Someone in this command needed them to stop."

The room was very quiet. Outside, the river moved past with its iron smell, carrying whatever it carried.

"Who was his handler?" I asked.

"That," said Holkos-Theodoros, "is what the documents under his nails will tell us, once we have identified what the substance is." He was already moving toward the door. "Come, Marcellinus. We have a body to examine more carefully, and I have sent for the garrison physician, who will not understand what he is looking for but who can be directed."

The garrison physician was a competent Antiochene named Paulos who had served enough frontier postings to have lost his squeamishness and retained his precision. He examined the body under lamplight in the garrison's small medical room, with Holkos-Theodoros at his shoulder asking questions that Paulos clearly found unusual.

"The substance," said Holkos-Theodoros. "Is it consistent with ink?"

Paulos examined the discolouration beneath the nails of the first two fingers of the right hand. "With a particular kind of ink," he said slowly. "I have seen something similar in the eastern trade — not Roman manufacture. certain scribes of the northern tribes use a preparation of oak-gall and metallic salts in different proportions than we do. It produces a darker residue and it sets more slowly." He looked up. "A man who had been writing with such an ink, writing quickly and at length, might have it under his nails if he had not washed carefully afterward."

"He was writing on the night of his death," said Holkos-Theodoros. "Writing in a foreign preparation. Writing, therefore, either in the Gothic script or in a cipher that used Gothic materials — which suggests either that his document was intended to be read on the northern shore, or that it was encrypted in a form that only someone familiar with Gothic scribal practice could decode." He paused. "Was there a document found with the body?"

"Nothing," said Paulos.

"No. There would not be. It was taken." He looked at the extended hand, still in its posture of pointing, and something shifted in his expression — a very slight tightening around the eyes, which I understood, watching him, was the closest he came to visible feeling. "He was composing his final report when they came for him. He had time to hide it. He did not have time to hide himself."

"Where?" I asked.

"If I were a man who knew he might not survive the night, and who needed his information to reach the right person, I would put it somewhere that the person who killed me would not think to look — because the person who killed me would know my habits and my resources, but would not know what I knew they did not know." He straightened. "We will want to examine his freight. Whatever he declared at customs on this final crossing."

The freight manifest for Alaric's last crossing listed sixteen dressed hides, three bolts of undyed wool, and a sealed ceramic jar of tallow declared for purposes of leather treatment. This last item had been impounded at customs upon the discovery of the body — standard procedure for goods whose owner had died before completion of his business — and sat in the customs store.

The tallow, when the jar was opened, was tallow. But the jar had a false bottom, fitted with such care that even Sosias, when shown it, admitted he would not have found it without being directed. Beneath the false bottom, wrapped in oiled skin and sealed with wax, was a roll of thin papyrus covered in writing.

Holkos-Theodoros broke the seal and read it without expression. He read it twice. Then he rolled it carefully and held it in both hands for a moment, as though weighing it.

"What does it say?" I asked.

"It says what I expected it to say," he said. "It names his handler — the officer through whom his reports passed before they reached Constantinople. It notes that in the last four dispatches, the handler had altered his reports before forwarding them — suppressing the most significant findings about Gothic military organisation, changing his estimates of the timeline of pressure from the east, softening his conclusions about the likelihood of large-scale westward migration." He looked at me steadily. "And it names who the handler reported to."

I waited.

"Tribune Gallus," he said, "is not the officer who altered the reports. He is not subtle enough. The name in this document is a name above Gallus. A name that appears in the official record as a vigorous defender of the frontier and a firm advocate of the position that the Gothic threat is manageable by conventional means — because if the Gothic threat is not manageable by conventional means, then the conventional means, and the men who command them, have failed."

He set the roll on the table.

"Alaric understood that his reports were being altered. He prepared this document as an insurance — a full account of his findings, uncorrupted, with evidence of the alterations. He intended it to reach Constantinople directly, bypassing his handler. Someone learned that he had prepared it, or that he intended to send it. And so he was killed, and the document he intended to send was taken from him — except that he had already hidden the original, which is what we are holding."

The lamp between us made a small sound, the wick shifting in its oil. Outside, the Danube moved past in the dark.

"What will you do with it?" I asked.

He was quiet for a long interval.

"The man who ordered his death is a senior officer of the Danubian command," he said at last. "If I present this document to the appropriate authorities, the appropriate authorities are, in significant part, subordinate to or dependent upon the man named in it. A formal accusation would be disputed, the evidence questioned, the document's provenance challenged. Alaric's handler — the intermediate officer — would likely be sacrificed as sufficient explanation. The man above him would survive. The reports to Constantinople would continue to be shaped by the same hands."

He stood and moved to the window. The fog had thinned enough to show the river's surface, black and moving.

"And the frontier?" I said.

"The frontier," he said, "will be swept away regardless of what this document reveals or conceals. I have spent fifteen years calculating what is coming. A prosecution that fails and produces no change in policy delays the catastrophe by weeks at most." He turned. "But there is one man in Constantinople who has read my reports — my actual reports, before they were altered — and who has used them to prepare what preparations can be made. Not many. But some. He needs to know that the information reaching him has been corrupted, and for how long, and by whom, so that he can calibrate what he was told against what was true."

"You will send him the document."

"I will send him this document, and I will send him my own account of this matter, and I will seal both under my own authority, and I will entrust them to a courier whose loyalties I know, because there are still one or two such couriers on this frontier." He picked up the roll. "The man who ordered Alaric's death will not be arrested. He will not be publicly accused. He will continue to serve, and to be promoted, and perhaps to believe that he has resolved his problem."

"And has he?"

"He has resolved it in the way that a man who blocks a river with his hand resolves the problem of the river," said Holkos-Theodoros. "Alaric is dead. The information Alaric carried is not. The man in Constantinople who needed it has it. What he does with it is beyond my calculation or my responsibility." He looked at me. "You have been recording this, Marcellinus."

"I have."

"Continue to do so. Everything I have described to you, every step of the reasoning, every finding, set it down completely and accurately. Not for the official archive — the official archive will record that Alaric the trader was found drowned and that the cause was consistent with accident or tribal dispute, and that the matter was resolved. That is what the archive will record because that is what I will tell Tribune Gallus, who will be relieved, and who will ensure that the record reflects his relief."

"Then for whom do I write it?"

He was already at the door.

"For the same reader who receives the document," he said. "He collects such accounts. He has been collecting them for twenty years. He says they are useful for understanding the shape of things — not the official shape, which is always the shape that power needs them to have, but the actual shape, which is usually considerably less convenient." He paused in the doorway, the lamp catching the grey of his eyes. "He is also, I should tell you, the only man I have met whose reasoning I respect without reservation. It is a short list. You will understand why when you meet him."

He left. I sat with my tablet and my lamp and the sound of the river, and I wrote.

Three days after the events described above, Holkos-Theodoros departed Noviodunum without formal announcement of his departure, which was consistent with his arrival. Tribune Gallus closed the matter of Alaric the trader as death by misadventure, possibly attendant upon intoxication, possibly involving persons unknown on the northern shore. This finding was transmitted to the relevant offices and produced no further inquiry.

The officer whose name appeared in Alaric's hidden document continued in his post for two more years, was elevated to a command position in the eastern Balkans, and died of a fever in the third year thereafter. Whether the man in Constantinople to whom Holkos-Theodoros dispatched his sealed account made any use of it, I do not know. I was not in a position to know.

What I know is this: the things Holkos-Theodoros described to me — the pressures from the east, the nature of Gothic movement, the inadequacy of conventional response — proved, in every particular, to be true. The frontier did not hold. I was not present when it broke. I had been transferred, by then, to administrative duties in the west.

I have set down this account because he asked me to, and because I believe he was right that such accounts should exist — not in the official archive, where the shape of things is always the shape that serves the present moment, but somewhere else, in some less official record, where the actual shape can be recovered by anyone who knows where to look and how to read.

He told me, on the morning before he left, that the reasoning was always simpler than it appeared, once the extraneous information had been set aside. "The mind," he said, "is like a well-kept storeroom. Everything in it must earn its place. What Alaric's nails told us, what the crossing records told us, what the posture of the body told us — these were not obscure. They were merely unread. The river had left its deposition plain enough for anyone who looked at it as a document rather than a misfortune."

I asked him if he had ever been wrong.

He considered this for longer than I expected.

"Once," he said. "I calculated everything correctly and arrived too late regardless. The calculation was sound. The variable I had not included was that the man I intended to warn had decided, before I reached him, that the warning was unnecessary." He paused. "I have not forgotten the lesson, but I confess I have not fully determined what the lesson was. Whether it is that human beings are incalculable, or simply that I had not yet learned to calculate them."

He did not explain further. He went to the river, and he took his sealed documents with him, and the fog came in again from the water, and after a little while I could not see him anymore.

I think about the body on the planking — the arm extended, pointing across the water to the Gothic shore, as though Alaric even in death was trying to send his information to where it needed to go. I think about what Holkos-Theodoros saw in that posture that Tribune Gallus did not. I think about the distance between looking and observing, which he spoke of as though it were a simple thing, though I have tried to practice it since and find it less simple than he made it appear.

The river, at any rate, still smells of iron and rotting reed. Some things, at least, can be relied upon.

Here ends the Account of Flavius Marcellinus, Notary of the Danubian Command, sealed in the Consulship of Gratian and Equitius.

Children:

- Hulmar the Goth — see next entry.

HULMAR THE GOTH

~370 AD — Frankish lands

He was born into the great movement — the churning of peoples across the face of Europe that the Romans called, with characteristic understatement, the migrations, and that those who lived through it experienced as the dissolution of everything that had been fixed. The Visigoths were on the march, pressed from the east by the Huns and pressing in turn against the crumbling frontiers of Rome, and Hulmar moved with them — not as a warrior chieftain but as something the migrations needed equally and acknowledged less: a man who could assess a situation, read the intentions of strangers, and negotiate passage through hostile country without provoking the violence that was always, in those years, the easiest outcome.

His name was a Gothic rendering of the old name-element the family had carried westward from Constantinople — "Hulmar," cognate with the Norse "Holmr" that his descendants would bear, the sound shifting as the languages shifted around it. He settled eventually in the territory between the Rhine and the Meuse, where Gothic and Frankish and Roman remnants were beginning the long process of becoming something new. He left his son a position rather than an estate — which, in a world where estates could be taken in an afternoon, was the more durable inheritance.

The account that follows is preserved not in a formal scribe’s hand but in fragments — a set of leather strips recovered from a travelling kit long after Hulmar’s death, their edges worn smooth, the ink partially faded where rain had seeped through the stitching. The writer is not named, but the tone is distinct: halting where memory falters, precise where the writer trusts his own recollection, and marked by a habit of noting what is seen before attempting to understand it. The author of this record is Hulmar himself, though he would not have described himself as such. He wrote only what he observed, and what he could not yet explain he left unfinished rather than complete.

The Leather Strips of Hulmar

Set Down in the Winter Quarter Between the Rhine and the Meuse, in the Year of the Great Pressing

The body was found before the morning mist had lifted off the river, and by the time I reached it the Frankish chieftain Ragomar had already decided who had done the killing.

This is the way of it, in these years. A man dies, and before the flies have found him the living are already arranging his death into a shape that serves them. Ragomar wanted it to be Chlodwin of the river-Franks, with whom he had a quarrel older than the dead man's boots. He said so three times before I had finished crouching beside the body, which is three times more than a man certain of his reasoning needs to say anything.

I will set down what I saw, because I did not trust myself to say it aloud in that camp, and because I have found that writing a thing fixes it in a way that speaking does not. Words spoken in a camp at dawn, among men who have been cold for two nights and are hungry and looking for permission to do what their hands already want to do — those words travel poorly. They arrive changed. But marks on leather do not change unless the leather rots, and I have oiled this leather well.

The dead man's name was Gaius Felicianus Sertor. He had told me this himself, three days prior, when our band passed through the waystation at the old mill crossing where he had set up his small commerce. He traded in lamp oil — the thick, dark kind pressed from river-reeds, which burns with more smoke than the olive oil of the south but costs a fraction of the price and can be procured without depending on a trade route that the Huns have made unreliable. His hands were stained permanently with it, and his cloak smelled of it in the particular way that reed-oil clings, which is not unpleasant — a low, green smell, like the river itself had been rendered down and bottled.

He was a cautious man, Sertor. I had noticed the way his eyes moved when our band came into the waystation yard: not to our weapons first, as most men's eyes moved, but to our boots, our packs, the condition of our animals. He was reading our capacity for purchase, not our capacity for violence. A trader's reflex, and a refined one. He had survived in the frontier territory for some years by correctly identifying which bands were worth selling to and which were too desperate to bargain with safely.

He had a mule — a grey animal, old but sound — and a horse he used for his own travel. A bay, with a blaze on the left side of its face. I noticed it because it stood unusually still while the mule fidgeted.

I mention the horse because it was the first thing I looked for when I came to the body, and it was not there.

He lay on his back in the shallow depression beside the old Roman road, the stone surface of which had not been repaired in perhaps forty years and had begun to return to its constituent gravel. The body had not been dragged — the soil around him was undisturbed except by his own boot-heels, which had furrowed the mud in a manner consistent with a man falling backward from standing, with some violence. The furrows ran from the road's edge inward. He had been standing on the road when the blow came.

The wound was on his left side, below the ribs and slightly toward the back. This is the detail that I have been turning over since I stood up from the body and looked at Ragomar and said nothing.

A blade enters the left side from the back, below the ribs, when the man wielding it is standing behind and to the right of the victim, or occasionally when the two men are face to face and the attacking hand reaches across the body to strike. It does not, under ordinary circumstances, enter a man from the front when the attacker is left-handed and the victim is facing him, because a left-handed man striking from the front strikes the right side, not the left.

I know this because I am left-handed. I have been thinking about the geometry of violence since I was old enough to notice that the world is built for the right hand, and a left-handed man must always approach a fight from a different angle than the instructors assume.

The wound that killed Sertor entered from the back.

He was struck from behind, on a road he knew well, by someone he had allowed to walk behind him.

This tells me something. Men do not let strangers walk behind them on the frontier roads. It was not a stranger.

Ragomar's quarrel with Chlodwin of the river-Franks concerned a cattle-driving right on the common land near the old Roman ford. It was a genuine quarrel and a serious one, the kind that produces real violence eventually. It was also entirely unconnected to the death of a Roman oil trader on a road that Chlodwin's people did not use, because they approached the waystation from the north, along the river, and Chlodwin himself was known to use a different ford two days' walk upstream.

I did not say this to Ragomar.

I also did not say the following, though I have no difficulty saying it here, on leather, in the privacy of what I hope will be read only by careful eyes:

The mule was still in the waystation yard when we found the body. I had confirmed this with my own eyes before walking out to the road. A trader does not stable his mule and ride his horse to a meeting he intends to survive. He takes the mule for commerce, the horse for flight. The mule remaining in the yard meant Sertor had gone to this meeting expecting it to be brief, local, and conducted by someone he had already agreed to meet. He had not expected to need flight.

The lamp oil in his smaller pack — the travelling pack, not the commercial stock — had been gone through. Not robbed. Gone through and largely replaced, but in the wrong order. The bladders had been repacked with the oldest one on top, and I knew from watching Sertor manage his stock three days earlier that he always put the newest pressing on top, to remind himself to sell the older stock first. He had not packed that bag. Someone else had, after searching it, and had made the small error of not knowing how he organized his merchandise.

They had found what they were looking for, or not found it, and repacked the bag to suggest the trader's goods had not been touched.

What they were looking for, I do not know. I have my suspicions — Sertor had the careful manner of a man who carries more than oil through the frontier territory, and there are organisations that move information and sometimes valuables along these roads, using the commercial traffic as a vehicle, and the frontier years have made such organisations both more necessary and more ruthless in policing their membership. But this is speculation, and I will not write speculation as fact.

What I can write as fact is this: the man who killed Gaius Felicianus Sertor was known to him, walked behind him, searched his pack with some familiarity, and had a reason to want him dead that had nothing to do with Ragomar's dispute about cattle-driving rights on a ford two days' walk from here.

Ragomar sent men to Chlodwin's camp at midday.

I had, by that time, thought carefully about what I would do with what I knew.

The truth, spoken in that camp on that morning, would have produced the following sequence: Ragomar would have felt that I was undermining his authority and protecting his enemy. My band and I would have become the obstacle between him and his desired war. Wars on the frontier do not wait for justification — they simply need permission, and denying a man his permission for a war he wants is a dangerous form of opposition in a season when every band is armed and cold and looking for a reason to move.

Chlodwin, for his part, was guilty of enough that Ragomar's hatred of him was not entirely without foundation. He had raided the mill crossing twice in the previous summer and had made the waystation commerce difficult and dangerous. He was not an innocent man. He was simply not guilty of this particular killing.

If Ragomar attacked Chlodwin and the gods of the frontier roads showed their usual indifference to justice, one of three things would occur: Chlodwin would be killed, which would settle a genuine irritant and leave the truth of Sertor's death untold; Ragomar would be killed, which would settle a different irritant and leave the same truth untold; or both bands would be weakened, the waystation would lose the marginal protection their rivalry provided, and the commerce on that road, already fragile, would collapse entirely for a winter.

In none of these outcomes did speaking the truth serve anyone the truth was capable of serving.

I have thought about this a great deal, walking in the days since.

What I believe — and I set this down not as wisdom but as honest record of my own reasoning — is that Sertor's killer will answer to the organisation he serves before he answers to any man's law in this territory. The frontier roads have their own justice, slower and less visible than Ragomar's, but more precise. The man who searches a dead courier's pack and does not find what he was sent to find, and returns to his masters empty-handed, has already stepped into a current that will carry him to a reckoning I cannot provide and Ragomar cannot provide and the crumbling law of Rome's former territories certainly cannot provide.

He will answer to his own shadow. That is not nothing.

The bay horse with the blaze on its left side was found three days later, grazing loose near the river-bend south of the waystation. Sertor's saddle-bag was still attached. The smaller pack was gone.

I did not report this either.

I have thought about what kind of man writes things down only to keep them secret, only to oil the leather against the rain and store the strips in the bottom of a travelling kit that may never be read by any eyes but his own.

I think it is the kind of man who cannot bear for a true thing to be entirely lost, even when speaking it would be worse than silence. The record is not for justice. Justice in these years is an improvised and local thing, and I have made my peace with that. The record is against the particular darkness of a true thing dissolving as if it never was.

Sertor read his buyers accurately for years and finally misread one. He deserved to have someone note the shape of how he died, even if that someone notes it only in private, only in leather, only for the rain to decide whether it survives.

The mist came off the river again this morning. It smells of reed-mud and cold water and, faintly, of the pressed-reed oil that traders move along these roads, in the year of the great pressing, in the territory between the Rhine and the Meuse, where everything that was fixed is learning to move.

I have written what I saw.

What I could not yet explain, I have left — not unfinished, but open. As a door is left open that the wind has not yet decided to close.

— H., son of H-T., setting down in the winter quarter

Children:

- Hulmar of the Rhine — see next entry.

HULMAR OF THE RHINE

~400 AD — Frankish territory

The Rhine in 400 AD was not a boundary but a battlefield — or rather, a place where the idea of a boundary was being continuously tested and continuously failing. Hulmar of the Rhine grew up on its western bank in the generation that watched the river cease to mean anything, the Vandals and Burgundians and Alans crossing it in force in the winter of 406 and dissolving whatever remained of the Roman frontier in the west. He was a man formed by flux, which is to say he was a man who had learned, before he could fully articulate the lesson, that the world did not hold still and that those who survived were those who could read its movements in advance.

He worked as an interpreter and factor between the various peoples competing for the rich agricultural land of the Rhineland — a role that required not merely linguistic facility but the subtler skill of understanding what each party actually wanted beneath what they said they wanted, and finding the formulation that allowed both to believe they had prevailed. He was rarely wrong in his assessments, and his value to those who employed him lay precisely in that accuracy.

The record that follows is written in the hand of a Burgundian clerk named Aegidius, who entered Hulmar's service as a junior scribe attached to a Frankish trading house. Aegidius was not trained in the habits of inference that Hulmar practiced, and his account reflects both admiration and incomprehension. Where Hulmar observed first and concluded later, Aegidius often recorded what he was told and only afterward attempted to understand its significance. The result is a record that preserves the sequence of events faithfully, though not always the full reasoning that underlies them.

The Matter of Sigiric the Trader

Recorded by Aegidius, Clerk to the House of Veldric, in the Fifth Year of the Consulship of None That I Could Name With Certainty

The fog that morning was the kind that does not lift. It lay on the Rhine as though the river had exhaled it in the night and could not be troubled to breathe it back in. I could not see the eastern bank. I am not certain, on mornings such as that one, that there was an eastern bank — only grey air thickening toward grey water, and somewhere beyond both, the sound of a barge moving through the murk like a blind thing finding its way by instinct.

Hulmar heard it before I did.

He was standing at the end of the dock, his cloak drawn across his shoulders against a cold that had no right to persist into what the farmers were calling spring, and he had gone still in the particular way he went still when something had engaged his attention fully. Not the stillness of a sleeping man. The stillness of a cat that has seen a thing move in the grass.

"Sigiric," he said.

I had heard nothing. I said so.

"The barge is weighted wrong for a return crossing from the east," he said, not turning from the fog. "He carries amber and salted fish going east, which distributes low and forward. Coming back, he carries wine and pressed oil when the season allows, which sits higher. That sound" — and here he tilted his head toward the grey air in a way I could not replicate if I tried — "is a flat-bottomed hull sitting too deep for what he declared to the Frankish toll-collector at Veldric's post yesterday. He has taken on additional cargo. Or additional weight of another kind."

I told him there were many barges on the Rhine.

"There are four that operate this stretch with a flat keel," he said. "Sigiric's is the only one that tacks left before the dock, because the rudder pin was replaced with oak after the iron one split three winters ago, and oak has more flex, and he compensates. Listen."

I listened. After a long moment, I heard it — a subtle correction, a hesitation in the rhythm of movement through the water.

"He tacks left," I said.

Hulmar turned and looked at me in the manner of a man who has just watched a dog perform an unexpected trick. It was not an unkind look. It was simply the look of a man perpetually surprised that others can, on occasion, observe the obvious.

"Come," he said. "And do not speak of amber or fish or wine. Speak of nothing."

I should say something of Sigiric before I describe what followed, because he was not a villain in any shape that the word ordinarily suggests. He was a broad, cheerful man of perhaps forty-five years, with a Burgundian father and a mother whose origins shifted slightly depending on which bank of the Rhine he was currently standing on. His Latin was adequate, his Frankish was excellent, his Burgundian was native, and he possessed the particular social intelligence of a man who has spent his entire life being useful to people who would otherwise regard each other with drawn blades. He was welcomed on both banks. He was trusted on both banks.

This, as Hulmar would later explain to me with the patience of a man who has accepted that he must always explain everything, was precisely the problem.

He came off the barge with his usual expansiveness, arms open, voice preceding him by several paces.

"Hulmar! And young Aegidius — writing things down as always, I see, which is either the sign of a very good memory or a very poor one." He laughed at his own remark. Most of what Sigiric said was, I came to understand, designed to create the impression that nothing needed to be examined too carefully. "I've brought oil from the Moselle valley — two amphorae cracked in transit, I regret to say, which I'll need to note for Veldric's accounts. A poor season for the vine."

"A poor season for many things," Hulmar agreed pleasantly. He was examining Sigiric's boots. I had learned, by then, to notice when Hulmar examined things, though I rarely understood what he was looking for until he explained it afterward. "You crossed last night?"

"Before dawn. The current is better in the dark hours, I find."

"And the toll post on the eastern bank — Radomar's men are still manning it?"

A small hesitation. Not large. A man who did not know Sigiric would not have seen it. "They are," Sigiric said. "Efficient as ever."

"Good," said Hulmar. "Come and drink something. I have questions about the road north of the Moselle — there are reports of movement that concern several of our clients."

This was true. It was also, I understood later, a door being opened.

We sat in the warehouse that Veldric's house used for meetings, which smelled of old wood and river-damp and the particular sweetness of oil that had soaked into the floor planks over decades. A boy brought wine, which Hulmar barely touched. He did this with wine — poured it, arranged it, and left it. He had explained to me once that wine softened the edges of thought and that he preferred his edges sharp. His vice, if it could be called that, ran in the other direction entirely: he had a collection of riddles, accumulated from every people he had ever traded with or interpreted for, written on scraps of hide and wooden tablets and the margins of documents, and when a problem would not resolve itself he would sit with these and work through them methodically, as though the solution to one kind of puzzle might loosen the mechanism of another. I had come home late on more than one occasion to find him surrounded by these fragments, eyes open, utterly absent.

"The movement north of the Moselle," Hulmar said to Sigiric. "Lord Gundomar's war-band. You passed through that country?"

"Skirted it."

"What direction were they moving?"

Sigiric's hand, curled around his cup, did not tighten. But a man who watches hands, as Hulmar had taught me to do, would have noted that it did not relax further, either. "South-west," he said. "Toward Treviris, I think."

"Mm." Hulmar nodded slowly, apparently satisfied. He then appeared to think of something. "And Bavo's riders? I had word they were ranging east of the road."

"I did not see them."

Hulmar said nothing. He simply looked at his cup.

The silence lasted long enough to become a presence in the room.

"I may have seen a campfire," Sigiric said. "On a ridge. It could have been Bavo's men."

"Which ridge?"

And here Sigiric answered, in the ordinary way a man answers a question he has been asked, and I watched Hulmar's expression do absolutely nothing, which I had come to understand was the expression he wore when he had confirmed something he already knew.

Afterward, when Sigiric had gone to oversee the unloading of his barge, Hulmar sat in the warehouse for a long time without speaking. I waited. One learned to wait.

"His boots," he said at last.

I asked what about them.

"When a man crosses the Rhine at this stretch in a laden barge, he gets his feet wet at the dock landing unless he is very careful or very accustomed to the crossing. Sigiric has been making this crossing for twenty years. His boots were wet to the ankle on the left foot and dry on the right. This is the pattern of a man who has gone ashore at the eastern bank and crossed ground that holds water on the left approach — there is only one place on the eastern side of this stretch where the ground pools that way, which is the inlet below Radomar's post."

I said I did not see the significance.

"He did not merely toll-cross," Hulmar said. "He stopped. He went ashore. He was on foot for long enough to get his boot thoroughly wet, which means he walked some distance inland — to a meeting point, in all likelihood. Men who are merely paying tolls do not leave their barges."

I thought about this. Then I said: and the cargo?

"Lighter than declared on the outward crossing, heavier on the return. He carries something in, leaves it, carries something out. Not goods — goods would show on the draft differently. He carries something small and dense. Documents. A message container of some kind — one of the Roman-style wax-sealed cylinders that the remnant administrators still use." He paused. "Or coin. But I think documents. Because coin is the payment, and I believe he has already been paid."

I asked how he knew.

"His mood," Hulmar said simply. "He is a man who carries anxiety the way some men carry fat — it distributes itself through him, visible in small ways. When he is owed money, he is subtly more expansive, more performative, because the performance is a distraction from the anxiety. Today he was merely himself. The anxiety is gone. The transaction is complete."

I sat with this. Then I said: whose documents?

"That," said Hulmar, rising and pulling his cloak around him, "is the more interesting question. And I think I know the answer. But I wish to verify it before I say it aloud, because I would prefer to be certain rather than merely confident."

He had, I noted, already finished his thinking. He was simply not yet finished with me.

What he had observed, and had been observing — as he explained to me in pieces over the following three days — was this:

For four months, Sigiric had been crossing the Rhine at intervals that corresponded with no commercial logic. Trade follows seasons and road conditions and the availability of goods. His crossings followed none of these. They followed, instead, the movements of Frankish war-bands — specifically, the movements of the three chieftains whose territories abutted the river at this stretch: Gundomar to the north, Bavo in the middle ground, and old Radulf whose lands ran south toward the Roman remnant city.

Each time there was a significant council meeting among these three — the kind of meeting at which the disposition of men and the direction of raids was discussed — Sigiric crossed within two days. Each time there was a crossing, within a further two days one of the three chieftains made a significant military decision that benefited specifically from knowing where the other two intended to be.

"The pattern is not perfect," Hulmar said. "He makes commercial crossings as well. He is genuinely a trader. This is what makes him effective — the genuine commerce conceals the other purpose. But the timing is not random."

I asked who on the eastern bank was buying the information.

"No one on the eastern bank," Hulmar said. "The eastern bank is simply the dead-drop. The point where information changes hands without direct contact. Sigiric collects what he hears from his welcome on the western bank — chieftains speak freely before factors and traders, it is an old habit — and he delivers it on the eastern bank, and the eastern party relays it back to the western bank by a different route to whoever has paid for it."

I said I did not follow.

Hulmar was patient with me. He usually was. "One of the three chieftains is selling the others. Sigiric is the mechanism. The chieftain cannot be seen to communicate with the eastern contacts directly — that would be treason visible to his own warband. So the information travels: chieftain to Sigiric unknowing, Sigiric to eastern contact, eastern contact to the purchasing chieftain by some path I have not yet traced." He paused. "Or rather, was unknowing. I believe Sigiric understands perfectly what he is doing. The question is whether the information flows one direction or two."

I asked which chieftain.

Hulmar picked up his cup, remembered it had wine in it, and put it down. "Radulf," he said. "Because the three meetings whose outcomes I have been able to verify all benefited Radulf's position. He is the one who should by all accounts be weakest — his lands are fragmenting, his Roman patrons are no longer capable of sustained support, his warband is the smallest. Yet his decisions have been, for four months, extraordinarily well-timed. He has not won, but he has not lost. A man who should be losing and is not losing is a man who can see better than he should."

He stood then, and went to the window that looked out over the dock.

"And I note," he said, to the fog rather than to me, "that Sigiric always stops to drink with Radulf's steward when he passes through on the western road. He has always stopped. I thought nothing of it. It is, of course, the collection point."

The question then became what to do.

I confess I had an answer ready. There was a Frankish judge — a senior man attached to Gundomar's household who had begun, tentatively, to collect testimony in cases too complex for simple ordeal or combat. I had spoken with him once. Hulmar had spoken with him several times, and I believed Hulmar respected him in the measured way he respected anyone whose function was to impose order on disorder.

I said we should go to the judge.

Hulmar looked at me.

"With what?" he said. "With the observation that a man's boot was wet? With the calculation of crossing-times and council meetings? These are not proofs. A judge requires a confession, or witnesses, or evidence that can be held in the hand. What I have is a pattern, and patterns are invisible to courts because courts work in sequences — this happened, then this happened, therefore this followed. What I do is different. I see the shape of a thing before the sequence is complete." He shook his head slowly. "To bring this to the judge now would be to warn Sigiric and to give Radulf time to destroy whatever evidence exists of payment. And we have not verified the eastern link. Without that, we have a theory. Theories do not stop treason."

He went back to his riddles that night. I left him surrounded by his fragments of hide and wood, eyes open, working on something I could not see.

The verification required a crossing of our own, which Hulmar arranged by means I will not record fully here, except to note that it involved a fisherman, a pre-dawn hour, and a cold I have not been warm enough since to forget. The eastern bank in the fog was exactly as I had imagined it — indistinguishable from the western bank except that it felt different in the way that any foreign shore feels different, a quality of the air or perhaps of one's own certainty about where one stood.

The inlet below Radomar's post was real, and it pooled on the left approach exactly as Hulmar had said.

What we found at the drop point — a hollow in a particular oak root, known to those who knew it and invisible to those who did not — was a wax-sealed cylinder of precisely the Roman administrative type, which Hulmar examined without opening and returned to its position.

"It has been here since his crossing," Hulmar said. "It has not yet been collected. Therefore the eastern collector has not yet come, which means the information has not yet reached Radulf." He stood for a moment, motionless. "I had thought to leave it."

I asked what he had decided.

"I had thought," he repeated, as though he had not heard me, "to leave it. If it is collected and reaches Radulf, and Radulf acts on it, I can then demonstrate the chain in a way that is visible even to a court. Sequence. This happened, then this happened." A pause. "But the information in that cylinder — I believe, from what Sigiric learned at the last council meeting — concerns Gundomar's western flank. If Radulf knows where Gundomar's flank is weak, men will die. Perhaps many men. For a demonstration of evidence." He was quiet. "The calculation does not resolve cleanly."

He left the cylinder. We crossed back. He did not speak on the water.

I did not fully understand what he had done, in the end, until he explained it to me — as he explained all things — in the sequence he chose, which was not the sequence in which events occurred.

He had sent word to Gundomar, through channels I will not identify, that his western flank had been observed and that a defensive re-positioning might be advisable as a matter of general prudence. No accusation. No explanation. Simply the recommendation of a well-regarded factor.

He had sent word to Bavo — the middle chieftain, the one most harmed by Radulf's covert advantage — that certain trading irregularities had come to his attention and that Bavo might wish to examine more carefully who had access to his council's deliberations. Again, no accusation. Simply the gentle naming of a thing.

And he had gone to Sigiric.

This conversation I witnessed, and I will record it directly.

They met on the dock, in the evening, when the other handlers had gone. Hulmar spoke quietly, as he always did. There was no drama in his voice. He laid out what he knew — the boots, the crossings, the cylinder, the timing — in the manner of a man listing the contents of a warehouse: item by item, value assessed, nothing dramatic.

Sigiric listened. His face did not change greatly. He was a man who had spent his life managing his face.

When Hulmar had finished, Sigiric said: "What do you want?"

"I want," Hulmar said, "to understand why."

A long silence. The river moved below them. Somewhere a gate hinge creaked in the wind, the particular complaint of iron that has been wet too many times — a sound that seems, in the dark, like a question being asked.

"Radulf pays well," Sigiric said. "And Radulf will survive. The others — Gundomar is already hollowed out, he simply does not know it yet. Bavo will be absorbed. In two years, perhaps three, this stretch of the river will be Radulf's to manage, and when that happens I would prefer to be the man Radulf trusts." He looked at Hulmar steadily. "I am not a loyal man. I have never pretended to be. I am a useful man. I have always pretended to be that, and it has always been true."

"Yes," said Hulmar. "That is what I thought."

Another silence.

"And now?" Sigiric said.

"You will stop," Hulmar said. "You will cross normally, trade normally, and allow the information that has already been transmitted to prove insufficient for Radulf's purposes — which it will, because Gundomar has re-positioned and the intelligence is now incorrect. Radulf will conclude that his source has become unreliable. The arrangement will end naturally, because natural endings leave no accusations." He looked at Sigiric without particular expression. "You are, as you say, a useful man. A factor who is known to have been a spy is no longer useful on either bank. I imagine you have already calculated this."

Sigiric was quiet for a moment. Then he said: "And you?"

"I will record that a trading irregularity was identified and corrected," Hulmar said. "Aegidius will note it as a matter of commercial accounts. The judge will see nothing, because there is nothing he needs to see." He paused. "Three chieftains in an open conflict, with the bodies of their warbands in the mud, serve no one's commerce. A contained situation, quietly redirected, serves everyone. Including, I note, the people who live on both banks and plant crops and have children and do not wish to be caught between armies."

Sigiric nodded slowly. "You are a practical man."

"I am a man," Hulmar said, "who dislikes waste."

I asked him, walking home through the fog — it had never fully lifted, that day, and by evening had thickened again to the consistency of wet wool — whether it troubled him. Letting Sigiric walk away.

"Trouble is imprecise," he said. "Ask me a better question."

I thought about it. I asked whether it was right.

"Also imprecise." He walked for a moment. "Three men are alive who would not be alive if Radulf had received that intelligence and acted before the cylinder was collected. I do not know their names. They do not know mine. The judge would have required a process of public accusation and testimony and counter-testimony, during which the correct information and the incorrect information would have become hopelessly entangled, and Radulf's men would have moved before any legal conclusion was reached, and those three men would still be dead." He was quiet. "What I have done is not justice. Justice is a Roman category and the Romans are losing their grip on it. What I have done is arithmetic. I have changed the numbers."

I said nothing to this. I wrote it down later, exactly as he said it, because I did not know what else to do with it.

Two months later, Sigiric crossed the Rhine as he always had, carrying amber east and oil west, speaking to everyone and committing to no one. He was, as he said, a useful man.

Radulf's position, without the covert advantage, began its slow deterioration in the spring. Gundomar's western flank, repositioned and now genuinely strong, repelled two probing moves. Bavo consolidated. The three chieftains returned, more or less, to the balance that had existed before.

Hulmar went back to his riddles.

I went back to my accounts.

The Rhine went back to being a river, which it was never really, and had never really been, and would not be, for a long time.

Here ends the account of this matter, as recorded in the warehouse ledger-book of the House of Veldric, in the section marked "Sundry Irregularities — Resolved." The formal entries record only the correction of a declared cargo discrepancy. The foregoing is my own addition, set down in the margins and on the supplementary leaves, because I did not know then and do not know now what manner of record this deserves to be — only that Hulmar's name deserves to be in it, and that the world he worked in was already beginning to be a different world before he had finished working in it.

— Aegidius, clerk

Children:

- Holmar of Burgundy — see next entry.

HOLMAR OF BURGUNDY

~435 AD — Burgundy, Frankish territory

The Burgundians had settled in the Rhône valley and the territory that would take their name, and Holmar grew up among them — a family of Rhine German origin now embedded in a Burgundian political world that was itself embedded in the decaying Roman administrative structure of the south. The name was shifting: less Gothic now, more Frankish in its sound, the family adapting its identity as it always had to the prevailing culture without quite dissolving into it.

Holmar served the Burgundian court as a scribe and record-keeper, maintaining the kind of documentation — land grants, tribute records, the terms of agreements between petty kings and their subordinates — that allowed a polity to function as something more than organised plunder. He understood that written records were power of a particular and durable kind, that the man who held the documents often outlasted the man who held the sword, and he organised his life accordingly. His records were accurate and his copies were good and he died in his bed, which in fifth-century Burgundy represented a considerable achievement.

The account that follows is preserved in a codex fragment copied by a later Burgundian archivist named Sigibert, who appended marginal notes correcting and clarifying earlier entries. Sigibert did not possess Holmar’s ability to perceive irregularities at a glance, but he possessed a careful hand and a respect for precision. His notes reveal both the strength and the limitation of his position: he recorded what was written and what he was told, but his conclusions lagged behind his observations, and where he suspected error he annotated rather than resolved it. The result is a layered record in which the original hand and the later commentary can be distinguished.

From the Codex Fragmentum Burgundiaci

Copying from the earlier record of Holmar, Scribe of the Royal Chancery, with marginal annotations preserved as footnotes. The dating places these events in the reign of Gundobad, in the consulship year reckoned as the four hundred and eighty-third since the birth of Our Lord.

The smell of the Rhône in autumn was a particular thing. Not the clean coldness of the river in flood, which carried the honest mud of Alpine meadows and the cold mineral clarity of snowmelt, but the thick-banked autumnal odour of low water and rotting reed, of the fish-traps left too long, of the silt in which the previous season’s smaller catastrophes had come to rest and begun their slow decomposition. Holmar had lived within a quarter-mile of the river for the better part of his forty years, and he had ceased to notice it as other men ceased to notice the sound of their own heartbeat—but on the morning that Theudebald’s man came hammering at the gate in the grey hour before lauds, the river smell rose with particular intensity through the fog, as though the water itself had something to announce.

He lay still for a moment after waking, staring at the underside of the thatch. He could hear three things: the hammering, which was rhythmic and suggested a man who had not yet exhausted his patience; the breathing of his copyist Ansgar in the outer room, which had shifted from the long regularity of deep sleep into the shallow irregularity of a man pretending not to be awake; and beneath both of these, half-heard, the particular creak of the river gate three streets over, which swung on an iron hinge that had not been oiled since Midsummer and announced each tide-fluctuation with a sound like a cat in dispute with a larger cat.

It was the gate-hinge that told him the hour more accurately than any water-clock. Low tide. Therefore shortly before the second hour of morning. Therefore Theudebald’s man had ridden through the night, which meant that Theudebald considered the matter urgent. Whether Theudebald’s assessment of urgency was reliable was a separate question.

He rose, wrapped his heavy wool cloak over his sleeping-tunic, and opened the door before the hammering could begin its fourth sequence.

The man on the step was young and mud-covered to the thigh and his horse, visible at the gate-post, stood with its head low and its sides still heaving in the lamplight. The man began to speak.

“Lord Theudebald—”

“Has sent you without waiting for morning,” Holmar said, “which means the matter concerns the court, and he fears what daylight will bring. Whether he fears it will bring an inquiry or an escape I cannot yet say. Come in. Ansgar—” he raised his voice only slightly— “stop pretending. Heat water.”

The sound of Ansgar moving, with great dignity and no acknowledgment of having been asleep, came from the outer room.

It is necessary to explain the positions of the parties as Holmar understood them by the time he had heard the messenger’s account and consumed the better part of a flask of the thin Burgundian wine he kept for morning necessities, which was not good wine but was warm and did the work required of it.

The land in question lay some four miles east of Lugdunum, toward the hills—a villa property of perhaps two hundred iugera, good river-bottom soil producing grain and some wine-grapes and a stand of managed woodland that provided both timber and grazing. It had been the property of the Carantii, a Gallo-Roman family that had survived the transition from imperial administration to Burgundian rule by the reliable method of making themselves useful to each successive power: they kept their land records accurate, paid their levies without argument, and attended the court at the appropriate festivals without drawing attention to their religious opinions, which were their own business. The father of the present Carantius had served as a minor fiscal assessor; Carantius himself had no court position but maintained good relations, and was not the kind of man who made enemies.

The document that had destroyed him was a land grant, or rather a confirmation of grant, drafted eighteen months prior and bearing the seal of Gundobad’s court—the kind of document that confirmed existing ownership against competing claims, a legal fortress built in parchment and wax. Carantius had paid the clerk’s fee, received his copy, and considered the matter settled.

The competing claim had been filed six weeks ago by Richomer, lord of Ambria, a Burgundian of the second generation whose holdings lay directly adjacent to the Carantii property on its eastern margin. Richomer was ambitious in the way of men who have enough but have calculated that more is possible. He had submitted a document of his own: a grant from an earlier date, predating the Carantii confirmation, which purported to assign the villa property to his family line in partial settlement of a military obligation. The seals were in order. The dating formula was correct. The signatures of two now-dead court witnesses appeared at the bottom.

The junior clerk accused of having falsified the Carantii confirmation to obscure Richomer’s prior claim was named Wulfo. He was nineteen years old, the son of a minor Burgundian landowner, and had been employed in the chancery for fourteen months. He had been, until four days ago, considered competent. He was now in a locked room in Theudebald’s hall, having been found in possession of—or rather, having had found in his possession—a stylus of the particular bronze alloy used for the wax-tablet drafts of formal documents, with traces of a composition of oak-gall and iron that matched the ink used in the Carantii confirmation.

The night officer of the court, a Burgundian named Hrodmund who had the permanent expression of a man whose dignity was never quite adequately recognised, had declared the matter resolved. Wulfo had tampered with the document at the instruction of Carantius or for his own purposes. The Carantii confirmation was void. The earlier grant took precedence. Richomer’s claim stood.

Hrodmund had made this determination on the afternoon of the previous day and had sent to Theudebald to confirm it. Theudebald, who held the position of court chamberlain and who was, among his other qualities, a man who had worked alongside Holmar for eleven years and had developed in that time a reliable sense of when a conclusion arrived too smoothly, had not confirmed it. Instead he had sent his man south through the October fog.

“He is right to be uncertain,” Holmar said, setting down his flask. “Tell me one thing before we go. Who had custody of the original parchment copy in the interval between its drafting and its submission by Carantius?”

The messenger looked at him with the blankness of a man who had not been asked this question before.

“That,” Holmar said, more to himself than to the messenger, “is what I will need to establish.”

He nodded to Ansgar, who was already folding a clean piece of linen around Holmar’s case of instruments with the efficient competence that Holmar relied upon and occasionally acknowledged.

Sigibert’s Note: What follows is my rendering of Holmar’s examination as he dictated it to me the following morning. I have preserved his account in his own sequence, though I confess that when he first described his methods to me I grasped his conclusions before I grasped his reasoning, which he found unsatisfying. He took considerable pains to explain it again. I believe I now understand the greater part of it, and the remainder I reproduce faithfully without claiming to understand it fully.

They arrived at the court hall at the third hour, while the fog was still absolute and the torches in the courtyard burned with their flames bent sideways by the river-wind, each one trailing its own thin column of oiled smoke that rose and was immediately swallowed by the grey.
