The smell of the lamp-oil—the cheaper variety, fish-based, that the court used for outdoor illumination and which carried a distinct marine undertone—mixed with the fog and the river and the smell of horses and made the courtyard air into something almost solid.

Hrodmund met them at the door with the expression of a man who had expected his conclusions to have already become the foundation of further events, and found instead that the foundation was being examined.

“The matter is resolved,” he said. He was a large man with a careful beard, and he wore his court position the way certain men wore new clothes—with a slight stiffness that suggested he had not yet entirely forgotten the effort required to acquire them.

“The matter is interesting,” Holmar replied. “Which is a different category. I require the original wax tablets, the parchment copy of the Carantii confirmation, the stylus you found on the boy, and the earlier grant submitted by Richomer. And the room where the documents are kept. And light.”

Hrodmund’s expression suggested that the list was longer than he had anticipated.

“I also require,” Holmar added, with a mildness that Ansgar had learned to recognise as the tone he used when he expected significant resistance, “that Carantius be brought from wherever he has been sent, and that Richomer be summoned from wherever he currently is, and that neither of them be informed of what the other is doing. And Wulfo. I will want Wulfo.”

“Wulfo is secured.”

“Unsecure him sufficiently to bring him here. He can be re-secured afterward if necessary.”

The document room was small and cold and smelled of the particular combination of dust, old parchment, and the wax used to seal document-chests that Holmar had always found slightly comforting, in the way that any smell associated with years of satisfactory work becomes comforting. Ansgar lit the lamps—two oil-lamps of the better quality, steady-flamed—and Holmar laid the documents on the reading-desk in the sequence he required.

The wax tablets first.

A wax tablet of the kind used in the Burgundian chancery was a practical instrument with a specific physical logic. The wooden frame held the wax surface. A stylus made impressions. If you smoothed the wax and reimposed it, you could reuse the tablet—but you could not, if you knew what to look for, eliminate the ghost of what had been written before. Wax retained memory the way certain silts retained the outlines of fish that had died in them.

The tablets bearing the draft record of the Carantii confirmation were three in number. Holmar examined them under lamplight at an oblique angle, tilting each one until the surface caught the flame from the side and the shadow-topography of the impressions became visible.

What he saw on the second tablet confirmed what he had already suspected before he entered the room.

“Ansgar,” he said. “Come here.”

Ansgar came. He was a solid young man from a Rhine German family, methodical and observant, and Holmar valued his observations precisely because they represented what an intelligent person without specialised knowledge would see.

“Tell me what you notice.”

Ansgar studied the tablet for a long moment, tilting it as Holmar had done. “There are two sets of impressions,” he said finally. “The upper ones are clear. The lower ones—underneath—are fainter. As though the tablet was smoothed and reused.”

“Good. Now tell me what the lower impressions say.”

Another silence. Ansgar was not a rapid reader of the older script-forms, but he was careful.

“It appears to be a land measurement,” he said. “Numbers. A boundary description.”

“It is a draft of a different document entirely,” Holmar said. “Specifically, it is a draft of Richomer’s grant—the earlier document, the one that predates the Carantii confirmation by three years. This tablet was used to draft Richomer’s grant, then smoothed and reused to draft the Carantii confirmation.”

He let that sit in the air of the small room for a moment.

“Is that significant?” Ansgar asked.

“Administratively, it is merely careless. Chronologically, it is impossible.”

He turned to face Ansgar directly, which he only did when he intended to explain something and wanted to observe whether the explanation had taken root.

“Richomer’s grant is dated three years before the Carantii confirmation. If Richomer’s grant is genuine and predates the Carantii confirmation, then the tablet used to draft it should have been smoothed and reused before the Carantii confirmation was drafted—which would mean the lower impressions, the earlier document, would be the Richomer grant, and the upper impressions, the later document, would be the Carantii confirmation. And this is indeed what we see. The lower impressions are the Richomer grant; the upper impressions are the Carantii confirmation.”

Ansgar nodded. “So the tablet is consistent.”

“The tablet is consistent with the dates as Richomer claims them. Yes.” Holmar set the tablet down. “But tablets are reused constantly; the consistency proves very little by itself. It would be easy to arrange. A scribe creating a false document with a false earlier date would be careful to use an old tablet for his draft. Now look at the parchment.”

The parchment copy of the Carantii confirmation was a good-quality document, calf-skin rather than sheep, which indicated a paying client who wanted permanence. The ink was of the standard court composition—iron gall with a small addition of lamp-black for density—and the hand was the formal chancery script that Holmar had himself helped to standardise across the court’s documents ten years prior.

He examined it first with the naked eye, then brought one of the lamps within three inches of the surface and tilted the parchment until he was reading it in near-raking light.

Ansgar waited.

“There are two texts here,” Holmar said after several minutes.

“I see only one,” Ansgar said carefully.

“You see one because only one was meant to be seen. But parchment, like wax, retains memory. When you scrape a parchment surface to remove text, you thin the skin. When you write new text over the scraped area, the underlying surface is slightly different in texture from the surrounding material—more absorbent in some places, slightly shinier in others, depending on the quality of the scraping. Under raking light, these differences become visible.”

He tilted the parchment further and Ansgar leaned in.

“The area around the date and the third boundary clause,” Holmar continued. “Do you see the slight difference in surface? Here—and here.”

Ansgar was quiet for a moment. Then: “It looks slightly different. I might not have noticed without looking for it.”

“Most people would not notice. That was the intention. The document as it left the chancery was correct. Carantius paid his fee, received his confirmation, and the text assigned him the correct property with the correct boundaries. At some point after it left the chancery, two elements were altered: the date formula—adjusted to appear more recent, so that Richomer’s supposedly earlier grant would seem to predate it—and the boundary clause, which was modified to show a slightly different eastern margin, one that could be argued to be ambiguous against Richomer’s claim.”

“Who altered it?”

Holmar set the parchment down with the care he gave all documents. “That,” he said, “is the question that Hrodmund believes he has already answered. Let us examine the stylus.”

The stylus found in Wulfo’s possession was bronze, of good quality, with the particular degree of wear at the point that indicated regular use. The traces of ink—iron gall, as specified—were visible in the grooves near the working end.

Holmar looked at it for a long time. Then he looked at the parchment again, specifically at the altered areas.

“Ansgar,” he said. “When you alter text on parchment—when you scrape and rewrite—what instrument do you use for the scraping?”

“A knife. Or a pumice stone, for finer work.”

“And for the rewriting?”

“A quill. Or a reed pen if the text is rough-draft only.”

“A stylus is for wax. For impression. Not for ink on parchment.” He held up the bronze instrument. “This is not the instrument that made the alterations on this document. The alterations were made with a quill cut to the court’s standard nib, loaded with iron-gall ink. This stylus may have ink on it because it was used to draft something on a tablet and then dipped carelessly, or because someone placed the ink there deliberately. But it was not the instrument of the falsification.”

He set it down.

“Furthermore,” he said, and now his voice took on the slightly quieter quality that Ansgar had learned meant he was approaching the point of the exercise, “Wulfo had custody of this document for six days, during the initial drafting phase. After Carantius paid and the document was sealed and entered into the court’s register, custody passed out of Wulfo’s hands. The register shows it was collected by Carantius on the day after sealing. But Carantius did not take it immediately home. He lodged it for safekeeping with the court archivist for a further three weeks while he settled a family matter that required his travel. During those three weeks, access to the archive chest required the key held by the duty chamberlain.”

He looked at Ansgar.

Ansgar said, carefully: “Theudebald is the court chamberlain.”

“Theudebald has been chamberlain for six years and is the most meticulous record-keeper I have worked with since I took my own position. But the duty key is not always held by Theudebald himself. It rotates to the officer of the court. And in those three weeks—” he had the archive duty-roster before him now, a document he had requested before leaving the building and which Ansgar had carried under his arm since— “the officer of the court who held the key was Hrodmund.”

The name settled in the room like a stone into water.

“And Richomer is Hrodmund’s wife’s brother,” Holmar said. He did not say it as a revelation. He said it the way he might note that the river was on their left as they walked north—a geographical fact that organised the landscape. “Which I knew before I entered this room. Which I expect Theudebald also knew, and which is why he sent for me rather than confirming Hrodmund’s conclusion.”

Sigibert’s Note: I record the following as Holmar described it to me. I was not present. I have attempted to render it faithfully and acknowledge that the account has almost certainly gained in clarity through the telling, as all accounts do.

He assembled them in the hall in a specific order that was not accidental. Hrodmund he positioned nearest the door—not to block escape, but because a man near a door can more easily imagine departure, and a man who imagines departure reveals his assessment of his own position. Carantius he placed to the left, in the chair. Wulfo—pale, bewildered, still showing the marks of a night in a locked room—he placed standing, in the centre, facing Hrodmund. Richomer he had not summoned. Richomer was a problem for a later stage.

Theudebald sat to one side and said nothing. He had the quality, rare in powerful men, of being able to observe without needing to visibly participate.

“Wulfo,” Holmar said. “You prepared the initial draft of the Carantii confirmation on tablets, transcribed to parchment, and presented it for the seal. Is this accurate?”

Wulfo, who had the dazed quality of a young man who has spent the night discovering that the world is different from what he understood it to be, said that it was.

“After sealing and registration, did you have any further contact with the document?”

“No. I—no. I completed the registration and then Carantius came for it and I recorded the collection and that was—that was all I did.”

“I believe you,” Holmar said.

The quality of silence in the room changed. Hrodmund, near the door, went very still.

“The document was altered after it left Wulfo’s hands,” Holmar continued, and now he addressed Theudebald rather than the room generally, which was a deliberate choice—Theudebald was the authority, and the performance was for the authority’s benefit. “The alteration required access to a locked archive. It required a knife or pumice and a quill and ink matching the court standard. It required enough time to work carefully, because the work was done carefully—I nearly missed the surface-change in the date formula, which is the more important falsification. And it required knowledge of Richomer’s intention to file a competing claim at some future date, so that the alteration would be useful when it was finally deployed. The stylus found on Wulfo is a false planting. The iron-gall on its tip is consistent with his ordinary work but is not consistent with the method used to alter the document, which did not use a stylus at all.”

Hrodmund said: “This is a reading of impressions by a man who—”

“The archive duty-roster,” Holmar said, and Ansgar placed it on the table, “shows that during the three weeks in which the Carantii confirmation was in archive custody awaiting collection, the officer of the court holding the duty key was yourself on fourteen of twenty-one days. Richomer is your wife’s brother. You knew what grant he intended to file. You needed the Carantii document to appear weaker than it was.” He paused. “I think you also placed the stylus on the boy, or arranged for it to be placed. I cannot prove that specific act. I do not need to.”

The hall was quiet except for the river sound—the gate-hinge, very distant, marking the hour in its unreliable way.

Hrodmund looked at Theudebald. Then he looked at the door. Then he appeared to reach a calculation, and the calculation appeared to come out in favour of a different approach.

“I acted in accordance with the interests of Burgundian land-right,” he said, and there was something almost dignified in the attempt. “The Gallo-Roman families hold land by old imperial titles that—”

“You acted in accordance with your family’s interests,” Theudebald said. It was the first time he had spoken. “Which is a different category.”

Sigibert’s Note: What follows is the part of the account that Holmar asked me twice to consider whether to include. I told him I would record it and he could decide. He decided to tell me, the second time, that the decision was mine, and I choose to record it because I believe it is the part that is most true, and because I suspect that what is most true is often what subsequent records omit.

Holmar walked home in the thinning fog, which by the mid-morning hour had begun to shred along its upper margin into a pale winter sky. Ansgar walked beside him and was, for a time, quiet—a quality Holmar valued in him more than he had ever explicitly said.

“Hrodmund will be stripped of the duty-key position,” Ansgar said, eventually. “What else?”

“What else depends on Theudebald’s judgment. The case against Hrodmund is clear but the case against Richomer is more complicated—he filed a document, and though I believe the document is also falsified, proving that requires access to the original witnesses, two of whom are dead. Richomer will argue that he received the document in good faith. Perhaps he did. It is not impossible that Hrodmund created the entire scheme without his knowledge.”

“Do you think that’s true?”

Holmar considered the fog-remnants over the river. A cormorant was standing on the far bank with its wings extended, which was a habit of the bird’s for drying its feathers, and which Holmar found he watched every time he saw it with a slight admiration for the creature’s total commitment to a practical necessity.

“I think it is probably not true,” he said. “But I also think that the destruction of Richomer—who is a Burgundian lord with three villages and thirty armed dependants—over a land-dispute with a Gallo-Roman family who will keep their land regardless of how the matter resolves, would create a local faction-problem that would concern the court for the next five years. Theudebald knows this.”

“So Richomer goes free.”

“Richomer loses his claim. That is not nothing. The Carantii keep their property. Hrodmund loses his position and, I expect, some land.” He paused. “There is a version of justice that would pursue every thread to its conclusion regardless of the fabric it unravels. And there is a version that preserves the fabric while cutting out the damage it can reach. The court practices the second version, as courts must. I am not the court.”

Ansgar was quiet again for a moment. “Is that what the law is for? Preserving the fabric?”

“The law is for predictability,” Holmar said. “Predictability allows people to plan their lives. Absolute justice, pursued without limit, makes everything unpredictable—because a perfectly just inquiry may destroy ten innocent people in the course of exposing one guilty one, and no one can know in advance who will be destroyed. I am not comfortable with this. But I am a record-keeper, not a philosopher, and I have found that the difference between those two professions lies precisely in whether you can live with unresolved questions.”

He stopped at the gate of his own house and looked back at the river, where the cormorant had closed its wings and plunged.

“What I know,” he said, “is that Wulfo is not in a locked room. Carantius has his land. The document has been corrected and re-registered. And Hrodmund will not hold a duty-key in this court again.” He opened the gate. “Record it correctly, Ansgar. Put down what was done and what was found and who was exonerated. The rest—what was considered and what was set aside—you can leave to the margins, where such things generally live.”

Sigibert’s Final Note: I have copied the above from Holmar’s own account, dictated over two mornings in the winter that followed these events. I add only the following: Holmar continued in the chancery’s service for a further nine years, after which age and a chest ailment retired him to a house near the river that he had purchased with his savings, where he lived four years more, dying, as his family tended to do, in his bed and at a reasonable hour, with his documents in order.

I have annotated where I found error or obscurity. Where I have found neither, I have tried to restrain myself from the archivist’s common temptation to annotate regardless, which is the vice of those who understand the importance of records without possessing the kind of mind that knows what to put in them and—more importantly—what to leave out.

His eyes, even in age, were remarkable. He noticed everything. He chose, very carefully, what to write down.

Children:

- Holmar the Frank — see next entry.

HOLMAR THE FRANK

~460 AD — Frankish territory

By Holmar the Frank’s generation, the family had completed its absorption into the Frankish world — the Gothic origins faded, the Rhine crossing two generations past, the name now sounding native to the country north of the Loire where the Franks were consolidating their hold on what had been Roman Gaul. He was a Frank in the way that his ancestors had been Romans and before that Greeks and before that Phoenicians: thoroughly, for as long as it lasted, and with one eye always on the horizon.

He administered land in the service of a Frankish noble house, a role that combined the functions of estate manager, legal officer, and intelligence agent — for the assessment of neighbouring estates, the loyalty of tenants, and the intentions of rival lords was not separable, in that world, from the daily business of keeping an account. He had his father’s gift for reading documents and his grandfather’s gift for reading men, and he applied both to the service of a family that was not his own with the professional detachment of someone who understood that competence was its own form of security.

The account that follows was recorded by Marcus, a notary in the household of Childeric, who kept careful notes of court proceedings and private consultations alike. His record is partial, shaped by what he was permitted to observe and by what he understood of the matters before him, yet it preserves with fidelity the sequence of events and the manner in which Holmar conducted himself.

The death of Wulfric the steward

Recorded by Marcus, notary to the household of Childeric, lord of the eastern lands, in the year of our Lord four hundred and ninety-two. What I have set down here is what I was permitted to observe. What I have omitted, I omit for reasons that will become apparent to any careful reader.

The body was found at the boundary stone between the estate of Childeric and the estate of his neighbour Gundomar — found, it was said, in the grey hour before dawn, by a swineherd who had no business being there and who could not satisfactorily explain what business he did have. The dead man was Wulfric, who had served Gundomar as steward for eleven years, a careful man, thin-fingered and methodical, known throughout the district for the precision of his accounts and the smallness of his personal habits. He had been struck once across the back of the skull. His scrip was intact. His boots — good boots, better than his station warranted — were still on his feet. Nothing had been taken from him, which is the first thing a man notices, and the first thing a careful man notes as significant.

It was Gundomar himself who came to Childeric with the report, arriving before the morning had properly established itself, his horse sweated and his expression arranged into the correct proportions of grief and outrage. He named a tenant of Childeric’s outer fields — a man called Hesso, who had been in dispute with Wulfric over the grazing line for the better part of two seasons — as the likely culprit. The logic was plain, he said. The boundary stone was the seat of the quarrel. The quarrel was Hesso’s. Hesso therefore was the man.

Childeric sent for Holmar.

I had seen Holmar at work before, and I had learned by then not to mistake his stillness for inattention. He sat in the hall and received Gundomar’s account without expression, his long hands resting flat on the table before him, his eyes moving between Gundomar’s face and the middle distance with the regularity of a man reading alternate lines of a document. When Gundomar had finished, Holmar thanked him with a courtesy so precisely calibrated as to contain no warmth whatsoever, and asked three questions.

He asked: at what hour had Gundomar last spoken with Wulfric the previous day?

He asked: had Wulfric carried any documents with him when he departed the estate?

He asked: was it usual for Wulfric to walk that road after dark?

Gundomar answered the first question with confidence — mid-afternoon, after the counting of the autumn tithe — and the second with dismissiveness — a steward always carried papers, this was unremarkable — and the third with a slight rearrangement of his features that I noted and that Holmar, I am certain, noted before me.

Not usual, Gundomar said. But Wulfric was a man of his own habits.

Holmar thanked him again, with the same precise courtesy, and asked to see the body.

The boundary stone stood at the edge of a woodlot, where the tree line broke and the cleared land of both estates met in a narrow strip of disputed pasture. The grass was wet. The morning had produced a low mist that sat in the hollows and made the distances uncertain. I had followed Holmar out of professional duty and found myself regretting the decision as the cold settled into my writing hand.

Holmar crouched beside the dead man for a long time without speaking. He examined the wound — that single blow, delivered from behind and above, by a right-handed man of ordinary height striking someone who had not been running, had not turned, had perhaps been standing still and waiting. He examined the boots. He examined the scrip, lifting from it three wax tablets and a folded skin of parchment, which he read in sequence with the same neutral attention he would have given to a bill of sale, then replaced without comment. He examined the ground around the body in expanding arcs, moving outward from the stone in a pattern that, from a distance, looked aimless and was not.

When he stood, he turned to me with the expression of a man who has finished reading a letter and understood every word of it.

“The swineherd,” he said. “Where does he live?”

The swineherd was a frightened man, which was not in itself remarkable. A man who discovers a corpse at a boundary stone before dawn has reasons for fright that require no elaboration. But Holmar had developed a theory about frightened men — he shared it with me as we walked, in the manner of a craftsman explaining his tools to an apprentice who has expressed no interest in the craft.

“There are two kinds of frightened men,” he said, his long stride carrying him easily over the wet ground while I laboured to keep pace. “Those who are frightened by what they have seen, and those who are frightened by what they have done. The second type does not look at you when they speak of the thing. The first type cannot stop looking at you, because they want to confirm that you believe them.”

“And which type is the swineherd?”

“I haven’t spoken to him yet,” Holmar said, with a patience that was not unkind. “I am explaining the distinction so that you will know which to observe for.”

The swineherd looked at us the entire time he spoke. He was a small man who smelled of woodsmoke and the animals he kept, and he could not have arranged his expression into deception if his life had depended upon it, which — in those days, in that territory — was a real possibility and not a figure of speech. He had found the body. He had not been there to find it — he had been, he admitted in the reddening manner of a man confessing to something minor in the shadow of something vast, meeting a girl from Gundomar’s milk-house at the woodlot edge, a thing that had been happening since midsummer and that his wife did not know about. He had come to the boundary stone and found Wulfric already cold.

He had not, he swore it, seen anyone. But he had, when pressed, heard something — a horse, moving away at a walk through the woodlot, deeper into Gundomar’s land. This he had not mentioned because he had not wished to be noticed having been where he was.

Holmar thanked him and advised him, with a gravity that the swineherd found disproportionate to the situation, to tell his wife about the girl before someone else found occasion to.

We returned to the hall of Childeric, and Holmar asked for the boundary records.

This is where I must make a confession of professional embarrassment. I had been present at the production of those records two seasons prior. I had copied three of them myself. I knew their contents, or believed I did, in the way that a man who has written a thing believes he has read it. Holmar proved to me, in the space of one hour, the difference between transcription and comprehension.

The disputed boundary was not simply a matter of where a stone stood. It was a matter of what a stone certified. Eleven years ago, before Wulfric had come into Gundomar’s service, Gundomar had borrowed a significant sum from a consortium of merchants in the manner common to lords who require immediate silver and prefer to defer the consequences — pledging future tithe revenues from three of his northern fields as security, with a schedule of repayment that had been carefully documented and as carefully neglected. The debt had passed through hands. It had been restructured twice. And two seasons ago, it had landed, through the mechanism of one merchant’s death and another’s acquisition of his papers, in the possession of Wulfric himself — who had, in the methodical way of a man who understood accounts, been quietly assembling the documentation necessary to present a legal claim.

“He was going to the boundary stone,” Holmar said, not to me but as though speaking a thing he was confirming for himself, “because the boundary stone is where such matters are formally presented under the old custom. A creditor may meet a debtor at the boundary of his estate and present the claim before witnesses. Wulfric had assembled his witnesses — there were two, noted in his personal tablets, who were to meet him there at dawn.”

“Then they are suspects,” I said, because something had to be said.

“They are frightened men who went home when they found the stone unattended and the hour past and thought better of their involvement. I will speak to them if you require it, but I do not require it.” He set the parchment down. “Gundomar knew what Wulfric was carrying. He knew the hour. He knew the road, because it is his road — Wulfric could not have reached that stone without walking through Gundomar’s eastern woodlot, which means Gundomar knew, or could have known, precisely when and where his steward would be standing still, in the dark, waiting.”

He paused. The lamp between us smelled of rendered fat, and the hall had grown very quiet in the way halls grow quiet when the fire settles and the night deepens.

“The blow,” Holmar continued, “was patient. A man who fears being found out strikes in panic — multiple blows, uneven, driven by feeling. This was one blow. Precise. The blow of a man who has decided, thought it through, and gone to the place in advance to wait. The scrip was not taken because taking it would have raised precisely the question it was taken to prevent — where are the documents? By leaving it, and leaving it apparently undisturbed, he hoped the tablets would be seen as routine steward’s papers, and that in the confusion of a tenant dispute, no one would read them carefully.”

I looked at the tablets. At the three lines of notation in Wulfric’s small, regular hand, naming the amount, the parties, and the date of intended presentation.

“He didn’t expect anyone to look,” I said.

“He expected Hesso to be arrested before anyone thought to look,” Holmar said. “Which would have been the conclusion of a man following the obvious path. The quarrel was real. The grievance was real. Gundomar simply borrowed it.”

What followed was not a simple matter, and I will not pretend otherwise.

Holmar brought his conclusions to Childeric in private, and I was not present for that conversation, being a notary and not a confidential advisor, and the distinction mattered. What I observed was the outcome: that Gundomar was summoned, that the debt documents were produced, that Gundomar’s expression upon seeing them was the expression of a man who has reached for a weapon and found the scabbard empty. What I observed was that Childeric — who was, in his way, a careful lord — accepted the presentation of evidence with the gravity it warranted, and that certain arrangements were made.

Holmar told me, afterward, what those arrangements were. He did not need to. He chose to, because he understood that I would record this, and that the record was the point.

“Gundomar will pay the debt,” he said. “The full sum, with the customary interest, to a fund that Childeric will administer for the benefit of Wulfric’s widow and children, of whom there are four. Gundomar will cede the disputed pasture to Childeric’s estate, formally and by document, which resolves the grazing question entirely and removes the boundary stone as a site of future contention. And Gundomar will, in recognition of his long service to the stability of the district, be permitted to retain his estate and his position, and this matter will be concluded.”

I looked at him.

“He killed a man,” I said.

“He did.”

“And he will not—”

“Hesso will not be charged,” Holmar said, which was not what I had asked but was what I needed to hear first. “Wulfric’s widow will not spend the winter without silver. The four children will be educated. The debt is paid. The pasture is settled. Gundomar will administer his estate for the remainder of his life in the knowledge that Childeric holds the documentation, that I retain a copy, and that any recurrence of his particular method of resolving financial difficulties will result in a conversation of a very different character.”

“That is not justice,” I said.

“No,” Holmar agreed, without apparent distress. “It is what justice produces when it operates through institutions capable of administering it. The alternative is a feud, a counter-claim, Gundomar’s allies on one side and Childeric’s on the other, and the pasture soaked in a great deal more blood than it can reasonably absorb.” He looked at me with the expression of a man who has made this calculation before and found it does not become easier. “I am not content with it. I am resolved to it. There is a difference, which I would ask you to record.”

I have recorded it.

I have recorded also — because Holmar asked me to, and because the habit of accurate notation is the only virtue a notary can claim without embarrassment — that Hesso the tenant came to Holmar three days later with a gift of bread and a young goat, and that Holmar accepted the bread and returned the goat with the explanation that he had nowhere to keep it.

I have recorded that the milk-house girl married the swineherd’s eldest son the following spring, which simplified several complications at once.

I have recorded that Wulfric’s eldest daughter, who had her father’s thin fingers and her father’s eye for a column of figures, entered Childeric’s household as a junior account-keeper at Holmar’s recommendation, and that she was, by all accounts, exceptionally good at the work.

These things are not in the official record of Childeric’s court, which notes the peaceful resolution of a boundary dispute and the unfortunate unsolved death of a steward on a contested road.

The official record is correct, as far as it goes.

This account goes further. I set it down in the knowledge that no one who matters will read it in my lifetime, and in the hope that someone who needs it will read it after.

— Marcus, notary, in the service of Childeric
Written in the forty-third year of my life, in the hall of the eastern estate, by lamplight, in the cold.

Children:

- Holmar of the Merovingians — see next entry.

HOLMAR OF THE MEROVINGIANS

~490 AD — Frankish territory

Clovis I converted to Catholic Christianity in 496 AD — an act of political calculation as much as spiritual conviction, aligning the Frankish kingdom with the bishops of Rome and the remaining Roman population of Gaul in a single stroke of extraordinary strategic intelligence. Holmar of the Merovingians was a child in the Frankish world when the conversion reshaped it, and he grew up in the court it had transformed — recognising as an adult what he had absorbed as a boy: the gift applied at the scale of kingdoms, the reading of a situation — religious, political, demographic — and the identification of the single decisive act that would reshape it permanently.

He served in the Merovingian administration through the consolidation of the Merovingian realm, managing the correspondence between the court and the ecclesiastical hierarchy that was now the kingdom’s most important political relationship. He was a Catholic, as all sensible men became Catholic in that decade, and he wore the faith with the same comfortable practicality with which his ancestors had worn their successive identities — sincerely enough to function, without allowing it to obstruct his judgement.

The account that follows is preserved in the hand of Lucius, a clerk attached to the royal chancery in Reims, whose duty it was to copy, collate, and retain records of correspondence between the court and the bishops. His notes reflect what he was permitted to see, and the order in which he encountered it, but his record preserves the sequence of events with care, and his uncertainty at certain points is itself part of what survives.

The matter of the Bishop of Nicaea and certain correspondence

Set down as faithfully as I am able, omitting those particulars which, were they to become known, would serve no one.

The fog that settles on Reims in the deep months does not blow in from anywhere. It rises. It comes up from the ground itself, from the river, from the low places between the old Roman walls, as though the earth is exhaling something it has held since the legions left. I have lived in this city seven years and I have not grown accustomed to it. The mornings smell of wet stone and old iron and something else I cannot name — the scent of a place that has been more things than it currently is, and remembers all of them.

It was on such a morning that I first saw the bishop’s mule tied at the gate.

I say the bishop’s mule because I recognised the animal before I recognised its owner — a grey-brown creature of particular ugliness, with a brand on its left flank that I had catalogued in correspondence the previous autumn, when the Bishop of Nicaea had sent three letters in rapid succession regarding the disposition of certain church properties in the valley of the Aisne. I had filed those letters. I remembered the seal. The mule’s brand matched what I had copied. It is the clerk’s habit to notice these things, though I confess I did not, at that time, understand what the noticing was worth.

Holmar was awake.

This is not a thing that can be stated simply, because Holmar’s relationship with sleep was not the relationship most men have with it. He did not retire in the evenings and rise in the mornings. He collapsed, occasionally, into a state resembling death, for periods ranging from two hours to two days, and then erupted into a condition of absolute, frightening attention that made the men around him feel slow and slightly guilty for having ordinary minds. I had been his clerk for fourteen months. I had learned to read the signs: the stillness that was not peace but calculation; the fingers that moved against his thigh as though writing something only he could see; the particular quality of his silence, which was different from the silence of a man thinking and different again from the silence of a man who has finished thinking and is simply waiting for everyone else to catch up.

He was in the latter state when I arrived that morning. He was standing at the window of the correspondence room, looking down at the courtyard, and he said, without turning:

“The Bishop of Nicaea has not slept. He has ridden through the night. He does not wish to be seen arriving.”

“I saw his mule,” I said.

“Yes.” He turned then, and I saw the expression that I had come to understand was not contempt but something more precise — a mild, professional disappointment, as at a calculation that contains no interesting errors. “You saw his mule. What else did you see?”

I had seen a courtyard, a gate, a fog. I said so.

“You saw a man’s travelling cloak folded across the saddle-pack rather than worn, in weather that demands it be worn. You saw boots that have been wiped but not cleaned — the mud is gone from the toe but remains in the seam, which means he stopped outside the city to present himself decently, but did so quickly, without light, without a servant. You saw a letter-case carried under the arm rather than in the pack, which means he does not trust the contents to be out of his reach.” He moved away from the window. “He is frightened of something he has received in writing. He has come to me because he cannot go to the court directly. Whatever is in that letter, he believes it would destroy him if the wrong person saw it first.”

He sat, and picked up a wax tablet he had apparently been working on before I arrived, and appeared to lose interest in the matter entirely.

“Shall I bring him up?” I asked.

“He has been waiting in the antechamber for twenty minutes,” Holmar said, without looking up. “He will have composed himself sufficiently by now. Yes. Bring him up.”

The Bishop of Nicaea was a man of perhaps fifty-five years, broad in the frame, with the hands of someone who had worked before he had prayed and had not entirely forgotten it. He had been a bishop for eleven years. He had navigated the conversion — the great seismic conversion, the one that had remade everything — with, as far as the official record showed, exemplary grace: he had written the right letters to the right men, attended the right councils, and emerged on the correct side of a transformation that had consumed less careful ecclesiastics entirely.

He sat across from Holmar with the posture of a man trying very hard not to appear afraid, which is to say, he was visibly afraid.

“I have received a letter,” he said.

“So I understood,” Holmar said. He was looking at the bishop with that quality of attention I had come to find unnerving — not the attention of a man who is listening, but of a man who is reading. “May I see it?”

The bishop produced it from beneath his cloak. He had not put it in the letter-case, I noticed. He had kept it against his body. Holmar took it, unfolded it, read it once, and set it on the table facing upward. He did not read it again. He looked instead at the bishop.

“Tell me the accommodation they refer to,” he said.

The bishop’s jaw tightened. “I don’t know what accommodation—”

“Bishop.” Holmar’s voice did not change in volume or warmth. It simply became more precise. “I manage the correspondence between this court and the ecclesiastical hierarchy of the Merovingian realm. I have done so for nine years. There is no bishop in this kingdom whose dealings with this court I do not have filed, summarised, and remembered. I ask not because I do not know there is an accommodation, but because I wish to hear your account of it, which will differ from the official record in ways that are relevant.”

A silence. Outside, the fog pressed against the window.

“In the winter of 484,” the bishop said finally, “before the — before everything changed — I received a request from the court of Syagrius. The last Roman ruler in Gaul. He was asking for the Church’s assistance in — in a matter of tax collection. There were Gothic foederati in three valleys who had not paid the Roman levy in four years. He wished the Church to — to apply persuasion. Spiritual persuasion.”

“And you did.”

“I wrote three letters. To three of my priests. Advising them to make clear to their congregations that — that the obligations of God and of Caesar were not in conflict.”

“You used the Church’s authority to collect Roman taxes from Gothic foederati on behalf of a Roman governor,” Holmar said, “two years before Clovis defeated that same ruler and absorbed his territory into the unified realm.”

The bishop said nothing.

“And the letter you have received suggests that this accommodation — service to Rome against the Franks — constitutes a disloyalty to the current regime, and threatens to bring it to the court’s attention unless certain conditions are met.”

“Yes.”

“What conditions?”

The bishop looked at his hands. “The letter names no conditions. It says only that the matter is known, and that the writer will determine, in time, what use to make of the knowledge.”

Holmar was quiet for a moment. Then he picked up the letter again. He did not read it. He held it, turning it in his fingers, feeling its weight and texture with a thoughtfulness that seemed almost medical.

“You may go,” he said.

The bishop blinked. “I — I beg your pardon?”

“I will send for you when I have something to tell you. Go. Eat something. Sleep, if you are capable of it.” He set the letter down. “Leave this with me.”

I have said that Holmar’s relationship with sleep was unusual. His relationship with food was stranger. He did not eat during a case. He did not, as far as I could observe, notice hunger. He sat in the correspondence room for the remainder of that day and most of the night with the bishop’s letter and seven years of filed correspondence spread across the table, and he moved through it with a systematic, unhurried attention that I found both impressive and slightly frightening.

He sent me for specific files. Not categories of files — specific letters, specific dates, specific senders. He knew what he was looking for before he looked for it, which is the only explanation I have for the precision of his requests.

“The letters from the Council of Orléans, the winter session,” he said at one point. “The attendance record and the drafts of the three disciplinary motions.”

I found them. He read them. He set them aside.

“The correspondence from Bishop Remigius regarding the preparation of the baptismal ceremony. All of it. Including the drafts.”

I found those too. There were eleven documents. He read each one and arranged them in a sequence that did not correspond to their dates.

“Now,” he said, holding the bishop’s threatening letter beside one of Remigius’s drafts, “do you see?”

I looked. I saw two letters. I said so.

“You see two letters,” he said, with that particular patience that was not quite patience. “I see one hand attempting to disguise itself as two different hands. Look at the e. The way the second stroke lifts before the scribe intends it to — a habit, not a decision. Look at the spacing between words when a line approaches the right margin. A man who was taught by someone who pressed from the left. Both letters. The same teacher. Possibly — though this I cannot confirm from examination alone — the same man.”

He set both letters down.

“Someone in the ecclesiastical hierarchy of this kingdom,” he said, “wrote a threatening letter to a bishop. They did not write it to extract money or favours. They named no conditions because they want nothing material. They wrote it to test him. To see whether, under pressure, he would run to the court — which would reveal him as a man who distrusts the Church — or whether he would run to the Church — which would reveal him as a man who distrusts the court — or whether he would go to ground and say nothing, which would reveal him as a man with something to hide.” He paused. “He came to me. Which is the correct answer, though the people who sent the letter did not anticipate it as a possibility.”

“Who sent it?” I asked.

He did not answer immediately. He was looking at the attendance record from the Council of Orléans.

“The new order,” he said, “requires loyalty tests. The Church has absorbed a great many men who served the old arrangements — Roman governors, Arian accommodators, political Christians who wore the faith like a travelling cloak and removed it when the weather changed. Bishop Remigius and his circle are building something permanent. They need to know who can be trusted. A threatening letter is a crude instrument, but it is efficient.” He closed the file. “The Bishop of Nicaea is being evaluated, not destroyed.”

“And the accommodation? The Roman tax matter?”

“Is irrelevant,” Holmar said. “Every bishop in this kingdom made accommodations. Constantine knew this. The emperor did not convert because his bishops were pure — he converted because the bishops were useful, and the bishops were useful because they were already embedded in the social fabric of Gaul, which required them to have dealt with whoever was governing that fabric at the time. An accommodation made in 484 to a Roman governor is, in the new political reality, evidence of pragmatism. It is not a crime. It is a qualification.”

He stood, moving to the window. The fog was still there. It had not shifted in twelve hours.

“The letter is not a threat,” he said. “It is an audition.”

The following morning, he sent for the Bishop of Nicaea, and he also sent — this was the part that surprised me, and I record it because it surprised me, which means it was deliberate — a note to the chancery of Bishop Remigius, requesting that the archdeacon responsible for the winter correspondence might be available to attend a brief administrative consultation.

The archdeacon arrived first. He was a young man, thin-faced and careful, with the eyes of someone accustomed to reading other people’s letters and writing nothing incautious in his own. He sat in the antechamber and waited.

The Bishop of Nicaea arrived and was shown to the correspondence room. Holmar kept him standing.

“I know who sent the letter,” Holmar said, without preface. “I know why. And I know what you should do.”

“Who—”

“That is not the useful question. The useful question is what the sending of the letter means, and what your response to it should be.” He moved to the table, where the filed correspondence was still spread. “The accommodation of 484 is not a liability. It is a record of a man who served the institution of the Church consistently across a change of regime — who used the Church’s authority as the Church’s authority, in the service of order and of obligation, regardless of which temporal power was making the request. Bishop Remigius does not need bishops who were loyal to the new capital before the conversion. He cannot find any — there were none. He needs bishops who were loyal to the Church before the conversion, and whose loyalty to the Franks now is an extension of that prior loyalty, not a replacement of it. Your letter of 484 demonstrates exactly that.”

The bishop stared at him. “They are not trying to destroy me.”

“They are trying to determine whether you are destroyable. There is a difference. Men who panic when threatened are dangerous in a hierarchy — they make unpredictable alliances, they seek protection in wrong places, they become liabilities. Men who hold steady, or who find the right counsel, are reliable.” He picked up the threatening letter. “You will not respond to this letter. You will do nothing. Within thirty days, you will receive an invitation — an entirely ordinary administrative invitation — to attend a meeting in Remigius’s chancery. You will attend. You will be offered a role in the new synodal structure that is being built. You will accept it.”

He held the letter out to the bishop, who took it.

“Burn that,” Holmar said. “It has served its purpose.”

The bishop left.

Holmar walked to the antechamber, where the archdeacon was waiting, and sat across from him with a pleasant and entirely uncommunicative expression.

“I have nothing for the chancery today,” he told the young man. “I apologise for the inconvenience. The matter resolved itself.”

The archdeacon looked at him for a moment with those careful, reading eyes, and then nodded, and left.

When he was gone, Holmar returned to the correspondence room and began filing the documents back in their correct places.

“You wanted him to know you knew,” I said.

“I wanted him to know that the channel between this court and the Church runs through me,” Holmar said, “and that anything passing through that channel — including loyalty tests conducted in the form of anonymous letters — is known to me. I wanted him to carry that information back to Remigius’s chancery without my having stated it directly, which would have required a response, which would have been complicated.” He closed a file. “Now Remigius knows that I know how the Church works. And I know that Remigius knows I know. And neither of us will need to discuss it, which is the ideal condition for a working relationship.”

He sat down. He was suddenly, visibly exhausted — the collapse that followed the energy, as predictable as the tide.

“Is there anything else?” I asked.

“There is always something else,” he said, closing his eyes. “But it can wait until I have slept.”

I have set this account down as faithfully as I am able, which is to say I have recorded what I witnessed and have omitted what I was not present for and what I was asked not to include.

The Bishop of Nicaea received his invitation, as Holmar said he would, and was given a position in the new synodal structure, where he served with distinction for fourteen years. The official record of the synodal appointments credits Bishop Remigius with the identification and elevation of his subordinates, which is accurate, as far as it goes.

The threatening letter is not in any archive I have access to. I assume it was burned.

Holmar continued to manage the correspondence between the court and the ecclesiastical hierarchy for a further eleven years, until Clovis’s death, after which he retired to a property in the valley of the Marne and, as far as I know, applied his particular gifts to the management of his own estate, where there was presumably less fog and fewer bishops.

Whether he was the first man to do what he did in the way that he did it, I cannot say. I suspect not. The capacity he possessed — the reading of a situation at every level simultaneously, the identification of the thing beneath the stated thing — is not a skill that can be acquired through study, or I would have acquired it, having watched him for fourteen months and copied more of his correspondence than I care to calculate.

I record this account for the same reason I record everything: because someone should, and because the official record will not.

Lucius, clerk of the royal chancery at Reims.

Children:

- Holmar of Austrasia — see next entry.

HOLMAR OF AUSTRASIA

~520 AD — Austrasia, Frankish Territory

Austrasia — the eastern Frankish realm, centred on the Meuse and Rhine valleys — was the more Germanic half of the Frankish world, rougher and less Romanised than the south, and Holmar grew up in it with the adaptability that had characterised his family across a dozen cultures and five centuries. The Merovingian dynasty was at the height of its power and already, to a careful observer, beginning to show the internal fractures that would eventually produce the long crisis of the rois fainéants — the do-nothing kings — and the rise of the Carolingian mayors of the palace.

Holmar was a careful observer. He served as a legal officer in the Austrasian court, adjudicating disputes between the Germanic and Romanised populations according to the separate legal codes each community used — a role that required him to hold two incompatible systems of law simultaneously in his head and find, between them, outcomes that both parties could accept. He was, in the terminology of a much later age, a remarkably effective mediator. The secret, as he might have explained it, was simple: listen to what each man says, identify what he has not said, and find the fact he is most afraid of you discovering. The rest follows naturally.

The record of these proceedings is preserved in the notes of Aurelius, a court clerk attached to the Austrasian judiciary, who was responsible for copying testimony and preserving rulings. His account reflects both what he observed and the limits of his understanding; he records faithfully the sequence of events, though not always the full significance of what he witnessed at the time.

The Third Account of Holmar of Austrasia

Set down by Aurelius, Clerk to the Judiciary, in the year of our Lord five hundred and fifty-two, in the reign of Theuderic’s successors, at Metz.

There are days in the court at Metz when the hall smells of wet wool and grievance in equal measure. The autumn had come early that year, driving rain sideways off the Rhine plain and through every gap in the stonework that the old Roman builders had not thought to seal — which was, after three generations of Frankish occupancy, a considerable number of gaps. The rushes on the floor had not been changed since the feast of Saint Remigius and they gave off, in the damp, a smell of something ancient and unhappy. The oil lamps along the eastern wall smoked badly in that weather; the tallow was river-rendered, cut with something cheaper, and it left a greasy, animal smell on everything it touched, including the documents one was attempting to read.

I record these details because Holmar, when he arrived that morning, remarked on all of them within the first thirty seconds of crossing the threshold, before he had seen the parties, before he had been told the nature of the complaint, and before, as far as I could determine, he had any particular reason to remark on anything at all.

“The plaintiff,” he said, removing his travelling cloak and handing it to no one in particular — I caught it — “is a man of some property but present anxiety. He arrived before dawn. He has been standing long enough for his left boot to have left a distinct compression in those rushes by the pillar, but he is too agitated to sit. The accused has been here perhaps an hour, no more, and is angry rather than frightened.” He paused. “What is the charge?”

I told him: livestock theft. Specifically, the alleged theft of four oxen, two milch cows, and a quantity of breeding swine from the estate of Lucius Flavianus Carus, Gallo-Roman landowner and citizen, by one Wigbald, a Frankish settler whose holding lay adjacent to the Carus estate along the Moselle.

Holmar looked at me with the expression I had come to associate with what I can only describe as professional satisfaction — a slight tightening at the corners of the eyes, a barely perceptible straightening of the spine. It was the look of a man who has been handed a puzzle and has already, before touching it, guessed the shape of the solution.

“Under which code,” he asked, “has the charge been filed?”

“Salic law,” I said. “Germanic procedure.”

“Naturally,” said Holmar.

He crossed to his seat and heard the matter in full: Carus’s accusation, Wigbald’s furious denial, and the testimony of a herdsman who claimed to have seen cattle driven along the road in darkness.

Then Holmar asked a different question.

“Aurelius,” he said. “The Carus estate. What is its Roman legal status?”

I answered: registered property, governed by Roman law. Which meant that any sale required written documentation.

“And such documents,” Holmar said, “would be held by the registrar at Metz.”

He requested the records.

The hall waited two hours.

When the documents arrived, Holmar found what he expected: a bill of transfer, dated before the alleged theft, recording the sale of precisely those animals to a dealer in Trier.

The livestock had not been stolen.

They had been sold.

Carus confessed under the weight of silence rather than accusation. Debt had driven him to attempt a fraudulent claim under Salic law, where witness and oath might suffice in place of documentation.

Wigbald, who had faced ruin, said only: “You would have destroyed me.”

Holmar dismissed the charge.

He did not pursue the fraud further. He instructed me to record only that the evidence did not support the claim.

Later, when the hall was empty, I asked him why.

“There are webs,” he said, “and there are strands. Today we addressed a strand.”

He paused, then added, as if in afterthought:

“A man has two keys. One opens every lock. One opens none. Which is more valuable?”

I answered incorrectly.

“The one that opens none,” he said, “because it tells you where the locks are.”

He requested the registrar’s records for three further quarters.

“There is a pattern in these frauds,” he said. “I have not yet found the shape of it.”

He went out into the rain.

I wrote what he told me to write.

I also wrote this.

Children:

- Holmar of the Rhine — see next entry.

HOLMAR OF THE RHINE

~550 AD — Rhine, Frankish territory

The second Holmar of the Rhine was not a migrant like the first but a settled man — a man of a particular valley and a particular estate, rooted in the rich agricultural land between Cologne and the sea that had been his family’s territory for three generations. The Frankish world in 550 was more stable than it had been in 400, more organised, more Christian, and considerably more violent in the specific, calculated way of a society that was building the structures of obligation and loyalty from which feudalism would eventually emerge.

He managed the family’s land holdings with the methodical precision that had characterised the line since the scribal schools of Nippur, recording every transaction, every tenancy, every obligation in documents that survived him by generations. He had a particular facility for detecting the falsified account — the altered measure, the understated yield, the inflated claim — that his estate workers quickly learned to respect, or rather to fear. Nothing was taken from him twice. He was a fair man, by the standards of his age, which is to say he was harder than mercy and kinder than the law required.

The surviving account of his administration is preserved in the records of Anselm, a steward in charge of the estate’s accounts, whose duty it was to reconcile the written records with the physical yields of the land. Anselm’s notes are uneven in places, reflecting both his diligence and his uncertainty, but they preserve the sequence of events as they unfolded under Holmar’s direction.

The Death of the Tenant Wulfrid

Recorded by Anselm, steward of accounts, in the year of Our Lord five hundred and seventy-three, set down here in faithful testament to what I witnessed, though I confess my hand trembles even now at the memory of those three days.

The fog came in from the Rhine that autumn as it always does — not the clean cold fog of the high hills but the low, vegetable fog of the river flats, smelling of rot and standing water and the dark silt that the Rhine deposits in its slow dominion over the valley. It settled on the estate like a judgement. The oxen would not move in it. The grain-stores sweated. The rushlight in the counting-house burned orange and thick, and the smoke from the hearth could not find the smoke-hole but pooled instead at the height of a man’s shoulders, so that all work was conducted in a permanent haze, the accounts laid out on the trestle-table appearing and disappearing as the smoke shifted.

It was in this fog, on the morning of the feast of Saint Remigius — which the older tenants still called by the older name they would not say before the priest — that the boy came running from the lower fields to say that Wulfrid the tenant was dead in his doorway.

I had seen Wulfrid the evening prior. He had come to submit his autumn yield-accounts at the hour appointed for such things, which was after the compline bell and before the watch was set — a peculiarity of our lord Holmar’s arrangement that I had always found excessive. Most estates received their tenants’ accounts at the morning hour. Holmar received them at night, by lamplight, because — as he once explained to me with the patient air of a man instructing a child — a man who falsifies figures does so in the morning, when he is fresh and his hope is high. A man who submits figures in the night, after a day’s labour, submits figures that are merely tired. The tiredness, he said, was legible.

Wulfrid had been tired. I remembered it now, standing over his body in the doorway of his holding — the low stone threshold, the body slumped half-in and half-out as though he had been attempting to leave or attempting to return and had not managed either. There was blood, black in the fog-light, at his temple. A blow. The fog moved over him without ceremony.

I sent the boy for Lord Holmar.

He came without haste, which was itself a kind of haste — the deliberate, measuring pace of a man who is already thinking and requires only the evidence to confirm what he has begun to suspect. He was a broad man, Holmar, dark-bearded in the Frankish fashion, with hands that had written more documents than they had held a sword and eyes that gave no indication of what they recorded. He wore no cloak against the fog. He stood over Wulfrid for some time without speaking.

I offered the obvious.

“A robbery, perhaps, my lord. He had his season’s reckoning with him last night. If someone knew—”

“His purse is on his belt,” Holmar said, without looking at the purse. He crouched beside the body. “The blow was delivered from the right side, and from above, which means his attacker was right-handed and taller than Wulfrid, who was not a short man. The strike was precise — one blow, no defensive marks on his hands, which means Wulfrid did not see it coming, which means the attacker was someone Wulfrid did not think to fear.” He looked up at me then. “What time did Wulfrid leave the counting-house last night?”

“After the watch was set. Past the second hour.”

“Who else was in the counting-house at that hour?”

“Myself,” I said. “And Brennus.”

Holmar stood. “Fetch me Wulfrid’s accounts,” he said. “The ones he submitted last night. And fetch me the accounts from the preceding three seasons.”

The counting-house smelled of tallow and old parchment and the particular mustiness of accumulated obligation — every hide of land represented, every basket of rye and measure of barley translated into figures that stood in for the real weight of things. Holmar sat at the trestle with Wulfrid’s accounts spread before him, and the season-accounts beside them, and his own master-ledger open at the relevant holdings, and he read.

After a long time he said: “Anselm. In your experience, how does a tenant farmer’s yield typically move across seasons?”

“It varies,” I said. “Good years and bad.”

He spread three documents side by side. “Look.”

I looked. And then I saw.

The measures were wrong — not in the figures, but in the conversion. A fraction altered, quietly, two years prior. Enough to bleed value without drawing notice.

“Someone,” I said, “altered the conversion rate.”

“And corrected the figures to match,” Holmar said. “Wulfrid was honest. His honesty was used.”

He closed the ledger. “He discovered it last night.”

“Brennus,” I said.

Holmar did not answer. He did not need to.

What followed I record briefly. Brennus confessed. Debt in Cologne. A plan to correct the accounts in time. A man caught between arithmetic and fear.

“I did not plan to kill him,” Brennus said.

“No,” Holmar said. “You planned to stop. And could not.”

Brennus was delivered to the Count’s justice.

On the evening before his removal, Holmar opened the ledger again.

“Wulfrid’s family,” he said.

“His wife and three children remain.”

Holmar placed a figure before me. “This was taken from their labour. It will be returned.”

“The Count’s household—”

“Will receive what the accounts show,” he said.

I corrected the accounts that night.

Children:

- Holmar Northward — see next entry.

HOLMAR NORTHWARD

~580 AD — Domburg, Frisia

Something in this generation turned toward the sea. It may have been a marriage into a Frisian trading family, or simply the pull that the North Sea coast has always exerted on men of restless and acquisitive intelligence — the sense that the horizon there is not a boundary but an invitation. Holmar Northward, as he came to be known in the family's oral tradition, left the Rhine valley of his father and moved north and west toward the coast, toward the flat salt marshes and the grey water and the Frisian merchants who were, in the late sixth century, in the process of building the most extensive trading network in northern Europe.

He arrived with nothing except the family's talent for assessment and the Rhine German's ingrained habit of keeping accurate records, and found that both were exactly what the Frisian trading houses needed. He settled among them, learned their language — which was not difficult, closely related as it was to the Germanic dialects he already knew — and made himself useful in the way that the family had always made itself useful: by being the most reliable reader of a situation in any room he entered.

The account of his work in Frisia is preserved in the notes of Eadric, a Frisian clerk in the service of a coastal trading house at Domburg, whose responsibility it was to record contracts, disputes, and settlements. His record reflects the rhythm of maritime commerce as well as the limits of his position within it, but it preserves with care the sequence of events as they unfolded.

The Account of Holmar Northward

Set Down by Eadric, Clerk to the House of Radulf at Domburg, in the Year of Our Lord 612

The salt smell comes first, always. Before the creak of the dock-timber, before the crying of the gulls, before even the grey light finding its way through the shutter-gaps of the counting house — the salt smell, rolling in off the Frisian sea like a living thing, heavy with brine and the faint rot of low tide and the tar-smoke from the boat-sheds along the quay. I have worked twenty years in this town and I have never ceased to notice it. Holmar, when I put this observation to him once, turned from the window with that characteristic stillness of his and said he noticed it every morning also, but for a different reason.

“It tells me which way the wind is running,” he said, “and therefore which routes are practicable, and therefore which cargoes moved last night, and therefore a great deal else besides.”

He was, at that time, a man of perhaps forty years — though he was vague on the point, dismissing the question with a wave that implied the precise number was the sort of information a well-ordered mind discarded to make room for more useful material. He was lean in the manner of men who forget to eat when they are thinking, with the pale eyes common to the Rhine Germans and a quality of absolute attention that his trading partners found unnerving until they understood it, and then found simply indispensable.

He had arrived in Domburg some three years before I encountered him, during the years when the Frisian trade-network was extending itself south and east along the river routes, and the trading houses here had need of men who could negotiate at the confluence of two commercial cultures without being captured entirely by either.

He spoke Frisian without an accent. This, I learned later, had been a deliberate project; he had spent his first winter on the coast doing nothing else.

“A man who still speaks like a foreigner,” he told me once, “is still being treated like one. I could not afford that luxury.”

I record these preliminary observations because they are necessary to understanding what followed, and because I have been asked by members of the House of Radulf — who know something of what occurred — to set down a true account before the details are lost to the comfortable fog that commercial families prefer to wrap around their more embarrassing episodes.

The official record of this matter, such as it is, credits the resolution to the diligence of Guildmaster Theodric and his men. I witnessed what actually occurred. I will record it accurately.

The factor’s name was Sigebert. He was the son of a family in the Rhine valley with whom the House of Radulf had recently established commercial relations — wool and dye-stuffs moving west, amber and coastal goods moving east — and he had been sent to Domburg to spend a season learning the trade from the inside, which is how such houses built trust in those years. A young man of perhaps twenty-two or -three, with good manners and a conscientious nature and an almost touching belief that the contracts he was given to study represented the full reality of the arrangements they described.

He arrived in late spring. By midsummer he was gone.

The circumstances, as they were initially reported to Theodric, were not immediately alarming. Young men of commercial families sometimes slipped away. Gunwald, an officer of the guild, made inquiries and found nothing indicating violence. He concluded the young man had left voluntarily.

I was present at the House of Radulf when Holmar arrived to review this conclusion. He spent two hours with the contract books. I watched him read in a manner I had not previously observed — not line by line, but in some other fashion that seemed to take in the whole page and its relation to the other pages simultaneously.

“The factor had access to the full ledger,” he said.

“That is so,” I answered.

“Then he has seen something that someone wished he had not seen.”

He requested the contracts of the last two years for the Heligum route and Gunwald’s report. He read the latter standing, as if confirming a conclusion already reached.

“The young man did not purchase passage,” he said. “He was transported as cargo.”

He was absent for four days.

He returned with Sigebert beside him — thin, weathered, but alive.

The following morning, before Radulf and his partners, he explained:

“The supplementary accounts contain entries not present in the primary ledger. This is common. What is uncommon is that the Heligum route contains entries that are not merely discreet but false. Cargo valuations recorded here do not correspond to those of identical goods traded in the same season elsewhere.”

He paused.

“Sigebert is conscientious. He would have noticed. And someone would have been informed.”

He traced the route by wind, by reachable coasts, and by fishermen whose patterns he had spent years learning without immediate purpose.

“A man who stores only the facts he expects to need will always arrive at the scene of the crime to find it empty.”

He found Sigebert in a coastal storehouse used for goods whose documentation was best kept away from Domburg.

He did not name those responsible.

“They are aware that I am aware,” he said. “This is frequently more effective than any proceeding.”

The official record credited Gunwald. I wrote that record. This account I write separately.

Weeks later, I asked Holmar why he left the guilty unnamed.

He stood at the window of his small house near the east quay.

“Sigebert is home,” he said. “He will not speak. The parties involved understand the consequences of repetition.”

He paused.

“Gunwald would have brought charges. Minor men would have been fined. The true authors would have remained untouched and more careful thereafter. Tell me what justice looks like in that accounting.”

I had no answer.

This account is set down by me, Eadric, in the confidence that it will not be read by those who would object to its existence.

The factor Sigebert returned to the Rhine valley. He has not returned to Domburg.

Holmar remains. Each morning the salt comes in off the sea and he tells it which way the wind is running.

Children:

- Holmar of Frisia — see next entry.

HOLMAR OF FRISIA

~610 AD — Dorestad, Frisia

The Frisian traders of the seventh century were the arteries of the northern world — their ships moving between the Rhine delta and the Thames, between the Seine and the Humber, carrying wool and wine and slaves and pottery and the thousand other commodities that a fragmented post-Roman Europe still needed to move between its scattered centres of production and consumption. Holmar of Frisia was born into this world and mastered it with the thoroughness of someone who understood that trade, at its root, is a problem of information — who has what, who needs it, at what price, and whether the man offering to broker the transaction can be trusted.

He could not always be trusted, in the way that all successful merchants cannot entirely be trusted, but he was consistently more honest than his competitors, which amounted to the same thing commercially and was considerably more comfortable to live with. He assessed cargoes with an accuracy that became locally legendary — running his hands over bales of wool in the dim holds of Frisian trading vessels and pronouncing on their quality, origin, and probable value with a confidence that was almost never wrong, and that he could not, when pressed, entirely explain. He simply knew. The family had always simply known.

Among those who kept account of his work was Eadric, a clerk in the service of the port-reeve at Dorestad. Eadric wrote in a careful hand on wax tablets before transferring his notes to parchment when the matter seemed worth preserving. His account of Holmar was not written as praise, nor as suspicion, but as record, though his own interpretations and hesitations are visible in the way he chose to arrange what he saw. What follows is preserved from his surviving tablets and fragments, which came into Holmar’s possession late in life and were copied into the family record.

The Death of Sigvard Hammerhand

The morning the body was found, the mist had not yet left the river.

It lay on the Waal as it always lay in autumn — not the clean sea-fog that rolls in from the west with salt on its tongue, but the inland kind, which has no taste at all and smells of reed-rot and cold mud and the particular damp of timber that has been wet too many times to ever again be truly dry. The trading quarter of Dorestad was still dark inside it. The post-Roman quays — stone footings the Franks had inherited and never quite maintained — emerged from the whiteness at a distance of perhaps fifteen paces, and the hulls of the vessels tied there appeared before their masts, which remained invisible above.

I was at my tablets when the boy came.

He was one of the dock-workers’ sons who served as message-runners for the port-reeve’s office, and he arrived breathless and with river-mud on his legs to his knees, having apparently waded some portion of the distance in his haste. He said that Sigvard Hammerhand, master of the Gull-Wing, had been found dead in the hold of his own vessel by his crew when they went to stow provisions for the morning tide. He said the crew were waiting, and that Radulf, the port-reeve’s officer for the eastern quays, had already gone down.

I took my tablets and my stylus and followed.

The Gull-Wing was a trading vessel of the middle sort — not one of the great cargo ships that ran the Frisian route to the Thames, but substantial enough, with a hold that could take twenty or thirty bales and still leave room for a man to stand and work. She was moored at the third post along the eastern quay, and she smelled, as all the wool-ships smell, of lanolin and salt and the particular mineral tang of fleece that has crossed the North Sea more than once in its existence.

Radulf was already aboard. He is a careful man, Radulf, in the way that careful men are sometimes also slow men, and he was standing at the hatch to the hold with his arms crossed when I arrived, wearing the expression he wears when he has encountered something he does not know how to write down.

The crew stood apart on the quay — four men, Frisian all of them, saying nothing to each other and not quite looking at the ship. One was very young. He had his back to the water.

“Eadric,” said Radulf, when I came aboard. “Write this down.”

Sigvard Hammerhand lay on his back among the wool bales, in the narrow working space between the cargo and the hull. There was a bruise at his temple, dark and already purpling, and the angle of his neck said what the bruise confirmed.

The wool bales were stacked as I remembered them from the previous day, when Holmar had assessed them. Fourteen bales, each sealed with the merchant’s cord and Holmar’s distinctive knot.

I wrote down what I saw. Radulf spoke uncertainty. I said: “Send for Holmar.”

He arrived before the mist had fully lifted.
